## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define local search algorithm. What is a local optimum?**

**Model Answer:**
A local search algorithm is an iterative improvement procedure that, starting from an arbitrary feasible solution $S$, repeatedly moves to a *neighbouring* solution $S' \in \mathcal{N}(S)$ if $S'$ has strictly better objective value $f(S')$.

A solution $S^*$ is called a **local optimum** if no neighbouring solution has a strictly better objective value, i.e., $f(S^*) \geq f(S')$ for all $S' \in \mathcal{N}(S^*)$. Local search halts precisely at a local optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> *(Examiner's note: A 3-mark question expects *both* the algorithmic notion and the halting condition. Students who omit the inequality lose 1 mark.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the approximation ratio achieved by local search for the MAX-CUT problem.**

**Model Answer:**
The vertex-flip local search algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of $\rho = 2$ for MAX-CUT, meaning any local optimum $S^*$ satisfies $\text{OPT}(S^*) \geq \frac{1}{2}\,\text{OPT}$. This ratio is **tight** — there exist graph instances where no local search move can reach the global optimum.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

#### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the generic structure of a local search algorithm. What is a $k$-exchange neighbourhood? Illustrate with an example.

**(b)** Apply local search to the **VERTEX COVER** problem using a 1-exchange neighbourhood. Prove that the resulting algorithm is a **2-approximation**.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Generic Structure of Local Search:**

A local search algorithm has five components:
1. **Feasible solution space** $\mathcal{F}(I)$ over instance $I$.
2. **Initial solution** $S_0 \in \mathcal{F}(I)$.
3. **Neighbourhood** $\mathcal{N}(S)$ — set of solutions reachable by one move.
4. **Selection rule** — best-improvement, first-improvement, or random.
5. **Termination** upon reaching a local optimum.

**$k$-Exchange Neighbourhood Definition:**

Given a solution $S$, the **$k$-exchange neighbourhood** is:

$$
\mathcal{N}_k(S) = \{ S' \in \mathcal{F}(I) \mid S \setminus S' \cup S' \setminus S \text{ has size } \leq k \}
$$

i.e., the symmetric difference $\lvert S \triangle S' \rvert \leq k$.

**Examples:**
- For **Vertex Cover**, $k = 1$ gives the add/remove neighbourhood.
- For **$k$-Median**, $k = 1$ gives single-center swaps; $k = p + q$ gives remove $p$, add $q$.
- For **TSP**, $k = 2$ corresponds to 2-opt (reverse a path segment).

**Trade-off:** Larger $k$ → better approximation ratio but exponentially larger neighbourhood ($\mathcal{O}(n^k)$).

---

##### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm:** Start with $C = V$. While there exists a vertex $v \in C$ such that every edge incident to $v$ has its other endpoint in $C$, remove $v$ from $C$.

**Approximation Proof:**

At the local optimum $C^*$, every vertex $v \in C^*$ has at least one *private edge* $e_v = \{u_v, v\}$ such that $u_v \notin C^*$. (If all of $v$'s neighbors were in $C^*$, removing $v$ would still leave every edge covered, contradicting local optimality.)

**Charging Argument:** Each private edge $e_v$ has at most 2 endpoints in $C^*$ (namely $v$ and possibly $u_v$ if $u_v$ were in $C^*$). Therefore:

$$
\sum_{v \in C^*} \mathbb{1}[e_v \text{ has } u_v \in C^*] \;\leq\; 2 \lvert C^* \rvert
$$

Equivalently, the optimal vertex cover $C_{opt}$ must include at least one endpoint of each of the $\lvert C^* \rvert$ private edges, so:

$$
\lvert C_{opt} \rvert \;\geq\; \frac{\lvert C^* \rvert}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lvert C^* \rvert \;\leq\; 2 \lvert C_{opt} \rvert
$$

Hence the algorithm is a **2-approximation**. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the MAX-CUT problem. Describe a local search algorithm for MAX-CUT using vertex-flip neighbourhood. Show that it terminates in polynomial time.

**(b)** Prove that the local search algorithm for MAX-CUT achieves an approximation ratio of $2$. Is this ratio tight?

---

##### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**MAX-CUT Definition:** Given graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w_e \geq 0$, partition $V$ into $(S, V \setminus S)$ maximizing $\sum_{e \in \delta(S)} w_e$.

**Algorithm:**
1. Start with arbitrary partition $(S_0, V \setminus S_0)$.
2. While there exists $v \in V$ whose flip to the other side strictly increases cut weight, flip it.
3. Halt at local optimum $S^*$.

**Polynomial Termination:**
Each flip strictly increases $\sum w_e$, so no partition repeats. The total improvement is bounded by $\sum_{e \in E} w_e$. If weights are integers, at most $\sum w_e$ flips occur — polynomial.

For arbitrary positive weights, scale weights to integers (with at most polynomially many distinct values) using standard techniques; alternatively, use the **binary encoding length** to argue polynomial time.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Proof of $\rho = 2$:**

For each vertex $v \in V$ at local optimum $S^*$, let $d(v)$ = total weight of edges from $v$ to vertices on its own side, and $\bar{d}(v)$ = total weight to vertices on the other side (i.e., cut edges incident to $v$). Local optimality implies:

$$
\bar{d}(v) \;\leq\; d(v) \quad \forall\, v \in V
$$

Summing over all $v$:

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \bar{d}(v) \;\leq\; \sum_{v \in V} d(v)
$$

By double-counting (each cut edge contributes to exactly one $\bar{d}(v)$):

$$
\text{OPT}(S^*) \;=\; \sum_{v} \bar{d}(v) \;\leq\; \sum_{v} d(v) \;=\; \sum_{e \in E} w_e - \text{OPT}(S^*)
$$

Hence $2\,\text{OPT}(S^*) \leq \sum_{e \in E} w_e$. Since $\text{OPT} \leq \sum_e w_e$:

$$
\text{OPT}(S^*) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{2}\,\text{OPT}
$$

**Tightness:** The ratio $2$ is **tight**. Consider a complete bipartite graph $K_{n,n}$: every edge crosses the cut, so the optimal value is $\lvert E \rvert$. Starting from any partition that moves one vertex, the local search cannot improve — but in fact any partition achieves the optimum. The tight instance arises from graphs with a *small cut improvement gradient* but large global optimum gap, e.g., graphs where the local optimum matches only half the edges of OPT. The PCP theorem and Håstad's result establish that MAX-CUT cannot be approximated better than $\frac{16}{17} + \epsilon$ in general, but for the **local search** specifically, ratio 2 is the best provable bound via this technique. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the **double-counting identity** and attempt to bound each $\bar{d}(v)$ individually by $d(v)$, missing the structural insight that ties per-vertex optimality to global optimality via summation. Always emphasize the **sum-over-vertices** step.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Local Search Approximation Proofs:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting termination proof** — A local search algorithm without polynomial termination is *not* a polynomial-time algorithm. Always prove an upper bound on the number of improving moves.
>
> 2. **Confusing local optimum with global optimum** — Local optima are *not* optimal in general. The approximation ratio captures this gap.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the neighbourhood size** — A 1-exchange neighbourhood is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ but a 5-exchange is $\mathcal{O}(n^5)$. The selection rule must scan in polynomial time.
>
> 4. **Mistaking local search for an exact algorithm** — Local search does *not* solve NP-hard problems exactly. It provides an approximation guarantee based on the *neighbourhood geometry*.
>
> 5. **Forgetting metric assumptions** — For TSP and $k$-Median, the **triangle inequality** is essential. Without it, local search ratios collapse.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Analysis of Local Search Algorithms:**

- [x] A **local search algorithm** iteratively moves to a better neighbouring solution and halts at a **local optimum**.
- [x] The **neighbourhood function** $\mathcal{N}(S)$ determines the power of the local search — larger neighbourhoods give better ratios but increase running time.
- [x] A local optimum satisfies $f(S^*) \geq f(S')$ for all $S' \in \mathcal{N}(S^*)$.
- [x] **Termination**: Bounded by $\mathcal{O}(nW)$ flips where $W$ is the maximum objective value (for integer weights).
- [x] **MAX-CUT** via vertex-flip LS gives $\rho = 2$ — proven by summing the local inequality $\bar{d}(v) \leq d(v)$ and double-counting cut edges.
- [x] **Vertex Cover** via 1-exchange LS gives $\rho = 2$ — proven by mapping each chosen vertex to a *private edge* and noting each edge has at most 2 endpoints in $C$.
- [x] **MAX-3-CNF-SAT** via variable-flip LS gives $\rho = 8/7$ — proven by classifying clauses by number of satisfied literals.
- [x] **$k$-Median** via $(p+q)$-exchange LS gives $\rho = 3 + 2/k$ — proven via swap-graph analysis with triangle inequality.
- [x] **Facility Location** via add/drop/swap LS gives $\rho = 3$ — a classical KTU result.
- [x] **TSP local search** does **not** provide a constant-factor approximation — local optima can be arbitrarily bad.
- [x] The key analytical technique is **double-counting / private-edge arguments** that translate a *local* optimality condition into a *global* approximation bound.
- [x] **PLS-completeness** (Polynomial Local Search) characterizes problems where local optima are hard to find and to escape — a theoretical limitation.
- [x] Local search is **combinatorially elegant**: it requires no linear programming, no semidefinite programming, no sophisticated relaxation — just careful combinatorial accounting.
- [x] Standard results to memorize for KTU ESE: $\rho_{\text{MaxCut}} = 2$, $\rho_{\text{VC}} = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Max3CNF}} = 8/7$, $\rho_{k\text{-Med}} = 3 + 2/k$.
- [x] Always verify: **(i) termination**, **(ii) local optimum existence**, **(iii) approximation ratio bound**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Linear Programming (LP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear Programming (LP)

A **Linear Program (LP)** is a mathematical optimization problem in which we seek to **maximize or minimize a linear objective function** subject to a finite set of **linear equality and/or inequality constraints** over **real-valued (continuous) decision variables**.

Formally, an LP in its most general form is expressed as:

$$
\text{(General LP Form)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{optimize } \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \\
& \text{subject to: } A\mathbf{x} \;\; \{\leq, \; =, \; \geq\} \;\; \mathbf{b} \\
& \qquad\qquad\quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of **decision variables**,
- $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of **objective coefficients** (cost/profit),
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **constraint matrix**,
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **right-hand side (RHS)** vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Approximation Algorithms*, an LP is not the final answer — it is a **relaxation** of an NP-hard Integer Program (IP). Solving the LP gives a *fractional* solution which is then **rounded** to obtain a feasible integral solution whose quality is provably bounded.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **factory manager** who must decide how many units of Product A and Product B to manufacture today.
- Every unit of A yields a profit of ₹50, every unit of B yields ₹30 — this is your **objective**.
- You have only 8 hours of machine time, and each unit of A consumes 2 hours while B consumes 1 hour — this is your **resource constraint**.
- Also, due to a packaging contract, you must produce at least 1 unit of B — this is a **demand constraint**.

Linear Programming answers: *"What is the best production mix?"* by drawing straight-line trade-offs (linearity) and finding the corner of the feasible region where profit is maximum.

> [!NOTE]
> The word *linear* in LP means two things:
> 1. The **objective function** is linear in the variables (no $x^2$, $xy$, $\sqrt{x}$, etc.).
> 2. Every **constraint** is linear in the variables (no nonlinear terms).
>
> If any of these become nonlinear → it is a **Non-Linear Program (NLP)**, not an LP.

### 1.3 Why LP Matters for Approximation Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Many hard combinatorial optimization problems (Vertex Cover, Set Cover, Facility Location, TSP, Knapsack, MAX-SAT, Scheduling, etc.) can be naturally expressed as **Integer Linear Programs (ILPs)**. Solving the ILP exactly is NP-hard, but **dropping the integrality requirement** (allowing fractional values) yields an LP that is solvable in **polynomial time**. The fractional LP optimum then *lower/upper-bounds* the integer optimum, which forms the bedrock of approximation analysis.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Linear Program

Every LP has three structural components:

1. **Decision Variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$:
   - Quantities we control.
   - In LP they are **continuous**: $x_j \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ in standard form).

2. **Objective Function** $z = \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$:
   - A single linear expression to be maximized or minimized.

3. **Constraints** $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$ (or $\geq$ / $=$):
   - A finite set of linear inequalities/equalities that define the **feasible region** $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} : A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b},\; \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\}$.

### 2.2 Standard, Canonical, and General Forms

| **Form** | **Objective** | **Constraints** | **Variables** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Form | Maximize | All $\leq$ inequalities (can be normalized) | $x_j \geq 0$ | Simplex method, duality theory |
| Canonical Form | Maximize | Equalities $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ | $x_j \geq 0$ | Basis/BFS theory |
| Slack Form | Maximize | Equalities with explicit slack variables | $x_j \geq 0$ | Simplex implementation |
| General Form | Minimize or Maximize | Mix of $\leq, =, \geq$ | $x_j$ unrestricted | Original problem statement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Slack Variables:** A constraint $a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 \leq b_i$ can be converted to equality by introducing a *slack* $s_i \geq 0$: $a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 + s_i = b_i$. Slack variables expose the **basic** vs. **non-basic** variable split that the Simplex algorithm exploits.

### 2.3 Geometric Interpretation

The feasible region $\mathcal{P}$ of an LP is a **convex polyhedron** (or polytope if bounded) in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.

- The objective $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ defines a family of **parallel hyperplanes** $c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n = z$.
- As $z$ varies, these hyperplanes *sweep* the space.
- The optimal solution always occurs at an **extreme point (vertex)** of $\mathcal{P}$ — never in the interior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of LP:** *If an LP has an optimal solution, then it has an optimal solution at a vertex of the feasible polyhedron.* This is the reason LPs are polynomially solvable: there are at most $\binom{n+m}{m}$ vertices, and Simplex/LP-type methods efficiently navigate them.

### 2.4 Basic Feasible Solutions (BFS)

For an LP in standard form with $n$ variables and $m$ equality constraints:
- Pick any $m$ linearly independent columns of $A$ → these form a **basis $B$**.
- The corresponding $m$ variables are **basic**, the remaining $n-m$ are **non-basic** (set to 0).
- Solve $B\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b}$ → unique $\mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
- If $\mathbf{x}_B \geq \mathbf{0}$, this is a **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)**, which corresponds geometrically to a **vertex** of $\mathcal{P}$.

### 2.5 LP Duality — The Cornerstone Theory

Every LP (the **primal**) has a companion LP called the **dual**. The Weak and Strong duality theorems form the theoretical foundation of LP-based approximation.

Given the **primal** in standard form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

its **dual** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A^{\top}\mathbf{y} \geq \mathbf{c} \\
& \mathbf{y} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Each primal **constraint** gives a dual **variable** $y_i$.
- Each primal **variable** $x_j$ gives a dual **constraint**.
- The primal RHS vector $\mathbf{b}$ becomes the dual objective coefficients.
- The primal objective $\mathbf{c}$ becomes the dual RHS.

**Weak Duality:** For any primal-feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual-feasible $\mathbf{y}$:
$$
\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}
$$

**Strong Duality:** If both are feasible, optimal values coincide:
$$
\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{\star} = \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}^{\star}
$$

### 2.6 LP Relaxation — The Approximation Algorithm Bridge

An **Integer Linear Program (ILP)** has the same structure as an LP but with integrality: $x_j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.

**LP relaxation** = remove the integrality constraints, allowing $x_j \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$.

Since the feasible region *grows* (or stays the same), we get:

$$
OPT_{LP} \geq OPT_{ILP} \quad \text{(for maximization)}
$$
$$
OPT_{LP} \leq OPT_{ILP} \quad \text{(for minimization)}
$$

The **integrality gap** of an ILP with respect to its LP relaxation is defined as:

$$
\text{Integrality Gap} \;=\; \max_{I} \frac{OPT_{ILP}(I)}{OPT_{LP}(I)} \quad (\text{max problem})
$$

For minimization:
$$
\text{Integrality Gap} \;=\; \max_{I} \frac{OPT_{LP}(I)}{OPT_{ILP}(I)}
$$

A small integrality gap ⇒ good LP-based approximations possible.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| General LP | $\max \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Canonical form |
| Slack transformation | $a_{i1}x_1 + \cdots + a_{in}x_n + s_i = b_i$, $s_i \geq 0$ | Converts $\leq$ to $=$ |
| Vertex count (upper bound) | $\binom{n+m}{m}$ | $n$ vars, $m$ constraints |
| BFS equation | $\mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{x}_N = 0$ | Basis $B \subseteq A$ |
| Weak Duality | $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ | For all feasible $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$ |
| Strong Duality | $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{\star} = \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}^{\star}$ | Optimal pair |
| LP Relaxation Bound (max) | $OPT_{ILP} \leq OPT_{LP}$ | Feasible region enlarged |
| Integrality Gap (max) | $\sup_{I} \frac{OPT_{ILP}(I)}{OPT_{LP}(I)}$ | Worst-case ratio |
| Farkas' Lemma | Exactly one of $\{A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b},\;\mathbf{x} \geq 0\}$ or $\{\mathbf{y}^{\top}A \geq 0,\;\mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{b} < 0,\;\mathbf{y} \geq 0\}$ has a solution | Separation theorem |
| Complementary Slackness | $y_i (b_i - \sum_j a_{ij}x_j) = 0$ for all $i$ | Optimality condition |
| Reduced Cost | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{\top}B^{-1}A_j$ | Used in Simplex pricing |

### 2.8 Engineering / Real-World Utility

LPs are pervasive across disciplines:
- **Operations Research:** Production planning, blending, transportation.
- **Telecommunications:** Network flow, routing, bandwidth allocation.
- **VLSI Design:** Floorplanning, wire-length minimization.
- **Machine Learning:** Linear classifiers (perceptron margin), SVMs (dual form), LP-decoding of error-correcting codes.
- **Economics:** Market equilibrium, game theory (LP-duality ↔ Walrasian equilibrium).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Formulating an LP (Production Planning)

**Problem Statement:**
A furniture company makes **Tables (T)** and **Chairs (C)**. Each table yields ₹200 profit, each chair ₹100. The workshop has only 60 hours of carpentry (a table needs 4 hrs, a chair needs 2 hrs) and 40 hours of painting (a table needs 2 hrs, a chair needs 4 hrs). Demand requires at least 5 chairs. Formulate as an LP.

**Step 1 — Identify decision variables:**
- $T$ = number of tables produced, $T \geq 0$
- $C$ = number of chairs produced, $C \geq 0$

**Step 2 — Write the objective function (maximize profit):**

$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 200T + 100C
$$

**Step 3 — Translate each constraint into a linear inequality:**

Carpentry time:
$$
4T + 2C \leq 60
$$

Painting time:
$$
2T + 4C \leq 40
$$

Minimum chair demand:
$$
C \geq 5
$$

Non-negativity:
$$
T \geq 0, \quad C \geq 0
$$

**Final LP Formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & 200T + 100C \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 4T + 2C \leq 60 \\
& 2T + 4C \leq 40 \\
& C \geq 5 \\
& T \geq 0, \; C \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Solving Graphically (Vertices & Enumeration)

We solve the LP from §3.1 by enumerating the vertices of the feasible polygon.

**Step 1 — Identify candidate vertices.** They are intersections of pairs of binding constraints (treated as equalities).

**Vertex A:** Intersection of $4T + 2C = 60$ and $2T + 4C = 40$.

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4T + 2C &= 60 \\
2T + 4C &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply the second equation by 2:
$$
4T + 8C = 80
$$

Subtract the first equation:
$$
6C = 20 \implies C = \frac{20}{6} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33
$$

But $C \geq 5$ violates, so this vertex is **infeasible**.

**Vertex B:** Intersection of $4T + 2C = 60$ and $C = 5$.

Substitute $C = 5$:
$$
4T + 10 = 60 \implies T = 12.5
$$

So Vertex B = $(12.5,\; 5)$.

**Vertex C:** Intersection of $2T + 4C = 40$ and $C = 5$.

Substitute $C = 5$:
$$
2T + 20 = 40 \implies T = 10
$$

So Vertex C = $(10,\; 5)$.

**Vertex D:** Intersection of $4T + 2C = 60$ and $T = 0$:
$$
2C = 60 \implies C = 30 \quad (\text{but } 2T + 4C = 160 > 40, \text{ violates painting})
$$

Infeasible.

**Vertex E:** Intersection of $2T + 4C = 40$ and $T = 0$:
$$
4C = 40 \implies C = 10
$$

So Vertex E = $(0,\; 10)$. Check $C \geq 5$: ✓.

**Vertex F:** Intersection of $C = 5$ and $T = 0$:
Vertex F = $(0,\; 5)$. Check $4(0)+2(5)=10 \leq 60$ ✓, $2(0)+4(5)=20 \leq 40$ ✓.

**Step 2 — Evaluate objective at each feasible vertex:**

| **Vertex** | $(T, C)$ | $Z = 200T + 100C$ |
|---|---|---|
| B | $(12.5, 5)$ | $2500 + 500 = 3000$ |
| C | $(10, 5)$ | $2000 + 500 = 2500$ |
| E | $(0, 10)$ | $0 + 1000 = 1000$ |
| F | $(0, 5)$ | $0 + 500 = 500$ |

**Step 3 — Identify optimum.**

Maximum is at **Vertex B** $(T, C) = (12.5, 5)$ with **$Z^{\star} = 3000$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using Level Curves:** The objective gradient is $(200, 100)$. The level curve $200T + 100C = z$ has slope $-2$. Pushing this line rightward (increasing $z$) until it last touches the polygon gives exactly Vertex B — confirming optimality.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: LP Relaxation for Vertex Cover (Approximation Bridge)

**Problem:** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, find a *minimum* vertex cover — a smallest set $S \subseteq V$ touching every edge.

**Integer Programming Formulation (ILP):**

Variables: $x_v \in \{0, 1\}$ for each $v \in V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall\, \{u,v\} \in E \\
& x_v \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

This forces at least one endpoint of every edge into the cover.

**LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_v \in \{0, 1\}$ with $x_v \in [0, 1]$ (or simply $x_v \geq 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall\, \{u,v\} \in E \\
& x_v \geq 0 \quad \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve the LP.** The optimal LP solution is **half-integral**: $x_v^{\star} \in \{0, \tfrac{1}{2}, 1\}$.

*Why?* For bipartite graphs, by total unimodularity, the LP already returns integer solutions (LP = IP). For general graphs, the LP may assign $\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 2 — Rounding scheme (classic 2-approximation):**
1. Compute optimal LP solution $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$.
2. Output $S = \{v \in V : x_v^{\star} \geq \tfrac{1}{2}\}$.

**Step 3 — Prove feasibility.** For every edge $\{u,v\} \in E$, the LP constraint $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ ensures at least one of $x_u, x_v \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$. So both can't be below $\tfrac{1}{2}$; at least one endpoint is included. Hence $S$ is a vertex cover. ✓

**Step 4 — Prove approximation ratio.** Every $v \in S$ contributes $x_v^{\star} \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$, so:

$$
|S| = \sum_{v \in S} 1 \leq \sum_{v \in S} 2 x_v^{\star} \leq 2 \sum_{v \in V} x_v^{\star} = 2 \cdot OPT_{LP}
$$

Combined with $OPT_{LP} \leq OPT_{IP}$:

$$
|S| \leq 2 \cdot OPT_{IP}
$$

Therefore the rounding scheme is a **2-approximation algorithm** for Vertex Cover, with integrality gap $\leq 2$.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Step** | **Action** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Formulate combinatorial problem as ILP | Exact model |
| 2 | Drop integrality → LP relaxation | Polynomial-time solvable |
| 3 | Solve LP optimally | Fractional vector $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ |
| 4 | Apply rounding rule | Integral feasible solution $S$ |
| 5 | Bound $\text{cost}(S)$ by $\alpha \cdot OPT_{LP}$ | Approximation factor $\alpha$ |
| 6 | Bound $OPT_{LP} \leq OPT_{IP}$ | Final ratio $\alpha$ |

```mermaid
    stepG --> stepH[Compare cost S with OPT LP and OPT ILP]

    stepI --> stepJ([alpha Approximation Algorithm Certified])

    stepD -.duality gap.-> dualA[Construct Dual LP]
    dualA --> dualB[Obtain Lower or Upper Bound via Weak Duality]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*Define a Linear Program. List the three essential components of an LP with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer:**

A Linear Program (LP) is an optimization problem that seeks to optimize (maximize or minimize) a linear objective function subject to a finite set of linear constraints over continuous real-valued variables.

The three essential components are:
1. **Decision Variables** — quantities under the decision-maker's control, e.g., $x_j \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Objective Function** — a linear expression $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ to be optimized.
3. **Constraints** — a finite set of linear inequalities $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$ (or equalities, or $\geq$) defining the feasible region.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

*State the Weak Duality and Strong Duality theorems of Linear Programming. What is their significance in approximation algorithms?*

**Model Answer:**

**Weak Duality Theorem:** For any primal-feasible solution $\mathbf{x}$ and any dual-feasible solution $\mathbf{y}$ of a primal-dual LP pair, the primal objective value is at most the dual objective value:
$$
\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}
$$

**Strong Duality Theorem:** If both primal and dual LPs have optimal solutions, then their optimal objective values are equal:
$$
\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{\star} = \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}^{\star}
$$

**Significance in Approximation Algorithms:**
- Weak duality provides **cheap lower/upper bounds** for NP-hard combinatorial problems — useful for proving approximation ratios.
- Strong duality gives a **certificate of optimality** for the LP, which often translates directly into a near-optimal integral solution via rounding.
- Dual variables carry **economically meaningful interpretations** (shadow prices), enabling **primal-dual approximation schemes** for problems like Set Cover, Steiner Tree, and Facility Location.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** *Explain with a suitable example the concept of LP relaxation as applied to a combinatorial optimization problem. Demonstrate how solving the relaxed LP leads to a 2-approximation for the Vertex Cover problem. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Consider the following LP:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & 3x_1 + 5x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \leq 6 \\
& 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
*Solve it using the graphical method. Identify all vertices and state the optimal solution with its objective value. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**LP Relaxation Concept:**
Given an Integer Linear Program (ILP) modeling a combinatorial optimization problem, we drop the integrality constraints $x_j \in \mathbb{Z}$ and replace them with $x_j \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $x_j \in [0,1]$). The resulting LP is polynomially solvable and yields a *fractional* solution whose objective value bounds the integral optimum.

**Vertex Cover ILP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1, \quad \forall \{u,v\} \in E \\
& x_v \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Relaxation (replace $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_v \in [0,1]$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1, \quad \forall \{u,v\} \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1, \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rounding Rule:** Let $\mathbf{x}^{\star}$ be the optimal LP solution. Define the integral cover
$$
S = \{\, v \in V : x_v^{\star} \geq \tfrac{1}{2} \,\}.
$$

**Feasibility Proof:** For every edge $\{u,v\}$, the LP constraint forces $x_u + x_v \geq 1$. Hence $\max(x_u, x_v) \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$, so at least one endpoint lies in $S$. Therefore $S$ is a valid vertex cover. ✓

**Approximation Ratio:** Each $v \in S$ satisfies $x_v^{\star} \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$, so $1 \leq 2 x_v^{\star}$. Therefore:
$$
|S| = \sum_{v \in S} 1 \leq \sum_{v \in S} 2 x_v^{\star} \leq 2 \sum_{v \in V} x_v^{\star} = 2 \cdot OPT_{LP} \leq 2 \cdot OPT_{IP}.
$$
Hence $|S| \leq 2 \cdot OPT$, yielding a 2-approximation. ∎

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Plot constraints.**

Constraint 1: $x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$ — passes through $(6,0)$ and $(0,6)$.
Constraint 2: $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8$ — passes through $(4,0)$ and $(0,8)$.
Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Identify vertices.**

| **Vertex** | **Constraints Active** | **Coordinates** |
|---|---|---|
| O | $x_1 = 0,\; x_2 = 0$ | $(0, 0)$ |
| P | $x_2 = 0,\; 2x_1 + x_2 = 8$ | $(4, 0)$ |
| Q | $x_1 + x_2 = 6,\; 2x_1 + x_2 = 8$ | $(2, 4)$ |
| R | $x_1 = 0,\; x_1 + x_2 = 6$ | $(0, 6)$ |

**Step 3 — Compute $Q$.**

Solve:
$$
x_1 + x_2 = 6
$$
$$
2x_1 + x_2 = 8
$$

Subtract:
$$
x_1 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 4.
$$

So $Q = (2, 4)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate objective at each vertex.**

| **Vertex** | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| O | $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| P | $(4, 0)$ | $12$ |
| Q | $(2, 4)$ | $6 + 20 = 26$ |
| R | $(0, 6)$ | $30$ |

**Step 5 — Identify optimum.**

Maximum objective value is **$Z^{\star} = 30$** at vertex **$(x_1, x_2) = (0, 6)$**.

(Note: Vertex $R$ is the unique optimum here — even though $Q$ might initially appear promising, the high coefficient on $x_2$ shifts the optimum toward the $x_2$-axis.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Write the dual of the following LP. Then state and explain Weak Duality, Strong Duality, and Complementary Slackness. (7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & 4x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \leq 10 \\
& 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** *Consider the Set Cover problem: Given a universe $U = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ and sets $S_1 = \{1,2,3\}$, $S_2 = \{2,4\}$, $S_3 = \{3,4\}$, $S_4 = \{4,5\}$. (i) Formulate the Set Cover ILP. (ii) Write its LP relaxation. (iii) Solve the LP by inspection and apply the standard rounding rule $x_S \geq \tfrac{1}{f}$ where $f$ is the maximum set frequency. State the resulting cover and its cost. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Primal:** $\max 4x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3$ s.t. $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$, where

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Dual Construction Rules:**
- 2 primal constraints → 2 dual variables $y_1, y_2 \geq 0$.
- 3 primal variables → 3 dual constraints.
- Primal RHS $\mathbf{b}$ → dual objective coefficients.
- Primal objective $\mathbf{c}$ → dual RHS.

**Dual LP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & 10 y_1 + 8 y_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_1 + 2 y_2 \geq 4 \quad (\text{from } x_1) \\
& y_1 + y_2 \geq 3 \quad (\text{from } x_2) \\
& y_1 \geq 2 \quad (\text{from } x_3) \\
& y_1, y_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theorems:**

1. **Weak Duality:** For every primal-feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual-feasible $\mathbf{y}$:
$$
4x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3 \leq 10y_1 + 8y_2.
$$
This provides an immediate lower bound on any maximization dual — used in approximation analysis.

2. **Strong Duality:** If the optimal primal value is $Z_P^{\star}$ and the optimal dual value is $Z_D^{\star}$, then $Z_P^{\star} = Z_D^{\star}$. Both LPs attain the same optimum.

3. **Complementary Slackness:** At optimality:
$$
y_i^{\star} \cdot (b_i - \sum_j a_{ij} x_j^{\star}) = 0, \quad \forall i.
$$
That is, a tight primal constraint corresponds to a positive dual variable, and a slack primal constraint corresponds to $y_i = 0$.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**(i) ILP Formulation.** Variables $x_j \in \{0,1\}$ for $j = 1, \ldots, 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 1) \\
& x_1 + x_2 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 2) \\
& x_1 + x_3 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 3) \\
& x_2 + x_3 + x_4 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 4) \\
& x_4 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 5) \\
& x_j \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) LP Relaxation.** Replace $x_j \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_j \leq 1$.

**(iii) Solve by inspection.** By symmetry of constraints and the small instance, note:
- Element 1 is only in $S_1$ → must have $x_1 \geq 1$, so $x_1 = 1$.
- Element 5 is only in $S_4$ → $x_4 \geq 1$, so $x_4 = 1$.
- Elements 2, 3, 4 are covered.

Thus $x_2 = x_3 = 0$ are feasible, and the optimal LP value is **$OPT_{LP} = 2$**.

**Maximum set frequency $f$:** Element 4 appears in $S_2, S_3, S_4$ → $f = 3$.

**Rounding rule $x_S \geq \tfrac{1}{f} = \tfrac{1}{3}$:** Pick all sets with $x_S^{\star} \geq \tfrac{1}{3}$. Since $x_1 = x_4 = 1$, we include $S_1$ and $S_4$.

**Resulting Cover:** $\{S_1, S_4\} = \{\{1,2,3\}, \{4,5\}\}$ — covers all of $U$. Cost = **2**.

**Ratio check:** Optimal integral set cover has cost 2 (e.g., $\{S_1, S_4\}$). So we achieved the *exact* optimum here.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse LP optimum with ILP optimum.** Always remember: $OPT_{LP} \leq OPT_{ILP}$ (for minimization). Forgetting this inequality inverts your approximation bound.
> 2. **In dual construction,** students frequently mix up the transposed direction. Memorize the rule: *Primal constraint $i$ → Dual variable $y_i$*. Drawing a primal-dual variable table during the exam saves marks.
> 3. **In rounding proofs,** the *critical* inequality is $\sum_{v \in S} 1 \leq \sum_{v \in S} 2 x_v^{\star}$, NOT $\sum_{v \in V} 2 x_v^{\star}$. The "summing over the chosen set $S$ only" detail is where most students lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **For Set Cover,** students often pick the wrong frequency $f$ (use the maximum frequency across *elements*, not sets). Miscomputing $f$ invalidates the $f$-approximation claim.
> 5. **Graphical LP solving** must list *all four* vertices (origin included) and check feasibility — examiners deduct marks for skipping the origin vertex or for misidentifying an infeasible intersection point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to Linear Programming**

- **Definition:** An LP optimizes a linear objective over a polyhedron defined by linear constraints and continuous (real) variables.
- **Three Components:** Decision variables $\mathbf{x}$, objective function $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$, constraint matrix $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$.
- **Standard Form:** Maximize $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ subject to $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$.
- **Slacks:** Convert $\leq$ inequalities into equalities by adding non-negative slack variables; this exposes basic vs. non-basic structure.
- **Geometric Fact:** Feasible region = convex polyhedron (or polytope if bounded).
- **Fundamental Theorem of LP:** Optimal solution always exists at a *vertex* (extreme point / basic feasible solution).
- **Vertex Count Bound:** At most $\binom{n+m}{m}$ vertices.
- **BFS:** Setting $n - m$ variables to zero and solving for the remaining $m$ yields a candidate vertex.
- **Primal-Dual Pairing:** Constraint $i$ ↔ dual variable $y_i$; variable $j$ ↔ dual constraint $j$; objective $\mathbf{c}$ ↔ dual RHS; RHS $\mathbf{b}$ ↔ dual objective; max ↔ min.
- **Weak Duality:** $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ — gives bounds for free.
- **Strong Duality:** $\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{\star} = \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{y}^{\star}$ — certificates of optimality.
- **Complementary Slackness:** Tight primal ↔ non-zero dual; slack primal ↔ zero dual.
- **LP Relaxation:** Drop integrality; solvable in polynomial time.
- **Integrality Gap:** Worst-case ratio of $OPT_{ILP}$ to $OPT_{LP}$ — measures how much we lose by relaxing.
- **Rounding Schemes:** Round fractional LP solutions to integers; bound cost via factor $\alpha$.
- **Vertex Cover 2-Approximation:** Round at threshold $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
- **Set Cover $f$-Approximation:** Round at threshold $\tfrac{1}{f}$ where $f$ = max element frequency.
- **Why LP Matters for Approximation:** Provides polynomial-time *upper bounds* (for min problems) or *lower bounds* (for max problems) on the integral optimum, plus structure for rounding arguments.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LP Relaxation of Combinatorial Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LP Relaxation of Combinatorial Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Programming (LP) Relaxation** is the process of converting a **discrete** combinatorial optimization problem — typically formulated as an **Integer Linear Program (ILP)** with integrality constraints on the variables — into a **continuous** Linear Program by replacing the constraint *"x ∈ ℤ"* (integer) with the weaker constraint *"x ≥ 0"* (non-negative real). The resulting LP is a *relaxation* of the original problem because it has a strictly larger (or equal) feasible region, and therefore a **better (or equal) objective value**.

**Mathematical Construct:**

Given an Integer Linear Program of the form:

$$\text{ILP: } \min \{ \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} : A\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x} \in \{0,1\}^n \}$$

its LP relaxation is defined as:

$$\text{LP: } \min \{ \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} : A\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{0} \leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{1} \}$$

where the binary constraint $\mathbf{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$ is **dropped** and replaced by the **box constraint** $\mathbf{0} \leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Consequence for Approximation:** Because the LP's feasible region strictly contains the ILP's, every feasible ILP solution is also an LP-feasible solution. Therefore:
> $$\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$$
> The LP optimum is a **lower bound** for minimization problems (and an upper bound for maximization). This gap is the engine that powers all LP-based approximation algorithms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Loosened Tournament Rules"

Imagine a strict chess tournament where each match must end with a clear winner (binary: win = 1, lose = 0). Now imagine the organizers **relax** the rules so players can score **fractional points** (e.g., 0.7 win, 0.3 draw — measured on a continuous 0-to-1 scale).

- The **integer program** is the strict tournament — you must be the full winner.
- The **LP relaxation** is the loosened tournament — fractional performance is allowed.
- The **LP optimum** is the best achievable *fractional* score, which is **always at least as good** as the best possible strict tournament score.
- **Rounding** (the next step in the algorithmic pipeline) is then like *converting fractional scores back to whole numbers* — paying a small penalty for the conversion.

This "loosening, then rounding" strategy is the cornerstone of LP-based approximation.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Approximation Algorithms

Many NP-hard combinatorial problems (Vertex Cover, Set Cover, Facility Location, TSP, Knapsack, Max-SAT) have natural ILP formulations. Solving an ILP exactly is NP-hard in general, but **solving the LP relaxation is polynomial-time** (via the Ellipsoid method or interior-point methods). The trick is:

1. Solve the LP (polynomial, easy).
2. Round the fractional solution to a valid integer solution (clever algorithm design).
3. Bound the loss incurred during rounding — this bound becomes the **approximation ratio**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an LP Relaxation — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of an LP relaxation follows a fixed logical pipeline:

### Step 1 — Formulate the Combinatorial Problem as an ILP
- Define **indicator (decision) variables** $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ — one for each "atomic" choice.
- Encode the objective as a linear function $\mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x}$.
- Encode the problem constraints as a system of linear inequalities $A\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{b}$.
- **All constraints must be linear** (no quadratic, no logical OR/AND, no "either/or").

### Step 2 — Identify the Integrality Constraints
- The constraints $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ are the only "hard" constraints.
- These force the solution into the corners of the unit hypercube $\{0,1\}^n$.

### Step 3 — Relax the Integrality Constraint
- Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$ (the *box constraint*).
- This expands the feasible region from $2^n$ vertices to a convex polytope with infinitely many points.

### Step 4 — Solve the LP via Polynomial-Time Methods
- Use the **Simplex method** (practical, exponential worst-case) or the **Ellipsoid method** (polynomial worst-case, theoretically clean).
- Modern solvers also use **interior-point methods** and the **barrier method**.

### Step 5 — Round the Fractional Solution
- Apply a rounding scheme (deterministic or randomized) to convert the fractional $\mathbf{x}^*$ into a valid binary $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$.
- Prove that $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ is feasible and that its objective value is within a known factor of OPT.

## 2.2 The Integrality Gap — The Measure of Relaxation Loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrality Gap (IG):** For a minimization problem, the *integrality gap* of an LP relaxation is the supremum (over all instances $I$) of the ratio:
> $$\text{IG} = \sup_{I} \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}(I)} \geq 1$$
> For maximization problems, the ratio is inverted. The integrality gap provides a **lower bound on the best possible approximation ratio** achievable by any rounding scheme applied to this LP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning / Condition** | **Notes for Minimization** | **Notes for Maximization** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$ | Integer (binary) solution vector | Original hard problem | Original hard problem |
| $\mathbf{0} \leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{1}$ | LP relaxation box constraint | Continuous relaxation | Continuous relaxation |
| $\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$ | LP bound on IP optimum | LP lower-bounds IP | LP upper-bounds IP |
| $\text{IG}_{\min} = \max_I \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}(I)}$ | Integrality gap (min problem) | $\text{IG} \geq 1$ | N/A |
| $\text{IG}_{\max} = \max_I \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)}$ | Integrality gap (max problem) | N/A | $\text{IG} \geq 1$ |
| $\alpha$-approximation via LP rounding | Rounding loss bounded by factor $\alpha$ | $\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}} \leq \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}$ | $\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$ |
| $H_n = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n}$ | Harmonic number (Set Cover bound) | Greedy/LP bound | N/A |
| $\mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x}$ | Linear objective function | Minimize or maximize | Minimize or maximize |
| $A\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{b}$ | Linear constraint matrix | All entries real-valued | All entries real-valued |
| Vertex Cover LP IG | $2$ (general graphs), $1$ (bipartite) | Rounding gives 2-approx | N/A |
| Set Cover LP IG | $\Theta(\ln n)$ | Rounding gives $H_n$-approx | N/A |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LP relaxation is not merely a theoretical construct — it powers critical engineering systems:

- **Telecommunications Network Design:** Vertex Cover and Set Cover LPs are used to optimally place cellular towers and base stations.
- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Knapsack LP relaxation underpins inventory optimization in warehouses.
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Facility Location LP relaxations guide the placement of circuit modules to minimize wire length.
- **Airline Crew Scheduling:** Set Cover LP relaxations schedule crews to flights with minimum cost.
- **Bioinformatics:** LP relaxations are used in protein folding and phylogenetic tree construction.

The ubiquity of LP relaxation arises because **modern LP solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, COIN-OR) can solve LPs with millions of variables in seconds**, making LP-based approximation algorithms practical for real-scale industrial problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Vertex Cover LP Relaxation

The **Minimum Vertex Cover** problem: Given an undirected graph $G = (V,E)$, find the smallest subset $C \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $C$.

### 3.1.1 The Integer Linear Program (ILP)

Assign a binary variable $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ to each vertex $v \in V$, where $x_v = 1$ if $v$ is selected in the cover.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ILP-VC)} \quad \min & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{subject to } & x_u + x_v \geq 1 && \forall\, \{u,v\} \in E \\
& x_v \in \{0,1\} && \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation of constraints:** For each edge $(u,v)$, at least one of $u$ or $v$ must be in the cover (so $x_u = 1$ or $x_v = 1$, hence $x_u + x_v \geq 1$).

### 3.1.2 The LP Relaxation

We drop the integrality constraint $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ and replace it with the continuous box constraint $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(LP-VC)} \quad \min & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{subject to } & x_u + x_v \geq 1 && \forall\, \{u,v\} \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 && \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

This LP is a **polytope** (a bounded convex region in $\mathbb{R}^{|V|}$) and can be solved in polynomial time.

### 3.1.3 Solving the LP for a Concrete Graph

Consider the triangle graph $G = K_3$ with $V = \{a, b, c\}$ and $E = \{(a,b), (b,c), (a,c)\}$.

The LP becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\min & \quad x_a + x_b + x_c \\
\text{s.t.} & \quad x_a + x_b \geq 1 \\
& \quad x_b + x_c \geq 1 \\
& \quad x_a + x_c \geq 1 \\
& \quad 0 \leq x_a, x_b, x_c \leq 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step A — Symmetry observation:** By symmetry, all three vertices are equivalent, so at the optimum $x_a = x_b = x_c = t$ for some scalar $t$.

**Step B — Substitute:** Every edge constraint becomes $t + t = 2t \geq 1$, hence $t \geq 0.5$.

**Step C — Minimize:** The objective is $3t$, minimized at $t = 0.5$, giving $\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} = 1.5$.

**Step D — Compare with ILP:** The ILP optimum is 2 (you must take at least 2 vertices to cover a triangle — e.g., $\{a,b\}$).

**Step E — Integrality gap on this instance:** $\text{IG} = \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} / \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}} = 1.5 / 2 = 0.75$? Wait — for minimization, IG $\geq 1$, so we should compute the ratio **ILP/LP** to express as the worst-case ratio:

$$\text{IG} = \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}} = \frac{2}{1.5} = \frac{4}{3}$$

Actually, the correct convention (Vazirani textbook) is:

$$\text{IG} = \max_{I} \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}(I)} \quad \text{(gives a number} \leq 1\text{) or its reciprocal depending on convention.}$$

We follow the standard approximation-algorithms textbook convention: **for minimization, IG = $\max_I \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}} \leq 1$**, and the **approximation ratio achievable by LP-rounding** is bounded by $1/\text{IG}$.

For $K_3$: IG = $1.5/2 = 0.75$, and $1/\text{IG} = 4/3$. However, the *worst-case* integrality gap for Vertex Cover over all graphs is **2** (achieved by a bipartite graph like $K_{n,n}$'s odd cycles).

### 3.1.4 The 2-Approximation via Rounding

**Algorithm (LP-Rounding for Vertex Cover):**

1. Solve (LP-VC) to obtain fractional optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$.
2. Define the rounded cover $\tilde{C} = \{ v \in V : x_v^* \geq 0.5 \}$.
3. Return $\tilde{C}$.

**Proof of 2-approximation:**

We must show (i) feasibility and (ii) cost bound.

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Feasibility:} & \quad \text{For every edge } \{u,v\} \in E,\ \text{the LP gives } x_u^* + x_v^* \geq 1. \\
& \quad \text{Thus } \max(x_u^*, x_v^*) \geq 0.5 \text{ (otherwise both} < 0.5 \text{ would sum to} < 1\text{).} \\
& \quad \text{Hence at least one of } u, v \text{ is in } \tilde{C}, \text{ so every edge is covered.} \\
& \\
\textbf{(ii) Cost bound:} & \quad |\tilde{C}| = \sum_{v : x_v^* \geq 0.5} 1 \leq \sum_{v : x_v^* \geq 0.5} 2 x_v^* \\
& \quad \text{(since } x_v^* \geq 0.5 \Rightarrow 1 \leq 2x_v^*\text{)} \\
& \quad \leq 2 \sum_{v \in V} x_v^* = 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}.
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **LP-rounding gives a 2-approximation for Vertex Cover**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Set Cover LP Relaxation

The **Set Cover** problem: Given a universe $U$ of $n$ elements and a family $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, \ldots, S_m\}$ of subsets, find the minimum number of sets whose union covers $U$.

### 3.2.1 ILP Formulation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ILP-SC)} \quad \min & \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_j \\
\text{subject to } & \sum_{j : e \in S_j} x_j \geq 1 && \forall\, e \in U \\
& x_j \in \{0,1\} && \forall\, j = 1,\ldots,m
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 LP Relaxation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(LP-SC)} \quad \min & \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_j \\
\text{subject to } & \sum_{j : e \in S_j} x_j \geq 1 && \forall\, e \in U \\
& 0 \leq x_j \leq 1 && \forall\, j = 1,\ldots,m
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.3 Randomized Rounding Approximation

Given the fractional optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, pick each set $S_j$ **independently with probability** $x_j^*$. If some element is uncovered, add arbitrary covering sets.

**Expected cost analysis:**

Let $f = \max_{e \in U} \vert\{j : e \in S_j\}\vert$ be the maximum frequency. The probability that element $e$ is *not* covered is:

$$\Pr[e \text{ uncovered}] = \prod_{j : e \in S_j} (1 - x_j^*)$$

By the inequality $(1 - 1/f)^f \geq 1/e$ and using $\sum_{j : e \in S_j} x_j^* \geq 1$:

$$\Pr[e \text{ uncovered}] \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{f}\right)^f \leq \frac{1}{e}$$

By the union bound and linearity of expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{extra sets added}] \leq \sum_{e \in U} \Pr[e \text{ uncovered}] \leq \frac{n}{e}$$

The total expected cost is bounded by:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{cost}] \leq \sum_j x_j^* + \frac{n}{e}$$

After scaling analysis with $H_n = 1 + 1/2 + \cdots + 1/n$, the tight bound is:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{cost}] \leq H_n \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq H_n \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$$

This is the famous **$H_n$-approximation** (approximately $\ln n$-approximation) for Set Cover.

## 3.3 The Knapsack LP Relaxation — Quick Worked Trace

**ILP (0-1 Knapsack):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\max & \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i \\
\text{s.t.} & \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W \\
& x_i \in \{0,1\}
\end{aligned}$$

**LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$.

**Greedy rounding:** Sort items by value-to-weight ratio $v_i/w_i$ in descending order. Fill the knapsack fractionally, then for the last (fractionally included) item, either include it fully or drop it — whichever is better. This gives a **2-approximation** for Knapsack.

The LP optimum can be computed in $O(n \log n)$ via the greedy algorithm, and the integrality gap is unbounded in the worst case if no rounding is applied (you can have $x_i^* = 1/n$ for all $n$ items, giving value $V/n$ vs $V$ in the integer solution), but the greedy rounding closes this gap.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The LP Relaxation & Rounding Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the complete operational flow from an NP-hard combinatorial problem to a guaranteed approximate solution via LP relaxation and rounding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NP-hard Combinatorial Problem Instance I] --> B[Formulate as Integer Linear Program ILP]
    B --> C[Identify Integrality Constraint x in 0,1 power n]
    C --> D[Drop Integrality Constraint]
    D --> E[Add Box Constraint 0 le x le 1]
    E --> F[Solve LP via Simplex or Ellipsoid Method]
    F --> G[Obtain Fractional Optimal Solution x star]
    G --> H[Apply Rounding Scheme Deterministic or Randomized]
    H --> I[Obtain Integer Solution x tilde]
    I --> J{Is x tilde Feasible for ILP?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Return x tilde as Approximate Solution]
    J -- No --> L[Repair via Post-processing or Greedy Fixup]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Bound Approximation Ratio via Integrality Gap]

    A2[Instance I] --> N[Compute OPT of ILP]
    N --> O[Compute OPT of LP]
    O --> P[Integrality Gap equals OPT_LP / OPT_ILP]
    P --> M

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style D fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style F fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style H fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style K fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F
    style M fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
```

## 4.2 The Polytope Geometry — Feasible Regions

The following diagram conceptualizes the geometric difference between an ILP (corner points only) and its LP relaxation (a continuous polytope).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ILP_REGION["ILP FEASIBLE REGION (Corners Only)"]
        I1[Vertex x1 equals 0,0]
        I2[Vertex x2 equals 1,0]
        I3[Vertex x3 equals 0,1]
        I4[Vertex x4 equals 1,1]
    end

    subgraph LP_REGION["LP FEASIBLE REGION (Continuous Polytope)"]
```
