<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define LP relaxation. Why is the LP optimum always at least as good as the ILP optimum for a minimization problem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LP relaxation is the process of removing the integrality constraint from an Integer Linear Program (ILP), replacing $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$. This enlarges the feasible region. Since the ILP's feasible region is a strict subset of the LP's feasible region, the LP optimum is taken over a strictly larger set, and therefore cannot be worse. Formally:

$$\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{P}_{\text{LP}}} \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} \leq \min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{P}_{\text{ILP}}} \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} = \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$$

The inequality holds because $\mathcal{P}_{\text{ILP}} \subseteq \mathcal{P}_{\text{LP}}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the integrality gap of an LP relaxation? Give the integrality gap for the Vertex Cover LP relaxation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The integrality gap measures the maximum ratio between the LP optimum and the ILP optimum over all instances. For minimization:

$$\text{IG} = \max_{I} \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)} \geq 1$$

For the **Vertex Cover LP relaxation** (with $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ edge constraints and $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$), the integrality gap is **2**, achieved in the worst case (e.g., complete bipartite graph or odd cycles). This is why the LP-rounding scheme gives a 2-approximation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider the Minimum Vertex Cover problem on an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$.**

**(a)** Formulate Vertex Cover as an Integer Linear Program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write its LP relaxation, prove it gives a 2-approximation via the threshold rounding scheme, and compute the integrality gap for the triangle graph $K_3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ILP Formulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Variables:** Define binary variables $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ for each $v \in V$.

$$x_v = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vertex } v \text{ is included in the cover} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Objective:** Minimize the number of vertices selected.

$$\min \sum_{v \in V} x_v$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:** Every edge must have at least one endpoint selected.

$$x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall\, \{u, v\} \in E$$

**Step 4 — Complete ILP:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ILP)} \quad \min & \quad \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} & \quad x_u + x_v \geq 1 && \forall\, \{u, v\} \in E \\
& \quad x_v \in \{0, 1\} && \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks awarded for: variables 2, objective 2, constraints 2, complete formulation 1.)**

---

**Part (b) — LP Relaxation, Rounding, Integrality Gap (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — LP Relaxation:** Drop $x_v \in \{0,1\}$, replace with $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(LP)} \quad \min & \quad \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} & \quad x_u + x_v \geq 1 && \forall\, \{u, v\} \in E \\
& \quad 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 && \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the LP for $K_3$:**

For the triangle $V = \{a, b, c\}$, by symmetry $x_a^* = x_b^* = x_c^* = t$. Edge constraint gives $2t \geq 1$, so $t = 0.5$. Thus $\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5$.

**Step 3 — ILP optimum for $K_3$:** Must pick $\geq 2$ vertices, so $\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}} = 2$.

**Step 4 — Integrality gap (this instance):**

$$\text{IG}(K_3) = \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}} = \frac{1.5}{2} = \frac{3}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Threshold Rounding Scheme:** Define $\tilde{C} = \{ v : x_v^* \geq 0.5 \}$.

**Step 6 — Feasibility proof:** For any edge $\{u, v\}$, $x_u^* + x_v^* \geq 1$, so $\max(x_u^*, x_v^*) \geq 0.5$. Hence at least one of $u, v$ is in $\tilde{C}$, so every edge is covered. ✓

**Step 7 — Cost bound:**

$$|\tilde{C}| = \sum_{v : x_v^* \geq 0.5} 1 \leq \sum_{v : x_v^* \geq 0.5} 2 x_v^* \leq 2 \sum_{v \in V} x_v^* = 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$$

Therefore, the algorithm is a **2-approximation** for Vertex Cover.

**(7 marks awarded for: LP formulation 1, $K_3$ computation 2, IG computation 1, rounding scheme 1, feasibility proof 1, cost bound proof 1.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Consider the Set Cover problem with universe $U$ of $n$ elements and a family of $m$ subsets.**

**(a)** Formulate Set Cover as an ILP and derive its LP relaxation. State the integrality gap for Set Cover. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the randomized rounding scheme for the Set Cover LP, and prove that it yields an $H_n$-approximation where $H_n = 1 + 1/2 + \cdots + 1/n$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ILP and LP Relaxation (7 marks):**

**Variables:** $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ for each set $S \in \mathcal{S}$, indicating whether $S$ is chosen.

**ILP:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\min & \quad \sum_{S \in \mathcal{S}} x_S \\
\text{s.t.} & \quad \sum_{S : e \in S} x_S \geq 1 && \forall\, e \in U \\
& \quad x_S \in \{0, 1\} && \forall\, S \in \mathcal{S}
\end{aligned}$$

**LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_S \leq 1$.

**Integrality Gap:** For Set Cover, the integrality gap of the natural LP is $\Theta(\ln n)$, i.e., there exist instances where $\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}} / \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} = \Omega(\log n)$, and this is tight — no rounding scheme can do better than $O(\log n)$.

**(7 marks: variables 1, ILP 3, LP relaxation 2, IG statement 1.)**

---

**Part (b) — Randomized Rounding (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Solve LP:** Obtain fractional optimum $\mathbf{x}^* = (x_{S_1}^*, \ldots, x_{S_m}^*)$.

**Step 2 — Round:** Include each set $S_j$ independently with probability $x_{S_j}^*$.

**Step 3 — Repair:** For each uncovered element $e$, add all sets containing $e$ to the cover (or any one of them).

**Step 4 — Coverage probability of element $e$:**

$$\Pr[e \text{ uncovered}] = \prod_{j : e \in S_j} (1 - x_{S_j}^*) \leq \prod_{j : e \in S_j} e^{-x_{S_j}^*} = e^{-\sum_{j : e \in S_j} x_{S_j}^*} \leq e^{-1}$$

using the inequality $1 - y \leq e^{-y}$ and the constraint $\sum_{j : e \in S_j} x_{S_j}^* \geq 1$.

**Step 5 — Expected extra cost:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{extra}] \leq \sum_{e \in U} \Pr[e \text{ uncovered}] \leq \frac{n}{e}$$

**Step 6 — Tight $H_n$ bound:** Using a more refined analysis with the inequality $(1 - 1/f)^f \leq 1/e$ where $f$ is the max frequency, one can show:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{total cost}] \leq H_n \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq H_n \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$$

since $H_n \approx \ln n + \gamma$ (where $\gamma \approx 0.577$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant).

**(7 marks: algorithm description 2, coverage analysis 2, expectation bound 2, $H_n$ conclusion 1.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the integrality constraint clearly:** When writing the ILP, students often omit "$x_v \in \{0,1\}$" or "$x_S \in \{0,1\}$". This costs 1–2 marks. **Always explicitly state the integrality constraint.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the direction of the integrality gap inequality:** Students sometimes write $\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \geq \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$ for minimization without proof. You must **prove** it by showing the LP feasible region contains the ILP feasible region. Just stating the inequality loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Rounding feasibility proof incomplete:** Many students only prove the cost bound but forget to prove that the rounded solution is feasible (i.e., all edges are covered / all elements are included). Both parts are essential for full marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the "tightness" of $H_n$:** The $H_n$ bound is not just an upper bound — it is **tight up to constants**, meaning there exist Set Cover instances where you cannot beat $H_n$. Students who don't mention tightness lose a follow-up mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Vertex Cover (IG = 2) with Set Cover (IG = $H_n$):** These are distinct problems with different integrality gaps. Confusing them is a critical error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **LP Relaxation Definition:** Drop integrality constraint $x \in \{0,1\}^n$, replace with box constraint $0 \leq x \leq 1$.
- [x] **LP $\leq$ IP (min):** LP feasible region strictly contains IP feasible region, so LP optimum is a lower bound.
- [x] **Polynomial Solvability:** LP can be solved in polynomial time via Ellipsoid or interior-point methods.
- [x] **Rounding is the Key:** LP gives a fractional solution; rounding converts it to integral; rounding loss = approximation ratio.
- [x] **Vertex Cover ILP:** Variables $x_v \in \{0,1\}$; objective $\min \sum x_v$; constraints $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ per edge.
- [x] **Vertex Cover IG = 2:** Threshold rounding at $0.5$ gives 2-approximation.
- [x] **Set Cover ILP:** Variables $x_S \in \{0,1\}$; constraints $\sum_{S \ni e} x_S \geq 1$ per element.
- [x] **Set Cover IG = $H_n$:** Randomized rounding gives $H_n \approx \ln n$ approximation, which is tight.
- [x] **Knapsack ILP:** $\max \sum v_i x_i$ s.t. $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$, $x_i \in \{0,1\}$; LP + greedy gives 2-approx.
- [x] **Integrality Gap Definition (min):** $\text{IG} = \max_I \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}} \geq 1$.
- [x] **Rounding Categories:** (i) Deterministic threshold rounding (Vertex Cover), (ii) Randomized rounding (Set Cover, Max-SAT), (iii) Primal–dual (also a valid approach in Module 3).
- [x] **General Bound:** Any LP-rounding that produces $\alpha$-approx implies the integrality gap $\leq \alpha$.
- [x] **Hardness Caveat:** Some problems (e.g., Independent Set) have integrality gap $= n$, making LP-rounding useless — strong inapproximability results apply.
- [x] **Engineering Relevance:** LP-based approximation is the workhorse of modern combinatorial optimization in telecom, VLSI, logistics, and operations research.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primal-Dual Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Primal-Dual Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Primal-Dual Method** is a canonical algorithmic paradigm used to design **polynomial-time approximation algorithms** for combinatorial optimization problems (covering, packing, network design, and facility location problems). The technique starts from an **Integer Linear Program (ILP)**, derives its **Linear Programming (LP) Relaxation** along with its **dual program**, and then iteratively constructs a *feasible* integer primal solution by exploiting the structure of the dual.

The cornerstone theoretical instruments are:

- **Primal Problem (P)**: A covering/packing LP of the form $P = \max \{ c^T x \mid Ax \leq b,\ x \geq 0\}$ or $\min \{ c^T x \mid Ax \geq b,\ x \geq 0\}$.
- **Dual Problem (D)**: A corresponding LP whose variables $y_j \geq 0$ (for $\leq$ constraints) or $y_j \leq 0$ (for $\geq$ constraints) act as *prices* or *dual certificates*.
- **Complementary Slackness Conditions**: A feasible primal–dual pair is *optimal* iff:
  $$\begin{aligned}
  \text{(i)}\quad & x_i \cdot \left(\sum_{j} a_{ji} y_j - c_i\right) = 0 \\
  \text{(ii)}\quad & y_j \cdot \left(b_j - \sum_{i} a_{ji} x_i\right) = 0
  \end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Primal-Dual Method sits at the intersection of **LP Duality Theory** and **Combinatorial Algorithm Design**. It is *not* the same as the Simplex Method — it is a *theoretical* construction tool. You must know complementary slackness, weak/strong duality, and the **dual-fitting / dual-rounding** argument.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Reverse Auction"

Imagine you are a **city planner** who needs to cover a set of neighborhoods with a minimum number of fire stations (a covering problem). Instead of brute-forcing combinations, you run a *reverse auction*:

- Each **unsatisfied constraint** (uncovered neighborhood) acts as a **bidder** willing to pay a *price*.
- Each **primal variable** (candidate fire station) is a **seller** who raises its price gradually.
- Sellers become *active* only when the combined bid reaches their **selling threshold** (cost).
- Once activated, the variable is fixed in the primal solution, and the constraints it covers are *satisfied* (and pay the dual).

The dual prices certify *why* the chosen primal solution is "almost optimal" — the total dual collected is at most **$\rho$** times the primal cost, yielding an **approximation ratio of $\rho$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The Primal-Dual method produces *both* an integer primal solution **AND** a feasible dual solution simultaneously. Comparing their objective values gives the approximation ratio directly via **Weak Duality**: $\text{OPT}_{\text{primal}} \geq \text{OPT}_{\text{dual}}$. Since primal cost $\leq \rho \cdot$ dual cost, the ratio follows.

---

## 1.3 Why Primal-Dual? — Practical Engineering Motivation

| Domain | Application | Why Primal-Dual? |
|---|---|---|
| **Telecom Networks** | Minimum-cost node placement to cover signal gaps | Combinatorial complexity rules out exact LP solving |
| **Supply Chain** | Facility-location / hub placement | LP solvers scale poorly for $n > 10^5$ |
| **VLSI Design** | Minimum-cost edge cover in circuit graphs | Need certified *provable bounds*, not just heuristics |
| **Bioinformatics** | Set cover for gene-expression experiments | Output must scale to millions of sets/elements |
| **Cloud Computing** | VM placement under capacity constraints | Need polynomial-time guarantees on latency/cost |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, remember that Primal-Dual algorithms are **non-constructive in their analysis** but **deterministic and constructive in execution** — they always run in *polynomial time*, with *no randomness*, and give a *bounded ratio* vs. OPT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Primal-Dual Schema

Let us write a *generic covering LP*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)}\quad \min\ & \sum_{e \in E} c_e \, x_e \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{e \in S_v} x_e \geq 1 \quad \forall v \in V \\
& x_e \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall e \in E
\end{aligned}$$

Its **dual** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)}\quad \max\ & \sum_{v \in V} y_v \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{v \in S_e} y_v \leq c_e \quad \forall e \in E \\
& y_v \geq 0 \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

The **Primal-Dual Algorithm** proceeds as follows (canonical structure):

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialize:** Set all primal variables $x_e = 0$ (unpicked), and all dual variables $y_v = 0$. Maintain a set $U \subseteq V$ of *unsatisfied* constraints.
2. **Initialize Unsatisfied Set:** $U = V$ (or the set of constraints violated by current $x$).
3. **Pricing Phase (Greedy Increase):** While $U \neq \emptyset$:
   - Select an unsatisfied constraint $v \in U$.
   - Uniformly raise the dual variables of all $v \in U$ until some dual constraint becomes **tight**:
     $$\sum_{v \in S_e} y_v = c_e$$
   - The corresponding primal variable $x_e$ is *eligible* for selection.
4. **Activation Phase:** If multiple variables become tight simultaneously, pick one (or all, depending on the algorithm).
5. **Satisfaction Phase:** Mark all constraints $v \in S_e$ as *satisfied* and remove them from $U$.
6. **Termination:** When $U = \emptyset$, return the chosen set of primal variables $F = \{e : x_e = 1\}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Primal (Covering)** | $\min\ c^T x\ \text{s.t.}\ Ax \geq b,\ x \geq 0$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| **Dual (Packing)** | $\max\ b^T y\ \text{s.t.}\ A^T y \leq c,\ y \geq 0$ | One dual var per primal constraint |
| **Weak Duality** | $\text{OPT}(P) \geq \text{OPT}(D)$ | Certifies any feasible pair |
| **Strong Duality** | $\text{OPT}(P) = \text{OPT}(D)$ | Holds for *feasible* LPs |
| **Complementary Slackness (i)** | $x_e > 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{v \in S_e} y_v = c_e$ | Active variables must be tight |
| **Complementary Slackness (ii)** | $y_v > 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{e \in S_v} x_e = 1$ | Paid prices must equal coverage |
| **Approximation Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{cost}(\text{Primal\ solution})}{\text{cost}(\text{OPT})}$ | Bounded by dual-ratio |
| **Integrality Gap (LP)** | $\max_{I} \dfrac{\text{OPT}_I}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}}$ | Lower bound on any LP-based ratio |
| **Set Cover Ratio** | $\rho = H(\max \vert S_v \vert) = O(\log n)$ | $H(k) = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k}$ |
| **Vertex Cover Ratio** | $\rho = 2$ | Tight, achievable |
| **Facility Location Ratio** | $\rho = O(1)$ (typically $3$ or $\log n$ depending on variant) | For *uncapacitated* metric FLP |
| **Steiner Forest Ratio** | $\rho = 2$ | Agrawal–Klein–Ravi (1995) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Pitfall (KTU Board Mark-loss):** Do **NOT** confuse **primal–dual schema** (this topic) with **dual–fitting** (a different but related technique). Dual-fitting proves $\sum c_e x_e \leq \rho \cdot \sum y_v$ by *redistributing* dual payments across variables. Primal-Dual constructs the solution *and* dual in lockstep.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

The Primal-Dual method has powered industry-grade algorithms:
- **Goemans–Williamson MAX-CUT** (randomized hyperplane rounding).
- **Jain–Vazirani prize-collecting Steiner tree** (network design).
- **Garg–Könemann framework** for multicommodity flows.
- **Set Cover in databases** for materialized-view selection.

> [!TIP]
> The **engineering takeaway**: any time a real-world optimization can be formulated as a covering/packing LP, the Primal-Dual method gives a *certifiable*, *fast*, and *deterministic* approximation algorithm — ideal for production systems where guarantees matter more than exactness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Primal-Dual for SET COVER

We derive the **$H(n)$-approximation algorithm** for **Set Cover** via the Primal-Dual schema, which is the most exam-relevant derivation under the KTU 2024 PECST749 syllabus.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

**Input:** A universe $U = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n\}$ and a family of $m$ subsets $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_m\}$ where $S_j \subseteq U$.

**Goal:** Find a sub-family $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ such that $\bigcup_{S \in \mathcal{C}} S = U$ and $\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S)$ is minimized.

### 3.1.2 Integer Program and LP Relaxation

The IP formulation is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(IP)}\quad \min\ & \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_j\, x_j \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{j : u_i \in S_j} x_j \geq 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \\
& x_j \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\}
\end{aligned}$$

Dropping the integrality constraint gives the **LP Relaxation (P)**.

### 3.1.3 The Dual Program

For each element $u_i \in U$, introduce dual variable $y_i \geq 0$. The dual is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)}\quad \max\ & \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{i : u_i \in S_j} y_i \leq c_j \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\} \\
& y_i \geq 0 \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic interpretation:** $y_i$ is the *price* we are willing to pay to "cover" element $u_i$. A set $S_j$ can only be activated when the *sum* of prices of elements it covers equals its *cost* $c_j$.

### 3.1.4 The Algorithm

```
SET-COVER-PRIMAL-DUAL(U, S, c):
1.  Initialize x_j ← 0 for all j; y_i ← 0 for all i.
2.  Let C ← ∅ (chosen sets) and U ← U (uncovered elements).
3.  While U ≠ ∅:
4.      Pick any uncovered element i ∈ U.
5.      Uniformly raise y_i for ALL i ∈ U at the same rate.
6.      Stop raising when some constraint becomes tight:
              ∑_{k ∈ S_j ∩ U} y_k = c_j   for some j.
7.      Add S_j to C: set x_j ← 1.
8.      Update U ← U \ S_j  (mark elements of S_j as covered).
9.  Return C.
```

### 3.1.5 Approximation Ratio Analysis — Exhaustive Derivation

We claim this algorithm achieves an **$H(d_{\max})$-approximation**, where $d_{\max} = \max_j \vert S_j \vert$ is the maximum set size, and $H(k) = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k}$.

#### Phase A — Bound the Primal Cost

Each time we activate a set $S_j$, we collect dual payments totalling exactly $c_j$ (since the constraint is tight). Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j &= \sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} \sum_{i \in S_j} y_i^{(j)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $y_i^{(j)}$ is the price of element $i$ at the moment set $S_j$ is activated. Since each element $i$ contributes to **at least one** set (the one that *first* covers it), and its price $y_i$ was built up by the algorithm while it was uncovered, we re-attribute each $y_i$ to *the first set that covered $i$*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this is only true if every $y_i$ is paid once. In reality, an element $i$ may contribute to *multiple* sets (it could be in $S_{j_1}$, then later in $S_{j_2}$ if $S_{j_1}$ is *not* the first to cover it). So:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j &\geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = \text{OPT}(D)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore $\sum c_j \cdot x_j \geq \text{OPT}(D)$ — so far, the *primal cost exceeds the dual value*, not what we want. We need to scale.

#### Phase B — The Reweighting Trick (Charging Argument)

Let $f$ denote the number of *sets selected* by the algorithm. For each selected set $S_j$, suppose it has $k_j = \vert S_j \setminus \text{(already covered)} \vert$ *newly covered* elements at the moment of activation. We *charge* its cost $c_j$ evenly across these $k_j$ elements:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{y}_i &= \sum_{j : i \text{ first covered by } S_j} \frac{c_j}{k_j}
\end{aligned}$$

Each $\bar{y}_i$ is the *average* per-element price at which $i$ was covered. Now:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j &= \sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} k_j \cdot \frac{c_j}{k_j} = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} \sum_{i \in S_j^{(new)}} \frac{c_j}{k_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{y}_i
\end{aligned}$$

#### Phase C — Dual Feasibility Check

For each original set $S_j$ (whether picked or not), we must verify $\sum_{i \in S_j} \bar{y}_i \leq H(d_{\max}) \cdot c_j$. Consider the elements of $S_j$ in the *order* they were covered by the algorithm: $i_1, i_2, \dots, i_t$ where $t \leq d_{\max}$. When $i_k$ was covered, there were at least $t - k + 1$ uncovered elements of $S_j$ remaining (namely $i_k, i_{k+1}, \dots, i_t$). Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{c_j}{t - k + 1} &\geq \text{(total dual raised during that step)} \cdot \frac{1}{t-k+1}
\end{aligned}$$

More precisely, at the moment $i_k$ was first covered by some chosen set $S_{j_k}$, the dual increase $\Delta y_{i_k}$ satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta y_{i_k} \cdot (t - k + 1) &\leq c_j
\end{aligned}$$

because the set $S_j$ has at most $t - k + 1$ uncovered elements at that moment, so $S_j$ cannot yet have been activated. Thus:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta y_{i_k} \leq \frac{c_j}{t - k + 1}
\end{aligned}$$

Summing over all elements of $S_j$ in cover order:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{t} \Delta y_{i_k} &\leq \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{c_j}{t - k + 1} = c_j \cdot \sum_{m=1}^{t} \frac{1}{m} \\
&\leq c_j \cdot H(t) \\
&\leq c_j \cdot H(d_{\max})
\end{aligned}$$

#### Phase D — Putting It Together

We have constructed a dual-feasible solution $(\bar{y}_i)$ such that $\sum_i \bar{y}_i \cdot H(d_{\max}) \geq \text{OPT}(D) = \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}$, and $\sum_i \bar{y}_i = \sum_j c_j x_j$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{y}_i \\
&\leq H(d_{\max}) \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \\
&\leq H(d_{\max}) \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Primal-Dual Set Cover algorithm is an **$H(d_{\max})$-approximation**. In the worst case $d_{\max} = n$, giving $H(n) \approx \ln n + \gamma$ (where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — VERTEX COVER via Primal-Dual (2-Approximation)

For completeness, here is the tightest case.

### 3.2.1 LP Formulation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)}\quad \min\ & \sum_{v \in V} w_v \, x_v \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& x_v \geq 0 \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)}\quad \max\ & \sum_{(u,v) \in E} y_{uv} \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{u : (u,v) \in E} y_{uv} \leq w_v \quad \forall v \in V \\
& y_{uv} \geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Algorithm

```
VERTEX-COVER-PRIMAL-DUAL(G, w):
1.  Initialize x_v ← 0, y_e ← 0 for all e ∈ E.
2.  Let C ← ∅ (chosen vertices), E' ← E (unsatisfied edges).
3.  While E' ≠ ∅:
4.      Pick any unsatisfied edge e = (u, v).
5.      Raise y_e (and simultaneously raise ALL y_{e'} for e' ∈ E').
6.      Stop when some vertex constraint becomes tight:
              ∑_{e incident to v} y_e = w_v.
7.      Add v to C: set x_v ← 1.
8.      Remove all edges incident to v from E'.
9.  Return C.
```

### 3.2.3 Ratio Derivation

Each edge $e$ has its dual raised at most until the *first* endpoint is added to $C$. Once one endpoint is in $C$, the edge is satisfied and removed from $E'$. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in C} w_v &= \sum_{v \in C} \sum_{e \in \delta(v)} y_e^{(v)}
\end{aligned}$$

Each edge $e = (u,v)$ contributes its dual $y_e$ to *exactly one* endpoint (the first one added). So:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in C} w_v &= \sum_{e \in E} y_e \leq \sum_{v \in V} w_v x_v^* = \text{OPT}(P)
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — we need the inequality the *other* way. We have $\sum_v w_v \cdot x_v \geq \sum_e y_e = \text{OPT}(D) \leq \text{OPT}(P)$. But the algorithm picks all tight vertices, so each $y_e$ is counted exactly once. To get the 2-approximation, observe:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in C} w_v &= \sum_{v \in C} \sum_{e \in \delta(v)} y_e^{(v)} = \sum_{e \in E} y_e \cdot (\text{no. of endpoints of } e \text{ in } C)
\end{aligned}$$

Every edge $e \in E$ has at most $2$ endpoints in $C$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in C} w_v &\leq 2 \sum_{e \in E} y_e \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(D) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(P)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tightness:** The ratio $2$ is *tight* — consider a triangle with $w_v = 1$ for all $v$. OPT = 2, but the algorithm may pick all 3 vertices if edges are processed in a certain order. Hence $\rho = 2$ is optimal for vertex cover via this LP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Primal-Dual Algorithm

The following diagram captures the *control flow* of a generic Primal-Dual approximation algorithm. Note that **all node identifiers are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters** to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards, and **node labels contain no markdown formatting**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Read Instance P and Dual D] --> A2[Initialize all primal x equals 0]
    A2 --> A3[Initialize all dual y equals 0]
    A3 --> A4[Define Unsatisfied Set U equals V]
    A4 --> A5{Is U empty?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Select active constraint v in U]
    A6 --> A7[Raise y uniformly for all v in U]
    A7 --> A8{Some primal constraint tight?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Activate corresponding primal variable x equals 1]
    A9 --> A10[Mark newly covered constraints]
    A10 --> A11[Remove satisfied constraints from U]
    A11 --> A5
    A5 -- Yes --> A12[Return Primal Solution F and Dual Y]
    A12 --> A13[Verify Dual Feasibility Y]
    A13 --> A14[Compute Approximation Ratio rho]
    A14 --> A15[End Report Approximation Certificate]
```

## 4.2 Primal-Dual Schema — Conceptual Cycle

This diagram illustrates the *dual-feedback loop* that is the heart of the method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Primal LP P] -- duality transform --> B2[Dual LP D]
    B2 -- raise prices y --> B3[Dual Feasible Region]
    B3 -- tight constraint --> B4[Identify Active Variable]
    B4 -- fix x equals 1 --> B1
    B5[Covering Constraint] -- covered by x --> B1
    B3 -- bound cost --> B6[Approximation Ratio rho]
    B1 -- cost estimate --> B6
    B6 --> B7[End Report Certificate]
```

## 4.3 Process Map — Set Cover Primal-Dual

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Universe U and Family S] --> C2[Pick arbitrary uncovered element i]
    C2 --> C3[Simultaneously raise y of all uncovered elements]
    C3 --> C4[Find set S_j whose dual sum equals cost]
    C4 --> C5[Pick S_j into cover C]
    C5 --> C6[Mark S_j elements as covered]
    C6 --> C7{All elements covered?}
    C7 -- No --> C2
    C7 -- Yes --> C8[Output cover C and reweighted dual y bar]
    C8 --> C9[Verify dual feasibility with factor H of d max]
    C9 --> C10[End Conclude H of d max Approximation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering View:** The diagrams above are *control-flow* abstractions rather than physical drawings. In real production systems (e.g., a network-coverage planner), each "tight constraint" corresponds to a hardware resource hitting capacity, and the "dual raise" corresponds to incremental bid-acceptance. The loop terminates when all demands are met — exactly mirroring the algorithmic termination condition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State the Complementary Slackness conditions for a primal–dual pair of linear programs.**

**Model Answer:**

Let (P) be $\min\ c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \geq b,\ x \geq 0$ and (D) be $\max\ b^T y$ s.t. $A^T y \leq c,\ y \geq 0$. For a feasible primal–dual pair $(x^*, y^*)$ to be optimal:

- **Primal CS:** $x^*_j \cdot \left(\sum_{i} a_{ij} y^*_i - c_j\right) = 0$ for all $j$.
- **Dual CS:** $y^*_i \cdot \left(\sum_{j} a_{ij} x^*_j - b_i\right) = 0$ for all $i$.

In words: a primal variable can be positive only if its dual constraint is **tight**, and a dual variable can be positive only if its primal constraint is **tight**.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why does the Primal-Dual method yield an approximation algorithm rather than an exact one in general?**

**Model Answer:**

The Primal-Dual method builds a *feasible integer* primal solution and a *feasible* dual solution, then compares their objective values via Weak Duality. However, the dual solution constructed by the algorithm is typically only **scaled within a factor $\rho$** of feasibility (i.e., $\sum_{i \in S_j} \bar{y}_i \leq \rho \cdot c_j$ rather than $\leq c_j$). This slackness, measured by the **integrality gap** of the LP relaxation, prevents us from claiming $\sum c_j x_j = \text{OPT}$; instead we get $\sum c_j x_j \leq \rho \cdot \text{OPT}$. Hence the algorithm is $\rho$-approximate, not exact.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 4–5 marks by (i) failing to write the **dual LP explicitly**, (ii) skipping the **dual feasibility verification** (Phase C of Section 3), and (iii) not stating the **integrality gap** explicitly. Always include all three for full marks.

---

### Question B.A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the **Weighted Vertex Cover** problem as a linear program. Write its dual. Describe the **Primal-Dual algorithm** for Weighted Vertex Cover with pseudocode.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that the algorithm achieves a **2-approximation**. Give an instance where the ratio is tight.

#### Model Solution

**(a) LP Formulation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)}\quad \min\ & \sum_{v \in V} w_v \, x_v \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}$$

Dual (with $y_{uv} \geq 0$ per edge):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)}\quad \max\ & \sum_{e \in E} y_e \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \sum_{e \in \delta(v)} y_e \leq w_v \quad \forall v \in V \\
& y_e \geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Pseudocode:** See `VERTEX-COVER-PRIMAL-DUAL` in Section 3.2.2.

**(b) Proof of 2-Approximation:**

Let $C$ be the chosen vertex cover. Each vertex $v \in C$ was added because $\sum_{e \in \delta(v)} y_e^{(v)} = w_v$ became tight. Charge $y_e$ to whichever endpoint was *first* added. Each edge $e$ contributes to **at most 2** vertices in $C$ (both its endpoints), so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in C} w_v &= \sum_{v \in C} \sum_{e \in \delta(v)} y_e^{(v)} \\
&= \sum_{e \in E} y_e \cdot \left( \text{number of endpoints of } e \text{ in } C \right) \\
&\leq 2 \sum_{e \in E} y_e \\
&\leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \\
&\leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Tight Instance:** Take a triangle $K_3$ with unit weights. OPT = 2 (any two vertices). The Primal-Dual algorithm may add *all 3* vertices if edges are picked adversarially, giving cost $3$. Ratio $= 3/2$, but for a star $K_{1,n}$ with central weight 1 and leaves weight $\epsilon$, OPT = 1 but the algorithm picks center + leaves, giving ratio $\approx 1$. The tight construction is actually: an odd cycle $C_{2k+1}$ where OPT = $k$ vertices but the algorithm picks $k+1$. Ratio $= \frac{k+1}{k} \to 2$ as $k \to \infty$.

---

### Question B.B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Primal-Dual schema** for the **Set Cover** problem. Provide the LP, its dual, and the algorithm steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove the algorithm is an **$H(d_{\max})$-approximation**, where $d_{\max}$ is the maximum set size.

#### Model Solution

**(a)** See Section 3.1 for the full LP, dual, and algorithm.

**(b)** See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1.5. Key steps:

1. **Reweighting:** Define $\bar{y}_i = \sum_{j: i \text{ first covered by } S_j} \frac{c_j}{k_j}$.
2. **Cost Equality:** $\sum_{j \in \mathcal{C}} c_j = \sum_i \bar{y}_i$ (each unit of cost charged to exactly one element).
3. **Dual Feasibility:** For any original set $S_j$ with elements $i_1, \dots, i_t$ in cover order:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{t} \Delta y_{i_k} \leq c_j \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{1}{t-k+1} = c_j \cdot H(t) \leq c_j \cdot H(d_{\max})
\end{aligned}$$

4. **Conclusion:** $\sum_i \bar{y}_i \leq H(d_{\max}) \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \leq H(d_{\max}) \cdot \text{OPT}$, hence $\rho = H(d_{\max}) = O(\log n)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The Primal-Dual method **simultaneously constructs** a primal integer solution and a dual feasible solution.
- ✅ It is used for **covering/packing** problems where the LP relaxation has a small integrality gap.
- ✅ The algorithm relies on **tight constraints** to identify when a primal variable should be activated.
- ✅ The **approximation ratio** comes from comparing primal cost to dual value via Weak Duality.
- ✅ Canonical applications:
  - **Vertex Cover** → $\rho = 2$ (tight)
  - **Set Cover** → $\rho = H(d_{\max}) = O(\log n)$
  - **Shortest Path** → $\rho = 1$ (exact, Dijkstra is a primal-dual algorithm!)
  - **Facility Location** → $\rho = 3$ (Jain–Vazirani)
  - **Steiner Forest** → $\rho = 2$
  - **Prize-Collecting Steiner Tree** → $\rho = 2$
- ✅ **Complementary Slackness** is the bridge: it tells us which primal variables should be non-zero.
- ✅ **Integrality Gap** is the fundamental barrier — it determines the best $\rho$ achievable via LP-based methods.
- ✅ Always write **both LPs explicitly** in exams; never refer to "the dual" without writing it.
- ✅ The **reweighting / charging argument** is the most exam-tested proof technique.
- ✅ **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm** is a special case where $\rho = 1$ — no integrality gap.
- ✅ **Duality is symmetric**: you may also start from a packing LP (e.g., MAX-CUT) and dual-rounding; the analysis is analogous.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PACT":**
> **P**rimal LP → **A**ctivate variables → **C**omplementary slackness → **T**ight constraints drive ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rounding Techniques - Randomized Rounding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rounding Techniques - Randomized Rounding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Rounding Techniques: Randomized Rounding

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Randomized Rounding** is a derandomization-free rounding paradigm introduced by **Raghavan & Thompson (1987)**. Given an optimal (or near-optimal) **fractional solution** $\bar{x} \in [0,1]^n$ to the **Linear Programming (LP) relaxation** of an Integer Program (IP), a randomized rounding scheme produces an integer solution $\hat{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$ by independently setting
> $$\hat{x}_i = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{with probability } \bar{x}_i \\ 0, & \text{with probability } 1 - \bar{x}_i \end{cases}$$
> The resulting solution is analyzed using **tail-bound inequalities** (Chernoff/Hoeffding bounds) to prove an expected approximation ratio.

In other words, instead of *deterministically* rounding a fractional value like $0.6$ up to $1$ (deterministic rounding) or down to $0$ (floor rounding), we **flip a biased coin** with probability $0.6$ of coming up heads.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biased Lottery" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Class-Attendance Lottery**
> Imagine a professor who must decide which students to **invite to a research seminar**. The optimal *fractional* solution suggests each student $i$ should attend with weight $\bar{x}_i = 0.7$. The professor does **not** invite all 70% of each student — instead, she puts each student's name in a hat with **70 green balls** and **30 red balls**. She draws one ball per student; green = invite, red = don't invite.
>
> - On average, **70% of students** get invited (matching the LP objective).
> - Sometimes she gets 65%, sometimes 75% — the variance is governed by **Chernoff bounds**.
> - She can then use probabilistic guarantees to certify the invitation list is "close to optimal" with high probability.

This technique is the **workhorse** of modern approximation algorithms because:
1. It **decouples** LP optimization from combinatorial structure.
2. It gives **probabilistic guarantees** that can often be converted to deterministic ones via the **method of conditional expectations** (derandomization).

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Master-List

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **LP Relaxation** | Replacing integrality constraints $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_i \in [0,1]$ |
| **Fractional Solution** | $\bar{x}$ satisfying relaxed constraints but not necessarily integral |
| **Random Variable $\hat{X}_i$** | Bernoulli$(\bar{x}_i)$ random variable for each coordinate |
| **Expectation Lemma** | $\mathbb{E}[\hat{X}_i] = \bar{x}_i$ (so $\mathbb{E}[\text{objective}] \ge c \cdot \text{OPT}_{LP}$) |
| **Tail Bound** | Bound on $P(\lvert \Sigma \hat{X}_i - \Sigma \bar{x}_i \rvert \ge \varepsilon \Sigma \bar{x}_i)$ |
| **Derandomization** | Converting the randomized algorithm into a deterministic one with the same guarantee |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Randomized Rounding Blueprint

Every randomized-rounding algorithm follows this template. **Memorize this flow** — KTU questions are built around it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Formulate IP:** Write the integer program for the combinatorial problem.
>
> **Step 2 — LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ by $x_i \in [0,1]$ (and similar for indicator variables).
>
> **Step 3 — Solve LP:** Obtain fractional optimum $\bar{x}$ with objective value $\text{OPT}_{LP} \le \text{OPT}_{IP}$.
>
> **Step 4 — Round Randomly:** For each $i$, independently set $\hat{x}_i = 1$ with probability $\bar{x}_i$.
>
> **Step 5 — Bound Expected Value:** Use linearity of expectation + Chernoff bound to show
> $$\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}(\hat{x})] \ge \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{LP}$$
> for some constant $\alpha$, then conclude $\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}(\hat{x})] \ge \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{IP}$.

---

## 2.2 The Chernoff Bound — The Engine of Randomized Rounding

The **Chernoff Bound** quantifies how tightly a sum of independent Bernoulli random variables concentrates around its mean. This is the **single most important inequality** in randomized rounding.

### 2.2.1 Setup

Let $\hat{X}_1, \hat{X}_2, \dots, \hat{X}_n$ be **independent** random variables with $\hat{X}_i \in \{0,1\}$ and $P(\hat{X}_i = 1) = p_i$. Define
$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \hat{X}_i, \qquad \mu = \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize These Five Inequalities** — they cover 95% of KTU problems on randomized rounding.

| # | Bound Name | Mathematical Statement | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chernoff Upper Tail** | $P\bigl(X \ge (1+\delta)\mu\bigr) \le e^{-\delta^{2}\mu/3}$ for $0 \le \delta \le 1$ | Showing $X$ does not exceed a target |
| 2 | **Chernoff Lower Tail** | $P\bigl(X \le (1-\delta)\mu\bigr) \le e^{-\delta^{2}\mu/2}$ for $0 \le \delta \le 1$ | Showing $X$ does not fall below a target |
| 3 | **Chernoff Simplified** | $P\bigl(\lvert X - \mu \rvert \ge \varepsilon \mu\bigr) \le 2e^{-\varepsilon^{2}\mu/3}$ for $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ | Two-sided concentration |
| 4 | **Hoeffding (General)** | $P\bigl(\lvert X - \mu \rvert \ge t\bigr) \le 2e^{-2t^{2}/n}$ for $X_i \in [a_i, b_i]$ | When $\hat{X}_i \in [0,1]$ and we have bounds per variable |
| 5 | **Union Bound Helper** | $P\bigl(\exists j : \text{constraint } j \text{ violated}\bigr) \le \sum_j P(\text{constraint } j \text{ violated})$ | Bounding probability that *any* of $m$ constraints fail |

> **Notation Note:** Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ with the **vertical pipe `|` replaced by `\vert`** in markdown tables to avoid syntax breakage. In displayed equations, use plain `\lvert` and `\rvert`.

### 2.2.3 The Three Engineering Use-Cases

| Domain | Why Chernoff Matters |
|---|---|
| **Network Design (Telecom Routing)** | Prove packet loss stays below $1/n$ with high probability |
| **Scheduling Algorithms** | Show makespan is within $(1+\varepsilon)$ of LP optimum w.h.p. |
| **Databases (Query Optimization)** | Bound deviation of sampled cardinalities from true values |

---

## 2.3 Why Randomized Rounding Beats Deterministic Rounding

> [!NOTE]
> **Deterministic rounding** (round up / round down) can destroy approximation ratios because the LP objective is not aligned with the rounding direction.
>
> **Randomized rounding**, in contrast:
> 1. **Preserves expectation** — by linearity, $\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}(\hat{x})] \ge \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{LP}$.
> 2. **Self-correcting** — variance is controlled via Chernoff.
> 3. **Often derandomizable** — the method of conditional expectations yields a deterministic algorithm with the *same* ratio.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Randomized Rounding for MAX-SAT

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (MAX-SAT)

Given:
- Boolean variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
- Clauses $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$, where each $C_j$ is a disjunction of literals over $\{x_1, \bar{x}_1, \dots, x_n, \bar{x}_n\}$.
- Weights $w_j \ge 0$ on clauses.

**Goal:** Maximize the total weight of simultaneously satisfied clauses.

### 3.1.2 Integer Program (IP)

Introduce indicator variables:
- $y_l \in \{0,1\}$: literal $l$ is TRUE.
- $z_j \in \{0,1\}$: clause $C_j$ is satisfied.

$$\max \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j z_j \quad \text{subject to:}$$
$$\begin{aligned} z_j &\le \sum_{l \in C_j} y_l && \forall j \\ y_{x_i} + y_{\bar{x}_i} &\le 1 && \forall i \\ y_l, z_j &\in \{0,1\} && \forall l, j \end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 LP Relaxation

Replace $\{0,1\}$ with $[0,1]$:

$$z_j \le \sum_{l \in C_j} y_l \le \vert C_j \rvert$$

Let the optimal LP solution be $\bar{y}_l, \bar{z}_j$ with objective $\text{OPT}_{LP} = \sum_j w_j \bar{z}_j$.

Since the LP is a relaxation, $\text{OPT}_{LP} \le \text{OPT}_{IP} = \text{OPT}$.

### 3.1.4 Randomized Rounding Step

For each variable $x_i$, independently flip a biased coin:
$$P(x_i = \text{TRUE}) = \bar{y}_{x_i}, \qquad P(x_i = \text{FALSE}) = 1 - \bar{y}_{x_i} = \bar{y}_{\bar{x}_i}$$

Therefore, $P(\text{literal } l \text{ is TRUE}) = \bar{y}_l$.

### 3.1.5 Probability That Clause $C_j$ Is Satisfied

Clause $C_j$ is satisfied iff **at least one** of its literals is TRUE.

$$P(C_j \text{ satisfied}) = 1 - \prod_{l \in C_j} \bigl(1 - \bar{y}_l\bigr)$$

Apply the **Weierstrass product inequality** $\bigl(1 - a_1\bigr)\bigl(1 - a_2\bigr)\dots\bigl(1 - a_k\bigr) \le \exp\!\Bigl(-\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i\bigr)$ for $a_i \in [0,1]$:

$$\prod_{l \in C_j} \bigl(1 - \bar{y}_l\bigr) \le \exp\!\Bigl(-\sum_{l \in C_j} \bar{y}_l\Bigr)$$

Therefore:

$$P(C_j \text{ satisfied}) \ge 1 - \exp\!\Bigl(-\sum_{l \in C_j} \bar{y}_l\Bigr)$$

Use the LP constraint $\sum_{l \in C_j} \bar{y}_l \ge \bar{z}_j$, and the **monotonicity of $1 - e^{-t}$**:

$$P(C_j \text{ satisfied}) \ge 1 - e^{-\bar{z}_j}$$

### 3.1.6 Computing Expected Objective Value

By **linearity of expectation**:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \hat{z}_j\right] = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot P(\hat{z}_j = 1) \ge \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \bigl(1 - e^{-\bar{z}_j}\bigr)$$

### 3.1.7 The Key Inequality $1 - e^{-t} \ge (1 - e^{-1}) \cdot t$ for $t \in [0,1]$

The function $f(t) = 1 - e^{-t}$ is **concave**. Its chord from $t=0$ to $t=1$ has slope $1 - e^{-1}$.

By concavity:

$$1 - e^{-t} \ge (1 - e^{-1}) \cdot t = \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) t \quad \text{for all } t \in [0,1]$$

Apply this with $t = \bar{z}_j \in [0,1]$:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_j w_j \hat{z}_j\right] \ge \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \sum_j w_j \bar{z}_j = \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \text{OPT}_{LP}$$

### 3.1.8 Final Approximation Ratio

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}(\hat{x})] \ge \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \text{OPT} \approx 0.632 \cdot \text{OPT}\;}$$

Thus randomized rounding yields a $\bigl(1 - \tfrac{1}{e}\bigr)$-approximation for MAX-SAT.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: 3-Variable, 3-Clause MAX-SAT**

**Instance:**
- Variables: $x_1, x_2, x_3$.
- Clauses (each with weight $w_j = 1$):
  - $C_1 = (x_1 \vee x_2)$
  - $C_2 = (\bar{x}_2 \vee x_3)$
  - $C_3 = (x_1 \vee \bar{x}_3)$

**LP Solution:** $\bar{y}_{x_1} = 0.5$, $\bar{y}_{\bar{x}_1} = 0.5$, $\bar{y}_{x_2} = 0.6$, $\bar{y}_{\bar{x}_2} = 0.4$, $\bar{y}_{x_3} = 0.7$, $\bar{y}_{\bar{x}_3} = 0.3$, $\bar{z}_j = 1.0$ for all $j$.

**Rounding:** Flip 3 coins with biases $(0.5, 0.6, 0.7)$.

**Outcome 1:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 1, 1)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.7 = 0.21$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: FALSE (since $\bar{x}_2 = 0$), $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 2 clauses.

**Outcome 2:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (0, 1, 1)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.7 = 0.21$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: TRUE, $C_3$: FALSE
- **Satisfied:** 2 clauses.

**Outcome 3:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 0, 1)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.14$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: TRUE, $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 3 clauses.

**Outcome 4:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (0, 0, 1)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.14$
- $C_1$: FALSE, $C_2$: TRUE, $C_3$: FALSE
- **Satisfied:** 1 clause.

**Outcome 5:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 1, 0)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.3 = 0.09$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: FALSE, $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 2 clauses.

**Outcome 6:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (0, 1, 0)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.3 = 0.09$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: FALSE, $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 2 clauses.

**Outcome 7:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 0, 0)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.06$
- $C_1$: TRUE, $C_2$: TRUE, $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 3 clauses.

**Outcome 8:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (0, 0, 0)$ with $P = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.06$
- $C_1$: FALSE, $C_2$: TRUE, $C_3$: TRUE
- **Satisfied:** 2 clauses.

**Expected Number of Satisfied Clauses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}] &= 2(0.21) + 2(0.21) + 3(0.14) + 1(0.14) + 2(0.09) + 2(0.09) + 3(0.06) + 2(0.06) \\
&= 0.42 + 0.42 + 0.42 + 0.14 + 0.18 + 0.18 + 0.18 + 0.12 \\
&= 2.06
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification against theoretical bound:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{obj}] \ge \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \text{OPT}_{LP} = 0.632 \times 3 = 1.896 \quad \checkmark$$

Our computed value $2.06$ exceeds $1.896$, validating the theoretical guarantee.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Randomized Rounding Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Integer Program IP] --> B[LP Relaxation]
    B --> C[Solve LP via Simplex or Interior Point]
    C --> D[Fractional Solution x_bar in 0 1 to the power n]
    D --> E{Independent Biased Coin Flip}
    E --> F1[Variable x1: Heads with prob x_bar1]
    E --> F2[Variable x2: Heads with prob x_bar2]
    E --> F3[Variable x3: Heads with prob x_bar3]
    E --> F4[Variable xn: Heads with prob x_barn]
    F1 --> G[Integer Solution x_hat in 0 1 to the power n]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Apply Chernoff Bound for Concentration]
    H --> I[Compute Expected Objective Value]
    I --> J[Approximation Ratio alpha Times OPT]
    J --> K{Derandomize Optional}
    K --> L[Deterministic Algorithm via Method of Conditional Expectations]
    L --> M[Final Output: Integrally Optimal Solution]
```

```mermaid
    Q1 -- No --> Stop[Consider Greedy or Local Search]

    Q2 -- No --> UseDet[Use Deterministic Rounding]

    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Do Constraints Have Natural Indicator Variables}
```
