# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECST749, Module 2]`
> *What is an Integer Linear Program (ILP)? Define the LP relaxation of an ILP and explain its role in approximation algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Integer Linear Program (ILP)** is a mathematical optimisation model in which the objective function and constraints are linear, but at least one decision variable is restricted to integer values:

$$
\max\{\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{x} : A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b},\, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^{n}_{\geq 0}\}.
$$

The **LP relaxation** is obtained by removing the integrality constraint and allowing $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}_{\geq 0}$. This gives a polyhedron $P_{L} \supseteq P_{I}$ (integer hull), solvable in polynomial time using simplex or interior-point methods. Its optimum $\mathrm{OPT}_{LP}$ is a bound on the integer optimum $\mathrm{OPT}_{IP}$, and the **integrality gap** $\gamma = \mathrm{OPT}_{LP}/\mathrm{OPT}_{IP}$ quantifies the loss. Since the ILP itself is NP-hard, the LP relaxation is the foundation of *rounding-based* approximation algorithms — solve the LP, then round the fractional solution to a feasible integer point with a provable factor-$\gamma$ guarantee.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECST749, Module 2]`
> *State the integrality gap for the Vertex Cover problem and explain the factor-2 rounding scheme.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **integrality gap** of Vertex Cover LP relaxation is **$\gamma = 2$**, i.e., $\mathrm{OPT}_{LP} \leq \mathrm{OPT}_{IP} \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_{LP}$. The **rounding scheme** is:
1. Solve LP: $x_v \in [0,1]$ for all $v$, with $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ for each edge.
2. **Round up:** $\hat{x}_v = 1$ if $x_v \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$, else $\hat{x}_v = 0$.
3. The vertex cover constraint is preserved because every edge $\{u,v\}$ has $x_u + x_v \geq 1$, forcing at least one rounded-up endpoint.
4. The cost increases by at most factor 2, since $x_v \geq \tfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 1 \leq 2 x_v$. Thus the rounded solution is a factor-2 approximation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
> **(a)** *Formulate the **Vertex Cover** problem as an Integer Linear Program. Show the LP relaxation and prove that rounding gives a 2-approximation.* **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** *Formulate the **Set Cover** problem as a 0-1 ILP. Discuss the LP relaxation and state the integrality gap.* **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — IP Formulation (2 marks):**
Given $G=(V,E)$ with $c_v \geq 0$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} c_v\, x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall\, \{u,v\} \in E \\
& x_v \in \{0,1\} \quad \forall\, v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LP Relaxation (1 mark):** Replace $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$.

**Step 3 — Rounding algorithm (2 marks):** Solve LP $\to$ get $x^*_v$. Set $\hat{x}_v = 1$ iff $x^*_v \geq \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 4 — Approximation proof (2 marks):**
* **Feasibility:** $\forall\{u,v\}\in E$, $x^*_u + x^*_v \geq 1 \Rightarrow \max(x^*_u, x^*_v) \geq \tfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \hat{x}_u + \hat{x}_v \geq 1$.
* **Cost:** $\sum c_v \hat{x}_v \leq \sum 2 c_v x^*_v = 2 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_{LP} \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_{IP}$. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — IP Formulation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j\, x_j \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{j : e_i \in S_j} x_j \geq 1 \quad \forall\, e_i \in U \\
& x_j \in \{0,1\} \quad \forall\, j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LP relaxation (1 mark):** $0 \leq x_j \leq 1$.

**Step 3 — Integrality gap derivation (3 marks):**
Let $d = \max_{e_i} \vert\{j : e_i \in S_j\}\vert$. For each element $e_i$, the LP constraint allows splitting $1$ across $d$ sets; the LP optimum is therefore at most $\tfrac{1}{d} \sum_j c_j$ when the family is "balanced". The greedy rounding pays at most $H_d \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_{LP}$ where $H_d = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \cdots + \tfrac{1}{d}$.

**Step 4 — Final bound (1 mark):** $\gamma_{\mathrm{SC}} = H_d \leq 1 + \ln d$, so **Set Cover is $O(\log n)$-approximable** (and tight unless $P=NP$).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
> **(a)** *Define LP relaxation. Show how it is obtained for the **Knapsack** problem and explain an FPTAS based on scaling and LP-rounding.* **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** *What are **cutting planes**? Explain Gomory's fractional cut and how it strengthens the LP relaxation.* **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** LP relaxation of an IP is the linear program obtained by removing the integrality constraints $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^{n}$ while keeping $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 2 — Knapsack IP and LP (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP: } \max \sum p_i x_i, \quad \sum w_i x_i \leq C, \quad x_i \in \{0,1\}. \\
\text{LP: } \max \sum p_i x_i, \quad \sum w_i x_i \leq C, \quad 0 \leq x_i \leq 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — FPTAS construction (3 marks):**
1. Scale profits: $p'_i = \lfloor p_i / \varepsilon \cdot (p_{\max}/n) \rfloor$ (polynomial size integers).
2. Solve LP on scaled instance — this is equivalent to a DP table of size $O(n^2/\varepsilon)$.
3. Output integral solution; loss is bounded by $\varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.

**Step 4 — Approximation ratio (1 mark):** The FPTAS achieves $(1-\varepsilon)$-approximation in time $O(n^3/\varepsilon)$.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** A **cutting plane** is a linear inequality $\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \leq b$ that is satisfied by all integer-feasible points (i.e., valid for $P_{I}$) but violated by some LP-feasible point in $P_{L}$. Adding the cut *shrinks* the LP polyhedron, pushing it closer to $P_{I}$.

**Step 2 — Gomory's fractional cut (3 marks):**
Consider the LP optimal BFS row of the simplex tableau: $x_{B_i} = b_i - \sum_{j \in N} a_{ij} x_j$, with $b_i \notin \mathbb{Z}$. The **Gomory cut** is:

$$
\sum_{j \in N^{+}} f_j\, x_j \geq f_0, \quad \text{where } f_0 = \{b_i\}, \;\; f_j = \{a_{ij}\},
$$

and $N^{+} = \{j : a_{ij} \leq 0\}$, $N^{0}$ similarly. This cut is valid for all integer points in the original polyhedron and cuts off the current fractional BFS.

**Step 3 — Convergence and role in approximation (2 marks):**
Gomory's pure cutting-plane algorithm converges to the IP optimum in **finite** steps. In approximation contexts, *partial* cutting (e.g., adding only cuts up to a Chvátal rank $r$) gives a polynomial-time relaxation with controlled integrality gap — the foundation of **branch-and-cut** solvers like CPLEX and Gurobi.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in PECST749 Integer Programming Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting integrality constraints** — writing LP instead of ILP costs 1–2 marks immediately. Always state $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "min" vs "max" integrality gap** — the gap is $\mathrm{OPT}_{LP}/\mathrm{OPT}_{IP}$ for maximisation and the *reciprocal* for minimisation. Mis-stating this is a classic 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Not proving feasibility of rounded solution** — students often show the cost bound but forget to show that the rounded solution actually satisfies the original constraints. The KTU examiner's key explicitly awards 1 mark for feasibility.
> 4. **Skipping the half-integrality claim** — for Vertex Cover, the half-integrality *theorem* (Nemhauser–Trotter) is what makes the factor-2 bound tight. Stating the bound without justification loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up "valid inequality" and "facet-defining inequality"** — a valid inequality is satisfied by all integer points, but a *facet* is a maximal-dimensional face of $P_{I}$. Conflating the two loses 1 mark in advanced questions.
> 6. **Ignoring the $\geq$ vs $\leq$ direction of constraints** — Vertex Cover is $x_u + x_v \geq 1$, not $\leq 1$. Direction errors are very common.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Integer Programming Formulation (Module 2)**

* **Core Definition:** An **ILP** maximises/minimises a linear function subject to linear constraints with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^{n}_{\geq 0}$.
* **LP Relaxation** is the LP obtained by *dropping* integrality; it gives a *polyhedron* $P_{L} \supseteq P_{I}$.
* **Integrality Gap** $\gamma$ measures the worst-case ratio $\mathrm{OPT}_{LP}/\mathrm{OPT}_{IP}$ — a *property of the problem*, not the algorithm.
* **Vertex Cover:** $\gamma = 2$; rounding via half-integrality is the canonical 2-approximation.
* **Set Cover:** $\gamma = H_d = O(\log d)$; greedy/randomised rounding; tight under $P \neq NP$.
* **Knapsack:** admits **FPTAS** $(1-\varepsilon)$ via LP-rounding + profit scaling.
* **TSP (metric):** $\gamma \leq \tfrac{3}{2}$ (Christofides); Held–Karp LP is the relaxation.
* **Standard Form** uses inequalities $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$ and non-negativity; conversions include slack variables and the big-$M$ method.
* **Total Unimodularity** of $A$ guarantees $P_{I} = P_{L}$ (no integrality gap) — sufficient for polynomial ILP.
* **Cutting Planes** are *valid* inequalities violated by fractional LP vertices; they shrink $P_{L}$ toward $P_{I}$.
* **Gomory Fractional Cut:** $f_0 - \sum f_j x_j \leq 0$ derived from a simplex tableau row with non-integer RHS.
* **Chvátal Rank:** depth of a cutting-plane derivation; rank-1 cuts are the simplest strengthening.
* **Branch-and-Cut** combines cutting planes with branch-and-bound; used in production solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, SCIP).
* **Penalty for forgetting** $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ in any formulation question — minimum 1-mark deduction in KTU 2024 scheme.
* **Memorise the integrality gaps** — they are the single most-tested fact across all 2019–2024 KTU papers.
* **Geometric intuition:** LP relaxation = *inflated polytope*; integer hull = *innermost lattice-polytope*; cutting planes = *slicing the inflated polytope*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting Plane Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cutting Plane Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Plane Methods** are a class of exact optimization techniques used to solve Integer Linear Programs (ILPs) by iteratively tightening the Linear Programming (LP) relaxation through the addition of linear inequalities (called *cuts* or *cutting planes*) that exclude infeasible fractional solutions while preserving all integer feasible solutions.

Mathematically, for an ILP of the form:

$$\max \{ c^T x : Ax \leq b, x \in \mathbb{Z}^n \}$$

a **cutting plane** is a valid inequality $\pi^T x \leq \pi_0$ such that:

1. It is satisfied by every integer feasible point (validity).
2. It is violated by the current fractional optimal solution of the LP relaxation (violation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Sculpting a Statue from Marble":**
>
> Imagine the LP relaxation as a large block of marble (a continuous convex polytope). The true feasible region — containing only integer points — is a tiny statue hidden inside. The LP optimum often lies on the smooth surface of the marble, far from the statue.
>
> A **cutting plane** acts like a chisel strike: it shaves off a smooth, fractional piece of marble that *cannot* contain any part of the hidden statue (because the cut respects all integer points) but does remove the current fractional optimum. After many such chisel strikes, the marble becomes so jagged that its corners (extreme points) coincide with the integer statue — solving the ILP exactly.

### 1.3 Why Cutting Planes Matter in Approximation Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> In the context of **Approximation Algorithms**, cutting planes serve a dual purpose:
> - **Exactness**: Combined with branch-and-bound, they form the backbone of modern ILP solvers (Branch-and-Cut).
> - **Approximation**: The LP relaxation strengthened by cuts provides a *tighter bound* on the optimal value, improving the integrality gap and yielding better approximation guarantees.

### 1.4 Canonical Notation Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $P = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : Ax \leq b\}$ | LP relaxation feasible region (polytope) |
| $S = P \cap \mathbb{Z}^n$ | Integer feasible set |
| $x^*$ | Current fractional LP optimum |
| $\pi^T x \leq \pi_0$ | A cutting plane inequality |
| $\text{conv}(S)$ | Convex hull of integer feasible solutions |
| $\pi_i, \pi_0$ | Row $i$ coefficients and right-hand-side of a cut |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Cutting Plane Loop

The cutting plane method proceeds via the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Solve Relaxation:** Compute the LP relaxation optimum $x^*$ of the current formulation.
- **Step 2 — Integrality Check:** Test whether $x^* \in \mathbb{Z}^n$. If yes, terminate with $x^*$ optimal for the ILP.
- **Step 3 — Cut Generation:** Search the LP tableau (or a combinatorial structure) for a row whose fractional RHS indicates a violated valid integer inequality.
- **Step 4 — Cut Addition:** Append the inequality to the formulation, shrinking $P$ toward $\text{conv}(S)$.
- **Step 5 — Re-solve:** Return to Step 1 with the strengthened LP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The convergence guarantee relies on the **Gomory Fractional Cut Theorem** (1958): for a pure ILP with rational data, there always exists a violated cut at any fractional basic feasible solution, and the process converges in finite steps.

### 2.2 Gomory's Mixed Integer (GMI) Cut — Derivation Skeleton

Given a tableau row in canonical form:

$$x_i + \sum_{j \in N} \bar{a}_{ij} x_j = \bar{b}_i$$

where $i \in B$ (basic), $\bar{b}_i \notin \mathbb{Z}$, and $N$ is the non-basic index set. Let $f_j = \bar{a}_{ij} - \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor$ be the fractional part. The **Gomory Mixed-Integer Cut** is:

$$\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i, \quad \text{where } f_i = \bar{b}_i - \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following core equations for the KTU Board Exam:**

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| LP Relaxation of ILP | $\max c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \leq b$, $x \geq 0$ | Provides upper bound; basis of duality theory |
| Gomory Fractional Cut | $\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i$ | Exact ILP solver primitive |
| Knapsack Cover Inequality | $\sum_{j \in C} x_j \leq \vert C \vert - 1$ for cover $C$ | Tightens 0–1 knapsack LP gaps |
| Lift Coefficient (Sequential) | $\alpha_j = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi_0 - \sum_{k \in L} \pi_k - \sum_{k \in T_j} \pi_k}{\bar{a}_j}\right)$ | Lifts a facet into higher dimensions |
| Integrality Gap | $\frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}}$ | Quantifies relaxation looseness |
| Disjunctive Cut (Balas) | $\bigvee_{k \in K} (\pi^k x \leq \pi^k_0)$ | General cut family via disjunctions |
| Total Unimodularity | $\det(A') \in \{-1, 0, 1\}, \forall A' \subseteq A$ | Guarantees LP = ILP, no cuts needed |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Cut Family

- **Why Gomory cuts?** They are *automatic*: any fractional basic variable row in the simplex tableau yields one. No problem-specific structure required.
- **Why cover cuts?** They exploit 0–1 knapsack structure: if a set $C$ of items cannot all be packed (cover), then at most $\vert C \vert - 1$ of them can be selected.
- **Why lift-and-project?** A facet of a lower-dimensional knapsack polytope can be *lifted* into the full space, producing a much stronger inequality.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **VLSI Design & Network Routing**: Chip placement problems as ILPs; cuts prune the search space.
- **Airline Crew Scheduling**: Set-covering ILPs solved via Branch-and-Cut.
- **Telecommunications**: Network design and frequency assignment.
- **Supply Chain & Logistics**: Vehicle routing with capacity constraints.
- **Machine Learning**: Integer SVM formulations use cutting planes for large-scale training.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gomory Fractional Cut

We begin with an LP tableau row corresponding to a basic variable $x_i$:

$$x_i + \sum_{j \in N} \bar{a}_{ij} x_j = \bar{b}_i$$

Since $x_j \geq 0$ for all $j$, and $x_i$ must be an integer (in a pure ILP), we apply the **integer decomposition**:

**Step 1** — Split coefficients into integer and fractional parts:

$$\bar{a}_{ij} = \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor + f_j, \quad \bar{b}_i = \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor + f_i, \quad 0 < f_i < 1$$

**Step 2** — Substitute into the row equation:

$$x_i + \sum_{j \in N} (\lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor + f_j) x_j = \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor + f_i$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange integer and fractional parts:

$$x_i + \sum_{j \in N} \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor x_j - \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor = f_i - \sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j$$

**Step 4** — Observe that the **left-hand side is an integer** (since $x_i, x_j \in \mathbb{Z}$ and floors yield integers), so the **right-hand side must also be an integer**.

**Step 5** — Since $0 \leq f_j < 1$ and $x_j \geq 0$, the term $\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq 0$, hence:

$$f_i - \sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \leq f_i < 1$$

Combined with the integrality of the LHS, we conclude:

$$f_i - \sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \leq 0$$

**Step 6** — Rearrange to obtain the **Gomory Fractional Cut**:

$$\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i$$

This inequality is valid for all integer solutions (because for any $x \in S$, the LHS minus $f_i$ equals an integer $\leq 0$, forcing LHS $\geq f_i$) and is violated by $x^* = \bar{b}_i$ when all non-basic variables are zero.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Gomory Cut

Consider the ILP:

$$\max x_1 + x_2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 5, \quad x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$$

**Step A — Solve LP relaxation:** The LP optimum occurs at the intersection of both constraints:

$$\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 &= 5 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 &= 4
\end{aligned}$$

Solving: multiply first by 2, subtract second:

$$4x_1 + 2x_2 - x_1 - 2x_2 = 10 - 4 \implies 3x_1 = 6 \implies x_1 = 2$$

Substitute: $x_2 = 5 - 2(2) = 1$. So $x^* = (2, 1)$ — **integer!** The LP happens to be integral here.

To force a fractional case, consider a modified problem with three constraints where the LP optimum is genuinely fractional:

$$\max x_1 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad -2x_1 + x_2 \leq 1, \quad 2x_1 - x_2 \leq 2, \quad x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$$

**Step B — LP optimum:** Adding both constraints gives $0 \leq 3$ (redundant). LP optimum: $x_1 = 3/2, x_2 = 4$ (intersection of $-2x_1 + x_2 = 1$ and $x_2 = 4$). Fractional!

**Step C — Tableau row:** In canonical form, a basic row is:

$$x_1 + \frac{1}{2} x_3 = \frac{3}{2}$$

Here $\bar{b}_1 = 3/2$, so $f_1 = 1/2$. The non-basic coefficient for $x_3$ is $\bar{a}_{13} = 1/2$, so $f_3 = 1/2$.

**Step D — Gomory cut:**

$$\frac{1}{2} x_3 \geq \frac{1}{2} \implies x_3 \geq 1$$

Since $x_3$ is the slack of $-2x_1 + x_2 \leq 1$, this gives $-2x_1 + x_2 \geq 1$, which is tight at the integer solution $(1, 3)$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strength Comparison of Cut Families

| Cut Family | Strength | Computation Cost | Problem-Specific? | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gomory Fractional | Weak (per cut) | Low | No (automatic) | **High** |
| Gomory Mixed-Integer | Moderate | Low | No | High |
| Knapsack Cover | Strong | Moderate | Yes (0–1 knapsack) | Medium |
| Lift-and-Project | Very Strong | High | No (general) | Medium |
| Disjunctive (Balas) | Very Strong | High | No | Low-Medium |
| Flow Cover | Strong | Moderate | Yes (network) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, **Gomory cuts are tested most frequently** because they require only tableau knowledge. Cover cuts and lift-and-project appear in 14-mark derivations.

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Lift-and-Project Cut (Sequential Lifting)

We start with a valid inequality for a subset of variables (a **facet-defining inequality** of a lower-dimensional knapsack polytope):

$$\sum_{j \in C_0} \pi^0_j x_j \leq \pi^0_0$$

valid for the knapsack $\{x \in \{0,1\}^n : \sum a_j x_j \leq b\}$, where $C_0$ is the original variable set. The inequality is **lifted** to include variables in $L = \{1, 2, \ldots, k\} \setminus C_0$.

**Step 1** — For variable $x_k$ not in the original inequality, compute the **lifting coefficient**:

$$\alpha_k = \min_{x \in \{0,1\}^n, \, x_j = 1 \forall j \in C_0, \, x_l = 0 \forall l > k} \frac{\pi^0_0 - \sum_{j \in C_0} \pi^0_j - \sum_{l \in L, l < k} \alpha_l x_l}{a_k}$$

**Step 2** — The lifted inequality becomes:

$$\sum_{j \in C_0} \pi^0_j x_j + \sum_{l \in L} \alpha_l x_l \leq \pi^0_0$$

**Step 3** — Verify validity: substituting $x_j = 1$ for $j \in C_0$ and the worst-case $x_l$ yields LHS = $\pi^0_0$, preserving validity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cutting Plane Iteration — High-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: ILP Formulation] --> B[Solve LP Relaxation]
    B --> C{Integrality Check: x* in Z^n?}

    E --> F[Generate Cutting Plane]

```


---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a cutting plane. State the two essential properties that any valid cutting plane must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**
A cutting plane is a linear inequality $\pi^T x \leq \pi_0$ added to an LP relaxation to exclude fractional solutions without removing any integer feasible solution. The two essential properties are:

1. **Validity:** The inequality must be satisfied by every integer feasible point in $S$.
2. **Violation:** The inequality must be violated by at least one point in the current LP feasible region (typically the fractional optimum $x^*$).

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Gomory fractional cut? Write its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**
The Gomory fractional cut is a cutting plane derived from a single tableau row of an LP relaxation that contains a fractional basic variable. For a row:

$$x_i + \sum_{j \in N} \bar{a}_{ij} x_j = \bar{b}_i$$

with $\bar{b}_i \notin \mathbb{Z}$, the cut is:

$$\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i$$

where $f_j = \bar{a}_{ij} - \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor$ and $f_i = \bar{b}_i - \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain the Gomory cutting plane algorithm for solving pure Integer Linear Programs. Derive the Gomory fractional cut from a simplex tableau row. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Apply the Gomory cutting plane method to solve the following ILP:

$$\max z = 3x_1 - x_2$$

subject to:
$$3x_1 - x_2 \leq 2, \quad x_1 + x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$$

Show all tableau iterations until an integer optimum is obtained. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The Gomory cutting plane algorithm operates in cycles:

1. **Initialize:** Solve the LP relaxation; let $x^*$ be the BFS.
2. **Check Integrality:** If $x^*$ is integer, terminate.
3. **Generate Cut:** Select a tableau row whose RHS $\bar{b}_i$ is fractional. Apply the Gomory formula.
4. **Append:** Add the cut as a new row and re-solve.

**Derivation:** From row $x_i + \sum_j \bar{a}_{ij} x_j = \bar{b}_i$, decompose $\bar{a}_{ij} = \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor + f_j$ and $\bar{b}_i = \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor + f_i$. Rearranging yields:

$$x_i + \sum_j \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor x_j - \lfloor \bar{b}_i \rfloor = f_i - \sum_j f_j x_j$$

The LHS is integer (sum of integer combinations), so RHS must be integer. Since $f_i - \sum_j f_j x_j < 1$, we obtain $f_i - \sum_j f_j x_j \leq 0$, giving:

$$\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i$$

This is the **Gomory Fractional Cut**.

**Part (b):**

**Iteration 1 — LP Relaxation:**
Standard form: $\max 3x_1 - x_2$ s.t. $3x_1 - x_2 + s_1 = 2$, $x_1 + x_2 + s_2 = 4$, all $\geq 0$.

Optimal BFS: $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 0$ (wait — check constraint $3(2) - 0 = 6 > 2$, infeasible).

Correcting: The feasible LP optimum requires both constraints active. Solving:
$$3x_1 - x_2 = 2 \quad \text{and} \quad x_1 + x_2 = 4$$

Add: $4x_1 = 6 \implies x_1 = 1.5, x_2 = 2.5$. So $x^* = (1.5, 2.5)$, $z = 3(1.5) - 2.5 = 2$.

**Tableau Row:** Express $x_1 = 1.5$ in terms of non-basic $s_1, s_2$:

Row for $x_1$: $x_1 + 0.5 s_1 - 0.25 s_2 = 1.5$ (after elimination). Here $f_i = 0.5$.

**Generate Cut:** $0.5 s_1 + 0.25 s_2 \geq 0.5$, i.e., $2s_1 + s_2 \geq 1$. Substituting back $s_1 = 2 - 3x_1 + x_2$ and $s_2 = 4 - x_1 - x_2$:

$$2(2 - 3x_1 + x_2) + (4 - x_1 - x_2) \geq 1 \implies 8 - 7x_1 + x_2 \geq 1 \implies 7x_1 - x_2 \leq 7$$

**Iteration 2:** Add $7x_1 - x_2 + s_3 = 7$ to LP. New intersection with previous active constraints yields $x^* = (1, 3)$, which is integer! Optimal $z = 3(1) - 3 = 0$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Define a knapsack cover inequality. Given the knapsack constraint $\sum_{j=1}^n a_j x_j \leq b$ with $x_j \in \{0,1\}$, explain how a "cover" $C$ is identified and how the cover inequality is constructed. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Consider the knapsack: $4x_1 + 5x_2 + 3x_3 + 6x_4 \leq 9$, $x_j \in \{0,1\}$. Identify a minimal cover, derive the corresponding cover inequality, and show how it tightens the LP relaxation compared to the original constraint alone. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

A **cover** $C \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ is a subset of items whose total weight exceeds the knapsack capacity:

$$\sum_{j \in C} a_j > b$$

A **knapsack cover inequality** states that at most $\vert C \vert - 1$ items from $C$ can be selected:

$$\sum_{j \in C} x_j \leq \vert C \vert - 1$$

A **minimal cover** is one where removing any single item destroys the cover property — i.e., for all $j \in C$, $\sum_{k \in C \setminus \{j\}} a_k \leq b$. Minimal covers yield the strongest cover cuts.

**Part (b):**

Given weights $\{4, 5, 3, 6\}$ and capacity $b = 9$:

- Try $C = \{2, 4\}$: $5 + 6 = 11 > 9$ ✓. Removing $x_2$: $6 \leq 9$ ✓. Removing $x_4$: $5 \leq 9$ ✓. So $C = \{2, 4\}$ is a **minimal cover**.
- Cover inequality: $x_2 + x_4 \leq 1$.

**LP relaxation tightening:** The original LP allows $x_2 = x_4 = 1$ (since $5 + 6 = 11$ but LP ignores integrality beyond the global constraint). However, $x_2 + x_4 = 2$ would force total weight $5 + 6 = 11 > 9$, which is infeasible. The cover cut $x_2 + x_4 \leq 1$ excludes this fractional corner point of the LP polytope, reducing the integrality gap.

The **lifted** form (sequential lifting of $x_1, x_3$) yields even stronger inequalities in the full 4-variable space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify validity:** Many students write the cut but do not justify why it is satisfied by *all* integer points. Always cite the integrality argument.
> 2. **Confusing fractional parts:** $f_j = \bar{a}_{ij} - \lfloor \bar{a}_{ij} \rfloor$ is the fractional part of the *coefficient*, not of $x_j$. Misuse is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Direction of inequality:** Gomory fractional cut for a *max* ILP uses $\geq$ for non-basic variable coefficient sum (in tableau row form). Reversing the sign costs full marks on the derivation.
> 4. **Skipping the convergence argument:** State that Gomory cuts converge in finite steps for rational data — this is a 1-mark bookmark.
> 5. **Not labelling tableau rows:** In numerical problems, always identify the basic variable, non-basic variables, and the fractional RHS explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Cutting plane** = valid + violated linear inequality added to LP relaxation.
- ✅ **Gomory Fractional Cut:** $\sum_{j \in N} f_j x_j \geq f_i$, derived from any fractional tableau row.
- ✅ **Fractional part function:** $f(x) = x - \lfloor x \rfloor$, satisfies $0 \leq f(x) < 1$.
- ✅ **Minimal cover:** Cover where removing any item breaks the cover property.
- ✅ **Cover inequality:** $\sum_{j \in C} x_j \leq \vert C \vert - 1$ for cover $C$.
- ✅ **Lifting** strengthens a low-dimensional facet into the full space via sequential or simultaneous procedures.
- ✅ **Disjunctive cuts (Balas):** Generated from unions of polyhedra $P = \bigcup_k P^k$.
- ✅ **Integrality gap:** $\text{Gap} = (\text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}} - \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}) / \text{OPT}_{\text{ILP}}$; cuts reduce this gap.
- ✅ **Total unimodularity:** If $A$ is TU, LP relaxation solves ILP exactly — no cuts needed.
- ✅ **Branch-and-Cut:** Practical framework combining cuts with branching on fractional variables.
- ✅ **Convergence:** Gomory's algorithm terminates in finite steps for pure ILPs with rational data.
- ✅ **Strength order (weakest to strongest):** Gomory fractional < Gomory mixed-integer < cover < lifted cover < disjunctive.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always show the *tableau row*, the *fractional decomposition*, and the *final cut form* in three explicit lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Network Design
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications in Network Design

# Applications in Network Design — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Design Problems (NDPs)** are a broad class of combinatorial optimization problems in which the goal is to construct a **cost-minimum sub-network** of a given weighted, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ such that the chosen sub-network satisfies a prescribed set of **connectivity**, **coverage**, or **capacity** requirements on the vertices and edges.

In the approximation-algorithms context, NDPs are **NP-hard**, so we design polynomial-time algorithms whose solution cost is provably within a multiplicative factor $\rho \geq 1$ of the optimal (i.e., an **$\rho$-approximation**).

Three canonical NDPs that appear repeatedly in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **The Steiner Tree Problem (STP)** — connect a given terminal set $S \subseteq V$ at minimum cost.
2. **The Metric Traveling Salesman Problem (Metric TSP)** — find a minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle.
3. **The $k$-Minimum Spanning Tree Problem ($k$-MST)** — find a minimum-cost tree spanning exactly $k$ specified vertices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Layman View):**
> Imagine a state government wants to lay **fibre-optic cable** to connect $k$ district headquarters. The geography gives a graph where every possible trench between two cities has a known digging cost. The goal is to find the **cheapest trench layout** that keeps all $k$ headquarters connected. Since checking all possibilities is impossible (combinatorial explosion), we use **approximation algorithms** that give a provably good-enough layout — say, never more than **twice the cost** of the optimal (impossible-to-compute) plan. This is the essence of NDP approximation.

In engineering parlance, NDPs underpin **VLSI circuit routing**, **telecommunication backbone design**, **water pipeline planning**, **road-network construction**, and **last-mile broadband delivery**. The unifying metric is the **approximation ratio** $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Cost(Alg)}}{\text{Cost(OPT)}}$, which the KTU 2024 syllabus flags as the **standard efficiency gauge**.

### 1.3 Key Parameters & Standard Metrics

- **Graph $G = (V, E)$**: Undirected weighted graph, $|V| = n$, $|E| = m$.
- **Edge cost function $c : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{\geq 0}$**: Non-negative weights, obeys **triangle inequality** for metric problems.
- **Terminal set $S \subseteq V$**: Required nodes (for Steiner Tree / $k$-MST).
- **Standard metrics**: Cost, running time $T(n)$, approximation ratio $\rho$, integrality gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Steiner Tree Problem (STP)

### Problem Statement
Given $G = (V, E, c)$ and a **terminal set** $S \subseteq V$, find a minimum-cost **Steiner tree** $T \subseteq G$ such that every vertex in $S$ lies in a single connected component of $T$. Non-terminal vertices in $T$ are called **Steiner vertices**.

### The MST-Based 2-Approximation (Awerbuch, Azar, Bartal — Classical)
The simplest KTU-grade algorithm:

1. Construct the **complete metric closure** $G_S$ on terminals: edge weight between $u, v \in S$ is the shortest-path distance in $G$.
2. Compute an **MST** $M$ of $G_S$.
3. Replace every edge of $M$ with its corresponding shortest path in $G$ to obtain $T$.

**Why does it work?** The optimal Steiner tree $T^*$ contains a **spanning tree of $S$** when edges are replaced by shortest paths; this spanning tree has cost at most $\text{cost}(T^*)$. The MST of $G_S$ has cost at most this spanning tree (MST is optimal for connectivity). Therefore:

$$\text{cost}(M) \leq \text{cost}(T^*) \implies \text{cost}(T) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(T^*)$$

The factor of $2$ arises because every edge of $M$ is doubled in the worst case when paths overlap.

## 2.2 The Metric Traveling Salesman Problem (Metric TSP)

### Problem Statement
Given a complete weighted graph $G = (V, E, c)$ satisfying the **triangle inequality**, find a minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle (a tour visiting every vertex exactly once).

### The MST-Doubling 2-Approximation
1. Compute an MST $M$ of $G$.
2. Double every edge to form an **Eulerian multigraph**.
3. Find an Eulerian tour of the doubled graph.
4. Shortcut the Eulerian tour by skipping previously visited vertices.

**Approximation guarantee:** Because shortcutting never increases cost (triangle inequality), the cost of the tour $C$ satisfies:

$$\text{cost}(C) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(M) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$$

### Christofides' 1.5-Approximation (KTU High-Yield)
1. Compute MST $M$.
2. Find a **minimum-weight perfect matching** $P$ on the set of odd-degree vertices of $M$.
3. Combine $M \cup P$ (Eulerian multigraph) and take an Eulerian tour.
4. Shortcut to obtain a Hamiltonian cycle.

**Approximation ratio: $\dfrac{3}{2}$** — a celebrated result (1976).

## 2.3 The $k$-Minimum Spanning Tree ($k$-MST)

### Problem Statement
Given $G = (V, E, c)$ and an integer $k \leq n$, find a minimum-cost tree spanning **exactly $k$ vertices** of $V$.

### Garg's 2-Approximation (KTU Pattern)
A classic 2-approximation uses a **Local Search / Augmentation** technique:

1. Start with any vertex as the current tree $T$.
2. While $|V(T)| < k$, greedily add the cheapest edge connecting $T$ to $V \setminus V(T)$.
3. Output $T$.

**Approximation ratio: $2$** — provable via an exchange argument comparing to an optimal $k$-MST.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU 2024 ESE**

| **Problem** | **Algorithm** | **Approx. Ratio $\rho$** | **Time Complexity** | **Key Inequality** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Steiner Tree | MST on metric closure | $2$ | $O(n^3)$ or $O(m \log n)$ | $\text{cost}(T) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$ |
| Metric TSP | MST-doubling | $2$ | $O(m + n\log n)$ | $\text{cost}(C) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(M) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$ |
| Metric TSP | Christofides | $\dfrac{3}{2}$ | $O(n^3)$ | $\text{cost}(C) \leq \text{MST} + \text{Match} \leq \dfrac{3}{2}\,\text{OPT}$ |
| $k$-MST | Greedy augmentation | $2$ | $O(kn + m\log n)$ | $\text{cost}(T) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$ |
| Metric TSP lower bound | MST weight | — | — | $\text{cost}(\text{MST}) \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$ |
| Triangle inequality | Definition | — | — | $c(u,w) \leq c(u,v) + c(v,w)$ |
| Approximation ratio | Definition | — | — | $\rho = \dfrac{\max_{I}\,\text{cost}(\text{Alg}(I))}{\text{cost}(\text{OPT}(I))}$ |
| Eulerian tour | Hierholzer | — | $O(m)$ | visits every edge exactly once |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility**: MST-based approximations are the **backbone of industrial network planners** — they appear in Cisco's OSPF routing weight setting, in Google's backbone fiber layout, and in power-grid transmission expansion planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: MST-Doubling 2-Approximation for Metric TSP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Prove that the MST-doubling algorithm returns a tour of cost at most $2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$.

### Setup
Let $G = (V, E, c)$ be a complete weighted graph satisfying the triangle inequality. Let $\text{OPT}$ be a minimum-cost Hamiltonian tour of cost $C^* = \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$.

### Step 1 — Lower Bound on OPT via MST
Removing any one edge of the tour $\text{OPT}$ yields a **spanning tree** of $G$. Since the MST is the cheapest spanning tree:

$$\text{cost}(\text{MST}) \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT} - \{e\}) \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(\text{MST}) &\leq C^* \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Construct Eulerian Multigraph
Take the MST $M$ and **double every edge** to obtain a multigraph $M'$ in which every vertex has even degree. Therefore $M'$ admits an **Eulerian tour** $\mathcal{E}$ that traverses every edge of $M'$ exactly once.

The cost of the Eulerian tour equals the cost of $M'$:

$$\text{cost}(\mathcal{E}) = 2 \cdot \text{cost}(M)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Triangle Inequality (Shortcutting)
The Eulerian tour $\mathcal{E}$ visits some vertices multiple times. Replace every revisit with a **direct edge** using the triangle inequality:

$$c(u,w) \leq c(u,v) + c(v,w)$$

Shortcutting never increases cost, so the resulting **Hamiltonian cycle** $H$ satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(H) &\leq \text{cost}(\mathcal{E}) \\
\text{cost}(H) &\leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(M) \\
\text{cost}(H) &\leq 2 \cdot C^* \quad \text{(by Step 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{\text{cost}(H)}{C^*} \leq 2}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Christofides' 1.5-Approximation

### Setup
Let $M$ be the MST. Let $O \subseteq V$ be the set of **odd-degree vertices** of $M$. A fundamental graph theory result: $|O|$ is **even**.

### Step 1 — MST Lower Bound on OPT
Same as before:

$$\text{cost}(M) \leq C^*$$

### Step 2 — Minimum Weight Perfect Matching on $O$
Compute a **minimum-weight perfect matching** $P$ on the complete graph induced by $O$. The weight of $P$ satisfies a critical inequality. Partition the edges of $\text{OPT}$:

- The tour $\text{OPT}$ restricted to $O$ gives a collection of paths whose endpoints lie in $O$.
- The minimum matching on $O$ is at most the cost of any perfect matching, in particular the one obtained by taking alternating edges of the tour.

This yields the key inequality:

$$\text{cost}(P) \leq \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C^*$$

### Step 3 — Form Eulerian Multigraph and Shortcut
The multigraph $M \cup P$ has all vertices of even degree (MST contributes odd-degree only to $O$, and $P$ covers exactly those). Take an Eulerian tour $\mathcal{E}'$ and shortcut to a Hamiltonian cycle $H'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cost}(H') &\leq \text{cost}(M) + \text{cost}(P) \\
&\leq C^* + \dfrac{1}{2} C^* \\
&= \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot C^*
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Approximation Ratio

$$
\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{3}{2}}
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Steiner Tree 2-Approximation

### Step 1 — Construct Metric Closure
For every pair of terminals $u, v \in S$, let $\text{dist}(u, v)$ be the shortest-path distance in $G$. Build $G_S = (S, \binom{S}{2}, c_S)$ where $c_S(u,v) = \text{dist}(u,v)$.

### Step 2 — Lower Bound via OPT
Let $T^*$ be an optimal Steiner tree. In $T^*$, every pair of terminals is connected by a path. Take any spanning tree $T_S$ of $S$ inside $T^*$:

$$\text{cost}(T_S) \leq \text{cost}(T^*)$$

### Step 3 — MST on Closure is Optimal on $S$
Let $M_S$ be the MST of $G_S$:

$$\text{cost}(M_S) \leq \text{cost}(T_S) \leq \text{cost}(T^*)$$

### Step 4 — Replace Each Edge of $M_S$
Each edge $(u, v)$ of $M_S$ corresponds to a shortest path $P_{uv}$ in $G$ of cost at most $c_S(u,v)$. Substitute each edge:

$$\text{cost}(T) = \sum_{(u,v) \in M_S} \text{cost}(P_{uv}) \leq \sum_{(u,v) \in M_S} c_S(u,v) = \text{cost}(M_S)$$

But each original edge of $T^*$ may appear in **two** such shortest paths (once per endpoint), giving the factor of $2$:

$$\text{cost}(T) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(T^*)$$

$$
\boxed{\rho = 2 \text{ for Steiner Tree via metric-closure MST}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: MST-Doubling Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Complete weighted graph G] --> B[Compute MST M using Kruskal or Prim]
    B --> C[Doubling Phase: Create multigraph M-prime by duplicating every edge of M]
    C --> D[Degree Check: Every vertex now has even degree]
    D --> E[Run Hierholzer Algorithm to find Eulerian Tour E]
    E --> F[Shortcutting Phase: Skip previously visited vertices using triangle inequality]
    F --> G[Output: Hamiltonian Cycle H]
    G --> H{Is cost of H within 2 times OPT?}
    H --> I[Yes - 2-approximation certified]
    H --> J[No - Algorithm failed - Debug]
    I --> K[Return H as final tour]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f0
    style G fill:#e1f0ff
    style H fill:#ffffcc
    style I fill:#ccffcc
    style K fill:#ccffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Christofides' Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Complete metric graph G] --> B[Step 1: Compute MST M]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify odd-degree vertices set O]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute min-weight perfect matching P on O using Edmonds or Blossom]
    D --> E[Step 4: Combine M and P to form Eulerian multigraph]
    E --> F[Step 5: Find Eulerian tour E via Hierholzer]
    F --> G[Step 6: Shortcut E to Hamiltonian cycle H]
    G --> H[Cost Analysis: cost of H is at most MST + Match]
    H --> I[Bound: cost of H is at most 1.5 times OPT]
    I --> J[Output: Hamiltonian Cycle H with ratio 3/2]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f0
    style G fill:#e1f0ff
    style H fill:#ffffcc
    style I fill:#ccffcc
    style J fill:#ccffff
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Steiner Tree Approximation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Graph G with terminals S] --> B[Phase 1: Compute all-pairs shortest paths on G]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Build metric closure G-S on terminals]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Compute MST M-S of G-S]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Map each edge of M-S back to a shortest path in G]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Union of all mapped paths gives Steiner tree T]
    F --> G[Cost Bound: cost of T is at most 2 times OPT]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f0
    style G fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map of Network Design Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Design Problems] --> B[Connectivity Based]
    A --> C[Coverage Based]
    A --> D[Tour Based]

    B --> B1[Steiner Tree - 2-approx via MST closure]
    B --> B2[k-MST - 2-approx via greedy augmentation]
    B --> B3[Survivable Network Design - LP rounding]

    C --> C1[Set Cover - log n approx]
    C --> C2[Facility Location - 1.488 approx]
    C --> C3[k-Center - 2-approx]

    D --> D1[Metric TSP - Christofides 1.5]
    D --> D2[Asymmetric TSP - log n approx]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**Q: Define the Steiner Tree Problem and state the approximation ratio of the MST-based algorithm on the metric closure. Justify why the approximation ratio is at most 2.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Given an undirected weighted graph $G = (V, E, c)$ and a terminal set $S \subseteq V$, the Steiner Tree Problem asks for a minimum-cost sub-tree $T$ such that every vertex of $S$ lies in $T$. Vertices in $T \setminus S$ are called Steiner vertices.
- **Algorithm (1 Mark):** Construct the metric closure on $S$ by setting $c'(u,v)$ to the shortest-path distance in $G$ between $u, v \in S$. Compute the MST $M$ of the closure, then map each edge of $M$ back to its shortest path in $G$.
- **Justification (1 Mark):** The optimal Steiner tree $T^*$ restricted to terminals gives a spanning tree of cost at most $T^*$. MST is optimal, so $\text{cost}(M) \leq \text{cost}(T^*)$. Each edge of $M$ is replaced by a shortest path whose cost equals the edge cost. Worst case, every original edge is covered twice, so $\text{cost}(\text{output}) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(T^*)$. Hence $\rho = 2$.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**Q: Explain Christofides' algorithm for the Metric TSP. What is the achieved approximation ratio and what is the key inequality used in its analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Algorithm Steps (2 Marks):**
  1. Compute MST $M$ of $G$.
  2. Find minimum-weight perfect matching $P$ on odd-degree vertices of $M$.
  3. Combine $M \cup P$ (Eulerian multigraph).
  4. Take Eulerian tour and shortcut using triangle inequality.
- **Approximation Ratio (1 Mark):** $\rho = \dfrac{3}{2}$, derived from the key inequality $\text{cost}(P) \leq \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$, so $\text{cost}(\text{Hamiltonian cycle}) \leq \text{cost}(M) + \text{cost}(P) \leq \text{OPT} + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{OPT} = \dfrac{3}{2}\text{OPT}$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | ESE Pattern]**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):**  
Consider the metric TSP on the following graph with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and edge weights: $c(1,2) = 3$, $c(1,3) = 5$, $c(1,4) = 4$, $c(2,3) = 2$, $c(2,4) = 6$, $c(3,4) = 7$. Apply the **MST-doubling 2-approximation algorithm** step-by-step and compute the final tour cost. Verify that the ratio is at most $2 \cdot \text{OPT}$.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sort edges by weight (1 Mark):**  
$(2,3)=2,\ (1,2)=3,\ (1,4)=4,\ (1,3)=5,\ (2,4)=6,\ (3,4)=7$.

**Step 2 — Run Kruskal's algorithm (2 Marks):**  
- Add $(2,3)$ with cost $2$. Components: $\{2,3\}, \{1\}, \{4\}$.
- Add $(1,2)$ with cost $3$. Components: $\{1,2,3\}, \{4\}$.
- Add $(1,4)$ with cost $4$. Components: $\{1,2,3,4\}$.  
MST cost $= 2 + 3 + 4 = 9$.

**Step 3 — Double every edge (1 Mark):**  
Multigraph has edges: $(2,3) \times 2,\ (1,2) \times 2,\ (1,4) \times 2$. Cost $= 18$.

**Step 4 — Eulerian Tour and Shortcutting (2 Marks):**  
A valid Eulerian tour: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1 \to 4 \to 1$.  
Shortcut: skip repeated vertices $\Rightarrow$ Tour: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$.  
Cost $= c(1,2) + c(2,3) + c(3,4) + c(4,1) = 3 + 2 + 7 + 4 = 16$.

**Step 5 — Verify ratio (1 Mark):**  
OPT is at least MST $= 9$. Final cost $= 16$. Ratio $\leq 16/9 < 2$. Hence 2-approximation certified.

**Part (b) (7 Marks):**  
Prove rigorously that the MST-doubling algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of $2$ for Metric TSP. Clearly state the role of the triangle inequality in your proof.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

- **Claim (1 Mark):** $\text{cost}(H) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$ where $H$ is the Hamiltonian cycle produced.
- **Lower bound on OPT (2 Marks):** Removing any single edge from OPT gives a spanning tree $T'$. MST is optimal, so $\text{cost}(M) \leq \text{cost}(T') \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$.
- **Eulerian doubling (2 Marks):** Doubling every edge of $M$ gives an Eulerian multigraph of cost $2 \cdot \text{cost}(M)$. Hierholzer finds an Eulerian tour of this cost.
- **Triangle inequality role (2 Marks):** The Eulerian tour revisits vertices. For any revisit at vertex $v$ between $u$ and $w$, we replace the sub-path with direct edge $(u, w)$, costing at most the sum by triangle inequality: $c(u,w) \leq c(u,v) + c(v,w)$. Hence shortcutting does not increase cost. Combining: $\text{cost}(H) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(M) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$. QED.

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 2 | ESE Pattern]**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):**  
Explain the **Steiner Tree Problem** with an example. Describe the **MST-based 2-approximation** on the metric closure with all algorithmic steps.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

- **Problem definition with example (2 Marks):** Consider $G$ with vertices $\{A, B, C, D\}$ and terminal set $S = \{A, B, C\}$. A Steiner tree is a minimum-cost sub-tree connecting $A, B, C$. Non-terminals may be used as Steiner vertices.
- **Metric closure construction (2 Marks):** Compute shortest-path distances between every pair of terminals; build a complete graph $G_S$ on $S$ with these distances as edge weights.
- **MST of closure (1 Mark):** Compute the MST $M_S$ of $G_S$ using Kruskal's algorithm.
- **Tree reconstruction (1 Mark):** Substitute each edge of $M_S$ with the corresponding shortest path in $G$. Take the union of all these paths as the Steiner tree.
- **Ratio bound (1 Mark):** $\text{cost}(T) \leq 2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$.

**Part (b) (7 Marks):**  
Describe the **$k$-MST problem**. Outline Garg's greedy 2-approximation algorithm and justify the approximation bound.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

- **Problem definition (2 Marks):** Given $G = (V, E, c)$ and integer $k \leq n$, find a minimum-cost tree spanning exactly $k$ vertices.
- **Greedy algorithm (2 Marks):** Start with an arbitrary vertex. Repeatedly add the cheapest edge connecting the current tree $T$ to a new vertex not in $T$. Continue until $|V(T)| = k$.
- **Exchange argument (2 Marks):** Let $\text{OPT}$ be the optimal $k$-MST. Each edge added by the greedy algorithm has cost at most the cost of some edge incident to a corresponding vertex in OPT (otherwise OPT would include a cheaper edge, contradicting its minimality). Thus the total greedy cost is bounded by $2 \cdot \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$ (factor 2 from the doubling argument on each matched edge of OPT).
- **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The greedy algorithm yields a 2-approximation for $k$-MST.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing MST lower bound with OPT (loses 1–2 marks):** Many students write $\text{cost}(\text{MST}) = \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$. This is **wrong**. The correct inequality is $\text{cost}(\text{MST}) \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT})$, since OPT is a *tour* (not a tree), and removing one edge yields a tree of cost at most OPT.
> 2. **Skipping the triangle inequality (loses 1 mark):** In MST-doubling and Christofides, the triangle inequality is the **engine** that makes shortcutting safe. Any answer missing this justification is incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting the metric closure step in Steiner Tree (loses 2 marks):** Many students jump directly to MST on $G$. Always build the closure $G_S$ on $S$ first.
> 4. **Misstating Christofides ratio as 2 (loses 1 mark):** It is $\dfrac{3}{2}$, not $2$. The matching bound $\text{cost}(P) \leq \dfrac{1}{2}\text{OPT}$ is the differentiator.
> 5. **Forgetting that odd-degree set has even cardinality (loses 1 mark):** This is what guarantees a perfect matching exists in Christofides.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Network Design Problems (NDPs)** seek minimum-cost sub-networks with connectivity/coverage constraints.
- **Steiner Tree** — connect a terminal set $S$; MST on metric closure gives $\rho = 2$.
- **Metric TSP** — minimum Hamiltonian tour with triangle inequality.
- **MST-doubling algorithm** for Metric TSP achieves $\rho = 2$; uses Eulerian tour + shortcutting.
- **Christofides' algorithm** achieves $\rho = \dfrac{3}{2}$; uses MST + minimum perfect matching on odd-degree vertices.
- **$k$-MST** — minimum tree spanning exactly $k$ vertices; greedy augmentation gives $\rho = 2$.
- **Triangle inequality** is essential for shortcutting operations; without it, no bounded approximation exists for general TSP (unless P $=$ NP).
- **Odd-degree vertex set** in any graph has even cardinality (handshaking lemma) — this is what makes Christofides' perfect matching valid.
- **Lower bound for TSP**: $\text{cost}(\text{MST}) \leq \text{cost}(\text{OPT}_{\text{TSP}})$.
- **Lower bound for Steiner**: MST of metric closure $\leq$ OPT.
- **Approximation ratio** definition: $\rho = \dfrac{\max_I \text{cost}(\text{Alg}(I))}{\text{cost}(\text{OPT}(I))}$.
- **Running time complexity** for MST: $O(m \log n)$ via Kruskal or $O(m + n\log n)$ via Prim.
- **Running time complexity** for perfect matching: $O(n^3)$ via Edmonds' Blossom algorithm.
- **Industrial applications**: VLSI routing, telecom backbones, power grids, water pipelines, fiber-optic networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Semi-Definite Programming (SDP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Semi-Definite Programming (SDP) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Semi-Definite Programming (SDP) A **Semi-Definite Program (SDP)** is a convex optimization problem in which we minimize (or maximize) a **linear objective function** of a matrix variable, subject to **linear matrix inequality (LMI) constraints** that require certain matrices to be **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Primal Form of an SDP (Primal Standard Form)** > > $$\min \; C \bullet X$$ > > $$\text{subject to } A_i \bullet X = b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$ > > $$X \succeq 0$$ > > where $X, C, A_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are symmetric matrices, and the **Frobenius inner product** is defined as: > > $$A \bullet B = \text{trace}(A^T B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij} B_{ij}$$ The key constraint is $X \succeq 0$, which means **$X$ is a Positive Semi-Definite matrix** (all eigenvalues $\lambda_i \ge 0$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD) Matrix — Three Equivalent Definitions** > > A symmetric matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is PSD ($X \succeq 0$) if **any** of the following hold: > 1. **Eigenvalue Form:** All eigenvalues of $X$ are non-negative: $\lambda_i \ge 0$. > 2. **Quadratic Form:** $v^T X v \ge 0$ for every non-zero vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^n$. > 3. **Factorization Form:** $X = V^T V$ for some matrix $V \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ (Cholesky-type decomposition). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box: The Spectrahedron Analogy** > > - A **Linear Program (LP)** searches for the best point inside a **polyhedron** (a region bounded by flat hyperplanes). > - A **Semi-Definite Program (SDP)** searches for the best matrix $X$ inside a **spectrahedron** — a convex region defined by intersecting a PSD cone with affine hyperplanes. > - Think of an SDP as an LP lifted into "matrix space." Instead of choosing a vector of real numbers, we choose a symmetric matrix, but the rules of the game (linear cost, linear constraints, convex PSD cone) remain the same. **Real-World Analogy (Geometric):** Imagine you are a city planner trying to design a seating chart. An LP would treat each guest as a number on a line. An SDP, however, treats each guest as a **vector in a high-dimensional space**, and the "comfort" between two guests is measured by the **angle between their vectors**. The PSD constraint $X \succeq 0$ guarantees that the assignment of vectors is geometrically valid (corresponds to a real inner product structure, i.e., a valid Gram matrix). This makes SDPs perfect for **Max-Cut, Max-SAT, Coloring, and Clustering** problems. --- ## 1.3 Why SDPs Matter in Approximation Algorithms > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Power of SDP Relaxations** > > Many NP-hard combinatorial problems can be expressed naturally as problems about *unit vectors* or *$\{-1, +1\}$ variables*. When we relax integer or discrete constraints into PSD matrix constraints, we obtain a **convex relaxation** that: > > 1. Can be **solved in polynomial time** (interior-point methods). > 2. Provides a **provable upper bound** on the integer optimum. > 3. Enables **rounding schemes** that convert the continuous SDP solution back to a discrete feasible solution with **approximation guarantees** (e.g., Goemans–Williamson 0.878 for Max-Cut). Explicit constants and parameters to remember: - **Feasible region of SDP:** Spectrahedron $\mathcal{S} = \{ X \succeq 0 : A_i \bullet X = b_i \}$. - **Goemans–Williamson constant:** $\alpha_{GW} \approx \mathbf{0.87856}$ (derived in Section 3). - **Diameter of unit sphere (used in rounding):** $2\pi$. - **$2/\pi \approx \mathbf{0.6366}$:** Worst-case ratio of linear cut-probability to quadratic cut-weight. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an SDP An SDP is built from three structural pillars: 1. **Matrix variable** $X \in \mathbb{S}^n$ (the space of $n \times n$ symmetric real matrices). This has $n(n+1)/2$ independent scalar variables. 2. **Linear cost** $C \bullet X$, which is a *linear* function of the entries of $X$. 3. **PSD cone constraint** $X \succeq 0$, which is a *convex* constraint defining a proper cone in $\mathbb{S}^n$. > [!NOTE] > **Connection to Linear Programming** > > If we restrict all matrices to be **diagonal**, $X = \text{diag}(x_1, \dots, x_n)$, then the SDP collapses to a Linear Program: > > $$\min c^T x \quad \text{subject to } a_i^T x = b_i, \quad x \ge 0$$ > > Hence **LP $\subseteq$ SDP**, and SDPs are strictly more expressive (they can model certain quadratic/non-linear problems that LPs cannot). --- ## 2.2 The Dual SDP Every SDP has a **dual**. The dual of the primal SDP (introduced in Section 1.1) is: $$\max \; b^T y$$ $$\text{subject to } \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i - C \succeq 0$$ Here $y = (y_1, \dots, y_m) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the dual variable vector, and the constraint is a single **matrix inequality** requiring the matrix $\sum y_i A_i - C$ to be PSD. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strong Duality:** For SDPs that satisfy a constraint qualification (e.g., Slater's condition: there exists a strictly feasible primal point $X \succ 0$), the primal and dual optima are **equal**. This is the same as LP's strong duality, and is essential for the **approximation ratio analysis** via SDP. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Conditions | |---|---------|----------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Frobenius Inner Product | $A \bullet B = \text{trace}(A^T B) = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij}$ | Symmetric matrices, real entries | | 2 | PSD Condition (Eigenvalue) | $\lambda_i(X) \ge 0 \; \forall i$ | Equivalent to $X \succeq 0$ | | 3 | PSD Condition (Quadratic) | $v^T X v \ge 0 \; \forall v \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Holds even for non-symmetric $X$ if test on $X+X^T$ | | 4 | Gram Matrix Identity | $X = V^T V \iff X \succeq 0$ | $V \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ (e.g., $k = \text{rank}(X)$) | | 5 | Primal SDP | $\min C \bullet X \;\text{s.t.}\; A_i \bullet X = b_i,\; X \succeq 0$ | Matrix variable $X$ | | 6 | Dual SDP | $\max b^T y \;\text{s.t.}\; \sum y_i A_i - C \succeq 0$ | Vector variable $y$ | | 7 | Max-Cut (Integer) | $\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \dfrac{1 - v_i v_j}{2}, \quad v_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ | NP-hard | | 8 | Max-Cut (SDP Relaxation) | $\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \dfrac{1 - \langle v_i, v_j \rangle}{2}, \quad \Vert v_i \Vert = 1$ | Relax: $v_i v_j \to \langle v_i, v_j \rangle$ | | 9 | Cut-Probability (GW Rounding) | $\Pr[\text{cut}(i,j)] = \dfrac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$ | $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(\langle v_i, v_j \rangle)$ | | 10 | GW Approximation Constant | $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in [0,\pi]} \dfrac{\theta/\pi}{(1-\cos\theta)/2} \approx \mathbf{0.87856}$ | Achieved at $\theta \approx 2.0$ rad | | 11 | Rank Reduction Trick | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ can be replaced by $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ with $X = V^T V$ | Use Cholesky to convert matrix to vectors | | 12 | Inner Product from Gram Matrix | $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = X_{ij}$ | Where $X = V^T V$ | > [!NOTE] > **No Vertical Pipes Inside Tables:** Where absolute value or conditional notation was needed in the table above, we used $\vert$ (LaTeX `\vert`) inside math mode, not the markdown pipe `|`, to preserve table integrity. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Control Theory:** Lyapunov stability analysis, robust control, LMIs (Linear Matrix Inequalities) are solved as SDPs. - **Signal Processing:** Sensor network localization, beamforming optimization. - **Machine Learning:** Kernel methods, Maximum Variance Unfolding, matrix completion. - **Combinatorial Optimization (this course):** Max-Cut, Graph Coloring, Satisfiability, Correlation Clustering, Approximation of quadratic objectives. - **Structural Engineering:** Truss topology optimization under stress constraints. > [!TIP] > For KTU exam purposes, the **Max-Cut problem** is the *canonical* application of SDP-based approximation. Master it deeply. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 From Max-Cut (Integer) to SDP Relaxation — Full Derivation ### Step 1: The Integer Formulation of Max-Cut Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, the **Max-Cut** problem asks for a partition of the vertices into two sets $(S, \bar{S})$ such that the number of edges crossing the partition is maximized. We can encode this with **$\{-1, +1\}$ variables**: $$v_i = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if vertex } i \in S \\ -1 & \text{if vertex } i \in \bar{S} \end{cases}$$ For any edge $(i, j) \in E$, the cut indicator is: $$\text{cut}(i, j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } v_i \ne v_j \\ 0 & \text{if } v_i = v_j \end{cases} = \frac{1 - v_i v_j}{2}$$ Hence: $$\text{MAX-CUT: } \quad \max \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - v_i v_j}{2} \quad \text{subject to } v_i \in \{-1, +1\} \; \forall i$$ This is an **NP-hard** combinatorial problem because of the discrete constraint $v_i \in \{-1, +1\}$. --- ### Step 2: Vector Reformulation Replace each scalar $v_i$ with a **unit vector** $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (a vector of unit norm). The product $v_i v_j$ (which was a scalar product of two scalars) is replaced by the **inner product** of two vectors: $$v_i v_j \quad \longrightarrow \quad \langle v_i, v_j \rangle = v_i^T v_j$$ This gives: $$\max \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - \langle v_i, v_j \rangle}{2} \quad \text{subject to } \Vert v_i \Vert^2 = 1 \; \forall i$$ This is still non-convex (and equivalent to the integer formulation) because the inner products $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle$ are *non-linear* in the variables. --- ### Step 3: The Gram Matrix and PSD Relaxation Define the **Gram matrix** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ as: $$X_{ij} = \langle v_i, v_j \rangle$$ Equivalently, if we stack the vectors as rows of a matrix $V = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, then: $$X = V^T V$$ By the **factorization form of PSD**, $X = V^T V$ implies that $X$ is **positive semi-definite**: $$X \succeq 0$$ Furthermore, the unit norm condition $\Vert v_i \Vert^2 = 1$ becomes: $$X_{ii} = \langle v_i, v_i \rangle = 1 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ The objective $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = X_{ij}$ is now **linear in the matrix entries of $X$**. Therefore, the relaxed problem becomes: > [!NOTE] > **MAX-CUT SDP RELAXATION (Primal Form)** > > $$\text{maximize } \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - X_{ij}}{2}$$ > > $$\text{subject to } X_{ii} = 1 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ > > $$X \succeq 0$$ This is a **convex optimization problem** (a true SDP) and can be solved in polynomial time using interior-point methods. --- ### Step 4: Putting the SDP in Standard Matrix Form Let $L$ be the **Laplacian matrix** of graph $G$: $L = D - A$, where $A$ is the adjacency matrix and $D$ is the diagonal degree matrix. We can show that the SDP objective is equivalent to: $$\sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - X_{ij}}{2} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i, j} A_{ij}(1 - X_{ij}) = \frac{1}{4} \left( \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} - \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} X_{ij} \right) = \frac{\vert E \vert}{4} - \frac{1}{4} L \bullet X$$ The first term is a constant, so **maximizing** the SDP objective is equivalent to **minimizing** $L \bullet X$. Putting it in the standard primal SDP form: $$\min \; L \bullet X \quad \text{subject to} \quad X_{ii} = 1 \; \forall i, \quad X \succeq 0$$ The "objective matrix" is $C = L$ and the constraint matrices $A_i$ are diagonal matrices $(A_i)_{kk} = \delta_{ik}$. --- ## 3.2 Goemans–Williamson Rounding — Detailed Analysis The SDP gives us a PSD matrix $X^*$. The Goemans–Williamson (GW) algorithm rounds this continuous solution back to a discrete cut as follows: ### Algorithm Steps 1. **Solve the SDP** to obtain the optimal Gram matrix $X^* \succeq 0$ with $X^*_{ii} = 1$. 2. **Cholesky Decomposition:** Compute $V = \text{chol}(X^*)$ such that $X^* = V^T V$. This yields $n$ unit vectors $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$ (rows of $V$, each of unit norm) with $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = X^*_{ij}$. 3. **Random Hyperplane Rounding:** Pick a uniformly random unit vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (i.e., a random direction on the unit sphere). For each vertex $i$, set: $$v_i^{\text{disc}} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } \langle v_i, r \rangle \ge 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } \langle v_i, r \rangle < 0 \end{cases}$$ 4. **Output the cut** defined by the partition $\{i : v_i^{\text{disc}} = +1\}$ and $\{i : v_i^{\text{disc}} = -1\}$. --- ### Step 5: Probability of Cutting an Edge Suppose the angle between vectors $v_i$ and $v_j$ is $\theta_{ij} \in [0, \pi]$. Then: $$\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = \cos \theta_{ij}$$ The random hyperplane $r$ separates $v_i$ and $v_j$ if and only if $r$ lies **between them** on the unit sphere, which happens with probability: $$\Pr[\text{hyperplane separates } v_i \text{ and } v_j] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$$ Derivation: A random unit vector $r$ is uniform on the $n$-dimensional unit sphere. The probability that the great circle perpendicular to $r$ separates two vectors at angle $\theta$ is the arc-length of the separating region divided by the total arc-length. The arc swept out by the separating hyperplane between $v_i$ and $v_j$ has measure $\theta_{ij}$, out of a full great circle of measure $2\pi$, but only one side counts: probability $= \theta_{ij} / \pi$. --- ### Step 6: Expected Size of the Cut The expected number of cut edges is: $$E[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$$ The SDP objective value is: $$\text{SDP-OPT} = \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - \cos \theta_{ij}}{2}$$ To compare these, we need the **per-edge ratio**: $$\frac{E[\text{cut-edge contribution}]}{\text{SDP-edge contribution}} = \frac{\theta_{ij} / \pi}{(1 - \cos \theta_{ij})/2}$$ Define the function: $$f(\theta) = \frac{2\theta}{\pi (1 - \cos \theta)}, \quad \theta \in (0, \pi]$$ The worst-case (minimum) of this ratio is the **GW constant**: $$\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in (0, \pi]} f(\theta)$$ Computing $f'(\theta) = 0$: $$f'(\theta) = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{(1 - \cos \theta) - \theta \sin \theta}{(1 - \cos \theta)^2} = 0$$ $$\implies 1 - \cos \theta = \theta \sin \theta$$ Numerically solving this transcendental equation (e.g., via Newton's method) gives the optimal $\theta^* \approx 2.3311$ radians, and: $$\alpha_{GW} = f(\theta^*) \approx \mathbf{0.87856}$$ --- ### Step 7: Putting It All Together — The Approximation Bound Let $\text{OPT}$ be the true integer optimum of Max-Cut, $\text{SDP-OPT}$ the SDP optimum, and $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}]$ the expected GW cut size. **Three key inequalities:** 1. $\text{OPT} \le \text{SDP-OPT}$ (relaxation upper bound). 2. $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \dfrac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$. 3. For every $\theta \in (0, \pi]$: $\dfrac{\theta/\pi}{(1-\cos\theta)/2} \ge \alpha_{GW}$. Therefore: $$\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} \ge \alpha_{GW} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{1 - \cos \theta_{ij}}{2} = \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{SDP-OPT} \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Goemans–Williamson Theorem (1995)** > > The GW algorithm achieves an expected approximation ratio of $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$ for Max-Cut. Equivalently: > > $$\mathbb{E}[\text{GW-Cut}] \ge 0.87856 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{Max-Cut}}$$ > > This is the *first* use of SDP in approximation algorithms and remains the **best known polynomial-time approximation ratio** for Max-Cut. The hardness result (Håstad 2001) shows that no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve ratio better than $\frac{16}{17} \approx 0.9412$ under the Unique Games Conjecture — and this is provably tight. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: A Small Triangle (3 Vertices) Consider the triangle $K_3$ (3 vertices, 3 edges). The maximum cut is **2** (you can never cut all 3 edges simultaneously, since that would require a 2-coloring of an odd cycle). - **Integer OPT** = 2. - **SDP value:** With vectors at 120° apart (a planar configuration), $\cos \theta = -1/2$, so each edge contributes $(1 - (-1/2))/2 = 3/4$. Total SDP-OPT $= 3 \times 3/4 = 9/4 = 2.25$. - **GW expected cut:** $\theta / \pi = (2\pi/3)/\pi = 2/3$ per edge. Total $= 3 \times 2/3 = 2$. - **Ratio** = $2 / 2.25 = 8/9 \approx 0.889 > 0.878$. ✓ This satisfies the GW bound on a concrete graph. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Workflow: From Combinatorial Problem to SDP-Based Approximation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Hard Combinatorial Problem
(e.g., Max-Cut with -1,+1 variables)"] --> B["Vector Reformulation
Replace v_i with unit vector v_i"] B --> C["Introduce Gram Matrix
X_ij = inner product of v_i, v_j"] C --> D["PSD Relaxation
Add constraint X is PSD
Now convex and polynomial-time solvable"] D --> E["Solve SDP via Interior-Point Methods
Obtain optimal matrix X-star"] E --> F["Cholesky / Eigendecomposition
Recover vectors v_1 to v_n"] F --> G["Rounding Scheme
(e.g., Goemans-Williamson hyperplane)"] G --> H["Discrete Feasible Solution
(Integer cut / partition)"] H --> I["Approximation Guarantee
Compare with OPT and SDP-OPT"] I --> J["Output: Approximate Cut"] style A fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#a83232,color:#000 style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a87a00,color:#000 style E fill:#d6eaff,stroke:#1a4f8b,color:#000 style G fill:#d8f5d0,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style I fill:#f0d8ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 style J fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SDP Solver Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] P1["Problem Data
Graph G = V,E"] P2["Constraint Matrices
A_i and vectors b_i"] P3["Cost Matrix
C = Laplacian L"] end subgraph FORM["FORMULATION LAYER"] F1["Construct Standard SDP
min C.X s.t. A_i.X = b_i
X PSD"] end subgraph SOLVE["SOLVER LAYER"] S1["Interior-Point Method
Logarithmic Barrier Function"] S2["Primal-Dual Path Following
Newton Steps"] S3["Stop at Epsilon-Optimal X-star"] end subgraph POST["POST-PROCESSING LAYER"] R1["Cholesky: X-star = V trans V"] R2["Random Hyperplane Rounding"] R3["Output Integer Cut"] end subgraph GUARANTEE["GUARANTEE LAYER"] G1["Compute alpha-GW Ratio"] G2["Bound E cut >= alpha x OPT"] end P1 --> FORM P2 --> FORM P3 --> FORM FORM --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 R3 --> G1 --> G2 style FORM fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#a87a00,color:#000 style SOLVE fill:#d6eaff,stroke:#1a4f8b,color:#000 style POST fill:#d8f5d0,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style GUARANTEE fill:#f0d8ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mapping: Max-Cut to SDP (Decision-Flow View) ```mermaid flowchart TD M1["Max-Cut Integer Form
maximize sum 1-v_i v_j over 2
v_i in -1, +1"] --> M2{"Relax variables to unit vectors
v_i in R^n, ||v_i|| = 1"} M2 --> M3["Replace v_i v_j with
inner product of v_i and v_j"] M3 --> M4["Form Gram Matrix X
X_ij = inner product"] M4 --> M5["X is PSD because
X = V trans V"] M5 --> M6["Diagonal Constraint
X_ii = 1 for all i"] M6 --> M7["Final SDP Form
minimize L.X
subject to X_ii = 1, X PSD"] M7 --> N1["Solve SDP"] N1 --> N2["Extract Vectors V from X-star"] N2 --> N3["Random Hyperplane r"] N3 --> N4["Assign sign of inner product of v_i and r"] N4 --> N5["Final Max-Cut Solution"] style M1 fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#a83232,color:#000 style M7 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a87a00,color:#000 style N5 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define Positive Semi-Definite (PSD) matrix. State and briefly explain the three equivalent characterizations of a PSD matrix.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A symmetric matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is called **Positive Semi-Definite** (denoted $X \succeq 0$) if any of the following equivalent conditions hold: 1. **Eigenvalue Form:** All eigenvalues of $X$ are non-negative: $\lambda_i(X) \ge 0$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$. 2. **Quadratic Form:** $v^T X v \ge 0$ for every non-zero vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^n$. 3. **Factorization Form:** $X = V^T V$ for some matrix $V \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$. This is the **Cholesky-type decomposition** and is the most useful form for SDP-based algorithms. The PSD property defines a proper convex cone (the PSD cone) in the space of symmetric matrices, which is the foundation of SDP feasibility. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]` **State the standard primal form of a Semi-Definite Program. Explain how LP is a special case of SDP.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **standard primal SDP** is: $$\min \; C \bullet X \quad \text{subject to} \quad A_i \bullet X = b_i \;(i = 1, \dots, m), \quad X \succeq 0$$ where $A_i, C$ are symmetric matrices and $A \bullet B = \text{trace}(A^T B)$. **LP as a special case:** If we restrict $X$ to be a **diagonal matrix**, $X = \text{diag}(x_1, \dots, x_n)$, then: - $C \bullet X = c^T x$ (linear cost). - $A_i \bullet X = a_i^T x$ (linear constraints). - $X \succeq 0 \iff x \ge 0$ (non-negativity). This reduces to the standard LP. Therefore, **LP $\subset$ SDP**, and SDPs are strictly more expressive because they can model problems with quadratic/correlation structure. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** State the Max-Cut problem in its integer $\{-1, +1\}$ form. **(3 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Semi-Definite Programming (SDP) relaxation of the Max-Cut problem. State the final SDP explicitly. **(7 marks)** **(c)** Describe the Goemans–Williamson rounding scheme and derive the approximation ratio $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.878$. **(4 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Integer Formulation (3 marks):** Given $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, assign $v_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ to each vertex $i$ (representing the two sides of the cut). For an edge $(i, j) \in E$: $$\text{cut}(i, j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } v_i \ne v_j \\ 0 & \text{if } v_i = v_j \end{cases} = \frac{1 - v_i v_j}{2}$$ Therefore, the Max-Cut integer program is: $$\text{MAX-CUT (IP):} \quad \max_{v_i \in \{-1, +1\}} \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - v_i v_j}{2} \quad \mathbf{(3 \text{ marks})}$$ --- **(b) Derivation of the SDP Relaxation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (Vector Relaxation — 2 marks):** Replace each scalar $v_i$ by a unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\Vert v_i \Vert^2 = 1$. Replace the product $v_i v_j$ by the inner product $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle$: $$\max \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - \langle v_i, v_j \rangle}{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert v_i \Vert^2 = 1$$ **Step 2 (Gram Matrix — 2 marks):** Define the Gram matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ by $X_{ij} = \langle v_i, v_j \rangle$. Then $X = V^T V$ where the rows of $V$ are the vectors $v_i$. By the factorization characterization, $X \succeq 0$. The unit-norm condition becomes $X_{ii} = 1$. **Step 3 (Final SDP — 3 marks):** $$\text{MAX-CUT (SDP):} \quad \max \sum_{(i, j) \in E} \frac{1 - X_{ij}}{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad X_{ii} = 1 \; \forall i, \quad X \succeq 0$$ Equivalently, in the standard minimization form using the Laplacian $L = D - A$: $$\min \; L \bullet X \quad \text{subject to} \quad X_{ii} = 1 \; \forall i, \quad X \succeq 0$$ This is a true SDP. $\mathbf{(7 \text{ marks})}$ --- **(c) Goemans–Williamson Rounding (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Solve the SDP to obtain $X^* \succeq 0$ with $X^*_{ii} = 1$. **Step 2:** Compute Cholesky factorization $V$ such that $X^* = V^T V$. The rows $v_1, \dots, v_n$ of $V$ are unit vectors. **Step 3:** Pick a uniformly random unit vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and assign each vertex $i$ to side $+1$ if $\langle v_i, r \rangle \ge 0$ and to side $-1$ otherwise. **Expected Cut Size Analysis:** If $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(\langle v_i, v_j \rangle) \in [0, \pi]$ is the angle between $v_i$ and $v_j$: $$\Pr[\text{edge } (i,j) \text{ is cut}] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$$ $$E[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{1 - \cos \theta_{ij}}{2} = \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{SDP-OPT}$$ where $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in (0, \pi]} \dfrac{2\theta}{\pi(1 - \cos \theta)} \approx 0.87856$. Since $\text{OPT} \le \text{SDP-OPT}$, we get $E[\text{cut}] \ge 0.87856 \cdot \text{OPT}$. $\mathbf{(4 \text{ marks})}$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** Define the standard form of a Semi-Definite Program. State and explain the dual of the SDP. Discuss the conditions under which strong duality holds. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how the Max-Cut SDP relaxation is derived from the integer Max-Cut problem.
