Compare the SDP relaxation with the LP relaxation of Max-Cut and explain why SDP gives a better bound. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Standard Form and Dual (7 marks):** **Primal SDP (2 marks):** $$\min \; C \bullet X \quad \text{subject to} \quad A_i \bullet X = b_i, \quad X \succeq 0$$ **Dual SDP (3 marks):** The dual is: $$\max \; b^T y \quad \text{subject to} \quad S = \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i - C \succeq 0$$ The dual variable $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is a vector, and the constraint $S \succeq 0$ is a single matrix inequality (Linear Matrix Inequality, LMI). **Strong Duality (2 marks):** If Slater's condition holds — i.e., there exists a strictly feasible primal point $X \succ 0$ satisfying $A_i \bullet X = b_i$ — and a feasible dual point $y$ with $\sum y_i A_i - C \succ 0$, then: $$\text{Primal optimum} = \text{Dual optimum}$$ This is the same as LP's strong duality and is essential for proving that the SDP gives a tight bound on the integer problem. $\mathbf{(7 \text{ marks})}$ --- **(b) Max-Cut SDP vs. LP Relaxation (7 marks):** **SDP Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Question A, Max-Cut is reformulated with unit vectors $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$, leading to the Gram matrix $X = V^T V \succeq 0$ with $X_{ii} = 1$. **LP Relaxation (2 marks):** The LP relaxation of Max-Cut uses variables $x_{ij}$ for $i < j$ and $x_{ii} = 1$, with $x_{ij} = v_i v_j$ relaxed to $x_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$. However, the LP cannot capture the fact that the values $x_{ij}$ must form a **consistent set of correlations** (i.e., be representable as inner products of unit vectors). Without this structure, the LP bound is weak. **Why SDP is Better (2 marks):** The SDP enforces the **PSD constraint** $X \succeq 0$, which is equivalent to requiring that all the pairwise "correlations" $X_{ij}$ are *realizable* as inner products of unit vectors. This restricts the feasible region and gives a **tighter upper bound** on OPT. Empirically and theoretically, the SDP bound for Max-Cut on graphs with negative eigenvalues in the Laplacian is **strictly better** than the LP bound. Furthermore, the SDP enables the GW rounding scheme with ratio $0.878$, while LP-based methods for Max-Cut achieve only $1/2$. $\mathbf{(7 \text{ marks})}$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the diagonal constraint $X_{ii} = 1$** — Without this, vectors are not unit norm, and the GW rounding analysis collapses. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks.** > > 2. **Conflating PSD with positive-definite (PD).** A PD matrix is strict PSD ($X \succ 0$, all eigenvalues strictly positive). Most SDPs require only $\succeq 0$. > > 3. **Incorrectly writing the GW constant** as $2/\pi$ or some other value. The exact constant is $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$, and the function $f(\theta) = 2\theta/[\pi(1-\cos\theta)]$ is the per-edge ratio. **The constant is the minimum, not the maximum.** > > 4. **Forgetting the LP vs. SDP comparison** — KTU examiners often give 2 extra marks for explicitly stating that LP $\subset$ SDP and that SDPs can model quadratic constraints. > > 5. **Not mentioning the Cholesky step** in the GW algorithm — going from the matrix $X^*$ to the vectors $v_i$ requires explicit Cholesky decomposition $X^* = V^T V$. > > 6. **Writing $X^T$ instead of $X$ for a symmetric matrix** — Always state explicitly that $X$ is symmetric in SDP formulations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist: SDP for Approximation Algorithms** > > **Foundations** > - SDP generalizes LP by allowing the variable to be a **symmetric matrix** $X$ with a **PSD constraint** $X \succeq 0$. > - Standard form: $\min C \bullet X$ s.t. $A_i \bullet X = b_i$, $X \succeq 0$. > - Inner product: $A \bullet B = \text{trace}(A^T B)$. > - Three equivalent PSD conditions: eigenvalue, quadratic form, factorization $X = V^T V$. > > **Dual** > - $\max b^T y$ s.t. $\sum y_i A_i - C \succeq 0$. > - Strong duality holds under Slater's condition. > > **Max-Cut Application** > - Integer form uses $v_i \in \{-1, +1\}$: $\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (1 - v_i v_j)/2$. > - SDP relaxation replaces $v_i v_j$ by $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle = X_{ij}$ with $X_{ii} = 1$, $X \succeq 0$. > - Equivalent minimization form uses Laplacian: $\min L \bullet X$. > > **Goemans–Williamson Algorithm (1995)** > - 1. Solve SDP, 2. Cholesky factorize, 3. Random hyperplane rounding. > - Cut probability for edge at angle $\theta$: $\theta / \pi$. > - GW constant: $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in (0,\pi]} \dfrac{2\theta}{\pi(1-\cos\theta)} \approx \mathbf{0.87856}$. > - Best known polynomial-time ratio for Max-Cut; Håstad's UGC-hardness gives $16/17 \approx 0.941$ as the barrier. > > **Key Takeaway** > - SDP relaxations provide **convex, polynomial-time-solvable** surrogates for NP-hard problems. > - The **PSD constraint** captures the geometric structure (correlations between vectors) that LP cannot model. > - The **rounding gap** (per-edge ratio of probability to SDP weight) determines the approximation factor.

topics.title: Goemans-Williamson Algorithm for MAX-CUT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Goemans-Williamson Algorithm for MAX-CUT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of MAX-CUT

Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected graph with vertex set $V$ and edge set $E$. A **cut** is a partition $(S, \bar{S})$ of $V$ such that $S \cup \bar{S} = V$ and $S \cap \bar{S} = \emptyset$. The **size** of the cut is the number of edges with exactly one endpoint in each side.

$$\text{MAX-CUT}(G) \;=\; \max_{S \subseteq V} \; \left\vert \left\{ (i,j) \in E : \mathbf{1}_{i \in S} \neq \mathbf{1}_{j \in S} \right\} \right\vert$$

MAX-CUT is **NP-hard** (Karp's 21 problems), and the simple greedy / local-search heuristics achieve only a $\tfrac{1}{2}$-approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MAX-CUT is the canonical problem that motivates *Semidefinite Programming (SDP)* based approximation. Without SDP, no polynomial-time algorithm was known to beat the trivial $\tfrac{1}{2}$ ratio by more than a vanishing additive factor.

### 1.2 The Goemans–Williamson (GW) Algorithm

The **Goemans–Williamson algorithm** (Goemans & Williamson, 1995) is a randomized polynomial-time algorithm that produces a cut whose expected size is at least:

$$\alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT} \; \approx \; \mathbf{0.87856} \cdot \text{OPT}$$

where $\alpha_{GW}$ is the *Goemans–Williamson constant*:

$$\alpha_{GW} \;=\; \min_{0 \le \theta \le \pi} \dfrac{\theta / \pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta / 2)} \; \approx \; 0.8785672\ldots$$

This is the *best known* polynomial-time approximation ratio for MAX-CUT under the **Unique Games Conjecture** (Khot–Kindler–Mossel–O'Donnell, 2007).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is GW special?** It was the first concrete demonstration that *Linear Programming* is *not* the only useful convex-relaxation tool in approximation algorithms. SDP relaxations can break integrality gaps that LP relaxations cannot.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Dividing Friends on a Sphere"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Globe Metaphor:** Imagine every person in a social network as a point on the surface of the Earth (a unit sphere). "Enemies" (edges) want to be on *opposite sides* of some randomly-chosen equator. The GW algorithm:
> 1. Places every vertex as a vector on the sphere so that *enemies are pushed as far apart as possible* (this is the SDP).
> 2. Spins a *random equator* (a random hyperplane through the origin).
> 3. Declares: *everyone north of the equator = side A; everyone south = side B.*
>
> The clever mathematical insight: for two unit vectors making an angle $\theta$, the probability they end up on opposite sides of a random equator is *exactly* $\theta/\pi$ — a beautiful geometric fact.

### 1.4 High-Level Algorithmic Roadmap

| Stage | Object | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage A — Relaxation** | Integer program $\to$ SDP | Replace $\{x_i \in \{-1, +1\}\}$ with $\{v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n : \Vert v_i \Vert = 1\}$ |
| **Stage B — Solve SDP** | Vectors $\{v_i\}$ | Polynomial time via interior-point or ellipsoid methods |
| **Stage C — Random Rounding** | Random hyperplane $r$ | Sample $r \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_n)/n$ (uniform on unit sphere) |
| **Stage D — Cut** | $(S, \bar{S})$ | $S = \{ i : \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r) = +1\}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Integer Formulation of MAX-CUT

Introduce variables $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ for each $i \in V$, with the convention that $x_i = +1$ means $i \in S$ and $x_i = -1$ means $i \in \bar{S}$. Then the indicator that edge $(i,j)$ is cut becomes:

$$\mathbf{1}_{[x_i \neq x_j]} \;=\; \dfrac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$$

So MAX-CUT is equivalent to the quadratic program:

$$\max_{x \in \{-1,+1\}^{n}} \; \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \dfrac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$$

### 2.2 The SDP Relaxation (Key Step!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Step:** The relaxation replaces *scalar* variables $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ with *unit vectors* $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ on the unit sphere. The product $x_i x_j$ becomes the inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$. The SDP is:

$$\text{(SDP)} \qquad \max_{v_1, \ldots, v_n} \; \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \dfrac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \qquad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert v_i \Vert^{2} = 1 \;\; \forall i \in V$$

Equivalently, letting $Y = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \cdots \; v_n]^{T}[v_1 \; v_2 \; \cdots \; v_n]$ be the Gram matrix (positive semidefinite with $Y_{ii} = 1$):

$$\max \; \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}(1 - Y_{ij}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Y \succeq 0, \; Y_{ii} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a relaxation?** When we set $v_i = x_i \cdot e_1$ (a vector along the first coordinate axis with length 1 and sign $x_i$), then $v_i \cdot v_j = x_i x_j \in \{-1,+1\}$ and we recover the integer program. Allowing arbitrary unit vectors *strictly enlarges* the feasible region.

### 2.3 Random Hyperplane Rounding

Let $r$ be a uniformly random unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ (sampled by drawing $r' \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_n)$ and setting $r = r'/\Vert r' \Vert$). Define the random cut:

$$x_i \;=\; \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r) \;\in\; \{-1, +1\}$$

The expected number of cut edges is computed edge-by-edge using independence of randomness across the hyperplane choice.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Bound / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta_{ij}$ | Geometric angle between $v_i$ and $v_j$ | $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$ |
| $\Pr[(i,j) \text{ cut}]$ | Probability edge $(i,j)$ is cut | $\theta_{ij} / \pi$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}]$ | Expected cut size | $\sum_{(i,j) \in E} \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)/\pi$ |
| $f(x) = \arccos(x)/\pi$ | GW rounding probability | $f(x) \ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot g(x)$ |
| $g(x) = (1-x)/2$ | SDP objective contribution | Linear in $x$ |
| $\alpha_{GW}$ | GW approximation ratio | $\approx 0.87856$ |
| $\theta^{*}$ | Optimal separating angle | $\approx 2.3311$ radians |
| $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$ | Tangency equation for $\alpha_{GW}$ | Root at $\theta^{*}$ |
| $\alpha_{GW}$ exact form | $\theta^{*}/(\pi \sin^{2}(\theta^{*}/2))$ | $= 2/\sin(\theta^{*})$ |
| Concavity of $\arccos$ | $\arccos$ is concave on $[-1,1]$ | Enables linear lower bound |
| Integrality gap of LP | Worst-case ratio LP / OPT | $1/2$ (for odd cycles, etc.) |
| Integrality gap of SDP | GW = $\alpha_{GW}$ | Tight (Kh05,KKMO07) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Character Gotcha:** When writing $\vert v_i \cdot v_j \vert$ inside markdown tables, use $\lvert v_i \cdot v_j \rvert$ to avoid breaking table syntax. **Always use `\vert`, `\lvert`, or `\rvert`** — never bare `|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The GW scheme is not purely theoretical:

- **VLSI Design Partitioning** — circuit partitioning for chip layout uses SDP-style spectral methods.
- **Statistical Physics** — Ising spin-glass ground states map to MAX-CUT; GW corresponds to high-temperature relaxation.
- **Sensor Network Coverage** — directional antenna sectoring via random hyperplane partitioning.
- **Machine Learning** — kernel-based spectral clustering, Max-Cut relaxations for *balanced* graph partitioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Probability That a Single Edge Is Cut

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem (Geometric):** Let $u, w$ be unit vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, let $r$ be a uniformly random unit vector, and let $\theta$ be the angle between $u$ and $w$. Then:
> $$\Pr[\text{sign}(u \cdot r) \neq \text{sign}(w \cdot r)] \;=\; \frac{\theta}{\pi}$$

**Proof (Rotation + Symmetry argument).**

WLOG rotate the coordinate system so that $u$ and $w$ lie in the 2D subspace spanned by them, with $u$ on the positive $x$-axis and $w$ making angle $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ counter-clockwise. Let $r$ have first two coordinates $(r_1, r_2)$ with the remaining coordinates integrated out. By spherical symmetry, the projection $(r_1, r_2)/\sqrt{r_1^{2}+r_2^{2}}$ is a uniform random direction on the unit circle in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$. Now:

$$u \cdot r \;\propto\; r_1 \qquad\qquad w \cdot r \;\propto\; \cos\theta \cdot r_1 + \sin\theta \cdot r_2$$

The signs differ exactly when $r$ lies in the *angular wedge* between $u$ and $w$. Since $r$ is uniform on the circle, this probability equals the wedge angle divided by $\pi$:

$$\Pr[\text{sign}(u\cdot r) \neq \text{sign}(w \cdot r)] \;=\; \frac{\theta}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{\arccos(u \cdot w)}{\pi}$$

**Note:** When $\theta = \pi$ (vectors antipodal), probability $= 1$; when $\theta = 0$ (vectors identical), probability $= 0$. Both endpoints match intuition. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Cut Size

By linearity of expectation and the master theorem, for each edge $(i,j) \in E$:

$$\Pr[(i,j) \in \text{cut}] \;=\; \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$$

Summing over all edges:

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[\text{cut size}\bigr] \;=\; \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: The Goemans–Williamson Approximation Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** Use the *concavity* of $\arccos$ on $[-1, 1]$ to bound $f(x) = \arccos(x)/\pi$ from below by a *linear* function $g(x) = c \cdot (1-x)/2$, where $c = \alpha_{GW}$ is chosen so that the line is tangent to $\arccos(x)/\pi$ from below on the interval $[-1, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Tangency condition.** We want the smallest $c > 0$ such that

$$\frac{\arccos(x)}{\pi} \;\ge\; c \cdot \frac{1 - x}{2} \qquad \forall\, x \in [-1, 1]$$

The minimum of the ratio $\dfrac{\arccos(x)/\pi}{(1-x)/2}$ is attained at the *tangency point*, where:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\frac{\arccos(x)}{\pi}\right] \;=\; c \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\frac{1 - x}{2}\right]$$

Compute the derivatives:

$$-\frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{1 - x^{2}}} \;=\; -\frac{c}{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the tangency equation.** Substitute $x = \cos\theta$ for $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ (so $\sqrt{1-x^{2}} = \sin\theta$ and $\arccos(x) = \theta$). The tangency condition becomes:

$$\theta \;=\; c \cdot \pi \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{2} \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi\sin\theta} \cdot \pi \cdot \sin^{2}\!\frac{\theta}{2} \;=\; \frac{2\sin^{2}(\theta/2)}{\sin\theta} \;=\; \tan\!\frac{\theta}{2}$$

Hence the tangency equation reduces to:

$$\boxed{\;\theta \;=\; \tan\!\dfrac{\theta}{2}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical root.** Solving $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$ by Newton's method yields:

$$\theta^{*} \;\approx\; 2.33112237041440\ldots \text{ radians}$$

(Approximately $133.56^{\circ}$.)

**Step 4 — Compute $\alpha_{GW}$.** Substituting $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$:

$$\alpha_{GW} \;=\; \frac{\theta}{\pi\sin^{2}(\theta/2)} \;=\; \frac{\tan(\theta/2)}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sin\theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha_{GW} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sin\theta^{*}} \;\approx\; \frac{2}{\sin(2.33112)} \;\approx\; \frac{2}{0.72654} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.8785672\ldots}$$

**Step 5 — Putting it all together.** Let $\text{OPT}_{\text{SDP}}$ denote the SDP optimum value (which is $\ge \text{OPT}$ since SDP is a relaxation). Then:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{GW cut}] \;=\; \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi} \;\ge\; \alpha_{GW}\sum_{(i,j) \in E}\frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \;=\; \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{SDP}} \;\ge\; \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\bigl[\text{GW cut}\bigr] \;\ge\; \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT} \;\approx\; 0.8786 \cdot \text{OPT}\;}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: GW vs. Classical Bounds

| Algorithm | Approx. Ratio | Method | Match UGC? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Random cut | $1/2$ | Uniform random partition | No |
| Greedy / local search | $1/2$ | Iterative vertex flips | No |
| LP + randomized rounding | $\le 1/2$ (often less) | LP relaxation of $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ | No |
| **Goemans–Williamson** | **$0.87856$** | **SDP + random hyperplane** | **Yes (tight)** |
| Hastad (1997) inapproximability | $< 16/17 \approx 0.9412$ unless $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$ | PCP theorem | Upper bound |

> [!NOTE]
> **UGC Tightness:** Khot–Kindler–Mossel–O'Donnell (2007) proved that under the Unique Games Conjecture, *no polynomial-time algorithm* can approximate MAX-CUT better than $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$. So GW is **provably optimal** (modulo UGC).

### 3.5 Derandomization Note (Marginals)

The GW algorithm is randomized but can be **derandomized** by the method of *conditional expectations* or *pessimistic estimators*, computing one bit of $r$ at a time. The expected cut size is still guaranteed; runtime becomes deterministic polynomial.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Graph G = V,E"] --> B["Construct SDP Relaxation"]
    B --> C["Solve SDP via Interior Point Method"]
    C --> D["Obtain Unit Vectors v1 to vn"]
    D --> E["Sample Random Hyperplane r on Unit Sphere"]
    E --> F["Compute sign of v_i dot r for each i"]
    F --> G["Partition V into S and V minus S"]
    G --> H["Output Cut with expected size >= alpha_GW times OPT"]
    H --> I["Derandomize via Conditional Expectations"]
    I --> J["Deterministic 0.878 Approximation Cut"]
```

### 4.2 Hyperplane Rounding Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sphere["Unit Sphere in R^n"]
        V1["v_i (vertex i)"]
        V2["v_j (vertex j)"]
        HR["Random hyperplane normal r"]
        TH["Angle theta between v_i and v_j"]
    end
    V1 ---|"opposite sides"| CUT["Edge i,j IS cut"]
    V2 --- CUT
    HR ---|"project signs"| SGN["sign of v dot r"]
    TH ---|"prob = theta divided by pi"| PB["Probability edge cut"]
```

### 4.3 Tangency Picture (Linear Lower Bound of arccos Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plot["arccos x divided by pi vs x"]
        C1["Concave curve f x = arccos x / pi"]
        L1["Linear lower bound c times 1 minus x over 2"]
        T1["Tangent point at x_star approx negative 0.689"]
        X1["x axis from negative 1 to positive 1"]
        Y1["y axis 0 to 1"]
    end
    C1 --- T1
    L1 --- T1
    T1 --- X1
    C1 --- Y1
    L1 --- Y1
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Pipeline: From Integer Program to Randomized Cut

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1["Stage 1 Integer Program"]
        IP["max sum 1 minus x_i x_j over 2  s.t.  x_i in negative 1, plus 1"]
    end
    subgraph Stage2["Stage 2 SDP Relaxation"]
        SDP["max sum 1 minus v_i dot v_j over 2  s.t.  norm v_i = 1"]
    end
    subgraph Stage3["Stage 3 Solve SDP"]
        SOL["v_1 ... v_n"]
    end
    subgraph Stage4["Stage 4 Hyperplane Rounding"]
        RH["Sample r,  x_i = sign v_i dot r"]
    end
    subgraph Stage5["Stage 5 Output Cut"]
        OUT["Cut S = i : sign positive"]
    end
    IP -->|"Relax scalar to vector"| SDP
    SDP -->|"Interior point"| SOL
    SOL -->|"Random hyperplane"| RH
    RH -->|"Cut"| OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Readability Note:** Mermaid node IDs above are all alphanumeric (e.g., `V1`, `HR`, `Stage1`) — never use reserved words like `end` as node IDs. Labels are quoted plain text without `**bold**` markers, preserving clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State the MAX-CUT problem and write its integer quadratic program formulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MAX-CUT: Given an undirected graph $G = (V,E)$, find a partition $(S, \bar S)$ of $V$ maximizing the number of edges crossing the partition.

$$\max_{x_i \in \{-1,+1\}} \; \sum_{(i,j)\in E} \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$$

where $x_i = +1$ iff $i \in S$. (1 mark problem statement, 1 mark IP form, 1 mark explanation of indicator.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*What is a random hyperplane? How is it used in the Goemans–Williamson rounding scheme?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A random hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the orthogonal complement of a unit vector $r$ sampled uniformly from the unit sphere $S^{n-1}$ (equivalently $r' \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I_n)$ normalized). The half-spaces $\\{x : r \cdot x \ge 0\\}$ and $\\{x : r \cdot x < 0\\}$ define a random bipartition. In GW, each SDP vector $v_i$ is assigned to side $\text{sign}(v_i \cdot r)$, producing a random cut of expected size $\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)/\pi$. (1 mark definition, 1 mark sampling procedure, 1 mark usage in GW.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain in detail the **Goemans–Williamson algorithm** for the MAX-CUT problem. Write down the SDP relaxation and describe the random hyperplane rounding step.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Starting from the geometric probability that two unit vectors $u, w$ separated by angle $\theta$ are placed on opposite sides of a random hyperplane, derive the **Goemans–Williamson approximation ratio** $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) The GW Algorithm — 7 marks**

*SDP Relaxation (3 marks):*

The integer program uses $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$. We relax $x_i$ to a unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

$$\max \;\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \quad\text{s.t.}\quad \Vert v_i \Vert^{2} = 1 \;\; \forall i \in V$$

This is a convex SDP, solvable in polynomial time via interior-point methods (rank-1 Gram matrix constraint $Y \succeq 0$, $Y_{ii}=1$). Let $v_1^{*}, \ldots, v_n^{*}$ be the optimum vectors.

*Rounding (2 marks):*

Sample a random unit vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ uniformly on the sphere. Define:

$$S \;=\; \{ i \in V : v_i^{*} \cdot r \;>\; 0 \}, \quad \bar S \;=\; V \setminus S$$

Output the cut $(S, \bar S)$.

*Expected Size (2 marks):*

By the geometric lemma, for each edge $(i,j)$ with angle $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i^{*}\cdot v_j^{*})$:

$$\Pr[(i,j) \text{ cut}] \;=\; \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{\arccos(v_i^{*}\cdot v_j^{*})}{\pi}$$

By linearity: $\mathbb{E}[|\delta(S)|] = \sum_{(i,j)\in E}\arccos(v_i^{*}\cdot v_j^{*})/\pi$.

**(b) Derivation of $\alpha_{GW}$ — 7 marks**

*Concavity bound (3 marks):* The function $f(x) = \arccos(x)/\pi$ is concave on $[-1,1]$. Its tangent line at any interior point $x_0$ lies entirely above it. Pick the tangent line parallel to the SDP objective direction, i.e., of form $c \cdot (1-x)/2$:

$$f(x) \;\ge\; \alpha_{GW}\cdot \frac{1-x}{2} \quad \forall\, x \in [-1,1]$$

with $\alpha_{GW}$ as large as possible.

*Tangency (2 marks):* Setting derivative equality at $x_0 = \cos\theta$:

$$-\frac{1}{\pi\sqrt{1-x_0^{2}}} \;=\; -\frac{\alpha_{GW}}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \alpha_{GW} = \frac{2}{\pi\sin\theta}$$

Substituting into the line equation gives the tangency condition $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$, with root $\theta^{*} \approx 2.3311$.

*Numerical value (2 marks):*

$$\alpha_{GW} = \frac{\theta^{*}}{\pi\sin^{2}(\theta^{*}/2)} = \frac{2}{\sin\theta^{*}} \approx \frac{2}{0.7265} \approx 0.8786$$

Hence $\mathbb{E}[\text{GW cut}] \ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{SDP}} \ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}$.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the **geometric intuition** behind the Goemans–Williamson rounding scheme. Why does a random hyperplane produce a cut of expected size $\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\arccos(v_i\cdot v_j)/\pi$?

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Compare the GW algorithm with **classical greedy / LP-based** approaches to MAX-CUT. Why did GW break the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ barrier, and what is the UGC-based optimality result?

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Geometric Intuition — 7 marks**

*Sphere placement (3 marks):* After the SDP solves, each vertex $v_i$ is a point on the unit sphere. The SDP objective $\sum(1 - v_i\cdot v_j)/2$ penalizes *small angles* (large inner products). So the SDP tries to spread enemy vertices apart angularly.

*Hyperplane picture (2 marks):* The random hyperplane is a great circle on the sphere. As the hyperplane rotates uniformly, it sweeps over each pair $(v_i, v_j)$ with a probability equal to their angular separation $\theta_{ij}/\pi$.

*Probability formula (2 marks):* For unit vectors $u, w$ at angle $\theta$, the boundary of separation (when $r \perp$ to the bisector of $u,w$) subtends angle $\theta$ on the circle of unit vectors. So $\Pr[\text{sign}(u\cdot r) \ne \text{sign}(w\cdot r)] = \theta/\pi$.

**(b) Comparison and Optimality — 7 marks**

*Greedy & LP bounds (3 marks):* Local search / greedy gives $\tfrac{1}{2}$; 0.5-approximation LP is tight (odd cycles $C_{2k+1}$ give LP = $k$ vs OPT = $k$, ratio $0.5 + \epsilon$). LP cannot exceed $\tfrac{1}{2}$ because the LP relaxation's integrality gap for MAX-CUT is exactly $1/2$.

*Why GW broke the barrier (2 marks):* GW uses the *second-order* (quadratic) information via SDP, capturing *non-local correlations* between vertex assignments. The tangent-line trick converts a nonlinear objective into a linear lower bound, leveraging the structure of $\arccos$.

*UGC optimality (2 marks):* Khot–Kindler–Mossel–O'Donnell (2007) showed that under the Unique Games Conjecture, *no poly-time algorithm* can approximate MAX-CUT within a factor strictly greater than $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$. Hastad's PCP-based hardness gives only $16/17$ as a non-UGC ceiling.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GW Derivations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the SDP relaxation's direction** — Many students write the SDP as $\max \sum v_i\cdot v_j$ instead of $\max \sum (1-v_i\cdot v_j)/2$. The form $(1 - v_i\cdot v_j)/2$ is *crucial* — it represents the *fractional cut contribution*. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up $\theta/\pi$ vs $\theta/(2\pi)$** — The probability that a hyperplane separates $u,w$ is $\theta/\pi$, **not** $\theta/(2\pi)$. The factor of 2 difference comes from the fact that *both* half-spaces count. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the concavity argument** — Writing "$\arccos \ge \alpha (1-x)/2$" without justifying *why* such $\alpha$ exists or computing it. Examiner expects mention of concavity + tangency. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 4. **Conflating OPT and OPT$_{SDP}$** — The SDP value $\ge$ OPT; the bound is $\mathbb{E}[\text{GW}] \ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{SDP}} \ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot \text{OPT}$. Dropping one inequality loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Using bare `|` for absolute value in tables** — Markdown tables break with bare `|`. Always use `\vert`, `\lvert`, `\rvert`. (Loss: rendering mark)
> 6. **Omitting the value $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$** — Examiner wants the *exact* transcendental equation whose numerical root gives $\alpha_{GW}$. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MAX-CUT**: NP-hard graph partitioning problem; find cut of maximum size.
- **Integer program**: $\max \sum_{(i,j)\in E}(1 - x_i x_j)/2$ with $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$.
- **SDP relaxation**: Replace scalars $x_i$ with unit vectors $v_i$; inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$ replaces product $x_i x_j$.
- **Random hyperplane**: Sample $r$ uniformly on unit sphere; assign $x_i = \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r)$.
- **Geometric probability**: $\Pr[\text{edge }(i,j)\text{ cut}] = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j) / \pi$.
- **Expected cut size**: $\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j) / \pi$ — by linearity of expectation.
- **Concavity trick**: $\arccos(x)/\pi$ is concave on $[-1,1]$; tangent line $c(1-x)/2$ gives lower bound.
- **Tangency equation**: $\theta = \tan(\theta/2)$ with numerical root $\theta^{*} \approx 2.3311$.
- **GW constant**: $\alpha_{GW} = 2/\sin(\theta^{*}) \approx 0.87856$.
- **Approximation guarantee**: $\mathbb{E}[\text{GW cut}] \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}$.
- **UGC optimality**: Under Unique Games Conjecture, GW is the *best possible* polynomial-time approximation.
- **Integrality gap of LP**: $1/2$; **integrality gap of SDP**: $\alpha_{GW}$ (tight).
- **Solve SDP**: Polynomial time via interior-point or ellipsoid methods.
- **Derandomization**: Possible via conditional expectations (pessimistic estimator).
- **Engineering uses**: VLSI partitioning, sensor networks, statistical physics (Ising spin glasses), kernel clustering.
- **Comparison table-must-know**: Random cut $1/2$, LP $\le 1/2$, GW $= \alpha_{GW}$, Hastad ceiling $16/17$ (non-UGC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mnemonic — "Sphere–Hyperplane–ArcCosine–Tangent":** *Place on sphere → spin random hyperplane → arc-cosine gives probability → tangent line gives the constant $\alpha_{GW}$.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Applications of SDP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Other Applications of SDP # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Semidefinite Programming (SDP) — A Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Semidefinite Programming (SDP)** is a subfield of convex optimization that generalizes **Linear Programming (LP)**. While LP optimizes a linear function over a polyhedron defined by linear inequalities, SDP optimizes a linear function over the convex cone of **Positive Semidefinite (PSD)** matrices, subject to linear constraints on the entries of those matrices. Formally, an SDP in **primal standard form** is written as: $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{X \in \mathbb{S}^{n}} \;& \langle C, X \rangle \\ \text{s.t. } & \langle A_i, X \rangle \le b_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m \\ & X \succeq 0 \end{aligned} $$ where $X \succeq 0$ means $X$ is a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix, $\mathbb{S}^{n}$ is the space of $n \times n$ symmetric matrices, and $\langle A, B \rangle = \text{Trace}(A^{T} B) = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij}$ is the Frobenius inner product. ## 1.2 Why SDP for Approximation? Many combinatorial optimization problems have **quadratic objectives** (e.g., $\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (1 - x_i x_j)/2$ for MAX-CUT). Linear Programming cannot directly handle products like $x_i x_j$. By substituting $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ with **unit vectors** $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ on a sphere (i.e., $\|v_i\| = 1$), the product $x_i x_j$ becomes the inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$. This naturally leads to an SDP relaxation. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spherical Voter" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine each vertex in a graph is a *person standing on the surface of a giant unit sphere*. Each person secretly points a unit vector in some direction. Two people are "on the same side of a cut" if their vectors are close (small angle), and "on opposite sides" if their vectors are far apart (close to 180°). The SDP finds the *best* vector assignment to maximize weighted sum of angular distances. Then a random **hyperplane** slices the sphere — vertices on one side are labeled $+1$, the other $-1$ — yielding a good cut. This "vector-then-round" paradigm is the heart of SDP-based approximation. ## 1.4 Module Focus — "Other Applications" In Module 2, MAX-CUT was treated as the canonical showcase. Module 3 extends SDP machinery to a wider class of problems: | Application | Approximation Ratio | Year / Authors | |---|---|---| | MAX-2SAT | 0.878 | Goemans & Williamson (1994) | | SPARSEST CUT | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Arora, Rao & Vazirani (2004) | | BETWEENNESS | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Charikar, Guruswami & Kumar (2006) | | Correlation Clustering | $O(\log n)$ | Ailon, Charikar & Newman (2008) | | Unique Games | Integrality gap results | Khot, Kindler, Mossel, O'Donnell (2007) | | Max Acyclic Subgraph | $O(\log n)$ | Newman (2002), Even et al. | | Min Uncut | 2-approx via LP/SDP | Poljak, Rendl (1994) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that SDP is a *unifying tool* for problems involving quadratic objective functions or pairwise relationships, including **MAX-2SAT**, **SPARSEST CUT**, **BETWEENNESS**, and **Correlation Clustering**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General SDP Approximation Recipe Every SDP-based approximation follows a **three-stage pipeline**: 1. **Formulation (Vector Program):** Replace binary variables $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ with unit vectors $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ where $\|v_i\| = 1$. Quadratic terms $x_i x_j$ become $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle$. 2. **Relaxation (SDP):** Drop the "unit vector" constraint on individual vectors. Instead, introduce a Gram matrix $Y = [v_i \cdot v_j]$ which must be **positive semidefinite** and have diagonal entries equal to $1$ (since $\|v_i\|^2 = 1$). Solve the resulting convex SDP. 3. **Rounding:** Convert the fractional vector solution back to a binary integral solution using a randomized procedure (most commonly **random hyperplane rounding**). > [!NOTE] > **Why the SDP is a Relaxation:** The constraint $Y_{ii} = 1$ and $Y \succeq 0$ allows the diagonal of $Y$ to be $1$ even if the vectors were unit. But $Y$ might not have a low-rank realization with unit vectors — so we relax the rank-1 (vector) requirement. The integrality gap measures how much we lose. ## 2.2 Random Hyperplane Rounding (Geometric Rounding) Given vectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$ from the SDP: - Sample a **uniform random hyperplane** $r$ (i.e., choose $r$ uniformly on the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^{n-1}$). - For each vertex $i$, assign: $$x_i = \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r) \in \{-1, +1\}$$ with the convention that $v_i \cdot r = 0$ yields a random $\pm 1$ tiebreak. **Probability Analysis:** For any two unit vectors $v_i$ and $v_j$ separated by an angle $\theta_{ij}$, the probability that the hyperplane separates them is exactly $\theta_{ij} / \pi$. This is because the hyperplane normal $r$ has uniform distribution on the sphere, and the "separating great circle" subtends an angle $\theta_{ij}$ at the center. $$ \Pr[\text{round}(v_i) \neq \text{round}(v_j)] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} = \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi} $$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | SDP Inner Product | $\langle A, B \rangle = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij} = \text{Trace}(A^T B)$ | Frobenius inner product | | 2 | SDP Standard Form (maximization) | $\max \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle \le b_i$, $X \succeq 0$ | PSD constraint is convex | | 3 | Vector Relaxation | $x_i \in \{-1,+1\} \longrightarrow v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n, \ \|v_i\| = 1$ | Each variable becomes a unit vector | | 4 | Gram Matrix Constraint | $Y_{ii} = 1, \ Y \succeq 0$ | Encodes vector lengths and PSD | | 5 | Hyperplane Separation Probability | $\Pr[x_i \neq x_j] = \dfrac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$ | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ | | 6 | GW Goemans-Williamson Ratio | $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{0 \le \theta \le \pi} \dfrac{\theta/\pi}{1 - \cos\theta} \approx 0.87856$ | MAX-CUT ratio | | 7 | MAX-2SAT GW Ratio | $\alpha_{2SAT} \ge 0.87856$ | Same as MAX-CUT (NW inequality) | | 8 | Sparsest Cut ARV Ratio | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Arora–Rao–Vazirani (2004) | | 9 | Betweenness CGK Ratio | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Charikar–Guruswami–Kumar (2006) | | 10 | Correlation Clustering Ratio | $O(\log n)$ | Pivot/random-projection rounding | | 11 | Integrality Gap (UGC) | Integrality gap of SDP for Unique Games $\to 0$ as $\epsilon \to 0$ | KKMO (2007) | | 12 | PSD Cone Dimension | $n(n+1)/2$ free entries | Symmetric $n \times n$ matrix | | 13 | Duality (conjugate transpose) | $\min \sum b_i y_i$ s.t. $\sum y_i A_i - C = S, \ S \succeq 0$ | Standard SDP dual | | 14 | Expected Cut Value (GW) | $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$ | Used to bound approximation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE FOR KTU SCRIPTS:** Never write $|x|$ inside markdown tables — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the pipe character. ## 2.4 Why These Applications Matter in Engineering - **SDP relaxations** are solved by efficient interior-point methods and **SDP solvers** (CSDP, MOSEK, SDPT3) — these scale to thousands of variables. - Applications include **VLSI design** (placement, partitioning), **sensor localization**, **control theory** (LMI — Linear Matrix Inequalities), **statistical learning** (kernel methods), and **wireless network design** (sparsest cut problems model network decomposition). - The **Goemans-Williamson result** for MAX-CUT was a turning point in combinatorial optimization, winning the 1999 Fulkerson Prize. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Application 1: MAX-2SAT via SDP Relaxation ### 3.1.1 Problem Definition (NP-hard) > [!NOTE] > **MAX-2SAT:** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in CNF with at most 2 literals per clause, find a truth assignment maximizing the number of satisfied clauses. For each clause $C_j$, define its contribution as a function of variables $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ (where $x_i = +1$ means TRUE). | Clause Type | Example | Unsatisfied when | Weight $w_j$ | |---|---|---|---| | Single literal | $C_j = (x_i)$ | $x_i = -1$ | 1 | | Two literals (same var) | $C_j = (x_i \lor x_i)$ | $x_i = -1$ | 2 (or weight $w_j$) | | Two literals (diff vars) | $C_j = (x_i \lor x_k)$ | $x_i = -1$ AND $x_k = -1$ | $w_j$ | The objective is: $$ \max \sum_{j} w_j \cdot \mathbb{1}[C_j \text{ satisfied}] $$ ### 3.1.2 Quadratic Reformulation Every clause $C_j$ can be written as $\frac{w_j}{4}(s_0 + s_1 x_{i_1} + s_2 x_{i_2} + s_3 x_{i_1} x_{i_2})$ where $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3 \in \{-1, +1\}$. The total objective is: $$ \max \sum_{j} \frac{w_j}{4} \left( s_0^{(j)} + s_1^{(j)} x_{i_1} + s_2^{(j)} x_{i_2} + s_3^{(j)} x_{i_1} x_{i_2} \right) $$ Grouping terms by $1, x_i, x_i x_j$, we get: $$ \max \alpha_0 + \sum_i \alpha_i x_i + \sum_{(i,j)} \alpha_{ij} x_i x_j $$ ### 3.1.3 Vector Relaxation (SDP) Substitute $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ with unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (introduce a coordinate for each variable, plus one extra $v_0$ — the all-ones vector used for constant terms). Then $x_i x_j \to v_i \cdot v_j$ and $x_i \to v_i \cdot v_0$. Define the Gram matrix $Y$: $$ Y_{00} = 1, \quad Y_{ii} = 1 \ \forall i, \quad Y \succeq 0 $$ The **SDP relaxation** becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} \max \;& \alpha_0 + \sum_i \alpha_i Y_{0i} + \sum_{(i,j)} \alpha_{ij} Y_{ij} \\ \text{s.t. } & Y_{00} = 1, \ Y_{ii} = 1 \ \forall i \ge 1 \\ & Y \succeq 0 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KEY CONCEPT:** Since $Y$ is PSD, it can be factored as $Y = V^T V$ for some matrix $V$. Each row of $V$ gives a vector $v_i$. The vectors have unit norm because $Y_{ii} = v_i \cdot v_i = 1$. ### 3.1.4 Nesterlov's Non-Linear Rounding After solving the SDP, we have unit vectors $v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_n$. We now need to round them to $\{-1, +1\}$ assignment. **Procedure:** 1. Pick a random unit vector $r$ uniformly from $\mathbb{S}^n$. 2. Set $x_i = \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r)$. ### 3.1.5 Derivation of Approximation Ratio Consider a clause $C_j$ of weight $w_j$. We need to bound the probability that it is satisfied. **Case 1: Single literal clause $C_j = (x_i)$.** The clause is unsatisfied iff $x_i = -1$, i.e., iff $r$ is on the "negative" side of the hyperplane through origin perpendicular to $v_i$. Geometrically: $$ \Pr[C_j \text{ satisfied}] = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cos \theta_{0i} $$ where $\theta_{0i}$ is the angle between $v_0$ and $v_i$. Since $v_0$ represents the constant $+1$ vector in the relaxation, $\cos \theta_{0i} = v_0 \cdot v_i = Y_{0i}$. **Case 2: Two-literal clause $C_j = (x_i \lor x_k)$.** This clause is unsatisfied only when both $x_i = -1$ AND $x_k = -1$. The probability of this is the joint probability that the random hyperplane separates $v_0$ from both $v_i$ and $v_k$ on the same (negative) side. **Key Lemma (Goemans & Williamson):** For any two unit vectors $v_i$ and $v_k$: $$ \Pr[\text{both on negative side of } v_0] \le \frac{1}{4}(1 - v_i \cdot v_k) $$ Using the bound $\Pr[\text{satisfied}] \ge \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}(v_i \cdot v_k) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}(v_i \cdot v_k)$ and the worst-case analysis: $$ \min_{0 \le \theta \le \pi} \frac{\Pr[\text{satisfied in integer}]}{\Pr[\text{satisfied in SDP}} = \alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856 $$ This gives the **$\alpha_{GW}$-approximation** for MAX-2SAT. > [!NOTE] > **Final Result:** MAX-2SAT can be approximated within ratio $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$ using SDP relaxation + Nesterlov's rounding. --- ## 3.2 Application 2: SPARSEST CUT ### 3.2.1 Problem Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sparsest Cut:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $c_e$ and vertex weights $d_v$, find a cut $(S, \bar{S})$ that minimizes the **sparsity**: > $$\Phi(S) = \frac{\sum_{e \in \delta(S)} c_e}{\sum_{u \in S, v \in \bar{S}} d_u d_v}$$ > where $\delta(S) = \{e \in E : e \text{ crosses } S\}$. This is fundamental for **graph partitioning** and **divide-and-conquer** algorithms. ### 3.2.2 Linear Programming Relaxation (Leighton-Rao) Introduce metric $d_{ij} = 1$ if $i, j$ are separated, else $0$. Minimize: $$ \min \sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} d_{ij} \quad \text{s.t. metric constraints} $$ **Leighton-Rao gave $O(\log n)$ ratio** for this LP. ### 3.2.3 SDP Relaxation (ARV) Arora, Rao, and Vazirani (2004) **strengthened the LP** by using an **SDP metric** — the Gram matrix $Y = V^T V$ where $V$ is a matrix whose rows are $v_i$. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Point:** The SDP relaxation is the LP relaxation with the additional constraint that the metric $d_{ij}$ can be *embedded into $\ell_2^2$* (Euclidean squared distances). This is strictly stronger than the Leighton-Rao LP. The SDP: $$ \begin{aligned} \min \;& \sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} \cdot \frac{\|v_i - v_j\|^2}{2} \\ \text{s.t. } & \sum_{i < j} d_u d_v \|v_i - v_j\|^2 = 1 \\ & \|v_i - v_j\|^2 = 2 - 2 v_i \cdot v_j \le 4 \\ & v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.4 ARV Rounding Procedure 1. **Solve the SDP** to get vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_n$. 2. **Embed in $\ell_1$** by projecting vectors onto a random line and grouping (Lévy projection, or "pseudorandom" rounding). 3. **Output cut $(S, \bar{S})$** based on the projection. **Theorem (ARV 2004):** This gives an $O(\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation for Sparsest Cut. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ Factor:** The $\sqrt{\log n}$ comes from the **distortion bound** of embedding squared Euclidean metrics into $\ell_1$ (Bourgain's theorem gives $O(\log n)$ for $\ell_2 \to \ell_1$, but for $\ell_2^2 \to \ell_1$ the bound improves to $O(\sqrt{\log n})$). --- ## 3.3 Application 3: BETWEENNESS ### 3.3.1 Problem Statement > [!NOTE] > **Betweenness (Minimum Linear Ordering with Triplet Constraints):** Given $n$ elements and a list of ordered triples $(i, j, k)$ with weights $w_{ijk}$, find a linear order $\prec$ on the elements minimizing the total weight of triples that are **violated**, i.e., where $i \prec k$ and $k \prec j$ (i.e., $k$ is between $i$ and $j$ in the order). ### 3.3.2 SDP Relaxation Replace the linear order with a **vector embedding** $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ and use: - $v_i \cdot v_j$ to encode the relative order. - **Half-plane rounding** assigns each vertex to one of two "halves" of a random line. The SDP for Betweenness can be written as a quadratic program over the unit sphere, relaxed to an SDP with PSD constraints. **Theorem (Charikar, Guruswami, Kumar 2006):** The SDP relaxation with appropriate rounding gives an $O(\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation for Betweenness. ### 3.3.3 Outline of the Analysis The expected cost of the rounded solution is bounded using a **local search** argument: - For each triple $(i, j, k)$ with $i, k, j$ assigned positions $a < b < c$ in the random linear order, the violation probability depends on the angular separations of $v_i, v_j, v_k$ in the SDP. - The SDP integrality gap on certain "almost-euclidean" instances yields the $\sqrt{\log n}$ factor. --- ## 3.4 Application 4: Correlation Clustering ### 3.4.1 Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correlation Clustering:** Given $n$ items and similarity weights $w_{ij}^+ \ge 0$ (positive, "similar") and $w_{ij}^- \ge 0$ (negative, "dissimilar"), partition items into clusters to **maximize agreement**: maximize > $$\sum_{(i,j): \text{agree}} w_{ij}^+ + \sum_{(i,j): \text{disagree}} w_{ij}^-$$ > or equivalently minimize disagreement. ### 3.4.2 SDP Formulation (Ailon-Charikar-Newman, 2008) The objective in vector form: $$ \max \sum_{(i,j) \in E^+} w_{ij}^+ \cdot \frac{1 + v_i \cdot v_j}{2} + \sum_{(i,j) \in E^-} w_{ij}^- \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} $$ Subject to $\|v_i\| = 1$, then relaxed to $Y \succeq 0$, $Y_{ii} = 1$. ### 3.4.3 Pivot Rounding After solving the SDP, **pivot rounding** works as follows: 1. Pick a random ordering $\pi$ of vertices. 2. For each vertex $i$ in order, place $i$ in a new cluster (or join an existing cluster) based on the SDP vectors and pivot positions. **Theorem:** This achieves an $O(\log n)$ approximation for Correlation Clustering. > [!NOTE] > **Why SDP for Clustering?** The Euclidean distances $\|v_i - v_j\|$ provide a *continuous* similarity that is robust to noise — clusters become "spherical" regions in the SDP solution space. --- ## 3.5 Application 5: Unique Games and Integrality Gaps The **Unique Games Conjecture** (UGC) of Subhash Khot (2002) posits that distinguishing whether a Unique Game has $1 - \epsilon$ or $\epsilon$ fraction of satisfiable constraints is NP-hard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KKMO Theorem (2007):** For any $\epsilon > 0$, there is a Unique Game instance where the SDP optimum is at most $1 - \epsilon$, but the integrality gap goes to $0$. This shows SDP **cannot beat $\epsilon$** for Unique Games under UGC. This was a landmark result establishing that SDP has **fundamental limitations** for distinguishing Unique Games. --- ## 3.6 Comparison Table: Approximation Ratios | Problem | LP Ratio | SDP Ratio | Tight? | |---|---|---|---| | MAX-CUT | 0.5 | 0.87856 | Yes (UGC) | | MAX-2SAT | 0.75 | 0.87856 | Yes (UGC) | | MAX-3SAT | 7/8 | 7/8 (LP) | Yes | | SPARSEST CUT | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Open | | BETWEENNESS | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ | Open | | Correlation Clustering | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Yes | | Unique Games (decide) | NP-hard for $1-\epsilon$ vs $\epsilon$ | Integrality gap $\to 0$ | Yes (UGC) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever asked about SDP applications, ALWAYS state the approximation ratio, the relaxation used, the rounding technique, and the hardness result (if any). Examiners allocate marks for *each* of these components. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Universal SDP Approximation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original Combinatorial Problem
e.g., MAX-2SAT, SPARSEST CUT] --> B[Quadratic Reformulation
Variables x_i in -1, +1
Objective uses x_i x_j products] B --> C[Vector Program
Replace x_i with unit vectors v_i
Inner product v_i dot v_j] C --> D[SDP Relaxation
Gram Matrix Y = V-transpose V
Y PSD and Y_ii equals 1] D --> E[Convex Solver
Interior Point or Ellipsoid Method
Yields optimal Y_star and v_i] E --> F[Rounding Procedure
Random Hyperplane or Pivot Rounding] F --> G[Integral Solution
x_i in -1, +1 assignment] G --> H[Approximation Guarantee
alpha times OPT_SDP approx OPT_ILP] subgraph CONE[Convex Optimization Step] D E end ``` ## 4.2 Random Hyperplane Rounding Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SPHERE[Unit Sphere S_n-1] V0[Vector v_0
Origin of Cut] V1[Vector v_1] V2[Vector v_2] V3[Vector v_3] end R[Random Hyperplane r
Uniform on S_n-1] --> S1[Sign v_0 dot r] R --> S2[Sign v_1 dot r] R --> S3[Sign v_2 dot r] R --> S4[Sign v_3 dot r] S1 --> X0[x_0 in -1, +1] S2 --> X1[x_1 in -1, +1] S3 --> X2[x_2 in -1, +1] S4 --> X3[x_3 in -1, +1] ``` ## 4.3 SDP Application Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD P{Problem Type?} P -->|Quadratic Objective| Q1[Quadratic over Sphere] P -->|Metric Embedding| Q2[Graph Partitioning / Sparsity] P -->|Clustering| Q3[Correlation Clustering] P -->|Ordering / Triplets| Q4[Betweenness] P -->|CSP-like| Q5[Max 2SAT, Max kSAT] Q1 --> S[SDP Relaxation
Max v_i dot v_j] Q2 --> S Q3 --> S Q4 --> S Q5 --> S S --> R{Rounding Scheme?} R -->|Hyperplane| R1[Random Hyperplane Rounding
0.878 for MAX-CUT and MAX-2SAT] R -->|Pivot| R2[Pivot Rounding
O log n for Correlation Clustering] R -->|L1 Embed| R3[Levy Projection / L1 Embedding
O sqrt log n for Sparsest Cut] R -->|Half-plane| R4[Half-plane Rounding
O sqrt log n for Betweenness] ``` ## 4.4 Integrality Gap Illustration (KKMO Unique Games) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SDP Optimum 1 minus epsilon] --> B[Rounded Integer Solution] B --> C[Integer Value epsilon] C --> D[Gap: SDP minus INT approx 1] style A fill:#e6f2ff style B fill:#fff2cc style C fill:#f8cecc style D fill:#ffcccc ``` ## 4.5 Comprehensive Application Matrix ```mermaid graph LR subgraph FOUNDATIONS[Mathematical Foundations] A1[Positive Semidefinite Cone] A2[Gram Matrix Representation] A3[Euclidean Vectors on Sphere] A4[Convex Duality] end subgraph PROBLEMS[Application Problems] B1[MAX CUT] B2[MAX 2SAT] B3[SPARSEST CUT] B4[BETWEENNESS] B5[CORRELATION CLUSTERING] B6[UNIQUE GAMES] end subgraph ROUNDING[Rounding Techniques] C1[Random Hyperplane] C2[Pivot Rounding] C3[L1 Embedding / Levy] C4[Half Plane Rounding] end subgraph RESULTS[Approximation Guarantees] D1[0.878 Approx] D2[O log n Approx] D3[O sqrt log n Approx] D4[Integrality Gap = 0] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B3 A3 --> B4 A4 --> B5 A2 --> B6 B1 --> C1 --> D1 B2 --> C1 --> D1 B3 --> C3 --> D3 B4 --> C4 --> D3 B5 --> C2 --> D2 B6 --> C1 --> D4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023] > **Q:** What is a Semidefinite Program (SDP)? Write its standard form. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Semidefinite Program (SDP)** is a convex optimization problem in which we optimize a linear objective function over the intersection of an affine subspace with the cone of positive semidefinite (PSD) matrices. **Standard (Primal) Form:** $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{X} \;& \langle C, X \rangle = \sum_{i,j} C_{ij} X_{ij} \\ \text{s.t. } & \langle A_i, X \rangle \le b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\ & X \succeq 0 \end{aligned} $$ where $X, C, A_i \in \mathbb{S}^{n}$ (symmetric $n \times n$ matrices), $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$, and $X \succeq 0$ means $X$ is **positive semidefinite**. The **dual** SDP is: $$ \begin{aligned} \min \;& \sum_{i=1}^{m} b_i y_i \\ \text{s.t. } & \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i - C = S, \quad S \succeq 0 \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > **Q:** Define the **Goemans–Williamson constant** $\alpha_{GW}$. Why is it important? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Goemans–Williamson constant** is defined as: $$ \alpha_{GW} = \min_{0 \le \theta \le \pi} \frac{\theta/\pi}{1 - \cos\theta} \approx 0.87856 $$ It represents the **worst-case ratio** between the expected weight of a cut produced by **random hyperplane rounding** and the SDP objective value. **Significance:** - It is the **best-possible approximation ratio** for MAX-CUT under the Unique Games Conjecture (UGC). - The same constant gives the **approximation ratio for MAX-2SAT** by Nesterlov's analysis. - It is the **smallest** $\alpha$ such that $\theta/\pi \ge \alpha(1 - \cos\theta)$ for all $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, defining the GW curve. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Tip for Students:** $\alpha_{GW} = 0.87856$. The integrality gap of MAX-CUT SDP is exactly $1/\alpha_{GW} \approx 1.138$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Application of SDP to SPARSEST CUT > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, S8 B.Tech CSE]** > > **(a)** Formulate the **Sparsest Cut** problem as an SDP relaxation. Clearly state the constraints and the objective. Show how the LP relaxation differs from the SDP relaxation. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **Arora–Rao–Vazirani (ARV) rounding** procedure and show that the SDP gives an $O(\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Definition (1 mark):** The Sparsest Cut of a graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $c_e$ and vertex weights $d_v$ is: $$ \Phi(S) = \frac{\sum_{e \in \delta(S)} c_e}{\sum_{u \in S, v \in \bar{S}} d_u d_v} $$ We want to find $S \subseteq V$ minimizing $\Phi(S)$. **Step 1: LP Formulation (2 marks):** Introduce variables $x_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ representing a metric (where $x_{ij} = 1$ means $i$ and $j$ are separated). The LP is: $$ \begin{aligned} \min \;& \sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} x_{ij} \\ \text{s.t. } & \sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Difference from LP:** The SDP enforces that the metric $x_{ij}$ is a *squared Euclidean metric* — it can be embedded into $\ell_2^2$. This is strictly stronger than the LP's general metric constraint, so the SDP optimum is **at least as large** as the LP optimum. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **ARV Rounding Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Solve the SDP to obtain vectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n \in \mathbb{R}^n$. 2. Pick a **pseudorandom direction** $r$ — a combination of $O(\log n)$ independent standard basis vectors scaled by Gaussian weights. 3. Project each $v_i$ onto $r$ to get scalars $\langle v_i, r \rangle$. 4. Sort vertices by these projections and split them into two sets $S, \bar{S}$ using a **threshold** (e.g., the median). **Approximation Analysis (4 marks):** The expected cut weight is bounded by: $$ \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{separated}]\right] \le O(\sqrt{\log n}) \cdot \text{SDP-OPT} $$ The expected volume (denominator) is at least $\Omega(1) \cdot \text{SDP-OPT}$. The bound uses **Bourgain's embedding theorem** in a key way: an $\ell_2^2$ metric can be embedded into $\ell_1$ with distortion $O(\sqrt{\log n})$. The $\sqrt{\log n}$ factor arises because we are rounding a *squared* Euclidean metric rather than a linear one. > [!NOTE] > **Theorem (ARV 2004):** The SDP-based algorithm achieves $O(\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation for Sparsest Cut, improving the $O(\log n)$ ratio of the LP-based algorithm of Leighton and Rao. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — SDP for MAX-2SAT > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S8 B.Tech CSE]** > > **(a)** Reformulate **MAX-2SAT** as a quadratic program over $\{+1, -1\}$. Show that any 2-clause can be written as a quadratic polynomial $a_0 + a_1 x_i + a_2 x_j + a_3 x_i x_j$ with appropriate coefficients. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Construct the **SDP relaxation** of MAX-2SAT and explain the **Nesterlov rounding** procedure. Show that this gives an $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$ approximation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1: Clause Classification (2 marks):** MAX-2SAT has clauses of the form: - $C = (x_i)$ — single literal (singleton) - $C = (x_i \lor x_j)$ — disjunctive clause with $i \ne j$ **Step 2: Indicator Function (2 marks):** A clause is satisfied under assignment $x \in \{-1, +1\}^n$ iff its indicator is $1$. We use the substitution $x_i = +1 \Leftrightarrow \text{TRUE}$, $x_i = -1 \Leftrightarrow \text{FALSE}$. | Clause | Satisfied when | Indicator | |---|---|---| | $(x_i)$ | $x_i = +1$ | $\frac{1 + x_i}{2}$ | | $(\bar{x_i})$ | $x_i = -1$ | $\frac{1 - x_i}{2}$ | | $(x_i \lor x_j)$ | NOT both $x_i = -1, x_j = -1$ | $1 - \frac{(1-x_i)(1-x_j)}{4} = \frac{3 + x_i + x_j - x_i x_j}{4}$ | | $(x_i \lor \bar{x_j})$ | NOT both $x_i = -1, x_j = +1$ | $\frac{3 + x_i - x_j + x_i x_j}{4}$ | | $(\bar{x_i} \lor \bar{x_j})$ | NOT both $x_i = -1, x_j = -1$ | $\frac{3 - x_i - x_j + x_i x_j}{4}$ | **Step 3: Generic Form (3 marks):** Every clause of weight $w_j$ has the form: $$ w_j \cdot \frac{s_0 + s_1 x_{i_1} + s_2 x_{i_2} + s_3 x_{i_1} x_{i_2}}{4} $$ with $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3 \in \{-1, +1\}$. Summing over all clauses: $$ \max_{x \in \{-1,+1\}^n} \sum_j \frac{w_j}{4}(s_0^{(j)} + s_1^{(j)} x_{i_1} + s_2^{(j)} x_{i_2} + s_3^{(j)} x_{i_1} x_{i_2}) $$ Grouping by monomial types: - Constant: $\alpha_0 = \sum_j \frac{w_j s_0^{(j)}}{4}$ - Linear: $\alpha_i = \sum_{j : i_1 = i} \frac{w_j s_1^{(j)}}{4} + \sum_{j : i_2 = i} \frac{w_j s_2^{(j)}}{4}$ - Quadratic: $\alpha_{ij} = \sum_{j : (i_1, i_2) = (i,j)} \frac{w_j s_3^{(j)}}{4}$ So: $$ \text{MAX-2SAT} = \max_{x \in \{-1,+1\}^n} \alpha_0 + \sum_i \alpha_i x_i + \sum_{i < j} \alpha_{ij} x_i x_j $$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Vector Relaxation (2 marks):** Introduce a "base" vector $v_0$ and unit vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_n$. Replace: - $1 \to v_0 \cdot v_0 = 1$ - $x_i \to v_0 \cdot v_i$ - $x_i x_j \to v_i \cdot v_j$ **Step 2: SDP Formulation (2 marks):** Let $Y$ be the Gram matrix with $Y_{00} = 1$ and $Y_{ii} = 1$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$, and $Y \succeq 0$. The SDP relaxation is: $$ \begin{aligned} \max \;& \alpha_0 + \sum_i \alpha_i Y_{0i} + \sum_{i < j} \alpha_{ij} Y_{ij} \\ \text{s.t. } & Y \succeq 0, \ Y_{00} = 1, \ Y_{ii} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Nesterlov Rounding (2 marks):** Solve the SDP to get vectors $v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_n$. Choose a uniform random vector $r \in \mathbb{S}^{n-1}$. Set $x_i = \text{sign}(v_i \cdot r)$ for each $i$. **Step 4: Approximation Ratio (1 mark):** For each clause, the probability of satisfaction under rounding is at least $\alpha_{GW}$ times the contribution of that clause to the SDP value. By linearity of expectation: $$ \mathbb{E}[\text{weight satisfied}] \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{SDP-OPT} \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}_{ILP} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** MAX-2SAT is $\alpha_{GW}$-approximable, with $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$. This matches the MAX-CUT result because the same Goemans–Williamson non-linear rounding simultaneously handles both. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $Y \succeq 0$ constraint** is the most common mistake — students often write it as an LP. Always emphasize **positive semidefiniteness**. > 2. **Conflating LP and SDP** — students often forget to mention that the SDP requires a *Gram matrix* structure ($Y = V^T V$) or unit-length vectors. > 3. **Omitting the dimension** of the SDP (which is $n+1$ variables for MAX-2SAT due to $v_0$) loses marks. > 4. **For Nesterlov / Goemans–Williamson rounding,** students must state that the random vector $r$ is chosen *uniformly* on the unit sphere — saying "random vector" alone is incomplete. > 5. **For SPARSEST CUT,** students forget that the ARV result is $O(\sqrt{\log n})$, not $O(\log n)$ — the $\sqrt{}$ comes from squared Euclidean embedding. > 6. **Always cite the original paper** (GW 1994, ARV 2004, CGK 2006) — KTU examiners value attribution. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Other Applications of SDP** ### A. Core Concepts to Master - [ ] **SDP standard form**: $\max \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle \le b_i$, $X \succeq 0$ - [ ] **Vector relaxation**: $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ → unit vector $v_i$ - [ ] **Gram matrix** $Y$ with $Y_{ii} = 1$ and $Y \succeq 0$ is the central object - [ ] **PSD cone dimension** is $n(n+1)/2$ (symmetric matrix has that many free entries) - [ ] **Random hyperplane rounding** probability: $\Pr[\text{separate}] = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)/\pi$ ### B. Five Critical Applications - [ ] **MAX-2SAT** — SDP gives $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$ via Nesterlov's non-linear rounding - [ ] **SPARSEST CUT** — SDP + ARV rounding gives $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ - [ ] **BETWEENNESS** — SDP + half-plane rounding gives $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ (Charikar–Guruswami–Kumar 2006) - [ ] **Correlation Clustering** — SDP + pivot rounding gives $O(\log n)$ (Ailon–Charikar–Newman 2008) - [ ] **Unique Games** — KKMO 2007 shows SDP has integrality gap → 0, validating UGC ### C. Key Constants and Values - [ ] $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.87856$ (MAX-CUT and MAX-2SAT approximation) - [ ] $1/\alpha_{GW} \approx 1.138$ (integrality gap of MAX-CUT SDP) - [ ] $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ — ARV for Sparsest Cut and CGK for Betweenness - [ ] $O(\log n)$ — Leighton–Rao LP for Sparsest Cut, Ailon–Charikar–Newman for Correlation Clustering ### D. Rounding Techniques — Know Each One - [ ] **Random Hyperplane** — projects vectors onto a random line, signs assigned - [ ] **Pivot Rounding** — random ordering, vertices join existing cluster or start new one - [ ] **Lévy Projection / L1 Embedding** — used by ARV for Sparsest Cut - [ ] **Half-plane Rounding** — used by CGK for Betweenness - [ ] **Goemans–Williamson Non-linear** — the original for MAX-CUT, uses $\theta/\pi$ curve ### E. Key Theorems — Memorize Authorship - [ ] **Goemans & Williamson (1994):** MAX-CUT $\alpha_{GW}$-approx, 1999 Fulkerson Prize - [ ] **Nesterlov (1998):** $\alpha_{GW}$-approx for MAX-2SAT - [ ] **Arora, Rao & Vazirani (2004):** Sparsest Cut $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ - [ ] **Charikar, Guruswami & Kumar (2006):** Betweenness $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ - [ ] **Ailon, Charikar & Newman (2008):** Correlation Clustering $O(\log n)$ - [ ] **Khot, Kindler, Mossel & O'Donnell (2007):** KKMO gap for Unique Games ### F. Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation - [ ] ❌ Writing $|x|$ inside a table — use $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown - [ ] ❌ Confusing "PSD" with "PD" (positive definite) - [ ] ❌ Forgetting $Y_{ii} = 1$ (unit vector norm constraint) - [ ] ❌ Stating LP ratio instead of SDP ratio - [ ] ❌ Missing the *integrality gap* discussion - [ ] ❌ Omitting the role of Bourgain's theorem in ARV analysis > [!NOTE] > **Quick Mnemonic — "MSBAC-K":** **M**AX-2SAT, **S**parsest Cut, **B**etweenness, **A**cyclic Subgraph, **C**orrelation Clustering, **U**nique Games (**K**KMO). > **Module 3 — Other Applications of SDP: COMPLETE. Next stop: Module 4 — typically covering Linear Programming in Approximation Algorithms (Set Cover, Randomized Rounding, Primal-Dual).**

topics.title: Approximation Schemes - Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (PTAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Approximation Schemes - Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (PTAS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Approximation Scheme An **Approximation Scheme** for an optimization problem $\Pi$ is a family of algorithms $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}$ indexed by a parameter $\epsilon > 0$, where each algorithm $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ produces a solution whose approximation ratio is at most $(1+\epsilon)$ for minimization problems (or $(1-\epsilon)$ for maximization problems). A **Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS)** is a special class of approximation scheme satisfying the polynomiality condition: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A PTAS is an approximation scheme $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}$ such that for **every fixed** $\epsilon > 0$, the running time of $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ on an input of size $n$ is bounded by a polynomial in $n$ — that is, $T(n, \epsilon) = n^{O(1)}$ for each fixed $\epsilon$. The exponent of the polynomial may, however, depend on $\epsilon$ (and may grow unboundedly as $\epsilon \to 0$). Formally, a PTAS for a minimization problem satisfies: $$\forall \epsilon > 0, \quad \rho(\mathcal{A}_\epsilon) \leq 1+\epsilon \quad \text{and} \quad T(n, \epsilon) = O(n^{c(\epsilon)})$$ where $c(\epsilon)$ is some constant depending on $\epsilon$, but not on $n$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tunable Precision Dial" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are a tailor stitching a custom suit. A **constant-factor approximation algorithm** is like a tailor who can only make suits of two sizes: "Small" or "Large" — guaranteed to fit within a factor of 2 of perfection, but never closer. A **PTAS**, on the other hand, is like a tailor with an infinitely adjustable dial: the customer specifies how close to the *perfect fit* they want (say within 1% of ideal), and the tailor then spends a reasonable (polynomial) amount of time to deliver that precision. The closer to perfection, the longer the tailoring takes — but it is always polynomial in the size of the customer (input), not exponential. In engineering terms: a PTAS gives you a **dial** to trade **running time** for **solution quality**, where each fixed setting of the dial yields a polynomial-time algorithm, but the polynomial degree may inflate as you crank the dial toward perfection. ### 1.3 Position of PTAS in the Approximation Hierarchy | Class | Approximation Ratio | Running Time | Strength | |-------|--------------------|--------------|----------| | **FPTAS** (Fully PTAS) | $1+\epsilon$ | $O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} \cdot n^c\right)$ — polynomial in **both** $n$ and $\frac{1}{\epsilon}$ | Strongest | | **PTAS** | $1+\epsilon$ | $n^{O(1)}$ for fixed $\epsilon$ — may be exponential in $\frac{1}{\epsilon}$ | Strong | | **APX** | Constant $c > 1$ | Polynomial in $n$ | Moderate | > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy:** $\text{FPTAS} \subseteq \text{PTAS} \subseteq \text{APX} \subseteq \text{NPO}$. Every FPTAS is a PTAS, but not every PTAS is an FPTAS (e.g., Bin Packing has a PTAS but no FPTAS unless $P=NP$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a PTAS A PTAS algorithm typically works through the following structured logic: 1. **Parameter Reception:** The algorithm receives the instance $I$ and a target precision $\epsilon > 0$ as inputs. 2. **Preprocessing / Scaling:** The instance is rescaled or partitioned so that the "cost granularity" becomes proportional to $\epsilon \cdot \text{OPT}$. 3. **State Space Reduction:** The number of distinct feasible states (e.g., subset sums, knapsack values, configurations) is reduced from potentially exponential to polynomial by rounding or grouping. 4. **Optimization on Reduced State:** A dynamic programming or exhaustive search is performed over the reduced state space, producing a near-optimal solution. 5. **Reconstruction:** The polynomial-size solution is mapped back to a valid solution for the original instance. ### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind PTAS Existence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A PTAS exists whenever the problem's combinatorial structure allows *graceful degradation* — meaning that by paying a multiplicative penalty of $(1+\epsilon)$ in solution quality, we can collapse an otherwise exponential search space into a polynomial one. This is typically possible when: > - The objective function values can be bounded (e.g., $V_{\max}$) and rounded. > - The "interesting" portion of the search space has size polynomial in $\frac{V_{\max}}{\epsilon}$. > - Optimal substructure exists for dynamic programming. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Approximation Ratio (min) | $\rho = \frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\text{OPT}(I)}$ | Multiplicative guarantee for minimization | | Approximation Ratio (max) | $\rho = \frac{\text{OPT}(I)}{\mathcal{A}(I)}$ | Multiplicative guarantee for maximization | | PTAS condition (min) | $\mathcal{A}(I) \leq (1+\epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$ | Approximation guarantee | | PTAS running time | $T(n,\epsilon) = n^{O(1)}$ per fixed $\epsilon$ | Polynomial in $n$ | | FPTAS running time | $T(n,\epsilon) = \text{poly}\!\left(n, \tfrac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Polynomial in both | | Knapsack value bound | $V_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | Sum of all item values | | Knapsack item count (PTAS) | $M = \lceil \tfrac{n}{\epsilon} \rceil$ | Number of "big" items kept exactly | | Knapsack dynamic states | $S = \left\{0, \tfrac{\epsilon V_{\max}}{n}, \tfrac{2\epsilon V_{\max}}{n}, \ldots\right\}$ | Rounded profit grid | | Bin Packing PTAS ratio | $\text{OPT} + 1$ bins (asymptotic) | Asymptotic PTAS | | Knapsack PTAS running time | $O\!\left(n^3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Polynomial in $n$ and $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$ (this is FPTAS) | > [!NOTE] > **Important Distinction:** The Knapsack problem has an **FPTAS** with running time $O(n^2 / \epsilon)$ — strictly stronger than a PTAS. Bin Packing has only a PTAS (no FPTAS unless $P=NP$). ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of PTAS PTAS algorithms are critically important in: - **Operations Research:** Vehicle routing with time windows, where logistics companies (e.g., Amazon, FedEx) need provably near-optimal delivery schedules within practical time limits. - **VLSI Design:** Component placement and wire routing where exact optimization is NP-hard but $(1+\epsilon)$-approximations are industry-acceptable. - **Bioinformatics:** Sequence alignment and phylogeny reconstruction with controlled error bounds. - **Network Design:** Steiner tree and facility location problems in telecommunications. - **Scheduling:** Makespan minimization on parallel machines ($P \Vert C_{\max}$) admits a PTAS. ### 2.5 Strong NP-Hardness Barrier > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Limitation (Vazirani's Theorem):** If a problem is **strongly NP-hard**, then it does **not** admit an FPTAS unless $P = NP$. It may, however, still admit a PTAS (e.g., Bin Packing, Euclidean TSP). This distinction is a frequent KTU exam question. A problem $\Pi$ is **strongly NP-hard** if it remains NP-hard even when all numerical parameters in the input are bounded by a polynomial in the input size $n$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: FPTAS for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem (Archetypal PTAS Construction) We present the canonical construction step by step. This problem is the **gold-standard KTU Module 3 exam problem**. #### Problem Statement Given $n$ items with values $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ and weights $\{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ and a knapsack of capacity $W$, find subset $S \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$ maximizing $\sum_{i \in S} v_i$ subject to $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq W$. #### Optimal Exact DP Formulation (Bellman, 1957) Define $P(i, p)$ = minimum weight achievable using items from $\{1, \ldots, i\}$ with total value **exactly** $p$. - Base case: $P(0, 0) = 0$, $P(0, p) = \infty$ for $p > 0$. - Recurrence: $P(i, p) = \min\{P(i-1, p),\; w_i + P(i-1, p - v_i)\}$. - Running time: $O(n \cdot V_{\max})$ where $V_{\max} = \sum v_i$ — **pseudo-polynomial**. #### PTAS / FPTAS Construction: Value Scaling via $\epsilon$-Rounding Let $\epsilon > 0$ be given. We want a solution with value at least $(1-\epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}$. **Step 1 — Compute the value bound.** $$V_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$$ **Step 2 — Define the scaling factor.** $$\theta = \frac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n}$$ This is the *granularity* of the rounding. **Step 3 — Round every value to the nearest multiple of $\theta$.** $$\hat{v}_i = \left\lfloor \frac{v_i}{\theta} \right\rfloor \cdot \theta$$ Since each value loses at most $\theta$, the total value loss across all $n$ items is bounded. **Step 4 — Bound the new maximum integer value.** $$\hat{V}_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \hat{v}_i \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i = V_{\max}$$ $$\hat{V}_{\max} \text{ in integer units} = \frac{V_{\max}}{\theta} = \frac{n}{\epsilon}$$ **Step 5 — Run exact DP on rounded values.** The DP table now has size $O(n \cdot \hat{V}_{\max}) = O\!\left(n \cdot \tfrac{n}{\epsilon}\right) = O\!\left(\tfrac{n^2}{\epsilon}\right)$. **Step 6 — Approximation Guarantee Derivation.** Let $\text{OPT}$ be the true optimal value, and let $\hat{\text{OPT}}$ be the optimal value under rounded profits. The rounded optimal solution is also a feasible solution for the original problem (same weights, smaller profits). $$\hat{\text{OPT}} \geq \text{OPT} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (v_i - \hat{v}_i)$$ Each rounding error satisfies: $$v_i - \hat{v}_i \leq \theta = \frac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n}$$ Therefore, summing over all $n$ items: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(v_i - \hat{v}_i) \leq n \cdot \theta = n \cdot \frac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n} = \epsilon \cdot V_{\max}$$ Since $\text{OPT} \leq V_{\max}$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(v_i - \hat{v}_i) \leq \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT}$$ Therefore: $$\hat{\text{OPT}} \geq \text{OPT} - \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT} = (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}$$ The algorithm's output value equals $\hat{\text{OPT}}$ (since weights are unchanged), giving: $$\mathcal{A}(I) = \hat{\text{OPT}} \geq (1-\epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}(I) \quad \blacksquare$$ **Step 7 — Running Time.** $$T(n, \epsilon) = O\!\left(n \cdot \frac{V_{\max}}{\theta}\right) = O\!\left(n \cdot \frac{n}{\epsilon}\right) = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\epsilon}\right)$$ This is polynomial in **both** $n$ and $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$ — hence this is actually an **FPTAS** (the strongest form of approximation scheme). ### 3.2 Example 2: PTAS for Makespan Minimization ($P \Vert C_{\max}$) Given $n$ jobs with processing times $p_1, \ldots, p_n$ to be scheduled on $m$ identical machines, minimize the makespan $C_{\max} = \max_j \sum_{i \in M_j} p_i$. **Algorithm (Longest Processing Time — LPT, with bin packing framing):** 1. Sort jobs in **decreasing** order: $p_1 \geq p_2 \geq \cdots \geq p_n$. 2. Apply the **first-fit decreasing** algorithm. 3. As $n \to \infty$, the schedule uses at most $\text{OPT} + 1$ machines asymptotically. **Approximation Ratio:** $$\frac{C_{\max}}{\text{OPT}} \leq \frac{4}{3} - \frac{1}{3m}$$ This is a **constant-factor** approximation. A PTAS (by Hochbaum-Shmoys, 1987) achieves $(1+\epsilon)\text{OPT}$ for any $\epsilon > 0$ using *dual approximation* and machine *rounding/counting*. ### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix | Problem | Best Known Scheme | Running Time | Has FPTAS? | NP-Hardness | |---------|-------------------|--------------|-----------|-------------| | 0/1 Knapsack | FPTAS | $O(n^2/\epsilon)$ | ✅ Yes | Weakly NP-hard | | Bin Packing | PTAS | $O(n \log n) + \text{poly}(\tfrac{1}{\epsilon})$ | ❌ No (unless $P=NP$) | Strongly NP-hard | | Euclidean TSP | PTAS (Arora '98) | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | ❌ No | Strongly NP-hard | | Makespan ($P \Vert C_{\max}$) | PTAS | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | ❌ No | Strongly NP-hard | | Vertex Cover | 2-approx (no PTAS) | $O(n+m)$ | ❌ No | Weakly NP-hard | | Independent Set on planar graphs | PTAS (Baker '94) | $O(k \cdot n)$ | ❌ No | NP-hard | | Subset Sum | FPTAS | $O(n^2/\epsilon)$ | ✅ Yes | Weakly NP-hard | | Steiner Tree | PTAS in planar graphs | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | ❌ No | NP-hard | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Approximation Hierarchy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef fptas fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef ptas fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef apx fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef npo fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 FPTAS[FPTAS
1 + epsilon approx
poly in n and 1 over epsilon]:::fptas PTAS[PTAS
1 + epsilon approx
poly in n for fixed epsilon]:::ptas APX[APX
constant factor c approx
poly in n]:::apx NPO[NPO
all NP optimization problems]:::npo FPTAS -->|subset| PTAS PTAS -->|subset| APX APX -->|subset| NPO ``` ### 4.2 PTAS Internal Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef input fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef process fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef output fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 INP[Input Instance I
and precision epsilon]:::input PRE[Preprocessing
compute value bound V max]:::process RND[Rounding Step
values scaled by epsilon V max over n]:::process RDP[Reduced DP on Rounded Values
state space collapses to O of n squared over epsilon]:::process RECON[Reconstruction
map rounded solution to original instance]:::process OUTP[Output Solution S
with guarantee 1 minus epsilon times OPT]:::output INP --> PRE PRE --> RND RND --> RDP RDP --> RECON RECON --> OUTP ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow — Does a PTAS Exist for a Given Problem? ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef yes fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef no fill:#ea9999,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 Q1{Is the problem
strongly NP-hard?}:::decision Q2{Does an FPTAS
already exist?}:::decision Q3{Is the objective
pseudo-polynomial?}:::decision YES_FPTAS[YES: FPTAS exists
e.g., Knapsack]:::yes YES_PTAS[YES: PTAS exists but
no FPTAS, e.g., Bin Packing]:::yes NO_PTAS[NO: only constant approx
e.g., Vertex Cover]:::no EXPLORE[Investigate problem-specific
PTAS techniques]:::no Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q1 -->|Yes| Q3 Q2 -->|Yes| YES_FPTAS Q2 -->|No| YES_PTAS Q3 -->|Yes| EXPLORE Q3 -->|No| NO_PTAS ``` ### 4.4 Trade-off Curve — Approximation Quality vs. Running Time ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef p1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef p2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef p3 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 LOW_EPS[Large epsilon = 0.5
fast but loose approx 1.5 times OPT]:::p1 MID_EPS[Medium epsilon = 0.1
