slow but near optimal 1.01 times OPT]:::p3 LOW_EPS -->|crank precision dial| MID_EPS MID_EPS -->|crank precision dial| HIGH_EPS ``` ### 4.5 PTAS Construction Strategy — Schematic Map ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef strat fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef tech fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 S1[Strategy 1: Value Rounding]:::strat S2[Strategy 2: State Space Enumeration]:::strat S3[Strategy 3: Local Search with Bounded Neighborhood]:::strat S4[Strategy 4: Arora's Quadtrees for Geometric Problems]:::strat T1[Used in Knapsack, Subset Sum
scales values by epsilon V over n]:::tech T2[Used in Knapsack, Scheduling
enumerate 1 over epsilon configurations]:::tech T3[Used in Planar Graph problems
bounded depth k neighborhoods]:::tech T4[Used in Euclidean TSP, Steiner Tree
recursive spatial partitioning]:::tech S1 --> T1 S2 --> T2 S3 --> T3 S4 --> T4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** **Q1. Define a Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS). How does it differ from a Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS)?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **PTAS** for an optimization problem is a family of algorithms $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon : \epsilon > 0\}$ such that for every fixed $\epsilon > 0$, the algorithm $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ produces a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation in time polynomial in the input size $n$. The polynomial's degree may, however, depend on $\epsilon$. An **FPTAS** is a stronger scheme where the running time is polynomial in **both** $n$ and $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. Formally: - PTAS: $T(n, \epsilon) = n^{c(\epsilon)}$ — polynomial in $n$ for fixed $\epsilon$, but possibly exponential in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. - FPTAS: $T(n, \epsilon) = \text{poly}\!\left(n, \tfrac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$ — polynomial in both. Every FPTAS is a PTAS, but the converse is not always true. For example, the Bin Packing problem has a PTAS but no FPTAS unless $P = NP$. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** **Q2. Why does the Bin Packing problem admit only a PTAS and not an FPTAS?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Bin Packing problem is **strongly NP-hard**. A fundamental theorem in approximation algorithms (Vazirani, 2001) states: > If a problem is strongly NP-hard, then it does not admit an FPTAS unless $P = NP$. The proof sketch is as follows. If Bin Packing had an FPTAS with running time polynomial in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$, then by choosing $\epsilon$ small enough (specifically, smaller than $\tfrac{1}{n}$), the FPTAS could be used to obtain an **exact** polynomial-time algorithm, solving the strongly NP-hard problem in polynomial time — contradicting the $P \neq NP$ assumption. However, Bin Packing **does admit a PTAS** (de la Vega and Lueker, 1981), which runs in time $O(n \log n) + \text{poly}(\tfrac{1}{\epsilon})$ — polynomial in $n$ for any fixed $\epsilon$, even though the dependence on $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$ is super-polynomial. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, ESE Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** [7 Marks] Design an FPTAS for the **0/1 Knapsack Problem**. Provide the complete algorithm, derive the approximation guarantee, and analyze the running time. **(b)** [7 Marks] A manufacturing company has $n = 1000$ jobs with values $v_i$ and weights $w_i$ (knapsack capacity $W = 50000$). Design an FPTAS with $\epsilon = 0.05$ and state the resulting running time bound. Justify why this is **not** merely a PTAS but a **fully polynomial** scheme. #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) FPTAS Design for 0/1 Knapsack:** **Algorithm:** ``` FPTAS-KNAPSACK(I, epsilon): Input: n items with values v_i, weights w_i, capacity W, precision epsilon Output: subset S with total weight at most W and value at least (1 - epsilon) times OPT 1. V_max = sum of v_i for i = 1 to n 2. theta = (epsilon * V_max) / n 3. For i = 1 to n: v_hat_i = floor(v_i / theta) * theta 4. Run exact DP on (v_hat_i, w_i) with table size O(n * V_max / theta) 5. Return the optimal subset found ``` **Approximation Guarantee:** For any item $i$, the rounding error is: $$0 \leq v_i - \hat{v}_i < \theta$$ Summing over all $n$ items used in the optimal solution $\text{OPT}$: $$\text{OPT} - \hat{\text{OPT}} \leq n \cdot \theta = n \cdot \frac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n} = \epsilon \cdot V_{\max} \leq \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT}$$ (The last inequality holds because $\text{OPT} \leq V_{\max}$.) Therefore: $$\hat{\text{OPT}} \geq (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}$$ **Running Time:** $$T(n, \epsilon) = O\!\left(n \cdot \frac{V_{\max}}{\theta}\right) = O\!\left(n \cdot \frac{n}{\epsilon}\right) = O\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\epsilon}\right)$$ **(b) Numerical Application:** With $n = 1000$ and $\epsilon = 0.05$: $$T(1000, 0.05) = O\!\left(\frac{1000^2}{0.05}\right) = O(2 \times 10^7)$$ This is approximately **20 million operations** — extremely fast in practice. **Why FPTAS and not merely PTAS:** The running time bound $O\!\left(\tfrac{n^2}{\epsilon}\right)$ is polynomial in **both** $n$ and $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$ (it is linear in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$ and quadratic in $n$). This satisfies the strict definition of FPTAS, which is stronger than the PTAS requirement where the polynomial degree may depend on $\epsilon$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the theorem that strongly NP-hard problems do not admit FPTAS unless $P = NP$. Explain why the Knapsack problem (which is weakly NP-hard) **can** admit an FPTAS. **(b)** [7 Marks] Construct a PTAS for the **Makespan Minimization** problem on $m$ identical machines ($P \Vert C_{\max}$). Explain the role of *dual approximation* and *rounding* in the Hochbaum-Shmoys framework. #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Strong NP-Hardness vs. FPTAS — Theorem and Proof:** **Theorem:** Let $\Pi$ be a strongly NP-hard optimization problem. Then $\Pi$ does not admit a Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme unless $P = NP$. **Proof:** Assume for contradiction that $\Pi$ admits an FPTAS $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}$ with running time polynomial in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. Let $\Pi_I$ be an instance with all integer parameters bounded by a polynomial $p(n)$ in input size $n$. By the definition of strong NP-hardness, deciding whether $\Pi_I$ has a solution of value at most $k$ (or exactly $k$) is NP-complete. Set: $$\epsilon_I = \frac{1}{p(n) + 1}$$ Run $\mathcal{A}_{\epsilon_I}$ on instance $\Pi_I$. The running time is polynomial in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon_I} = p(n) + 1$, hence polynomial in $n$. Now, since all parameter values are bounded by $p(n)$, any solution value $V$ satisfies: $$V \leq n \cdot p(n)$$ Choosing $\epsilon_I$ small enough, the FPTAS returns a solution $\mathcal{A}_{\epsilon_I}(I)$ with: $$V^* \leq (1 + \epsilon_I) \cdot \text{OPT} \quad \text{and} \quad V^* \geq (1 - \epsilon_I) \cdot \text{OPT}$$ Since both $\text{OPT}$ and $V^*$ are integers bounded by $n \cdot p(n)$, and $\epsilon_I < \tfrac{1}{n \cdot p(n)}$, we have $\epsilon_I \cdot V^* < 1$, meaning $V^*$ and $\text{OPT}$ must be **equal** integers. Hence $\mathcal{A}_{\epsilon_I}$ solves $\Pi$ exactly in polynomial time, contradicting NP-hardness. $\blacksquare$ **Why Knapsack admits FPTAS:** The 0/1 Knapsack problem is **weakly** NP-hard — its decision version (does a solution of value at least $V$ exist?) is NP-complete only when $V$ is encoded in binary. The value parameter $V_{\max}$ can be exponential in $n$. This weak NP-hardness allows the pseudo-polynomial DP to be made polynomial via value scaling, yielding an FPTAS. **(b) PTAS for Makespan Minimization (Hochbaum-Shmoys, 1987):** **Dual Approximation Framework:** Instead of fixing the number of machines $m$ and minimizing the makespan, we **dualize**: fix a target makespan $T$ and ask: *is the optimum makespan at most $T$?* This binary search reduces to a decision problem. **Algorithm Outline:** 1. **Binary Search on $T$:** Find the smallest $T$ such that all jobs can be scheduled on $m$ machines within time $T$. 2. **Rounding:** Scale all processing times $p_i$ to integer values via: $$\hat{p}_i = \left\lfloor \frac{p_i \cdot m}{T} \right\rfloor$$ After rounding, all $\hat{p}_i$ are integers bounded by $m$. 3. **Configuration Enumeration:** The number of distinct machine *configurations* (multisets of rounded jobs fitting in $m$) is bounded by $O\!\left(m^{1/\epsilon}\right)$ for any $\epsilon > 0$. Enumerate all such configurations. 4. **Integer Programming:** Solve an integer program of polynomial size (variables = configurations, constraints = job coverage). By total unimodularity of the constraint matrix, the LP relaxation suffices. 5. **Approximation Guarantee:** The constructed schedule has makespan at most $(1+\epsilon) \cdot T$, hence $(1+\epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}$. 6. **Running Time:** Polynomial in $n$ for fixed $\epsilon$, but exponential in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing PTAS with FPTAS:** Students frequently state "the running time is polynomial in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$" for both. PTAS allows the degree to depend on $\epsilon$ — only FPTAS requires the time to be polynomial in $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. > 2. **Forgetting the factor $n$ in $\theta$:** The correct scaling is $\theta = \tfrac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n}$, not $\tfrac{\epsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{1}$. Missing the $n$ in the denominator is a classic 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Stating $V_{\max} \leq \text{OPT}$:** The reverse is true: $\text{OPT} \leq V_{\max}$ because the optimum cannot exceed the sum of all values. > 4. **Mixing up approximation ratio definitions:** For maximization, $\rho = \tfrac{\text{OPT}}{\mathcal{A}(I)} \leq 1+\epsilon$; for minimization, $\rho = \tfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\text{OPT}} \leq 1+\epsilon$. > 5. **Omitting the strong vs. weak NP-hardness distinction:** When asked "why no FPTAS for Bin Packing?", students often give vague answers. The examiner expects the explicit invocation of **strong NP-hardness** and the consequence via Vazirani's theorem. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **PTAS Definition:** A family $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}$ with $1+\epsilon$ approximation ratio and running time $n^{O(1)}$ for each fixed $\epsilon$. - ✅ **FPTAS:** Stronger — polynomial in **both** $n$ and $\tfrac{1}{\epsilon}$. - ✅ **Hierarchy:** $\text{FPTAS} \subset \text{PTAS} \subset \text{APX} \subset \text{NPO}$. - ✅ **Knapsack FPTAS:** Uses value rounding with $\theta = \tfrac{\epsilon V_{\max}}{n}$; running time $O\!\left(\tfrac{n^2}{\epsilon}\right)$; guarantee $(1-\epsilon)\text{OPT}$. - ✅ **Bin Packing PTAS:** Strongly NP-hard ⇒ no FPTAS unless $P=NP$; has PTAS with $O(n \log n) + \text{poly}(\tfrac{1}{\epsilon})$ time. - ✅ **Makespan PTAS:** Hochbaum-Shmoys dual approximation + configuration enumeration; $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ time. - ✅ **Euclidean TSP PTAS:** Arora's quadtree partitioning (1998) gives $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$. - ✅ **Subgraph of planar graphs PTAS:** Baker's technique (1994) — bounded layered neighborhoods. - ✅ **Vazirani's Barrier:** Strong NP-hardness $\Rightarrow$ no FPTAS (unless $P=NP$); PTAS may still exist. - ✅ **Trade-off Principle:** Smaller $\epsilon$ ⇒ better approximation ⇒ higher (still polynomial) running time. - ✅ **Engineering Relevance:** Vehicle routing, VLSI design, bioinformatics, scheduling, network optimization. - ✅ **Exam Trigger Words:** "Define PTAS," "Distinguish PTAS/FPTAS," "Strong vs. weak NP-hardness," "Why FPTAS for Knapsack?," "PTAS for Bin Packing."

topics.title: Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (FPTAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (FPTAS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS)** for an optimization problem $\Pi$ is an algorithm $\mathcal{A}(\cdot, \cdot)$ such that for every instance $I$ of $\Pi$ and for every parameter $\varepsilon \in (0, 1)$:

- $\mathcal{A}(I, \varepsilon)$ returns a feasible solution $S$ of value $\mathcal{A}(I, \varepsilon)$ that satisfies a **$(1 + \varepsilon)$-approximation guarantee**, i.e.

$$
\mathbb{R}_{\mathcal{A}}(I) \;\leq\; (1+\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}(I) \quad \text{(minimization)}
$$

$$
\mathrm{OPT}(I) \;\leq\; (1+\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{R}_{\mathcal{A}}(I) \quad \text{(maximization)}
$$

- The running time $T(n, \varepsilon)$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is **bounded by a polynomial in BOTH the input size $n$ AND $1/\varepsilon$**. Formally,

$$
T(n,\varepsilon) \;\in\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\mathrm{poly}\!\left(n, \tfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (verbatim class):**
> An FPTAS is a family of algorithms $\{{\mathcal{A}_\varepsilon}\}_{\varepsilon>0}$, each $\mathcal{A}_\varepsilon$ being a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation whose running time is polynomial in $n$ **and** in $1/\varepsilon$. This dual polynomial dependence is what makes the scheme *fully* polynomial.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **cab driver** in Kochi trying to compute the *shortest* tour covering 50 tourist spots (the Travelling Salesman Problem, which is NP-hard). The exact answer might take 10 years to compute.

- A **PTAS** is like asking a friend: *"Give me a tour within 10% of optimal, and you can think for one hour."* — he might need years if you ask for 0.1% accuracy, because his time scales *exponentially* in the precision requested.
- An **FPTAS** is like asking a friend: *"Give me a tour within 10% of optimal, and I will pay you ₹100 for every minute you think."* — the time to think grows only **linearly** (or polynomially) in $1/\varepsilon$, so getting 1% accuracy costs roughly $10\times$ more time, not $2^{100}\times$ more.

> [!NOTE] **Key Mental Model**
> The difference between PTAS and FPTAS lies in *how the running time depends on $\varepsilon$*:
>
> - **PTAS** $\;:\;$ time is $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ but may be exponential in $1/\varepsilon$ (e.g., $n \cdot 2^{1/\varepsilon}$).
> - **FPTAS** $\;:\;$ time is $\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\varepsilon)$ — a *single* polynomial in both.

## 1.3 The Hierarchy of Approximation Schemes

$$
\text{Exact Poly-Time} \;\;\subset\;\; \text{FPTAS} \;\;\subset\;\; \text{PTAS} \;\;\subset\;\; \text{APX}
$$

> [!TIP] **KTU Quick Recall Trick — "P–F–P–A":**
> **P**olynomial $\to$ **F**ully **P**olynomial $\to$ **A**pproximation **S**cheme, where the running time window **widens** as you move right. FPTAS is *strictly stronger* than PTAS, and PTAS is *strictly stronger* than APX (constant-factor approximation).

## 1.4 Why FPTAS Matters in Engineering Practice

In real-world engineering — logistics, VLSI design, network routing, bioinformatics — the input size $n$ is huge (millions of items), but engineers often accept a 5% or 10% gap from optimal in exchange for *predictable, scalable* runtimes. FPTAS provides exactly this trade-off: a **smooth, graceful degradation** of solution quality with time budget.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions that *Block* the Existence of FPTAS

Not every NP-hard problem admits an FPTAS. The classical impossibility result relies on polynomial-time reductions that **preserve approximation ratios**:

> [!WARNING] **Impossibility Criterion (Garey–Johnson style):**
> If a problem $\Pi$ is **APX-hard** (i.e., as hard to approximate as any constant-factor problem) and has a lower bound $\rho > 1$ such that achieving a $\rho$-approximation is NP-hard, then $\Pi$ admits **no FPTAS** unless $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$.

**Canonical FPTAS-Incompatible Problems:**

| Problem | Best known ratio | FPTAS exists? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bin Packing | $3/2$ (asymptotic) | **No** (unless P=NP) | APX-hard with $3/2-\delta$ inapproximability |
| Max 3-SAT | $7/8$ | **No** (unless P=NP) | Håstad's optimal inapproximability |
| Independent Set (general graphs) | $O(n/\log^2 n)$ | **No** | Håstad–Zuckerman |
| Minimum Vertex Cover (bounded degree) | $2 - o(1)$ | **No** | Dinur–Safra barrier |
| **0/1 Knapsack** | Exact in pseudo-poly | **Yes** | Classical FPTAS via profit scaling |
| **Subset Sum** | Exact in pseudo-poly | **Yes** | Classical FPTAS via sum scaling |
| **Makespan Scheduling ($P \Vert C_{\max}$, identical machines)** | $4/3$ | **Yes** | Horowitz–Sahni / Hochbaum–Shantikumar |
| **Knapsack-type problems** | — | **Yes** | Whenever pseudo-poly DP exists, FPTAS often follows |

## 2.2 The General Recipe for Building an FPTAS

Most FPTAS constructions follow the **"trimming / scaling + dynamic programming"** paradigm:

1. **Identify a pseudo-polynomial algorithm** $\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{pp}}$ whose runtime is polynomial in $n$ and a *numeric parameter* $P$ (e.g., $\sum p_i$).
2. **Introduce a scaling factor** $K = \varepsilon P / n$ (or $K = \varepsilon P_{\max}$).
3. **Round / scale** the numeric values: $\hat{p}_i = \lfloor p_i / K \rfloor$.
4. **Run** $\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{pp}}$ on the scaled instance — runtime is now polynomial in $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$.
5. **Prove the approximation guarantee** by showing that rounding each value by at most $K$ causes a total error of at most $nK \leq \varepsilon P \leq \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of items / input size | Integer, $\geq 1$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Approximation parameter | $(0,1)$, dimensionless |
| $1/\varepsilon$ | Inverse precision | "Pseudo-dimension" |
| $C$ | Knapsack capacity | Positive integer / weight unit |
| $p_i$ | Profit of item $i$ | Currency / utility |
| $w_i$ | Weight of item $i$ | Mass / size unit |
| $P = \sum p_i$ | Total profit bound | Currency |
| $P_{\max} = \max p_i$ | Maximum single profit | Currency |
| $K$ | Scaling factor $= \varepsilon P / n$ (or $\varepsilon P_{\max}$) | Currency per scaled-unit |
| $\hat p_i = \lfloor p_i / K \rfloor$ | Scaled profit | Integer, dimension-less |
| $\hat P = \sum \hat p_i \leq n^2 / \varepsilon$ | Scaled total profit | Integer |
| $M[j]$ | DP table — min weight achieving scaled profit $j$ | Weight |
| $T(n,\varepsilon)$ | FPTAS runtime | $\mathcal{O}(n^3 / \varepsilon)$ for Knapsack |
| $\mathcal{R}(I)$ | Algorithm's objective value | Same unit as OPT |
| $\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ | Positive integers | — |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Numerical Heuristic**
> Always ensure that the *rounding-induced error per item* (at most $K$) multiplied by *the number of items* (at most $n$) gives a **total error** of at most $\varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$. That is, the *magic inequality* to verify is:
>
> $$nK \;\leq\; \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of FPTAS

- **Cloud Resource Allocation** — packing jobs (Knapsack) into VM slots with provably near-optimal revenue.
- **Supply Chain Finance** — capital budgeting with budgeted capital, profit scaled from forecasts.
- **Bioinformatics** — Subset Sum / Sequence alignment scoring with bounded approximation in genome assembly.
- **VLSI Floorplanning** — assigning rectangular modules to bounded regions where the FPTAS guarantees a near-optimal area usage.
- **Network Packet Scheduling** — Makespan scheduling on parallel routers/machines.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical Construction: FPTAS for **0/1 Knapsack**

We will derive the classical FPTAS due to **Ibarra & Kim (1975)** and refined by **Lawler (1979)**, which is the textbook example universally required in KTU examinations.

### 3.1.1 Problem Restatement

**Instance.** $n$ items, each with profit $p_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ and weight $w_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$, and a knapsack capacity $C \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$.

**Goal.** Find $S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ maximizing $\sum_{i \in S} p_i$ subject to $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq C$.

The exact **pseudo-polynomial DP** runs in $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot P)$ time where $P = \sum_i p_i$. Since $P$ can be exponential in $n$, this is *not* polynomial in $n$. The FPTAS will bring the runtime down to polynomial in $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$.

### 3.1.2 Algorithm Steps

**Input.** Instance $(p_1,\ldots,p_n)$, $(w_1,\ldots,w_n)$, $C$, and $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the profit bound.**
$$
P \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

**Step 2 — Define the scaling factor.**
$$
K \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{\varepsilon P}{n} \right\rfloor \;+\; 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad K \;=\; \Theta\!\left(\dfrac{P}{n/\varepsilon}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why this choice of $K$?**
> $K$ is chosen so that *each rounded-down profit* loses at most $K$, and the *total loss over at most $n$ items* is at most $nK \leq \varepsilon P \leq \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.

**Step 3 — Scale and round every profit.**
$$
\hat p_i \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{p_i}{K} \right\rfloor \quad \text{for all } i = 1, \ldots, n
$$

This gives integer scaled profits. The maximum scaled profit satisfies
$$
\hat P \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \hat p_i \;\leq\; \frac{P}{K} \;\leq\; \frac{n}{\varepsilon} + n \;\in\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\tfrac{n}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Run the pseudo-polynomial DP on the scaled instance.**

Define a table $M[0 \ldots \hat P]$, all initialized to $+\infty$. Set $M[0] = 0$.

For each item $i = 1, \ldots, n$ (in arbitrary order), and for $j$ from $\hat P$ down to $\hat p_i$:

$$
M[j] \;=\; \min\!\left( M[j],\ M[j - \hat p_i] + w_i \right) \quad \text{provided the result} \leq C
$$

**Step 5 — Recover the answer.**
Find
$$
j^{\star} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\, j \;:\; M[j] \leq C \,\right\}
$$

The set of items achieving $j^{\star}$ is the FPTAS solution. Its original (unscaled) profit is at least $K \cdot j^{\star}$.

### 3.1.3 Runtime Analysis

The DP table has $\hat P + 1 = \mathcal{O}(n/\varepsilon)$ entries. Each entry is updated in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time for each of the $n$ items.

$$
T(n,\varepsilon) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(n \cdot \hat P\right) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( n \cdot \frac{n}{\varepsilon} \right) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{n^{2}}{\varepsilon} \right)
$$

**Lower-order step overhead** (table initialization, scan) is $\mathcal{O}(n/\varepsilon)$ which is dominated. The algorithm is **fully polynomial** in $(n, 1/\varepsilon)$. ✔

### 3.1.4 Correctness and Approximation Guarantee

Let $S^{\star} \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ be the **optimal set** for the original instance with profit $\mathrm{OPT} = \sum_{i \in S^{\star}} p_i$.

Let $\mathrm{OPT}' = j^{\star}_{\mathrm{scaled}}$ be the **optimal scaled profit** of the scaled instance, and let $S_{\mathrm{alg}}$ be the set returned by our algorithm on the scaled instance.

**Sub-claim 1 — The algorithm solves the scaled instance optimally.**

The DP is a textbook exact algorithm for 0/1 Knapsack (pseudo-polynomial version). Hence
$$
j^{\star} \;=\; \mathrm{OPT}'
$$
and the set $S_{\mathrm{alg}}$ is *optimal* for the scaled instance.

**Sub-claim 2 — Bounding the rounding loss per item.**

For every $i$,
$$
K \cdot \hat p_i \;\leq\; p_i \;<\; K \cdot (\hat p_i + 1) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p_i \;=\; K\hat p_i + \delta_i,\ \ 0 \leq \delta_i < K
$$

So each item's contribution drops by $\delta_i < K$ when scaled.

**Sub-claim 3 — Total scaled profit of $S^{\star}$ is close to $\mathrm{OPT}/K$.**

$$
\sum_{i \in S^{\star}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i \in S^{\star}} p_i - n \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{OPT}}{K} - n
$$

The inequality uses $|S^{\star}| \leq n$ and the per-item drop $\delta_i < K$.

**Sub-claim 4 — Final profit bound.**

By optimality of $S_{\mathrm{alg}}$ on the scaled instance:
$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \sum_{i \in S^{\star}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \frac{\mathrm{OPT}}{K} - n
$$

Multiplying by $K$ and using the per-item lower bound $K \hat p_i \leq p_i$ for the **left side** (note: we *recover* original profit from the algorithm's chosen set):

$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; K \cdot \sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \mathrm{OPT} - nK
$$

Now invoke the design choice of $K$:
$$
nK \;\leq\; n \cdot \frac{\varepsilon P}{n} + n \;\leq\; \varepsilon P + n
$$

For any **non-trivial** knapsack instance, $\mathrm{OPT} \geq P / 2$ (a single item can always be picked), so $P \leq 2\,\mathrm{OPT}$. Substituting:
$$
nK \;\leq\; 2\varepsilon\,\mathrm{OPT} + n
$$

For instances where $\mathrm{OPT} \geq n / (2\varepsilon)$ (a mild assumption — if $\mathrm{OPT} < n/(2\varepsilon)$ the absolute error is at most $n$ which is dominated), we get:

$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; \mathrm{OPT} - 2\varepsilon\,\mathrm{OPT} \;=\; (1 - 2\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}
$$

Replacing $\varepsilon$ by $\varepsilon/2$ in the algorithm specification gives a clean $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation. Formally, the algorithm with parameter $\varepsilon' = \varepsilon / 2$ achieves:

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; (1-\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT} \;\;\;\text{or equivalently}\;\;\; \mathrm{OPT} \;\leq\; \tfrac{1}{1-\varepsilon}\,\mathbb{R}_{\mathcal{A}} \;\leq\; (1+2\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{R}_{\mathcal{A}}\;}
$$

for the maximum-profit version, confirming a **$(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation**. ∎

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

Consider $n=3$, profits $(p_1,p_2,p_3) = (9, 11, 7)$, weights $(w_1,w_2,w_3) = (5, 7, 4)$, capacity $C = 10$, $\varepsilon = 0.4$.

**Step 1.** $P = 9+11+7 = 27$.

**Step 2.** $K = \lfloor 0.4 \cdot 27 / 3 \rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 3.6 \rfloor + 1 = 4$.

**Step 3.** Scaled profits $\hat p_1 = \lfloor 9/4 \rfloor = 2$, $\hat p_2 = \lfloor 11/4 \rfloor = 2$, $\hat p_3 = \lfloor 7/4 \rfloor = 1$.

**Step 4.** DP on scaled instance with $C=10$:

- After item 1 ($w=5$, $\hat p=2$): $M[2] = 5$.
- After item 2 ($w=7$, $\hat p=2$): $M[2] = \min(5,7) = 5$; $M[4] = 5+7 = 12$ (exceeds $C$, reject).
- After item 3 ($w=4$, $\hat p=1$): $M[1] = 4$; $M[3] = 4+5 = 9$ (item 1+3); $M[5] = 4+5 = 9$.

**Step 5.** Largest $j$ with $M[j] \leq 10$ is $j^{\star} = 5$ achieved by items $\{1,3\}$ with $w=9 \leq 10$.

**Recovered profit.** $\sum p_i = 9 + 7 = 16$.

**OPT (brute force).** Best is items $\{2,3\}$: weight $7+4=11$ — exceeds $C=10$. So OPT $= 9+7 = 16$ via items $\{1,3\}$. (The scaling happened to preserve optimality here.) The approximation ratio is $1.0$, well within $1+\varepsilon = 1.4$.

> [!NOTE] **Verification of the bound** $nK \leq \varepsilon P$:
> $nK = 3 \cdot 4 = 12$, $\varepsilon P = 0.4 \cdot 27 = 10.8$. Here $nK = 12 > 10.8$, *technically* violating the strict inequality. Using the *upper-bound* version of the analysis with $\varepsilon' = \varepsilon/2 = 0.2$ would give $K = \lfloor 0.2 \cdot 27 / 3 \rfloor + 1 = 2$, scaled profits $(4, 5, 3)$, and would safely guarantee $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation. KTU examiners **love** this pitfall — see §5.4.

## 3.2 FPTAS for the **Subset Sum** Problem

### 3.2.1 Problem Restatement

Given integers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and a target $T$, find $S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $\sum_{i \in S} a_i$ is as large as possible without exceeding $T$.

### 3.2.2 Algorithm (Sketch)

Let $A = \sum_i a_i$. Set $K = \lfloor \varepsilon A / n \rfloor + 1$. Round each $a_i$ to $\hat a_i = \lfloor a_i / K \rfloor$, and run the pseudo-polynomial DP on $(\hat a_1, \ldots, \hat a_n, T)$. Runtime: $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot A/K) = \mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$.

### 3.2.3 Approximation Proof (compact form)

For optimal set $S^{\star}$ with value $V^{\star}$:
$$
\sum_{i \in S^{\star}} \hat a_i \;\geq\; \frac{V^{\star}}{K} - n
$$

The DP finds $\hat V$ with $\hat V \geq \frac{V^{\star}}{K} - n$. The original sum returned is at least $K \hat V$ (since original values $\geq$ scaled values $\times K$). Hence:

$$
\mathbb{R}_{\mathcal{A}} \;\geq\; V^{\star} - nK \;\geq\; V^{\star} - \varepsilon A \;\geq\; (1-\varepsilon)\,V^{\star}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Schemes

| Property | Heuristic | APX | PTAS | FPTAS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Approximation ratio | Unbounded | Constant $\rho$ | $1+\varepsilon$ | $1+\varepsilon$ |
| Runtime dependence on $n$ | — | poly | poly | poly |
| Runtime dependence on $1/\varepsilon$ | — | — | **may be exponential** | **polynomial** |
| Tunable in practice | No | No | Poorly | **Yes** |
| Existence is conditional | — | For many NP-hard | For many NP-hard | **Rare — requires structure** |
| Knapsack example | Greedy | $1/2$ (greedy) | $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \cdot 2^{1/\varepsilon})$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$ |
| Bin Packing example | FFD | $11/9 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}+1$ | ? | **None** (APX-hard) |

## 3.4 The Karp–Luby Technique (for #P-style enumeration)

For problems where the exact algorithm enumerates solutions of *value* in a range (e.g., counting feasible assignments whose total weight lies in $[T, T+W]$):

- Replace weights by **rounded** weights with granularity $\delta = \varepsilon P / n$.
- Group consecutive rounded values into **buckets** of width $\delta$.
- Within each bucket, *any* one of the original values is at most $\delta$ away from the bucket's representative.
- Total error per solution $\leq n \delta = \varepsilon P \leq \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.

> [!TIP] **KTU Viva Cue**
> When asked *"Why does FPTAS exist for Knapsack but not Bin Packing?"*, the cleanest answer is:
> *Knapsack admits a pseudo-polynomial DP in the profit dimension, so rounding profits by $\Theta(\varepsilon P / n)$ yields a polynomial table. Bin Packing's pseudo-poly algorithms are exponential in $1/\varepsilon$, and the problem is APX-hard beyond factor $3/2$ — hence no FPTAS unless P=NP.*

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: FPTAS Pipeline for 0/1 Knapsack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Input n items, profits p_i, weights w_i, capacity C, epsilon]) --> A1[Compute P = sum of all p_i]
    A1 --> A2[Set K = floor of epsilon times P over n plus 1]
    A2 --> A3[For each i compute scaled profit pHat_i = floor of p_i divided by K]
    A3 --> A4[Initialize DP table M of size n divided by epsilon plus n plus 1 entries to infinity]
    A4 --> A5[Set M of 0 equals 0]
    A5 --> A6{For i = 1 to n}
    A6 --> A7[For j from pHat_i down to 0]
    A7 --> A8[Update M of j as min of M of j and M of j minus pHat_i plus w_i]
    A8 --> A9[Reject update if result exceeds capacity C]
    A9 --> A10[Increment j loop]
    A10 --> A7
    A6 --> A11[Increment i loop]
    A11 --> A6
    A6 --> A12[Find jStar = max j such that M of j is at most C]
    A12 --> A13[Trace back item set S achieving jStar]
    A13 --> A14[Output original profit sum of p_i over S as answer]
    A14 --> A15([End: Return profit and item set])
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Approximation Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Exact Polynomial Time] --> N1[FPTAS]
    N1 --> N2[PTAS]
    N2 --> N3[APX Constant Factor]
    N3 --> N4[Unbounded Heuristic]
    N1 -.gap.- G1[PTAS exists but FPTAS may not]
    N2 -.gap.- G2[APX hard problems have no PTAS]
    N3 -.gap.- G3[Even heuristics may be far from OPT]
```

## 4.3 DP Table Snapshot (Visualizing FPTAS for Knapsack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Row i=0: only M of 0 equals 0] --> S1[Row i=1 item with pHat and w]
    S1 --> S2[Row i=2 item with pHat and w]
    S2 --> S3[Row i=3 item with pHat and w]
    S3 --> S4[Row i=n done]
    S4 --> S5[Scan rightmost column for max j with M of j at most C]
    S5 --> S6[Output jStar and corresponding items]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — When to Use FPTAS vs PTAS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the problem NP hard?} --> Q2{Does a pseudo polynomial DP exist?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Can numeric values be scaled by K = epsilon times P over n?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Use FPTAS via rounding plus DP]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5[Consider other techniques: random sampling, LP rounding, dual fitting]
    Q2 -- No --> Q6{Is exact polynomial time possible?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use exact algorithm]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Use heuristic or APX algorithm]
    Q1 -- No --> Q7
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS). Distinguish it from a PTAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **FPTAS** for an optimization problem is an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ that, for every instance $I$ of size $n$ and every $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$, produces a solution whose value is within a factor of $(1+\varepsilon)$ of the optimum, in time bounded by a polynomial in **both** $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$.

The distinction from a PTAS is purely in the dependence of the running time on $\varepsilon$:

- **PTAS** has running time polynomial in $n$ but *may be exponential* in $1/\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\mathcal{O}(n^{1/\varepsilon})$ or $\mathcal{O}(2^{1/\varepsilon}\, n)$).
- **FPTAS** has running time polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$).

Thus FPTAS is a **strictly stronger** notion than PTAS: every FPTAS is also a PTAS, but not vice-versa. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for PTAS, 1 for distinction.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. State any two NP-hard problems that admit an FPTAS. Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **0/1 Knapsack** — admits an FPTAS because the exact pseudo-polynomial DP runs in time $\mathcal{O}(nP)$ where $P$ is the total profit, and scaling each $p_i$ by a factor $K = \varepsilon P / n$ reduces the effective range to $\mathcal{O}(n/\varepsilon)$, yielding an $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$ algorithm.
2. **Subset Sum** — admits an FPTAS using the same scaling trick on the input integers; the DP works in time $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$.

*(Any two from {Knapsack, Subset Sum, Makespan Scheduling on identical machines, Shortest Path in DAGs with bounded weights} are acceptable.)* **(3 marks: 1 per problem + 1 for justification.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Describe the FPTAS for the 0/1 Knapsack problem. State the algorithm precisely and analyse its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the algorithm in part (a) achieves a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation for any $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Algorithm & Complexity (7 marks)**

**Algorithm** (FPTAS-Knapsack):

1. Let $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ be the total profit.  
2. Choose scaling factor $K = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\varepsilon P}{n} \right\rfloor + 1$.  
3. For each item $i$, compute the **scaled profit** $\hat p_i = \left\lfloor \dfrac{p_i}{K} \right\rfloor$.  
4. Run the standard pseudo-polynomial DP for 0/1 Knapsack on the instance with profits $\hat p_i$, weights $w_i$, and capacity $C$. The DP table $M[0 \ldots \hat P]$ is filled as:  

$$
M[j] \;=\; \min\!\left( M[j],\ M[j - \hat p_i] + w_i \right) \quad \text{for } j \geq \hat p_i
$$

5. The output is the set achieving $j^{\star} = \max\{j : M[j] \leq C\}$.

**Complexity analysis** (3 sub-marks):

- Size of the table: $\hat P + 1 = \mathcal{O}\!\left( n / \varepsilon \right)$ entries.  
- Total table updates: $n \times \hat P = \mathcal{O}\!\left( n^2 / \varepsilon \right)$.  
- Each update is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.  
- Therefore $T(n,\varepsilon) = \mathcal{O}\!\left( n^2 / \varepsilon \right)$, which is polynomial in **both** $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$. ✔  
- Space: $\mathcal{O}\!\left( n / \varepsilon \right)$ for the DP table.  

**(7 marks: 2 algorithm, 1 scaling, 2 DP recurrence, 2 runtime analysis.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Approximation Proof (7 marks)**

Let $S^{\star}$ be an optimal set for the original instance, with value $\mathrm{OPT} = \sum_{i \in S^{\star}} p_i$. Let $S_{\mathrm{alg}}$ be the set returned by our algorithm.

**Step 1 — Bounding rounding error per item.** For every $i$:
$$
K \hat p_i \;\leq\; p_i \;<\; K(\hat p_i + 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Scaled value of $S^{\star}$.** Summing over $S^{\star}$:
$$
\sum_{i \in S^{\star}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i \in S^{\star}} p_i - |S^{\star}| \;\geq\; \frac{\mathrm{OPT}}{K} - n
$$

**Step 3 — Algorithm's scaled value.** Since $S_{\mathrm{alg}}$ is optimal for the scaled instance:
$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \sum_{i \in S^{\star}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \frac{\mathrm{OPT}}{K} - n
$$

**Step 4 — Recovering the original value.** Using $p_i \geq K \hat p_i$:
$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; K \cdot \sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} \hat p_i \;\geq\; \mathrm{OPT} - nK
$$

**Step 5 — Using the choice of $K$.** By construction, $K \leq \varepsilon P / n + 1$. For non-trivial instances, $P \leq 2\,\mathrm{OPT}$, hence:
$$
nK \;\leq\; \varepsilon P + n \;\leq\; 2\varepsilon\,\mathrm{OPT} + n
$$

For instances with $\mathrm{OPT} \geq n / (2\varepsilon)$:
$$
\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; (1 - 2\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}
$$

**Step 6 — Final formulation.** Running the algorithm with parameter $\varepsilon' = \varepsilon/2$ yields:
$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{i \in S_{\mathrm{alg}}} p_i \;\geq\; (1 - \varepsilon)\,\mathrm{OPT}\;}
$$

which is the definition of a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation for the maximization version. ∎

**(7 marks: 1 per Step 1–5, 2 for the final synthesis.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the concept of an FPTAS. Show that the **Subset Sum** problem admits an FPTAS. State its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Give an example of a problem that admits a PTAS but **not** an FPTAS. Justify your answer using complexity-theoretic arguments. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — FPTAS for Subset Sum (7 marks)**

**Subset Sum Problem.** Given integers $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and target $T$, find $S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ maximizing $V(S) = \sum_{i \in S} a_i$ subject to $V(S) \leq T$.

**Algorithm:**

1. Let $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$.  
2. Choose scaling factor $K = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{n} \right\rfloor + 1$.  
3. Round each $a_i$ to $\hat a_i = \lfloor a_i / K \rfloor$.  
4. Run pseudo-poly DP: table $M[0 \ldots \hat A]$, where $M[j] = $ largest subset-sum equal to $j$ achievable. Recurrence:  

$$
M[j] = \max\!\left(M[j],\ j\right) \quad \text{if } M[j - \hat a_i] + \hat a_i = j
$$

Equivalently, the standard knapsack-style DP with $p_i = a_i$, $w_i = a_i$ (each "item" weighs its own profit).  

5. Find $j^{\star} = \max\{j \leq T/K : M[j] \text{ feasible}\}$.  

**Time Complexity:** $T(n,\varepsilon) = \mathcal{O}\!\left( n \cdot \hat A \right) = \mathcal{O}\!\left( n^2 / \varepsilon \right)$, fully polynomial.

**Approximation proof** (concise): identical to Knapsack — the rounding loss per item is at most $K$, total loss is at most $nK \leq \varepsilon A \leq \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.

**(7 marks: 1.5 problem statement, 2 algorithm, 1 scaling, 1 DP, 1.5 complexity.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — PTAS without FPTAS (7 marks)**

**Classical example:** The **Bin Packing** problem admits a PTAS (de la Vega–Lueker, 1981) running in time $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot (1/\varepsilon) \log(1/\varepsilon) \cdot \log n)$ — wait, this is *already* an FPTAS! 

**Better example:** **Bin Packing** is APX-hard — there is no polynomial-time algorithm achieving ratio $3/2 - \delta$ unless $\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{NP}$. Therefore **Bin Packing has NO PTAS** (and a fortiori no FPTAS).

**Refined example — Independent Set on planar graphs:**  
- Admits a PTAS (Baker's technique, 1983) with time $\mathcal{O}(2^{1/\varepsilon} \cdot n)$.  
- It is open whether FPTAS exists; current best is PTAS, *not* FPTAS.

**Cleaner example — Maximum Independent Set on general graphs:**  
- The problem is APX-hard with lower bound $n^{1-\varepsilon}$ on approximation.  
- There exists **no PTAS** at all unless P = NP.  
- Hence trivially no FPTAS.

**Best KTU-friendly example:** **The Bandwidth Problem** or **k-Center with $k$ part of the input**: admits a PTAS (by Arora's technique) but no FPTAS known (open question) — and explaining *why* is itself worth 2 marks.

**Justification sketch (3 marks):**  
A problem that admits a PTAS but no FPTAS must be such that any $\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\varepsilon)$-time algorithm would imply $\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{NP}$. This typically happens for problems that are APX-hard with a *non-constant* lower bound (e.g., $\Omega(\log n)$ or $\Omega(n^\delta)$). Bin Packing is the canonical case: it is NP-hard to approximate within factor $3/2 - \delta$, which immediately rules out FPTAS. The PTAS construction is non-trivial and uses linear grouping.

**(7 marks: 2 problem choice, 2 for PTAS existence, 3 for FPTAS impossibility with reduction-based argument.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 3, FPTAS topic)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to prove feasibility of the returned solution.** The DP must respect the capacity $C$; many students forget to add the feasibility check `if (M[j - pHat_i] + w_i <= C)` and lose **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Wrong choice of $K$.** Students often use $K = \varepsilon P / n$ without the `+ 1` floor, leading to a possible $K = 0$ collapse. Always state $K = \lfloor \varepsilon P / n \rfloor + 1$. Examiner penalizes **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing the approximation direction.** For **maximization** (Knapsack), the algorithm's value is *below* OPT. For **minimization** (Makespan), the algorithm's value is *above* OPT. Mixing these loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Failing to bound the rounding error per item correctly.** The bound $p_i \geq K \hat p_i$ must be stated explicitly, not just "rounding is small." Examiner reads the proof line-by-line. **2 marks at stake**.
> 5. **Claiming FPTAS for Bin Packing or Max 3-SAT.** This is the most common conceptual error. Always cross-check APX-hardness before claiming FPTAS. **Up to 3 marks lost** in Part B.
> 6. **Miscounting the runtime as $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot P)$** instead of $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot \hat P) = \mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$. The whole point of FPTAS is that the *scaled* range is polynomial in $1/\varepsilon$. Examiner deducts **2 marks** for missing this.
> 7. **Ignoring the $nK \leq \varepsilon P$ magic inequality.** This is the linchpin of the proof. Omitting it = no proof = **3 marks lost**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **Rapid Revision Checklist — FPTAS (Module 3)**

- **FPTAS** = $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation, runtime $\in \mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\varepsilon)$.
- **PTAS** = $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation, runtime $\in \mathrm{poly}(n)$ but may be $\exp(1/\varepsilon)$.
- **APX** = constant-factor approximation; contains problems with *no* PTAS.
- **Hierarchy:** Exact $\subset$ FPTAS $\subset$ PTAS $\subset$ APX $\subset$ Heuristics.
- **Knapsack FPTAS:**
  - Scale $p_i$ by $K = \lfloor \varepsilon P / n \rfloor + 1$.
  - Round: $\hat p_i = \lfloor p_i / K \rfloor$.
  - Run pseudo-poly DP in time $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$.
  - Returned profit $\geq (1-\varepsilon) \mathrm{OPT}$.
- **Subset Sum FPTAS:** identical technique, same $\mathcal{O}(n^2/\varepsilon)$ bound.
- **Magic inequality:** $nK \leq \varepsilon P \leq \varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ (this is the *only* inequality that matters for the proof).
- **Impossibility:** Bin Packing, Max 3-SAT, Independent Set on general graphs — **no FPTAS** unless P = NP.
- **Planar Independent Set:** has a PTAS (Baker 1983), open whether FPTAS exists.
- **Standard construction technique:** "trimming / scaling + DP", also called the **Ibarra–Kim / Lawler** technique.
- **Caveat on small OPT:** when $\mathrm{OPT} < n/(2\varepsilon)$, the absolute error is at most $n$, but the analysis must be stated with the $\varepsilon/2$ substitution to be rigorous.
- **Memory usage** of Knapsack FPTAS: $\mathcal{O}(n/\varepsilon)$, which is **fully polynomial in space** as well.
- **Karp–Luby trick:** for FPTAS of counting/enumeration problems, round by $\delta$ and group into buckets.
- **Real-world analogies:** cloud resource allocation (Knapsack), packet scheduling (Makespan FPTAS), genome assembly (Subset Sum).
- **Examiner's mantra:** *"The proof of an FPTAS is only as strong as the rounding-error bound."* Always write $p_i \geq K \hat p_i$ and $p_i < K(\hat p_i + 1)$ explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples: Knapsack Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples: Knapsack Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Knapsack Problem** is one of the canonical **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems** in combinatorial optimization and computer science. Given a set of $n$ items, each with a weight $w_i$ and a value (profit) $v_i$, and a knapsack of capacity $W$, the goal is to select a subset of items $S \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ that maximizes the total value while keeping the total weight within the capacity.

$$
\max_{S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}} \sum_{i \in S} v_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq W
$$

The problem appears in **two canonical forms** in KTU-style examinations:

| Variant | Statement |
|---|---|
| **Optimization (Knapsack)** | Find a subset $S$ with maximum total value such that total weight $\leq W$. |
| **Decision (Knapsack)** | Given an extra target $V$, does there exist a subset $S$ with total weight $\leq W$ and total value $\geq V$? |

The **0/1 Knapsack Problem** allows each item to be taken either entirely or not at all. The **Fractional Knapsack Problem** permits splitting items into fractions. The fractional variant admits an exact greedy solution; the 0/1 variant is NP-hard and is the focus of approximation algorithm design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 0/1 Knapsack problem is NP-hard (its decision version is NP-complete). Exact algorithms run in pseudo-polynomial time using Dynamic Programming, while polynomial-time algorithms are forced to be **approximate**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **treasure hunter with a single backpack** of limited carrying capacity before a long expedition. The hunter has access to $n$ valuable items (gold bars, gems, artifacts), each with a known weight and market value. The hunter cannot take everything, so they must choose items that **maximize total value while keeping the total weight within the backpack's capacity**.

Now imagine the items have **highly irregular shapes** — gold is heavy but valuable, feathers are light but cheap. A natural greedy heuristic is to **fill the bag with items having the highest value-to-weight ratio** (like picking gold coins over bricks). However, this greedy choice does **not always produce the optimal set**, because a slightly less ratio-efficient item might combine beautifully with other items. Hence, designing approximation algorithms with **provable worst-case guarantees** becomes both non-trivial and pedagogically rich.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Engineering Metrics:** We denote $n$ as the number of items, $W$ as the maximum allowable weight (capacity), $v_i$ as value (profit, often in monetary units), $w_i$ as weight (in kg, lb, or grams). The optimal value is denoted by $\text{OPT}$, and the value returned by an approximation algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ on input $I$ is denoted by $\mathcal{A}(I)$.

---

## 1.3 Why Approximation?

Since 0/1 Knapsack is **NP-hard**, no polynomial-time algorithm can compute $\text{OPT}$ for all instances (assuming $P \neq NP$). Three response strategies exist:

1. **Heuristics without guarantees** — fast but unreliable.
2. **Approximation algorithms** — polynomial-time with a provable ratio $\rho$.
3. **Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (FPTAS)** — for any $\epsilon > 0$, returns a $(1-\epsilon)$-approximation in time polynomial in both $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

The Knapsack problem is the **textbook example** where an FPTAS exists, illustrating the conceptual gap between NP-hard problems (no exact poly-time) and FPTAS-friendly problems (arbitrarily good poly-time approximations).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Greedy Heuristics — Operational Logic

### Greedy-by-Ratio Algorithm
The simplest heuristic sorts items in **non-increasing order of value-to-weight ratio** $v_i / w_i$ and inserts each item into the knapsack if it still fits.

$$
\frac{v_1}{w_1} \geq \frac{v_2}{w_2} \geq \cdots \geq \frac{v_n}{w_n}
$$

**Approximation Ratio:** This algorithm achieves a worst-case ratio of **1/2**, i.e., $\text{GREEDY}(I) \geq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$.

### Combined Heuristic (Stronger Bound)
A stronger simple bound is obtained by taking the best of three candidate solutions:
1. $S_1$ = output of Greedy-by-Ratio.
2. $S_2$ = output of Greedy-by-Value (fill with highest-value items first).
3. $S_3$ = a **single item** with the largest value $v_i \leq W$.

The best of these three achieves a 1/2-approximation in the worst case, and even better guarantees in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1/2 is tight for greedy:** Consider two items: Item A has $(w_A, v_A) = (1, 1)$, Item B has $(w_B, v_B) = (1, 1)$. Capacity $W = 1$. Greedy picks A or B with value 1, while OPT = 1. But with $(w_A, v_A) = (1, 2), (w_B, v_B) = (1, 1+\epsilon)$, capacity $W = 1$: Greedy picks A (ratio 2), giving value 2; OPT also picks A and gets 2. Ratio 1. The tight case arises when greedy must leave one valuable item behind because of weight.

## 2.2 The Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS)

A **PTAS** is an algorithm family $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}_{\epsilon > 0}$ such that for every fixed $\epsilon > 0$:

- $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ runs in time polynomial in $n$ (but may depend arbitrarily on $1/\epsilon$).
- $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon(I) \geq (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$ for every instance $I$.

A **Fully PTAS (FPTAS)** additionally requires the running time to be polynomial in $1/\epsilon$.

The 0/1 Knapsack admits an FPTAS. The construction relies on the **value-scaling (rounding) technique**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Optimization Problem | $\max \sum_{i \in S} v_i$ s.t. $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq W$ | Formal problem statement |
| Value-to-weight ratio | $\rho_i = v_i / w_i$ | Used in greedy sorting |
| Greedy Ratio Bound | $\text{GREEDY} \geq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Worst-case guarantee |
| Scaling parameter | $\theta = \dfrac{\epsilon \cdot v_{\max}}{n}$ | Quantization step for FPTAS |
| Scaled value | $v_i' = \left\lfloor \dfrac{v_i}{\theta} \right\rfloor$ | Truncation to multiples of $\theta$ |
| New value range | $V' = \sum v_i' \leq \dfrac{n^2}{\epsilon}$ | Bounded DP table size |
| FPTAS Approximation | $\mathcal{A}(I) \geq (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$ | Final guarantee |
| FPTAS Running Time | $O\!\left(n^2 \cdot \dfrac{n}{\epsilon}\right) = O\!\left(\dfrac{n^3}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$ |
| DP Recurrence | $S[i, v] = \min(S[i-1, v], \, S[i-1, v - v_i] + w_i)$ | Exact pseudo-poly solver |
| DP Complexity | $O(n \cdot V)$ | Where $V = \sum v_i$ |
| Approximation Ratio | $\rho = \max_I \dfrac{\text{OPT}(I)}{\mathcal{A}(I)}$ | Definition of ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The bound $O(n^2/\epsilon)$ on $V'$ arises because after scaling by $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$, each $v_i' \leq n/\epsilon$, so the total $\sum v_i' \leq n^2/\epsilon$. Do not confuse this with $n/\epsilon$ (which is a per-item bound).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Knapsack Approximation

- **Resource Allocation:** Budget-constrained project selection in operations research.
- **Cutting Stock / Cargo Loading:** Loading containers with maximum-revenue items.
- **Cryptography:** Subset-sum-based cryptosystems use knapsack hardness.
- **Bioinformatics:** Selecting high-scoring DNA subsequences under length constraints.
- **Cloud Computing:** Virtual machine placement under memory/CPU constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exact Solution via Dynamic Programming (Pseudo-Polynomial Baseline)

Define $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$. Construct a table $S[i, v]$ representing the **minimum weight** achievable using a subset of the first $i$ items with **exact total value** $v$.

**Base cases:**

$$
S[0, 0] = 0, \qquad S[0, v] = +\infty \quad \text{for } v > 0
$$

**Recurrence** (item $i$ either excluded or included):

$$
S[i, v] = \min\!\left(S[i-1, v], \, S[i-1, v - v_i] + w_i\right), \quad \text{if } v_i \leq v
$$

$$
S[i, v] = S[i-1, v], \quad \text{if } v_i > v
$$

**Recovery:** The optimal value is $\text{OPT} = \max\{v : S[n, v] \leq W\}$.

**Time complexity:** $O(n \cdot V)$. Since $V$ may be exponential in $n$ (when values are encoded in binary), this is **pseudo-polynomial** and not polynomial in the input size.

## 3.2 Greedy-by-Ratio Algorithm — Step-by-Step Trace

Let $n = 4$, $W = 5$, items $(w_i, v_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Item 1:} &\quad (w_1, v_1) = (2, 8) \\
\text{Item 2:} &\quad (w_2, v_2) = (3, 9) \\
\text{Item 3:} &\quad (w_3, v_3) = (4, 11) \\
\text{Item 4:} &\quad (w_4, v_4) = (5, 13)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute ratios.**

$$
\rho_1 = 4.0, \quad \rho_2 = 3.0, \quad \rho_3 = 2.75, \quad \rho_4 = 2.6
$$

**Step 2: Sort by ratio (already sorted):** $1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 3: Greedy insert.**
- Pick Item 1: weight $= 2 \leq 5$. Running weight $= 2$.
- Pick Item 2: weight $= 3 \leq 5 - 2 = 3$. Running weight $= 5$.
- Skip Item 3 (would exceed $W$).
- Skip Item 4 (would exceed $W$).

**Output:** $S_{\text{greedy}} = \{1, 2\}$, total value $= 17$, total weight $= 5$.

**OPT check:** Items $\{1, 3\}$ give value $8 + 11 = 19$, weight $6 > 5$ (infeasible). Items $\{2, 4\}$ give $9 + 13 = 22$ with weight $8 > 5$. Items $\{3\}$ alone: value 11. Items $\{1, 2\}$: value 17. Items $\{4\}$: value 13, weight 5. So $\text{OPT} = 17$. Ratio $= 1$.

**Worst-case construction:** To force ratio $\to 1/2$, consider $(w_1, v_1) = (1, 1)$, $(w_2, v_2) = (W, W)$, capacity $W$. Greedy picks item 1 (ratio 1 vs item 2's ratio 1), then cannot pick item 2, getting value 1. $\text{OPT} = W$. Ratio $\to 1/2$ as $W \to \infty$ (relative comparison: $\mathcal{A} = 1, \text{OPT} = W \gg 1$).

## 3.3 FPTAS via Value Scaling — Full Derivation

**Goal:** Given $\epsilon > 0$, construct $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ such that $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon(I) \geq (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$.

### Step 1 — Establish an Upper Bound on OPT

Since $\text{OPT} \leq n \cdot v_{\max}$ (we cannot take more than $n$ items, each of value at most $v_{\max}$):

$$
\text{OPT} \leq n \cdot v_{\max}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Scaling Parameter

Choose

$$
\theta = \frac{\epsilon \cdot v_{\max}}{n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this choice?** We want the rounding error per item to be at most $\theta$. Since at most $n$ items contribute to OPT, the cumulative error is at most $n \theta = \epsilon \cdot v_{\max} \geq \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT}$.

### Step 3 — Scale and Truncate

For each item $i$, define the **scaled value**:

$$
v_i' = \left\lfloor \frac{v_i}{\theta} \right\rfloor
$$

This produces a scaled instance where each value is an integer.

### Step 4 — Solve Exactly via DP on the Scaled Instance

The new total value is bounded:

$$
V' = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i' \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{v_i}{\theta} \leq \frac{n \cdot v_{\max}}{\theta} = \frac{n \cdot v_{\max}}{\epsilon \cdot v_{\max} / n} = \frac{n^2}{\epsilon}
$$

DP complexity becomes:

$$
O(n \cdot V') = O\!\left(\frac{n^3}{\epsilon}\right)
$$

This is **polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$**.

### Step 5 — Convert Back and Bound the Loss

Let $S^*$ be the optimal subset for the scaled instance, and $S_{\text{opt}}$ be the true optimal subset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i \in S^*} v_i &\geq \theta \cdot \sum_{i \in S^*} v_i' \\
&\geq \theta \cdot \sum_{i \in S_{\text{opt}}} v_i' \quad \text{(since $S^*$ is optimal for scaled instance)} \\
&\geq \theta \cdot \sum_{i \in S_{\text{opt}}} \left(\frac{v_i}{\theta} - 1\right) \quad \text{(since } v_i' \geq v_i/\theta - 1\text{)} \\
&= \sum_{i \in S_{\text{opt}}} v_i - \theta \cdot \vert S_{\text{opt}} \vert \\
&\geq \text{OPT} - n \theta \\
&= \text{OPT} - \epsilon \cdot v_{\max} \\
&\geq \text{OPT} - \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT} \\
&= (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the algorithm is a $(1-\epsilon)$-approximation.

### Step 6 — Complexity Summary

$$
T(n, \epsilon) = O\!\left(\frac{n^3}{\epsilon}\right)
$$

Polynomial in $n$ **and** in $1/\epsilon$, confirming **FPTAS** status.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Time | Approximation Ratio | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Greedy-by-Ratio | $O(n \log n)$ | $\geq 1/2$ | Constant-factor |
| Greedy-by-Value | $O(n \log n)$ | $\geq 1/2$ (combined with others) | Constant-factor |
| Best Single Item | $O(n)$ | $\geq 1/2$ (combined) | Constant-factor |
| DP (Exact) | $O(nV)$ | $1$ (exact) | Pseudo-polynomial |
| FPTAS | $O(n^3/\epsilon)$ | $(1-\epsilon)$ | FPTAS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FPTAS Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input items, capacity W, epsilon] --> B[Compute vmax = max value]
    B --> C[Set theta = epsilon * vmax / n]
    C --> D[For each item i: compute vi_prime = floor vi / theta]
    D --> E[Construct DP table S on scaled values]
    E --> F[Find best scaled subset S_star with weight <= W]
    F --> G[Compute true total value of S_star]
    G --> H[Return S_star as approximate solution]
    H --> I[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"]
        P1[Items: weights w_i and values v_i]
        P2[Capacity W]
        P3[Accuracy parameter epsilon]
    end

    subgraph SCALING_MODULE["SCALING MODULE"]
        M1[Compute v_max]
        M2[Set theta = epsilon times v_max divided by n]
        M3[Truncate: v_i_prime equals floor of v_i divided by theta]
    end

    subgraph DP_ENGINE["DP ENGINE"]
        D1[Initialize table S of size n times V prime]
        D2[Apply recurrence for each item and value]
        D3[Track back-pointers for subset recovery]
    end

    subgraph RECOVERY_LAYER["RECOVERY LAYER"]
        R1[Reconstruct optimal scaled subset S_star]
        R2[Compute true total value of S_star]
        R3[Verify weight constraint satisfied]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Emit S_star as epsilon approximation]
        O2[Emit certificate of value >= 1 minus epsilon times OPT]
    end

    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M3
    P3 --> M2
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    P1 --> M3
    M3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    P2 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> O1
    R3 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Greedy-by-Ratio Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive n items and capacity W] --> B[Compute ratio r_i = v_i divided by w_i for each i]
    B --> C[Sort items in non-increasing order of r_i]
    C --> D[Initialize current weight = 0, solution set = empty]
    D --> E{Take next item i}
    E --> F{w_i plus current weight less than or equal to W}
    F -->|Yes| G[Add item i to solution set]
    G --> H[Update current weight]
    H --> I{More items remaining?}
    F -->|No| I
    I -->|Yes| E
    I -->|No| J[Output solution set as Greedy approximation]
    J --> K[End]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the 0/1 Knapsack Problem **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
The 0/1 Knapsack problem is a combinatorial optimization problem in which we are given $n$ items, each with a weight $w_i > 0$ and value $v_i > 0$, and a knapsack with capacity $W > 0$. The objective is to choose a subset $S \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ that maximizes $\sum_{i \in S} v_i$ subject to $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq W$. Each item is taken entirely or not at all (the "0/1" choice). The decision version is NP-complete, while the optimization version is NP-hard.

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between PTAS and FPTAS? **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PTAS | FPTAS |
|---|---|---|
| Approximation ratio | $(1 - \epsilon)$ for any fixed $\epsilon > 0$ | $(1 - \epsilon)$ for any $\epsilon > 0$ |
| Running time | Polynomial in $n$ for each fixed $\epsilon$ | Polynomial in **both** $n$ and $1/\epsilon$ |
| Example | Planar graph independent set PTAS | 0/1 Knapsack FPTAS |

An FPTAS is strictly stronger than a PTAS.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Design a greedy algorithm for the 0/1 Knapsack problem that achieves a $1/2$-approximation. Prove its approximation ratio formally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct an FPTAS for the 0/1 Knapsack problem using value scaling. State and prove its approximation guarantee and analyze its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Algorithm (Greedy-by-Ratio):**

1. For each item $i$, compute $\rho_i = v_i / w_i$.
2. Sort items so that $\rho_1 \geq \rho_2 \geq \cdots \geq \rho_n$.
3. Iterate items in sorted order; add item $i$ to $S$ if $\sum_{j \in S \cup \{i\}} w_j \leq W$.

**Approximation Proof:**

Let $S^*$ be the optimal subset and $S$ the greedy subset. Consider the iteration in which the greedy algorithm stopped (could not add the next item). Let $i^*$ be the first item (in sorted order) that the greedy algorithm excluded because it would have violated capacity.

Split $S^*$ into $S^* \cap S$ (items also picked by greedy) and $S^* \setminus S$ (items not picked by greedy, all with index $\geq i^*$). The items in $S^* \setminus S$ have ratio $\leq \rho_{i^*}$. The greedy set $S$ has weight $\geq W - w_{i^*}$, so:

$$
\sum_{j \in S} v_j \geq \rho_{i^*} \cdot (W - w_{i^*})
$$

Adding the value of $i^*$:

$$
\sum_{j \in S} v_j + v_{i^*} \geq \rho_{i^*} \cdot W \geq \sum_{j \in S^*} v_j
$$

Hence $\max\!\left(\sum_{j \in S} v_j, \, v_{\max}\right) \geq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$, giving the $\geq 1/2$ guarantee.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Algorithm:**

1. Let $v_{\max} = \max_i v_i$.
2. Set $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max} / n$.
3. For each item, set $v_i' = \lfloor v_i / \theta \rfloor$.
4. Run the exact DP on scaled values $v_i'$ with capacity $W$.
5. Output the corresponding subset $S^*$ of original items.

**Approximation Guarantee:**

Let $\text{OPT}$ be the true optimum, and let $S'$ be the optimal subset for the scaled instance. Then:

$$
\sum_{i \in S'} v_i \geq \theta \sum_{i \in S'} v_i' \geq \theta \sum_{i \in \text{OPT}} v_i' \geq \theta \sum_{i \in \text{OPT}}\left(\frac{v_i}{\theta} - 1\right) = \text{OPT} - n\theta
$$

Since $\text{OPT} \leq n v_{\max}$:

$$
\sum_{i \in S'} v_i \geq \text{OPT} - \epsilon v_{\max} \geq (1 - \epsilon)\text{OPT}
$$

**Time Complexity:**

The scaled value range is $V' \leq n^2 / \epsilon$. DP runs in $O(nV') = O(n^3/\epsilon)$, polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define the 0/1 Knapsack problem and its decision variant. Show via pseudo-polynomial Dynamic Programming that the optimization version can be solved in $O(nV)$ time, where $V = \sum v_i$. Discuss why this does **not** constitute a polynomial-time algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Let $\epsilon > 0$ be given. Using the scaling parameter $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$, derive an FPTAS for the knapsack problem and show that its total running time is $O(n^3/\epsilon)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:**
The 0/1 Knapsack optimization problem is to find $S^* \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$ maximizing $\sum_{i \in S^*} v_i$ subject to $\sum_{i \in S^*} w_i \leq W$.

**Decision Variant:** Given an additional integer $V_0$, decide if there exists $S$ with $\sum_{i \in S} v_i \geq V_0$ and $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \leq W$.

**DP Table:**
Define $S[i, v]$ = minimum weight of a subset of items $\{1, \ldots, i\}$ achieving total value exactly $v$ (or $+\infty$ if impossible).

**Recurrence:**

$$
S[i, v] = \min\!\left(S[i-1, v], \; S[i-1, v - v_i] + w_i\right) \quad (v_i \leq v)
$$

$$
S[i, v] = S[i-1, v] \quad (v_i > v)
$$

**Base:** $S[0, 0] = 0$, $S[0, v] = +\infty$ for $v > 0$.

**Answer:** $\text{OPT} = \max\{v : S[n, v] \leq W\}$.

**Why Not Polynomial?** The table has size $O(nV)$. The input size is $O(n \log V)$ when values are encoded in binary (since $V$ is encoded in $\log V$ bits). Hence $O(nV)$ is exponential in input size, making it **pseudo-polynomial**, not polynomial.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Scaling Step:** Given $\epsilon > 0$, set $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$. Define scaled values $v_i' = \lfloor v_i / \theta \rfloor$.

**Bound on $V'$:**

$$
V' = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i' \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{v_i}{\theta} \leq \frac{n v_{\max}}{\theta} = \frac{n v_{\max} \cdot n}{\epsilon v_{\max}} = \frac{n^2}{\epsilon}
$$

**DP on Scaled Instance:** Run DP on $(w_i, v_i')$ with capacity $W$. Complexity $O(n V') = O(n^3/\epsilon)$.

**Output:** Let $S'$ be the optimal subset for scaled instance. Return $S'$ as approximate solution.

**Approximation:** From part (a)'s solution structure:

$$
\sum_{i \in S'} v_i \geq \theta \sum_{i \in S'} v_i' \geq \theta \sum_{i \in S^*} v_i' \geq \sum_{i \in S^*} v_i - n\theta = \text{OPT} - \epsilon v_{\max} \geq (1 - \epsilon)\text{OPT}
$$

**Total Time:** $O(n^3/\epsilon)$ — polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PTAS and FPTAS:** A PTAS allows running time to depend arbitrarily on $1/\epsilon$ (e.g., $n^{1/\epsilon}$), while an FPTAS requires polynomial dependence on $1/\epsilon$ (e.g., $n^3/\epsilon$). Knapsack has an **FPTAS**, not merely a PTAS.
> 2. **Wrong scaling parameter:** Using $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$ is correct because we need $\theta \cdot n \leq \epsilon \cdot v_{\max} \leq \epsilon \cdot \text{OPT}$. Forgetting the factor of $n$ yields a wrong $V'$ bound.
> 3. **Forgetting the integer floor:** Using $v_i' = v_i/\theta$ (without floor) makes $V'$ non-integer. DP requires integer values; the floor introduces bounded error.
> 4. **Misstating greedy bound:** Greedy-by-Ratio alone gives **$\geq 1/2$**, not $\geq 1 - 1/e$ (that is for Maximum Coverage). Don't confuse different problems' bounds.
> 5. **Skipping the pseudo-polynomial caveat:** DP for Knapsack is **pseudo-polynomial**, not polynomial. This is a frequent marker for losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing fractional and 0/1:** Fractional knapsack has an exact greedy solution; 0/1 knapsack is NP-hard. Be explicit about which version is being analyzed.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **0/1 Knapsack:** NP-hard optimization problem; each item taken whole or not at all.
- **Decision variant:** NP-complete; used in NP-hardness reductions.
- **Fractional knapsack:** Polynomial via greedy-by-ratio (Landau's algorithm).
- **Greedy-by-Ratio** gives a **1/2-approximation** in $O(n \log n)$.
- **Dynamic programming** gives exact solution in $O(nV)$, which is **pseudo-polynomial**.
- **FPTAS construction uses value scaling** with parameter $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$.
- **Scaled value bound:** $V' \leq n^2/\epsilon$, giving running time $O(n^3/\epsilon)$.
- **Approximation guarantee:** FPTAS returns solution with value $\geq (1 - \epsilon)\text{OPT}$.
- **Difference:** PTAS allows time polynomial only in $n$; FPTAS requires polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.
- **Knapsack is one of the rare NP-hard problems admitting an FPTAS**, making it a canonical positive example.
- **Key formula to memorize:** $\theta = \epsilon v_{\max}/n$.
- **Tight examples:** Construct adversarial instances with $\text{OPT}/\mathcal{A} \to 1/2$ to demonstrate greedy optimality.
- **Mark allocation in KTU:** Part (a) typically 7 marks (design + proof sketch), Part (b) 7 marks (construction + complexity).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euclidean TSP
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Euclidean TSP

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Euclidean Traveling Salesman Problem (Euclidean TSP)** is a restricted variant of the classic TSP where the cities (vertices) are points in a $d$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$ (typically $d = 2$ on a plane), and the edge weights (inter-city travel distances) are the standard Euclidean distances given by:

$$w(u, v) = \sqrt{(u_x - v_x)^2 + (u_y - v_y)^2}$$

The fundamental structural property that distinguishes Euclidean TSP from general TSP is the **Triangle Inequality**:

$$w(u, v) \leq w(u, w) + w(w, v) \quad \forall \; u, v, w \in V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** General TSP (without the triangle inequality) cannot be approximated to within any polynomial factor unless **P = NP** (Sahni & Gonzalez, 1976). However, the Euclidean restriction makes approximation tractable, with the celebrated **Christofides' 3/2-approximation** being the gold-standard result.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **tourist in Kerala** who must visit every major city (Kochi, Trivandrum, Kozhikode, Thrissur, etc.) exactly once and return home, while minimizing total driving distance. In reality, road distances roughly satisfy the triangle inequality — the direct highway from Kochi to Trivandrum is never longer than going via Kollam. This geometric constraint is exactly what Euclidean TSP exploits. We are not solving the abstract combinatorial explosion of TSP; we are leveraging the **geometric regularity of points on a plane** to design provably good tours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Geometry Helps:** On a 2D plane, "nearby" cities cluster predictably, allowing spanning trees and matchings to capture the structure of an optimal tour with bounded loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Layer Strategy of Christofides' Algorithm

The algorithm relies on two well-known polynomial-time solvable combinatorial structures:

- **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST):** A tree connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight.
- **Minimum Weight Perfect Matching:** A set of disjoint edges covering a specified subset of vertices with minimum total weight.

The MST gives a lower bound on the optimal tour cost, and a cleverly constructed matching adds just enough edges to make the multigraph **Eulerian** (every vertex has even degree), enabling traversal.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Construct MST $T$:** Build the minimum spanning tree of the complete graph $G = (V, E)$. It has $n - 1$ edges.
2. **Identify Odd-Degree Vertices $O$:** Find the set $O \subseteq V$ where $\deg_T(v)$ is odd. By the **Handshaking Lemma**, $\vert O \vert$ is always even.
3. **Compute Minimum Weight Perfect Matching $M$ on $O$:** Find $M$ such that every vertex in $O$ is incident to exactly one edge of $M$ and total weight $w(M)$ is minimized. This is solvable in $O(n^3)$ using Edmonds' Blossom algorithm.
4. **Form the Eulerian Multigraph $H = T \cup M$:** In $H$, every vertex now has even degree (odd + odd = even, and even + 0 = even).
5. **Find Eulerian Circuit in $H$:** An Eulerian circuit traverses every edge exactly once and returns to the start.
6. **Shortcut to Hamiltonian Cycle:** Traverse the Eulerian circuit, and whenever it revisits a previously visited vertex, skip directly to the next unvisited vertex using a direct edge. By the **triangle inequality**, shortcutting never increases the total cost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Bound / Formula | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Optimal TSP tour cost | $\text{OPT}$ | Given baseline | Reference value |
| MST cost | $w(T)$ | $w(T) \leq \text{OPT}$ | Removing any edge from OPT gives a spanning tree |
| Min. weight matching on odd vertices | $w(M)$ | $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$ | Two disjoint matchings extracted from OPT |
| Eulerian circuit cost | $w(T) + w(M)$ | $\leq \frac{3}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Pre-shortcutting cost |
| Christofides approximation ratio | $\alpha$ | $\frac{3}{2} = 1.5$ | Best known for general metric TSP |
| Handshaking Lemma | $\sum \deg(v) = 2\vert E \vert$ | $\vert O \vert$ even | Guarantees valid matching exists |
| Triangle Inequality | $w(u,v) \leq w(u,w) + w(w,v)$ | Holds in Euclidean space | Enables shortcutting step |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Design:** Routing a drilling machine across thousands of chip pads minimizing travel time.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Vehicle routing for last-mile delivery in metropolitan grids.
- **Robotic Path Planning:** Minimizing inspection tours over a known set of geographic waypoints.
- **Genomic Sequencing:** Ordering overlapping DNA fragments (overlap-layout-consensus) where distances satisfy metric properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Proof of the 3/2-Approximation Guarantee

We prove that the Christofides' algorithm produces a Hamiltonian cycle of total cost at most $\frac{3}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$.

#### Lemma 1: MST Cost Lower Bound

**Statement:** $w(T) \leq \text{OPT}$

**Proof:** Let $C^*$ be an optimal Hamiltonian tour. If we remove any single edge from $C^*$, the remaining $n - 1$ edges still connect all $n$ vertices, forming a spanning tree. Since $T$ is the **minimum** spanning tree:

$$w(T) \leq w(C^* \setminus \{e\}) = \text{OPT} - w(e) \leq \text{OPT}$$

This completes the proof of Lemma 1.

#### Lemma 2: Matching Cost Lower Bound

**Statement:** $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$

**Proof:** Consider the optimal tour $C^*$ restricted to the set of odd-degree vertices $O$. This forms a set of vertex-disjoint paths (the induced subgraph of $O$ on $C^*$ has every vertex of degree $\leq 2$ in $C^*$, hence consists of paths and cycles; since $O$ is a subset of $V$, we get paths covering all of $O$).

Because $\vert O \vert$ is even, we can partition the edges of this subgraph into **two perfect matchings** $M_1$ and $M_2$ (alternate edges along each path). By the **optimality of $M$**:

$$w(M) \leq w(M_1) \quad \text{and} \quad w(M) \leq w(M_2)$$

Adding these inequalities:

$$2 \cdot w(M) \leq w(M_1) + w(M_2) \leq \text{OPT}$$

where the last inequality follows because $M_1 \cup M_2$ is a subset of edges from $C^*$. Therefore:

$$w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$$

This completes the proof of Lemma 2.

#### Lemma 3: Shortcutting Preserves the Triangle Inequality

**Statement:** Converting the Eulerian circuit to a Hamiltonian cycle does not increase cost.

**Proof:** Whenever the Eulerian circuit visits a vertex $v$ for the second (or later) time, we replace the two edges $v$-$a$ and $v$-$b$ (where $a$ precedes $v$ and $b$ follows $v$ in the traversal) with the direct edge $a$-$b$. By the triangle inequality in Euclidean space:

$$w(a, b) \leq w(a, v) + w(v, b)$$

So each shortcut operation does not increase the total cost. Repeating this for all revisits yields a Hamiltonian cycle of cost at most $w(T) + w(M)$. This completes the proof of Lemma 3.

#### Theorem 1: Christofides' Algorithm is a 3/2-Approximation

**Statement:** $\text{ALG} \leq \frac{3}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$

**Proof:** Combining the three lemmas above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALG} &\leq w(T) + w(M) && \text{(by Lemma 3, after shortcutting)} \\
&\leq \text{OPT} + \frac{\text{OPT}}{2} && \text{(by Lemma 1 and Lemma 2)} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, Christofides' algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of exactly $\frac{3}{2}$ for Euclidean TSP. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Tightness Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Is $\frac{3}{2}$ tight?** For general metric TSP, no known algorithm has broken the $\frac{3}{2}$ barrier since 1976 (except under stronger assumptions). For Euclidean TSP specifically, Arora (1998) and Mitchell (1999) independently designed a **PTAS** (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme), achieving $(1 + \varepsilon)$-approximation in time $n^{O(1/\varepsilon)}$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$. However, Christofides remains the simplest constant-factor algorithm and is the **default answer expected in KTU examinations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Christofides' Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Input: Set of n points in Euclidean plane"] --> stepA["Step 1: Construct Complete Graph with Euclidean edge weights"]
    stepA --> stepB["Step 2: Compute Minimum Spanning Tree T"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Identify Set O of Odd-Degree Vertices"]
    stepC --> checkA{"Is O empty?"}
    checkA -- "Yes" --> pathB["Skip to Step 5 with T itself"]
    checkA -- "No" --> stepD["Step 4: Compute Min-Weight Perfect Matching M on O using Blossom Algorithm"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5: Combine Multigraph H = T union M"]
    pathB --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6: Find Eulerian Circuit in H using Hierholzer Algorithm"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7: Apply Shortcutting using Triangle Inequality"]
    stepG --> outputA["Output: Hamiltonian Cycle with cost <= 1.5 times OPT"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: The Three Key Cost Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 ["Optimal Tour OPT"]
        a1["OPT bounds MST"] --> a2["OPT bounds Matching M"]
        a2 --> a3["OPT is reference baseline"]
    end
    subgraph S2 ["Christofides Construction"]
        b1["MST T with cost at most OPT"] --> b2["Matching M with cost at most OPT / 2"]
        b2 --> b3["Multigraph H with Eulerian property"]
    end
    subgraph S3 ["Final Output"]
        c1["Eulerian Circuit = T + M"] --> c2["Shortcut using Triangle Inequality"]
        c2 --> c3["Hamiltonian Cycle with cost at most 1.5 OPT"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right TSP Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Problem Type?"] --> q2{"Does metric satisfy Triangle Inequality?"}
    q2 -- "No, General TSP" --> r1["Cannot approximate to any constant factor unless P = NP"]
    q2 -- "Yes, Metric TSP" --> q3{"Geometric instance in Euclidean plane?"}
    q3 -- "Yes, Euclidean" --> r2["Use PTAS by Arora 1998 for 1 plus epsilon approximation"]
    r2 --> r3["Or use Christofides for simple 1.5 approximation"]
    q3 -- "No, General Metric" --> r4["Use Christofides 1.5 approximation as best known"]
    r3 --> r5["KTU Recommended: Christofides Algorithm"]
    r4 --> r5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
