### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
*State the Euclidean TSP problem and explain why the triangle inequality is crucial for designing approximation algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Euclidean TSP is the problem of finding a minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle in a complete graph whose vertices are points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and whose edge weights are Euclidean distances. The triangle inequality $w(u, v) \leq w(u, w) + w(w, v)$ is crucial because it allows **shortcutting** in tours without increasing cost, enables MST-based lower bounds to hold as $\text{MST} \leq \text{OPT}$, and is essential for proving the perfect matching bound $w(M) \leq \text{OPT}/2$ in Christofides' algorithm. Without it, no polynomial-factor approximation is possible unless P = NP. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
*Why is the set of odd-degree vertices in a spanning tree guaranteed to have even cardinality?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

By the **Handshaking Lemma**, the sum of degrees of all vertices in any graph equals twice the number of edges, hence is always even. Partitioning vertices into even-degree and odd-degree sets, the sum of degrees of odd-degree vertices must itself be even (because the total sum minus even-degree contributions is even). Since each odd-degree vertex contributes an odd number, the count of such vertices must be even to make their sum even. Thus $\vert O \vert$ is even, ensuring a perfect matching on $O$ exists. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]`

**(a)** Describe the **Christofides' approximation algorithm** for Euclidean TSP in detail. List all the steps and the data structures/computational subroutines used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove that Christofides' algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of $\frac{3}{2}$ for the metric TSP (and hence Euclidean TSP). State all necessary lemmas with rigorous proofs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Algorithm Description (7 Marks):**

The Christofides' algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Input:** A complete graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ cities in Euclidean plane and edge weights satisfying the triangle inequality.

2. **Step 1 — MST Construction:** Compute the **Minimum Spanning Tree $T$** using either Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm. Time complexity: $O(n^2)$ using Prim's on dense graphs. $T$ has $n - 1$ edges. **(2 marks)**

3. **Step 2 — Odd-Degree Vertex Identification:** Traverse $T$ and collect the set $O = \{v \in V : \deg_T(v) \text{ is odd}\}$. By the Handshaking Lemma, $\vert O \vert$ is even. **(1 mark)**

4. **Step 3 — Minimum Weight Perfect Matching:** Compute the **minimum weight perfect matching $M$** on the induced subgraph of $O$ using **Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm**. Time complexity: $O(n^3)$. **(2 marks)**

5. **Step 4 — Eulerian Multigraph:** Form $H = (V, T \cup M)$. Since every odd-degree vertex in $T$ gains exactly one additional edge from $M$, every vertex in $H$ now has even degree, making $H$ Eulerian. **(1 mark)**

6. **Step 5 — Eulerian Circuit:** Find an Eulerian circuit in $H$ using **Hierholzer's algorithm** in $O(n)$ time. **(0.5 marks)**

7. **Step 6 — Shortcutting:** Convert the Eulerian circuit into a Hamiltonian cycle by skipping already-visited vertices. By the triangle inequality, this does not increase the total weight. Output the resulting cycle. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Proof of Approximation Ratio (7 Marks):**

Let $C^*$ denote the optimal tour with cost $\text{OPT}$.

**Lemma 1: $w(T) \leq \text{OPT}$ (2 marks)**  
Removing any edge $e$ from $C^*$ yields a spanning tree. Since $T$ is the minimum spanning tree, $w(T) \leq w(C^*) - w(e) \leq \text{OPT}$.

**Lemma 2: $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$ (3 marks)**  
The optimal tour $C^*$ restricted to $O$ forms a collection of paths covering all vertices in $O$ (since each vertex in $O$ has degree 2 in $C^*$, and removing vertices outside $O$ leaves paths). Since $\vert O \vert$ is even, we can alternate-edge-color these paths to obtain two perfect matchings $M_1$ and $M_2$ on $O$, with $M_1 \cup M_2 \subseteq C^*$. By optimality of $M$: $w(M) \leq w(M_1)$ and $w(M) \leq w(M_2)$. Adding: $2 w(M) \leq w(M_1) + w(M_2) \leq \text{OPT}$, hence $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$.

**Lemma 3: Shortcutting does not increase cost (1 mark)**  
Each shortcut replaces edges $(a, v)$ and $(v, b)$ with $(a, b)$. By triangle inequality, $w(a, b) \leq w(a, v) + w(v, b)$.

**Final Bound (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALG} &\leq w(T) + w(M) && \text{(by Lemma 3)} \\
&\leq \text{OPT} + \frac{\text{OPT}}{2} && \text{(by Lemmas 1 and 2)} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the algorithm achieves a $\frac{3}{2}$-approximation. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Minimum Weight Perfect Matching**. State Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm idea and justify why it gives an optimal matching in polynomial time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In Christofides' algorithm, the **minimum weight perfect matching** is computed on the set of odd-degree vertices of the MST. Prove the **matching bound** $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$ rigorously. Show that without the triangle inequality, this bound can fail. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Minimum Weight Perfect Matching and Blossom Algorithm (7 Marks):**

A **perfect matching** in a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a subset $M \subseteq E$ such that every vertex is incident to exactly one edge of $M$. A **minimum weight perfect matching** minimizes $\sum_{e \in M} w(e)$. **(1 mark)**

**Idea of Blossom Algorithm (Edmonds, 1965):** The algorithm finds augmenting paths via BFS/DFS on an auxiliary graph, but handles the case where two augmenting paths share an intermediate vertex by **contracting odd-length cycles (blossoms)** into a single super-vertex. When an augmenting path is found, the blossom is "lifted" back to expose the actual augmenting structure. **(3 marks)**

**Optimality & Polynomial Time:** Edmonds proved that the algorithm finds a maximum cardinality matching in $O(n^3)$ time. For weighted version (using primal-dual or Micali-Vazirani), the complexity is $O(n^3)$. Optimality follows from **Berge's theorem** (a matching is maximum iff no augmenting path exists) combined with the polynomial procedure for finding augmenting paths after blossom contraction. **(3 marks)**

**(b) Matching Bound Proof (7 Marks):**

**Proof of $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$:**  
Consider optimal tour $C^*$ restricted to vertices in $O$. Each vertex of $O$ has degree exactly 2 in $C^*$. When we delete vertices of $V \setminus O$, the induced subgraph on $O$ in $C^*$ becomes a set of vertex-disjoint paths covering all of $O$. **(2 marks)**  
Since $\vert O \vert$ is even, each such path has odd length (even number of vertices, odd number of edges). Alternating edges along each path yields **two perfect matchings** $M_1, M_2$ on $O$ with $M_1 \cup M_2 = $ edges of the restricted tour. **(2 marks)**  
By optimality of $M$: $w(M) \leq w(M_1)$ and $w(M) \leq w(M_2)$. Summing:

$$2 w(M) \leq w(M_1) + w(M_2) \leq w(C^* \vert_O) \leq w(C^*) = \text{OPT}$$

Hence $w(M) \leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$. **(2 marks)**

**Failure without triangle inequality (1 mark):**  
If the triangle inequality is violated, the matching $M$ on $O$ may include edges not present in any near-optimal tour, and the "two matchings from $C^*$" extraction step fails to give a valid bound because $C^*$ restricted to $O$ may not form simple paths. Counter-examples in general TSP instances demonstrate the bound can be arbitrarily violated.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Christofides' Proof (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Handshaking Lemma explicitly** when justifying why $\vert O \vert$ is even — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Using shortcutting before stating the triangle inequality** — this is a logical order-of-operations error worth losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Eulerian circuit with Hamiltonian cycle** — always clarify that the multigraph $H = T \cup M$ is Eulerian, and the final output is a Hamiltonian cycle obtained via shortcutting.
> 4. **Writing $w(M) \leq \text{OPT}$ instead of $\frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$** — students frequently forget the factor of 2 from the alternating-path decomposition.
> 5. **Omitting the time complexity** — KTU 2024 examiners expect sub-routine complexity (MST: $O(n^2)$, Blossom: $O(n^3)$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Euclidean TSP** = TSP on points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with Euclidean distances (satisfying triangle inequality).
- ✅ General TSP has **no polynomial approximation** unless P = NP.
- ✅ **Christofides' Algorithm (1976):** MST + Minimum Weight Perfect Matching + Eulerian Circuit + Shortcutting.
- ✅ **Approximation Ratio:** $\frac{3}{2}$, the best known for general metric TSP.
- ✅ **Key Lemma 1:** MST cost $\leq$ OPT (remove any edge from optimal tour).
- ✅ **Key Lemma 2:** Perfect matching on odd-degree vertices $\leq \frac{\text{OPT}}{2}$ (extract two matchings from OPT).
- ✅ **Key Lemma 3:** Triangle inequality ensures shortcutting does not increase cost.
- ✅ **Handshaking Lemma:** $\vert O \vert$ is always even — guarantees matching feasibility.
- ✅ **Sub-routines:** Kruskal/Prim for MST ($O(n^2)$), Blossom for Matching ($O(n^3)$), Hierholzer for Eulerian ($O(n)$).
- ✅ **Beyond Christofides:** Arora's PTAS (1998) gives $(1 + \varepsilon)$-approximation for Euclidean TSP but with super-polynomial dependence on $\varepsilon$.
- ✅ **The 3/2 barrier has remained unbeaten for nearly 50 years** — a famous open problem in approximation algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Inapproximability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Inapproximability ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inapproximability** is the formal study of computational optimization problems for which there exists a provable *lower bound* on the approximation ratio achievable by any polynomial-time algorithm, conditional on standard complexity-theoretic assumptions such as **P ≠ NP** or stronger conjectures like the **Unique Games Conjecture (UGC)**. More precisely, a minimization problem $\Pi$ is said to be **inapproximable within a factor $\rho$** if, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there is no polynomial-time algorithm that produces a solution whose cost is at most $\rho \cdot \text{OPT} - \varepsilon$ (for minimization) or whose value is at least $\text{OPT}/\rho + \varepsilon$ (for maximization), unless **P = NP**. For a maximization problem, $\Pi$ is inapproximable within factor $\rho > 1$ if no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee a solution value $S$ such that: $$S \geq \frac{\text{OPT}}{\rho}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **lost hiker** trying to find the *shortest path* to the summit of a mountain in dense fog (you can only check nearby regions). Approximation algorithms are like having a *compass* — they guarantee you'll be within some factor of the optimal distance. **Inapproximability** is the grim mathematical discovery that for certain problems, *no compass can ever be built* that brings you closer than, say, a factor of $\ln(n)$ from the summit in polynomial time. The "fog" is the **exponential search space**, and the impossibility result is the *fog itself* — a structural barrier embedded in the **NP-hardness** of the problem. ### 1.3 The Central Question > [!NOTE] > The central question of inapproximability is the converse of approximation theory: *Given that an NP-hard optimization problem cannot be solved exactly in polynomial time (assuming P ≠ NP), how close to optimal can we provably get in polynomial time?* And for which problems is even a useful approximation ruled out? ### 1.4 Why Inapproximability Matters | Aspect | Approximability | Inapproximability | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Find algorithms with small ratio | Prove no such algorithm exists | | **Tool** | Greedy, LP rounding, dynamic programming | Reductions from PCP, gap amplification | | **Output** | Upper bound on hardness | Lower bound on hardness | | **Complexity Assumption** | Usually unconditional | Conditional (P ≠ NP, UGC, ETH) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics in Inapproximability:** > - **Approximation Ratio** $\rho$ — multiplicative factor from optimum. > - **Gap** — additive or multiplicative separation between cost of YES-instances and NO-instances. > - **Hardness Factor** $\alpha$ — the smallest $\alpha$ such that $(1 + \alpha)$-approximation is NP-hard. > - **Dichotomy Constant** — for problems like Max-3SAT, the magic constant is $\frac{7}{8}$ (Håstad, 2001). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Theoretical Pillars of Inapproximability The entire field rests on three foundational pillars: 1. **The PCP Theorem (1992)** — Arora, Safra, Lund, Motwani, Sudan, Szegedy. 2. **Gap-Preserving Reductions** — transfer hardness from decision problems to optimization problems. 3. **Gap Amplification** — techniques (like alphabet reduction, alphabet extension, and Raz's parallel repetition) to strengthen weak gaps into arbitrarily strong gaps. ### 2.2 The PCP Theorem — The Engine of Hardness > [!IMPORTANT] > **PCP Theorem (Arora et al., 1992; Arora & Safra, 1998):** > $$\text{NP} = \text{PCP}[O(\log n), O(1)]$$ > Every language in NP has a *probabilistically checkable proof* that can be verified using only $O(\log n)$ random bits by reading only $O(1)$ bits of the proof. The verifier accepts YES-instances with probability 1, and rejects NO-instances with probability at least $\frac{1}{2}$. The PCP theorem is the *Rosetta Stone* of inapproximability — it converts decision problems with "all-or-nothing" acceptance into optimization problems with measurable gaps. ### 2.3 Gap Problems — The Language of Hardness > [!NOTE] > **Gap Problem Definition:** A *gap problem* $\text{Gap}_c \Pi$ takes an instance $x$ of $\Pi$ and: > - If $x$ is a YES-instance (i.e., $\text{OPT}(x) = 1$), then output *YES*. > - If the optimum value $\text{OPT}(x) < 1/c$, then output *NO*. > - Otherwise (instances with intermediate optimum), the answer can be arbitrary. Showing that $\text{Gap}_c \Pi$ is NP-hard *immediately* implies that $\Pi$ cannot be approximated within factor $c$ in polynomial time (unless P = NP). ### 2.4 Fundamental Reductions in Inapproximability Three reduction paradigms are essential: | Reduction Type | Purpose | Key Property | |---|---|---| | **L-reduction** (Papadimitriou–Yannakakis) | Preserves approximation ratio linearly | $\text{OPT}_f(x) \leq \text{OPT}_g(x') \leq \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_f(x)$ | | **FPTAS-reduction** | Preserves fully polynomial approximation schemes | Polynomial-time computable, size-preserving | | **AP-reduction** | Preserves membership in APX | Approximation ratio preserved within a multiplicative constant | ### 2.5 The Hierarchy of Approximation Classes The complexity zoo for approximation is layered as follows: $$\text{FPTAS} \subseteq \text{PTAS} \subseteq \text{APX} \subseteq \text{NPO} \subseteq \text{NP-optimization}$$ A problem is **APX-hard** if every APX problem can be AP-reduced to it. A problem is **APX-complete** if it is both APX-hard and lies in APX. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Problem | Approximable to | Inapproximable beyond | Source | |---|---|---|---| | **General TSP** | None for general metric violation | Any polynomial factor | Sahni & Gonzalez, 1976 | | **Metric TSP** | $\frac{3}{2}$ (Christofides) | $\frac{220}{219}$ under UGC | Papadimitriou & Vempala, 2000; Karpinski et al. | | **Set Cover** | $\ln n$ (greedy) | $(1 - \varepsilon) \ln n$ unless P = NP | Feige, 1998 | | **Max-3SAT** | $\frac{7}{8}$ (random assignment) | $\frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon$ unless P = NP | Håstad, 2001 | | **Clique / Ind. Set** | $O\!\left(\frac{n}{(\log n)^2}\right)$ | $n^{1-\varepsilon}$ unless P = NP | Håstad, 1999 | | **Vertex Cover** | $2 - \frac{\log \log n}{2 \log n}$ | $\sqrt{2} - \varepsilon$ under UGC | Khot & Regev, 2008 | | **Steiner Tree** | $\ln 4 \approx 1.39$ (Klein–Ravi) | $\frac{96}{95}$ unless P = NP | Chlebík & Chlebíková, 2008 | | **Max Cut** | $0.8785$ (Goemans–Williamson) | $\frac{16}{17} + \varepsilon$ under UGC | Trevisan et al. | | **k-Median** | $3 + \frac{2}{p}$ (Arya et al.) | $1 + \frac{2}{e}$ unless P = NP | Jain et al., 2002 | | **Facility Location** | $1.488$ (Li, 2011) | $\frac{97}{96}$ unless P = NP | Guha & Khuller, 1998 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** Approximability results give *upper bounds* (we CAN achieve this ratio). Inapproximability results give *conditional lower bounds* (we CANNOT beat this ratio unless a major conjecture fails). Both are required to fully characterize a problem's tractability. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Inapproximability is not merely abstract — it directly guides engineering decisions: - **Network Design** — If Steiner Tree is inapproximable beyond $\frac{96}{95}$, network engineers must accept heuristic gaps. - **VLSI Routing & Placement** — Hardness results on Set Cover explain why heuristic, non-worst-case tools are unavoidable in chip layout. - **Bioinformatics & Phylogenetics** — Hardness of Nearest Neighbor/String problems dictates that exact inference is intractable on large genomes. - **Machine Learning Inference** — Hardness of MAP inference in graphical models justifies the use of variational and sampling methods. - **Combinatorial Auctions** — Hardness of winner determination justifies iterative combinatorial auctions over optimal mechanisms. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Gap-3SAT to Max-3SAT Inapproximability The canonical derivation shows how a *gap decision problem* becomes an *inapproximability theorem*. We follow Håstad's path. **Step 1 — Start with Gap-3SAT($\varepsilon$):** Define the gap problem $\text{Gap-3SAT}_{1, 1-\varepsilon}$: - Given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi$ on $n$ variables. - If $\varphi$ is *satisfiable* (i.e., $\text{OPT}(\varphi) = 1$), output **YES**. - If *every* assignment satisfies strictly less than a $(1 - \varepsilon)$-fraction of clauses, output **NO**. By the PCP theorem (in its strong form due to Håstad), this gap problem is **NP-hard** for every $\varepsilon > 0$. **Step 2 — Reformulate as Max-3SAT:** Max-3SAT asks: given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi$, find an assignment that maximizes the fraction of satisfied clauses. The reduction is now trivial — Gap-3SAT is *literally* an instance of Max-3SAT with a gap. **Step 3 — Convert the Gap into an Inapproximability Bound:** Suppose, for contradiction, there exists a polynomial-time algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ that, given $\varphi$, outputs an assignment satisfying at least a $\left(\frac{7}{8} + \delta\right)$-fraction of clauses for some fixed $\delta > 0$. **Step 4 — Choose the Right $\varepsilon$:** Apply Håstad's result with $\varepsilon = \delta$. Then: - If $\varphi$ is satisfiable, $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies *all* clauses (since $\mathcal{A}$ is correct on satisfiable instances? — we must verify this carefully). The standard form of Håstad's result says that given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi$: - We can construct a new 3-CNF formula $\psi$ such that: - If $\varphi$ is satisfiable, then $\psi$ is also satisfiable. - If every assignment to $\varphi$ satisfies at most a $(1 - \varepsilon)$-fraction of clauses, then every assignment to $\psi$ satisfies at most a $\left(\frac{7}{8} + \frac{\varepsilon}{8}\right)$-fraction. **Step 5 — Apply the Algorithm:** Run $\mathcal{A}$ on $\psi$. - **Case A — $\varphi$ is satisfiable:** $\mathcal{A}$ returns an assignment satisfying at least a $\left(\frac{7}{8} + \delta\right)$-fraction. This is *consistent* with $\psi$ being satisfiable (which would give fraction $1$). - **Case B — $\varphi$ has max clause satisfaction $\leq 1 - \varepsilon$:** The Håstad construction ensures max fraction for $\psi$ is at most $\frac{7}{8} + \frac{\varepsilon}{8}$. With $\varepsilon = 8\delta$, this becomes $\frac{7}{8} + \delta$. In both cases, $\mathcal{A}$ returns fraction $\geq \frac{7}{8} + \delta$. This *cannot distinguish* the two cases! So $\mathcal{A}$ does *not* solve the gap problem — contradiction. **Step 6 — Conclude:** Hence, no polynomial-time algorithm can approximate Max-3SAT within factor better than $\frac{7}{8}$, unless $\text{P} = \text{NP}$. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Set Cover Inapproximability within $(1 - \varepsilon)\ln n$ **Step 1 — Problem Setup:** Given a universe $U$ with $|U| = n$ and a collection of subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m \subseteq U$, find a minimum subcollection that covers $U$. **Step 2 — Start from Gap-3SAT$(c, s)$ with unbounded gap:** Using the PCP theorem with *unbounded* alphabet reduction (essentially, a system of $\binom{n}{2}$ consistency checks in a low-degree test), we obtain a gap problem where: - YES-instances have a structure with very many satisfying assignments. - NO-instances have very few *fractionally* satisfying assignments. **Step 3 — Reduce to Label Cover:** Construct a *Label Cover* instance: a bipartite graph $G = (A \cup B, E)$ where: - Each vertex $a \in A$ has label set $L_A$ of size $k_A$. - Each vertex $b \in B$ has label set $L_B$ of size $k_B$. - Each edge $e = (a, b)$ carries a constraint $\pi_e : L_A \to L_B \cup \{\text{rej}\}$. - Goal: maximize fraction of edges satisfied. **Step 4 — Show Large Gap in Label Cover:** The PCP construction yields: - YES-instances: there is a labeling satisfying *all* edges. - NO-instances: every labeling satisfies at most a $\frac{1}{N^{\delta}}$-fraction of edges, for some $\delta > 0$. **Step 5 — Reduce Label Cover to Set Cover:** Given a Label Cover instance, construct a Set Cover instance as follows: - For each pair $(a, \ell_A)$ where $\ell_A \in L_A$, create a set $T_{a, \ell_A}$. - $T_{a, \ell_A}$ contains every edge $e = (a, b)$ such that $\ell_A$ can be extended to a satisfying label for $b$. **Step 6 — Compute the Cover Size:** In the YES case: We can cover all edges using $|A|$ sets (one per vertex). So optimal cover size $C_{\text{YES}} = |A|$. In the NO case: By a covering argument (Lovász-style), any cover must have size at least $\delta' \cdot |E| / N^{\delta''}$, leading to $C_{\text{NO}} \geq |A| \cdot N^{\Omega(\delta)}$. **Step 7 — Conclude:** The ratio $C_{\text{NO}} / C_{\text{YES}} \geq (1 - \varepsilon) \ln N$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$. So approximating Set Cover within $(1 - \varepsilon) \ln n$ is NP-hard. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note:** This result was first established by Lund & Yannakakis (1993) and tightened by Feige (1998), who showed Set Cover is hard to approximate within $\ln n - O(\ln \ln n)$ under P ≠ NP. ### 3.3 Derivation: The PCP Theorem Implication Chain The full implication chain is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{3SAT} &\xrightarrow{\text{PCP encoding}} \text{PCP}[\log n, 3] \text{ verifier} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Alphabet reduction}} \text{Label Cover with gap} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Constraint satisfaction}} \text{Max-CSP}_c \text{ hard} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Problem-specific reduction}} \text{Concrete inapproximability} \end{aligned} $$ Each arrow is a polynomial-time, gap-preserving reduction. The chain proves that *every* NP-hard optimization problem with a "local" structure inherits hardness from the PCP theorem. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When is Inapproximability Tight? | Scenario | Approximation | Inapproximability | Tight? | |---|---|---|---| | **Set Cover** | $H(n) = \ln n + 1$ | $(1-\varepsilon)\ln n$ | **Open** (close to tight) | | **Max-3SAT** | $\frac{7}{8}$ | $\frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon$ | **Tight** | | **Vertex Cover** | $2 - o(1)$ | $\sqrt{2} - \varepsilon$ (UGC) | **Gap** under UGC | | **Metric TSP** | $\frac{3}{2}$ | $\frac{4}{3}$ (UGC) | **Gap** under UGC | | **Max Cut** | $0.8785$ | $0.844$ (UGC) | **Gap** | | **Ind. Set** | $O\!\left(\frac{n}{\log^2 n}\right)$ | $\frac{n}{2^{(\log n)^{1-\varepsilon}}}$ | **Far gap** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Approximation Class Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["NPO
Nondet Polynomial Optimization"] nodeB["APX
Constant-factor approximable"] nodeC["PTAS
Polynomial Time Approx Scheme"] nodeD["FPTAS
Fully PTAS"] nodeE["PO
Polynomial Optimizable"] nodeF["NP-hard problems
(no constant ratio)"] nodeG["APX-complete
problems"] nodeH["NPI
NP Intermediate"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeA classDef hard fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px classDef easy fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px class F,G,H hard class D,C easy class A,B,E mid ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hardness Reduction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1["Hardness Source Layer"] nodeH1["3SAT
NP-complete"] nodeH2["PCP Theorem
NP = PCPlogn,3"] nodeH3["Label Cover
Gap Problem"] end subgraph L2["Gap Amplification Layer"] nodeA1["Parallel Repetition
Raz 1998"] nodeA2["Alphabet Reduction
Dinur 2006"] nodeA3["Long Code Test
Bellare et al. 1998"] end subgraph L3["Target Optimization Layer"] nodeT1["Max-3SAT"] nodeT2["Set Cover"] nodeT3["Independent Set"] nodeT4["Clique"] nodeT5["Max Cut"] nodeT6["Steiner Tree"] nodeT7["Min Bisection"] end nodeH1 --> nodeH2 nodeH2 --> nodeH3 nodeH3 --> nodeA1 nodeH3 --> nodeA2 nodeA1 --> nodeA3 nodeA2 --> nodeA3 nodeA3 --> nodeT1 nodeA3 --> nodeT2 nodeA3 --> nodeT3 nodeA3 --> nodeT4 nodeA3 --> nodeT5 nodeA3 --> nodeT6 nodeA3 --> nodeT7 classDef source fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px classDef amp fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px classDef target fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px class H1,H2,H3 source class A1,A2,A3 amp class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6,T7 target ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of a Gap-Preserving Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeI["Source Instance
x in L"] nodeQ1["Is OPT x equals 1?"] nodeYES["YES branch
cost 1"] nodeNO["NO branch
cost less than 1 slash c"] nodeR["Reduction
Algorithm f"] nodeII["Target Instance
y = f x in Pi"] nodeOPT["Compute OPT y"] nodeCOMP["Compare ratios:
OPT x vs OPT y"] nodeI --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 --> nodeYES nodeQ1 --> nodeNO nodeQ1 --> nodeR nodeR --> nodeII nodeII --> nodeOPT nodeOPT --> nodeCOMP nodeCOMP --> nodeCONC["Conclusion:
Pi is c-inapproximable"] classDef q fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px classDef r fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef c fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px class I,Q1,YES,NO q class R,II,OPT,COMP r class CONC c ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flow: Proving an Inapproximability Result ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS1["Step 1: Identify
PCP-based gap source"] nodeS2["Step 2: Construct
gap problem Gap-c L"] nodeS3["Step 3: Design
reduction f from L to Pi"] nodeS4["Step 4: Verify
gap preservation"] nodeS5["Step 5: Apply
PCP Theorem"] nodeS6["Step 6: Conclude
inapproximability"] nodeS1 --> nodeS2 nodeS2 --> nodeS3 nodeS3 --> nodeS4 nodeS4 -->|"Gap preserved"| nodeS5 nodeS4 -->|"Gap not preserved"| nodeS3 nodeS5 --> nodeS6 classDef step fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 step ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define inapproximability. State the gap-preserving reduction framework used to establish inapproximability results.* **Model Answer:** Inapproximability refers to the property of an optimization problem $\Pi$ such that there is a factor $\rho > 1$ for which no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee an approximation ratio better than $\rho$, assuming $\text{P} \neq \text{NP}$ (or another complexity-theoretic conjecture). The gap-preserving reduction framework proceeds as follows: - Start from a gap decision problem $\text{Gap-}c\text{-}L$ known to be NP-hard (typically derived from the PCP theorem). - Construct a polynomial-time reduction $f$ from instances of $L$ to instances of $\Pi$. - Show that YES-instances of $L$ (with optimum 1) map to instances of $\Pi$ with optimum $\geq 1$. - Show that NO-instances of $L$ (with optimum $\leq s$) map to instances of $\Pi$ with optimum $< 1/c$. - Conclude that any $c$-approximation algorithm for $\Pi$ would solve the gap problem, hence NP-hard. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *State Håstad's optimal inapproximability result for Max-3SAT. What complexity-theoretic assumption underlies this result?* **Model Answer:** **Håstad's Theorem (2001):** For every $\varepsilon > 0$, it is NP-hard to approximate Max-3SAT within a factor of $\frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon$. The underlying complexity-theoretic assumption is $\text{P} \neq \text{NP}$. The proof relies on a strong form of the PCP theorem (specifically, the *optimal PCP* with parameters $\text{PCP}[1-\varepsilon, 7/8+\varepsilon]$) and a careful *Fourier-analytic* analysis of the long code. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the PCP theorem and its role in establishing inapproximability results. Use the formal statement: $\text{NP} = \text{PCP}[O(\log n), O(1)]$. (7 Marks) **(b)** Using the PCP theorem, prove that Set Cover cannot be approximated within a factor of $c \log n$ for some constant $c > 0$, unless $\text{P} = \text{NP}$. State the assumptions and outline the gap-preserving reduction. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) The PCP Theorem — Formal Statement and Role** The PCP (Probabilistically Checkable Proofs) theorem, in its classical form due to Arora, Lund, Motwani, Sudan, and Szegedy (1998), states: $$\text{NP} = \text{PCP}[O(\log n), O(1)]$$ This means every language $L \in \text{NP}$ has a verifier $V$ such that: - For input $x$ of length $n$, $V$ uses $r = O(\log n)$ random bits. - $V$ queries the proof $\pi$ at $q = O(1)$ locations. - If $x \in L$, there exists a proof $\pi$ accepted with probability $1$. - If $x \notin L$, every proof $\pi$ is accepted with probability at most $\frac{1}{2}$. **Role in Inapproximability:** The PCP theorem provides a *gap* in the acceptance probability: YES-instances have acceptance $1$, while NO-instances have acceptance $\leq \frac{1}{2}$. By viewing the PCP verifier as a constraint satisfaction problem (CSP), this gap translates directly to hardness of approximating the maximum fraction of satisfiable constraints. From this foundation, gap-preserving reductions carry the hardness to specific optimization problems. **(b) Set Cover Inapproximability** We prove the hardness following Lund–Yannakakis and Feige: **Step 1 — Start with Gap-3SAT:** By Håstad's strong PCP theorem, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the gap problem $\text{Gap-}3\text{SAT}_{1, 1-\varepsilon}$ is NP-hard: distinguish whether a 3-CNF has all clauses satisfiable or at most a $(1-\varepsilon)$-fraction satisfiable. **Step 2 — Reduction to Label Cover:** Using standard transformations (constraint-graph reductions, alphabet extension), construct a Label Cover instance: - Bipartite graph $G = (A \cup B, E)$, with $|A| = n$, $|B| = m$. - Labels: each $a \in A$ has label set $[k]$, each $b \in B$ has label set $[k]$. - Constraints: each edge $e = (a, b)$ has a constraint $\pi_e$ requiring $\sigma(a) = \pi_e(\sigma(b))$ for some assignments. **Step 3 — Gap in Label Cover:** YES-instances admit a labeling satisfying *all* edges. NO-instances admit a labeling satisfying at most a $\frac{1}{N^\delta}$-fraction of edges, for some $\delta > 0$ depending on $\varepsilon$. **Step 4 — Reduce Label Cover to Set Cover:** Construct universe $U = E$ (edges of label cover). For each pair $(a, \ell)$ where $a \in A, \ell \in [k]$, define set $S_{a,\ell} = \{e = (a, b) : \pi_e(\ell) \in [k]\}$, i.e., edges $e$ where the constraint can be satisfied when $a$ is labeled $\ell$. **Step 5 — Bound Cover Sizes:** - **YES case:** The satisfying labeling picks at most one set per vertex of $A$, giving cover size $\leq |A| = n$. - **NO case:** Any cover must include $\Omega(N^\delta \cdot |E|)$ sets, because each set covers at most $k^2$ edges. **Step 6 — Compute Gap:** The ratio between cover sizes in NO and YES cases is $\Omega(N^\delta) = \Omega(c \log N)$ for some constant $c = c(\delta)$. Any polynomial-time $c \log n$-approximation algorithm for Set Cover would solve Gap-3SAT, hence NP-hard. $\blacksquare$ **Conclusion:** Set Cover is hard to approximate within $c \log n$ for some universal constant $c > 0$, assuming $\text{P} \neq \text{NP}$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** Define the classes FPTAS, PTAS, APX, and APX-completeness. Show their containment relations with a brief justification. (7 Marks) **(b)** Prove that Max Independent Set is inapproximable within factor $n^{1-\varepsilon}$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, unless $\text{P} = \text{NP}$. Outline the gap-preserving reduction. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Approximation Classes** | Class | Definition | |---|---| | **NPO** | Class of all NP optimization problems. | | **APX** | Problems admitting a polynomial-time $c$-approximation algorithm for some constant $c$. | | **PTAS** | Problems admitting a $(1 + \varepsilon)$-approximation algorithm for every $\varepsilon > 0$, with runtime polynomial in $n$ but potentially exponential in $\frac{1}{\varepsilon}$. | | **FPTAS** | PTAS with runtime polynomial in *both* $n$ and $\frac{1}{\varepsilon}$. | | **APX-hard** | Problems to which every APX problem can be AP-reduced. | | **APX-complete** | APX-hard and in APX. | **Containments:** - $\text{FPTAS} \subseteq \text{PTAS} \subseteq \text{APX} \subseteq \text{NPO}$ **Justifications:** - Any FPTAS is trivially a PTAS by definition. - Any PTAS with approximation ratio $(1+\varepsilon)$ for all $\varepsilon > 0$ is also a constant-factor approximation (set $\varepsilon = 1$). - APX ⊆ NPO by definition. **(b) Inapproximability of Maximum Independent Set** **Step 1 — Start from Gap-3SAT$(1, 1-\varepsilon)$:** By the PCP theorem, this is NP-hard. **Step 2 — Convert to Gap-Independent Set:** Use a *canonical* reduction. Given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi$ with $n$ variables and $m$ clauses, construct a graph $G_\varphi$: - For each variable $x_i$ and each truth value $T \in \{0, 1\}$, create a vertex $v_{i,T}$. - For each clause $C_j = (\ell_{j,1} \vee \ell_{j,2} \vee \ell_{j,3})$, create a triangle of vertices $w_{j,1}, w_{j,2}, w_{j,3}$. - Connect a variable vertex $v_{i,T}$ to a clause vertex $w_{j,k}$ with an edge if assigning $x_i = T$ does *not* falsify the $k$-th literal of $C_j$. **Step 3 — Analyze Independent Set Size:** - An independent set corresponds to a partial truth assignment. - For *satisfiable* $\varphi$: there exists a satisfying assignment $x^*$; for each $i$, pick the vertex $v_{i, x^*_i}$. For each clause, all three clause-vertices are non-adjacent to the chosen variable vertices, so we can pick all three clause-vertices. Total: $n + 3m$. - For $\varphi$ where every assignment satisfies at most $(1-\varepsilon)m$ clauses: any assignment falsifies at least $\varepsilon m$ clauses; the corresponding independent set has size $\leq n + 3(1-\varepsilon)m$. **Step 4 — Compute Gap:** The ratio of independent set sizes is: $$\frac{n + 3(1-\varepsilon)m}{n + 3m} \leq 1 - \frac{3\varepsilon m}{n + 3m}$$ For dense enough formulas (with $m \geq n$), this gives a multiplicative gap of $\Omega(1 - \varepsilon)$ — not yet $n^{1-\varepsilon}$. **Step 5 — Boost the Gap via Håstad's PCP:** Using Håstad's optimal PCP $(1 - \varepsilon, 7/8 + \varepsilon)$ and a *super-constant* number of repetitions, we amplify the gap to: $$\text{OPT}_{\text{YES}} \geq N \quad \text{and} \quad \text{OPT}_{\text{NO}} \leq N^{1-\delta}$$ for some $\delta > 0$, on a graph with $N = n^{O(1)}$ vertices. **Step 6 — Conclude:** Any $n^{1-\varepsilon}$-approximation algorithm for Max Independent Set would distinguish YES from NO instances, solving Gap-3SAT, hence NP-hard. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing "no approximation" with "inapproximable within factor X"** — A problem may admit a *terrible* approximation but still be approximable. Inapproximability requires proving a *specific factor* is unachievable. > > 2. **Forgetting the conditional nature** — Inapproximability results are *conditional* on $\text{P} \neq \text{NP}$, UGC, or ETH. Students who claim unconditional results lose 2–3 marks immediately. > > 3. **Mixing up PCP Theorem variants** — The "basic" PCP theorem ($\text{NP} = \text{PCP}[\log n, O(1)]$) is *not* sufficient for optimal inapproximability. Håstad's *optimal* PCP with parameters $(1-\varepsilon, 7/8+\varepsilon)$ is needed for tight bounds. > > 4. **Forgetting gap amplification steps** — A direct reduction from 3SAT gives only a *weak* gap. Students must explicitly invoke parallel repetition (Raz) or alphabet extension (Dinur) to amplify. > > 5. **Confusing APX-completeness with NP-completeness** — APX-completeness refers to the *approximation* preserving reduction class, not decision problem class. The "complete" problems are different. > > 6. **Skipping the "OPT" calculation** — In derivation questions, students often omit the explicit computation of OPT in YES and NO cases, which carries 2–3 marks. > > 7. **Stating without proof that "PCP implies inapproximability"** — Examiners want to see the *gap-preserving reduction* explicitly constructed, not just an appeal to authority. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Inapproximability** = provable lower bound on approximation ratio, conditional on $\text{P} \neq \text{NP}$. - ✅ **PCP Theorem** ($\text{NP} = \text{PCP}[\log n, O(1)]$) is the *engine* — converts decision hardness to optimization hardness. - ✅ **Gap problems** are the *language* of inapproximability: $\text{Gap-}c\text{-}L$ NP-hard $\Rightarrow$ $L$-type optimization is $c$-inapproximable. - ✅ **Gap-preserving reductions** are the *vehicles* that carry hardness from PCP to specific problems. - ✅ **Håstad's Theorem (2001)** — Max-3SAT is hard to approximate beyond $\frac{7}{8}$ (tight). - ✅ **Feige (1998)** — Set Cover hard beyond $(1-\varepsilon)\ln n$. - ✅ **Zuckerman (2006)** — Independent Set hard beyond $\frac{n}{2^{(\log n)^{1-\varepsilon}}}$. - ✅ **Classes hierarchy:** $\text{FPTAS} \subseteq \text{PTAS} \subseteq \text{APX} \subseteq \text{NPO}$. - ✅ **APX-complete problems** include Metric TSP, Steiner Tree, Max-SAT, Vertex Cover. - ✅ **Reductions used:** L-reductions, FPTAS-reductions, AP-reductions (preserve APX-membership). - ✅ **Unique Games Conjecture (UGC)** — Khot (2002) — strongest known hardness assumption, gives tight bounds for many problems (Max Cut, Vertex Cover, Metric TSP). - ✅ **Dinur's Proof (2006)** — Combinatorial (graph-theoretic) proof of the PCP theorem, alternative to the algebraic proof. - ✅ **Parallel Repetition (Raz 1998)** — Amplifies the gap in two-prover games; foundational for tight hardness. - ✅ **Long Code + Fourier Analysis** — The "inner workings" of optimal inapproximability proofs. - ✅ **Open Problems**: Tight bound for Set Cover remains open. Gap between $\frac{3}{2}$ and $\frac{4}{3}$ for Metric TSP. Gap between $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{2}$ for Vertex Cover under standard assumptions. - ✅ **Practical Takeaway**: When a problem is APX-hard, *no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee a constant-factor approximation*, so engineering applications must use heuristics, ILP solvers, or approximation algorithms that exploit special structure. --- > **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: INTRODUCTION TO INAPPROXIMABILITY**

topics.title: Reductions and Hardness of Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reductions and Hardness of Approximation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reductions in Approximation Algorithms (Definition)** > A *reduction* in the context of approximation algorithms is a polynomial-time transformation $\mathcal{R}$ that maps an instance $I$ of a source optimization problem $\Pi_1$ to an instance $\mathcal{R}(I)$ of a target problem $\Pi_2$, such that the **optimum values** and the **quality of solutions** are related in a quantitatively controllable way. Reductions are the principal tool used to **propagate inapproximability** (or approximability) from one problem to another. There are three principal classes of reductions used in the hardness-of-approximation theory: 1. **L-Reduction (Linear Reduction)** — Papadimitriou & Yannakakis (1991). Preserves approximation ratios **linearly**. 2. **AP-Reduction (Approximation-Preserving Reduction)** — A general framework that preserves approximation guarantees modulo a controlled factor. 3. **Gap-Reduction** — Used in the **PCP-based** hardness regime; introduces a *promise gap* between the optimum and near-optimum instances. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping** > This topic satisfies **CO4** of PECST749: *"Analyze inapproximability results and reductions for NP-hard optimization problems."* It sits at the intersection of complexity theory (PCP theorem) and algorithm design (approximation schemes). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Family Tree of Hard Problems"** > Imagine a royal lineage. If a king is declared *incorruptible* (impossible to bribe), then by blood relation (lineage), his sons, grandsons, and descendants inherit this trait. Similarly, if we *prove* that one NP-hard problem (say, **MAX-3SAT**) cannot be approximated within factor $(1-\epsilon)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then via a **reduction** we can show that *descendant* problems (like **MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET**, **MIN-VERTEX-COVER**, **MIN-SET-COVER**) inherit inapproximability bounds. > > The reduction is the *lineage link* — it shows that a good approximation for the child would yield a good approximation for the parent, contradicting the parent's known hardness. Therefore the child must be just as hard (or harder) to approximate. ### 1.3 Standard Inapproximability Metrics (Bold Constants) The key inapproximability ratios students must memorize for KTU valuation: - **MIN-VERTEX-COVER**: NP-hard to approximate within factor **$2 - \epsilon$** (assuming $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$). - **MIN-SET-COVER**: NP-hard to approximate within factor **$(1 - o(1)) \cdot \ln n$**. - **MAX-3SAT**: NP-hard to approximate within factor **$\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon$**. - **MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET**: NP-hard to approximate within factor **$n^{1-\epsilon}$**. - **MIN-TSP (general)**: NP-hard to approximate within factor **any polynomial function** of $n$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 L-Reduction — Structured Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **L-Reduction (Papadimitriou & Yannakakis, 1991)** > Problem $\Pi_1$ is **L-reducible** to problem $\Pi_2$, written $\Pi_1 \le_L \Pi_2$, if there exist two polynomial-time computable functions $f$ and $g$ and constants $\alpha, \beta > 0$ such that for every instance $I$ of $\Pi_1$: > > **(C1 — Optimum Preservation):** $\mathrm{OPT}_2(f(I)) \le \alpha \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_1(I)$. > > **(C2 — Approximation Preservation):** For any feasible solution $S'$ of $f(I)$, the function $g$ maps it back to a feasible solution $S = g(S', I)$ of $I$ with > > $$\vert \mathrm{OPT}_1(I) - c(S) \vert \le \beta \cdot \vert \mathrm{OPT}_2(f(I)) - c(S') \vert$$ **Interpretation of Conditions:** - **Condition (C1)** controls how the optimum can *grow* under the reduction. It says the new optimum is at most a constant factor $\alpha$ larger. - **Condition (C2)** says the *gap* between optimum and the recovered solution in $\Pi_1$ is at most $\beta$ times the gap in $\Pi_2$. This is what makes the reduction "approximation-preserving." > [!NOTE] > **Composability Theorem (KTU High-Yield)** > If $\Pi_1 \le_L \Pi_2$ with constants $(\alpha, \beta)$ and $\Pi_2 \le_L \Pi_3$ with constants $(\gamma, \delta)$, then $\Pi_1 \le_L \Pi_3$ with constants $(\alpha\gamma, \beta\delta)$. ### 2.2 AP-Reduction — Structured Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **AP-Reduction (Approximation-Preserving Reduction)** > A problem $\Pi_1$ is **AP-reducible** to $\Pi_2$ (written $\Pi_1 \le_{AP} \Pi_2$) if there exist polynomial-time functions $f$ and $g$ such that: > > 1. $f$ maps instance $I$ of $\Pi_1$ to instance $f(I)$ of $\Pi_2$. > 2. $g$ maps a $c$-approximate solution of $f(I)$ to a $h(c)$-approximate solution of $I$. > 3. The function $h$ is non-decreasing, and $h(c) = c$ for $c \ge 1$ (for minimization) or $h(c) = c$ for $c \le 1$ (for maximization). > > The reduction is **strict** (S-reduction) if $h(c) = c$ exactly, and is **linear** (L-reduction) if $h(c) = 1 + \beta(c - 1)$ for some $\beta > 0$. ### 2.3 Gap-Reduction & The PCP Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gap-Reduction Framework** > Given a decision problem $L$, define the **gap problem** $\mathrm{Gap}_c \, L$: distinguish between instances with YES-answer having objective $\ge c \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ and instances with NO-answer having objective $< \mathrm{OPT}$. A gap-reduction from $L$ to $\Pi$ proves that $\Pi$ is NP-hard to approximate within factor $c$. **The PCP Theorem (Arora et al., 1998):** $$\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[\log n, 1]$$ This says every NP proof can be verified by reading a *constant* number of bits, with completeness $1$ and soundness $\frac{1}{2}$. This is the cornerstone of modern hardness of approximation. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Hardness of Approximation** | Problem | Best Known Approximation | Inapproximability Bound | Assumption | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | MIN VERTEX COVER | $2 - \frac{\log \log n}{2\log n}$ (Halldórsson) | $1.3606$ (Dinur) | $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$ | | MIN SET COVER | $(\ln n)$ greedy | $(1 - o(1))\ln n$ | $\mathbf{NP} \not\subseteq \mathbf{DTIME}(n^{\log \log n})$ | | MAX 3SAT | $7/8$ (random) | $7/8 + \epsilon$ | $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$ | | MAX INDEPENDENT SET | $O\!\left(\frac{n}{\log^2 n}\right)$ (Ais) | $n^{1-\epsilon}$ | $\mathbf{NP} \neq \mathbf{ZPP}$ | | MIN TSP (general) | $O(\log n)$ (Arora) | $1 + \epsilon$ for any $\epsilon$ | $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$ | | MIN DOMINATING SET | $\log n$ (greedy) | $\log n$ | $\mathbf{NP} \neq \mathbf{DTIME}(n^{\log \log n})$ | | MAX CLIQUE | $O\!\left(\frac{n}{\log^2 n}\right)$ | $n^{1-\epsilon}$ | $\mathbf{NP} \neq \mathbf{ZPP}$ | **L-Reduction Composition Rule:** $$\Pi_1 \le_L^{\alpha_1, \beta_1} \Pi_2 \le_L^{\alpha_2, \beta_2} \Pi_3 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Pi_1 \le_L^{\alpha_1\alpha_2, \beta_1\beta_2} \Pi_3$$ **Gap Theorem (Håstad):** For every $\epsilon > 0$, MAX-3SAT is NP-hard to approximate within ratio $\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **VLSI Design**: Hardness of MIN-VERTEX-COVER and MIN-DOMINATING-SET directly translates to intractability of transistor-count minimization and fault-coverage maximization. - **Bioinformatics**: L-reductions from SET-COVER model probe-selection in DNA microarrays and primer-design in PCR. - **Network Engineering**: Hardness of MAX-CLIQUE reflects intractability of dense-subgraph extraction in social/communication networks. - **Cloud Computing**: Inapproximability of SET-COVER bounds the optimality of VM consolidation and rule-mining in auto-scaling systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivation — L-Reduction from MIN-VERTEX-COVER to MIN-INDEPENDENT-SET > [!NOTE] > **Why this example?** It is the *canonical* L-reduction taught in KTU board papers. Mastering this shows examiners that you can convert a minimization problem to another minimization problem with explicit bounds on the constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$. **Source:** MIN-VERTEX-COVER (VC) — given graph $G = (V, E)$, find minimum-size set $C \subseteq V$ covering every edge. **Target:** MIN-INDEPENDENT-SET (MIS) — given graph $G' = (V', E')$, find minimum-size set $I \subseteq V'$ with no two vertices adjacent. **Note:** The classical *complement* reduction from VC to MAX-IS preserves the optimum but we derive the L-reduction to **MIN-IS** (which is trivially solvable — the minimum IS is a single vertex). To obtain a *non-trivial* L-reduction, we instead show: **L-Reduction: MAX-3SAT $\le_L$ MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET** This is the high-value derivation asked in KTU exams. --- **Setup (MAX-3SAT instance):** Given $n$ variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and $m$ clauses $C_1, \ldots, C_m$, each clause has exactly 3 literals. We want to maximize the number of satisfied clauses. **Step 1: Construct the graph $G = (V, E)$.** The graph $G$ has a vertex for every *satisfying assignment of each clause*. A clause with $k$ satisfying assignments contributes $k$ vertices. Total $|V| = 7m$ (since each 3-CNF clause has exactly 7 satisfying assignments out of 8). **Step 2: Add edges.** For every pair of vertices $u, v \in V$ that correspond to **inconsistent** partial assignments (i.e., they assign different Boolean values to the same variable), add an edge $(u, v) \in E$. Formally, vertex $u$ corresponds to an assignment $\sigma_u : \text{vars}(C_i) \to \{0, 1\}$ satisfying $C_i$. Two vertices are adjacent iff $\sigma_u$ and $\sigma_v$ disagree on a common variable. **Step 3: Verify Condition (C1) — Optimum Preservation.** Let $S$ be the set of vertices forming a consistent assignment across the *entire* formula (i.e., a global truth assignment). This is exactly an independent set in $G$ of size equal to the number of clauses satisfied by that assignment. The *global* consistent assignment that satisfies the maximum number of clauses corresponds to a maximum independent set in $G$. We claim $\alpha = 1$. Note: $$\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) \ge m$$ because a global assignment either satisfies a clause or does not, and an IS of size $m$ corresponds to a fully consistent truth assignment picking the right vertex for each clause. And: $$\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) \le m$$ because the projection of a global truth assignment onto each clause gives at most one vertex per clause, hence at most $m$ vertices in the IS. Therefore: $$\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) = m = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}}(\phi)$$ This gives $\alpha = 1$. **Step 4: Verify Condition (C2) — Gap Preservation.** Given an independent set $S'$ of size $k$ in $G$, we want to extract a truth assignment $T = g(S')$ that satisfies at least some number of clauses. Since $S'$ is an independent set, *no two vertices in $S'$ disagree* on any variable. So all vertices in $S'$ can be *merged* into a single partial truth assignment $T$ consistent with all of them. The number of clauses satisfied by $T$ is exactly $|S'|$ (each vertex in $S'$ lies in some clause, and $T$ satisfies precisely those clauses). So the recovered solution satisfies $c(T) = \vert S' \vert$ clauses. The gap in the IS instance is: $$m - \vert S' \vert = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) - c(S')$$ The gap in the 3SAT instance is: $$m - c(T) = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}} - c(T) = m - \vert S' \vert$$ Therefore: $$\vert \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}} - c(T) \vert = 1 \cdot \vert \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}} - c(S') \vert$$ This gives $\beta = 1$. **Step 5: Conclude the L-reduction.** We have shown that for every $\phi$, the graph $G = f(\phi)$ satisfies: $$\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}}(\phi)$$ $$\vert \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}}(\phi) - c(T) \vert \le 1 \cdot \vert \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) - c(S') \vert$$ Therefore MAX-3SAT $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ MAX-IS. The reduction is *tight* with $\alpha = \beta = 1$. --- ### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Hardness of MIN-SET-COVER > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Show MIN-SET-COVER cannot be approximated within factor $(1 - o(1)) \ln n$ unless $\mathbf{NP} \subseteq \mathbf{DTIME}(n^{\log \log n})$. **Step 1: Recall the greedy lower bound.** The greedy algorithm gives approximation ratio $H(d) \le \ln d + 1$ where $d$ is the maximum set size. We show this is essentially *tight*. **Step 2: Set up the L-reduction from MIN-DOMINATING-SET.** It is known (Feige, 1998) that MIN-DOMINATING-SET cannot be approximated within factor $(1 - \epsilon)\ln n$. We show MIN-DOMINATING-SET $\le_L$ MIN-SET-COVER. **Construction of the SET-COVER instance:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$: - Create a universe $U = V$ (one element per vertex). - For each vertex $v \in V$, create a set $S_v = N[v]$ (the closed neighbourhood of $v$). - The family $\mathcal{F} = \{S_v : v \in V\}$. **Step 3: Verify the reduction.** - Any dominating set $D$ in $G$ corresponds to sets $\{S_v : v \in D\}$ that cover $U$ (since $D$ dominates every vertex $u$, there exists $v \in D$ with $u \in N[v] = S_v$). - Conversely, any set cover $\{S_{v_1}, \ldots, S_{v_k}\}$ induces a dominating set $\{v_1, \ldots, v_k\}$ in $G$. So the two problems have the same optimum value: $\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{SC}}(\mathcal{F}) = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{DS}}(G)$. This gives $\alpha = 1$. **Step 4: Gap preservation.** Let $C' \subseteq \mathcal{F}$ be a set cover of size $k$ with $k > \mathrm{OPT}$. The corresponding vertices $D' = \{v : S_v \in C'\}$ form a dominating set of size $k$. The gap in the DS instance equals the gap in the SC instance: $$\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{DS}} - \vert D' \vert = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{SC}} - \vert C' \vert$$ So $\beta = 1$. **Step 5: Conclude.** By L-reduction, if MIN-SET-COVER had a $\rho$-approximation, then MIN-DOMINATING-SET would have a $\rho$-approximation, contradicting the known $(1 - \epsilon)\ln n$ inapproximability. Hence MIN-SET-COVER is NP-hard to approximate within $(1 - \epsilon)\ln n$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reduction Lattice > [!NOTE] > **Reduction Lattice (Examiners often sketch this)** | Source $\backslash$ Target | MAX-IS | MIN-VC | MIN-DS | MIN-SC | MAX-3SAT | MAX-CUT | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | MAX-3SAT | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | — | $\equiv$ | $\le_L$ | | MAX-IS | $\equiv$ | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ (complement) | — | — | — | — | | MIN-VC | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | $\equiv$ | — | — | — | — | | MIN-DS | — | — | $\equiv$ | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | — | — | | MIN-SC | — | — | $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ | $\equiv$ | — | — | | MAX-CUT | — | — | — | — | $\le_L$ | $\equiv$ | *Read row to column: "source is L-reducible to target."* $\equiv$ denotes polynomial-time equivalence (not just L-equivalence). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Reduction Hierarchy (L-Reduction DAG) > [!NOTE] > The diagram below traces the *lineage* of inapproximability: an L-reduction $\Pi_1 \to \Pi_2$ means hardness of $\Pi_2$ propagates to $\Pi_1$. This is the visual counterpart of the analytical matrix in Section 3.3. ```mermaid flowchart TD baseA["MAX 3SAT
Hardness 7/8 plus eps"] baseB["LABEL GAP Problem
Hastad 3 Bit PCP"] baseC["INDEP SET
Gap via Long Code"] baseD["VERTEX COVER
Complement of IS"] baseE["DOMINATING SET
Feige 1998"] baseF["SET COVER
Tight ln n Bound"] baseG["CLIQUE
n to the 1 minus eps"] baseH["GRAPH COLOURING
NP hard within n to eps"] baseI["MIN TSP
No poly approximation"] baseB -->|FGLSS Reduction| baseA baseB -->|Long Code| baseC baseA -->|L Reduction alpha 1 beta 1| baseD baseC -->|Complement| baseD baseC -->|Self Embedding| baseG baseD -->|N closed Neighbourhood| baseE baseE -->|Identity Family| baseF baseG -->|Complement of Colouring| baseH baseF -.Inapproximability.-> baseI baseH -.Inapproximability.-> baseI ``` ### 4.2 Approximation Landscape — Practical vs. Hardness Gap > [!NOTE] > This schematic visualizes the **approximation gap**: the *vertical distance* between the best-known approximation ratio (lower curve) and the proven inapproximability threshold (upper curve). When the curves *touch*, the problem is essentially solved up to the hardness limit. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Hardness ["Proven NP Hardness Upper Bound"] hvc["VC Hardness 1.36"] hsc["SC Hardness ln n"] h3sat["3SAT Hardness 7/8"] hds["DS Hardness ln n"] end subgraph Practice ["Best Known Approximation"] avc["VC Practice 2 minus o 1"] asc["SC Practice ln n"] a3sat["3SAT Practice 7/8"] ads["DS Practice ln n"] end subgraph Gap ["The Open Gap"] gvc["Open for VC"] gsc["Tight for SC"] g3sat["Tight for 3SAT"] gds["Tight for DS"] end Hardness --> Gap Practice --> Gap ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an L-Reduction > [!NOTE] > Use this as the mental blueprint when examiners ask "describe the structure of an L-reduction." This is the **fallback functional architecture** in lieu of an actual physical drawing. ```mermaid flowchart TD inp["Input Instance I of Pi1"] block1["f
Polynomial Time Encoder"] out1["Instance J equals f of I of Pi2"] solver["Solver for Pi2
c Approximate"] out2["Solution S prime of J"] block2["g
Solution Decoder"] out3["Solution S equals g of S prime I of Pi1"] bound1["Condition C1
OPT 2 of f of I at most alpha times OPT 1 of I"] bound2["Condition C2
Gap in Pi1 at most beta times Gap in Pi2"] inp --> block1 block1 --> out1 out1 --> solver solver --> out2 out2 --> block2 block2 --> out3 bound1 -.Monitors.-> out1 bound2 -.Monitors.-> out3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define an L-reduction between two optimization problems. State the two conditions with mathematical precision.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Bullet Points):** - An L-reduction $\Pi_1 \le_L \Pi_2$ is a polynomial-time transformation $f$ of instances and $g$ of solutions with constants $\alpha, \beta > 0$. **(1 mark)** - **Condition (C1):** $\mathrm{OPT}_2(f(I)) \le \alpha \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_1(I)$ for every instance $I$ of $\Pi_1$. **(1 mark)** - **Condition (C2):** Given a $c$-approximate solution $S'$ of $f(I)$, $g(S', I)$ is a $h(c)$-approximate solution of $I$ where $h(c) \le 1 + \beta(c-1)$ (for minimization). **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the PCP theorem. How does it imply hardness of approximation for MAX-3SAT?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **PCP Theorem (1 mark):** $\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[\log n, 1]$. Every NP proof can be verified by reading $O(1)$ bits. - **Connection to MAX-3SAT (1 mark):** Håstad's *3-bit PCP* shows it is NP-hard to distinguish 3SAT instances with all clauses satisfiable (completeness $1$) versus at most $\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon$ satisfiable (soundness $\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon$). - **Hardness implication (1 mark):** A polynomial-time $\left(\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon\right)$-approximation would *break* the gap, so MAX-3SAT is NP-hard to approximate within $\frac{7}{8} + \epsilon$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — L-Reduction Mastery** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** *Define an L-reduction formally. Show that MAX-3SAT is L-reducible to MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET with constants $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$. Clearly describe the instance transformation and the solution recovery.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Using the L-reduction in part (a), or otherwise, prove that MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET is NP-hard to approximate within factor $n^{1-\epsilon}$ for any $\epsilon > 0$. Discuss the connection with the PCP theorem.* **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the L-reduction definition with both conditions (C1) and (C2) and constants $(\alpha, \beta)$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Construct the instance transformation $f$. Given a 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with $m$ clauses, build graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - Each clause $C_i$ contributes 7 vertices (one for each of the 7 satisfying assignments to its 3 literals). - Total $|V| = 7m$. - Edge $(u, v) \in E$ iff $u, v$ correspond to inconsistent partial assignments (disagree on some variable). - **Step 3 (2 marks):** Verify (C1). Show $\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{IS}}(G) = m = \mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{3SAT}}(\phi)$, hence $\alpha = 1$. The key argument: an IS corresponds to a globally consistent truth assignment, and the IS size equals the number of clauses satisfied. - **Step 4 (2 marks):** Verify (C2). Given IS $S'$ of size $k$, recover a truth assignment $T$ by merging all vertices in $S'$ (possible because they are consistent). The recovered solution satisfies exactly $k$ clauses, so the gap is preserved: $\beta = 1$. **Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:** - **Step 1 (2 marks):** State the inapproximability of MAX-3SAT from Håstad's 3-bit PCP theorem: for any $\epsilon > 0$, it is NP-hard to distinguish MAX-3SAT instances with optimum $m$ versus optimum $\le (7/8 + \epsilon)m$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the L-reduction. Given a $(1 - \delta)$-approximation to MAX-IS (i.e., finds IS of size $\ge (1-\delta) \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$), use $g$ to recover a $(1 - \delta)$-approximation to MAX-3SAT with the same gap. The constants $\alpha = \beta = 1$ propagate the gap unchanged. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** For a graph on $N = 7m$ vertices, an $n^{1-\epsilon}$ approximation gives IS of size $\ge N^{1-\epsilon} = (7m)^{1-\epsilon} \approx m^{1-\epsilon}$, which exceeds $m - (7/8)m = m/8$ gap when $\epsilon$ is small. This would distinguish the PCP gap, contradicting NP-hardness. - **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude: under $\mathbf{NP} \neq \mathbf{ZPP}$, MAX-IS is hard to approximate within $n^{1-\epsilon}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — AP-Reduction & Hardness Propagation** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** *Define an AP-reduction. Distinguish it from an L-reduction with appropriate examples. Show that MIN-VERTEX-COVER is AP-reducible to MIN-SET-COVER.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Using the L-reduction between MIN-DOMINATING-SET and MIN-SET-COVER (with $\alpha = \beta = 1$), derive the inapproximability bound for MIN-SET-COVER. Show that the greedy algorithm achieves a matching bound of $\ln n - \ln \ln n + \Theta(1)$.* **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:** - **Step 1 (2 marks):** Define AP-reduction: polynomial-time $f, g$ such that a $c$-approximate solution of $f(I)$ maps to a $h(c)$-approximate solution of $I$, with $h$ non-decreasing and $h(1) = 1$. - **Step 2 (1 mark):** State the relationship: every L-reduction is an AP-reduction, but AP-reductions are more general. Strict reductions (S-reductions) preserve the ratio exactly. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** Construct the AP-reduction VC $\to$ SC. Given graph $G = (V, E)$, create a universe $U = E$ (one element per edge) and for each vertex $v$ a set $S_v = \{\text{edges incident to } v\}$. - **Step 4 (2 marks):** Verify the reduction. A vertex cover of size $k$ corresponds to a set cover of size $k$ (each set $S_v$ covers exactly the edges incident to $v$). Conversely, a set cover of size $k$ yields a vertex cover of size at most $k$ (select the corresponding vertices). So the optimum is preserved exactly, and a $c$-approximation yields a $c$-approximation, giving a strict S/AP-reduction. **Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall Feige's 1998 result: MIN-DOMINATING-SET is NP-hard to approximate within $(1 - \epsilon)\ln n$ for any $\epsilon > 0$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the L-reduction DS $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ SC (constructed in Section 3.2). Since the constants are $(\alpha, \beta) = (1, 1)$, a $c$-approximation for SC yields a $c$-approximation for DS. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** Conclude: if SC had a $c(n)$-approximation with $c(n) = o(\ln n)$, then DS would also have a $o(\ln n)$-approximation, contradicting Feige's result. Hence SC is NP-hard to approximate within $(1 - \epsilon)\ln n$. - **Step 4 (2 marks):** Show the greedy matching upper bound. The greedy algorithm for SC picks the set covering the maximum number of uncovered elements at each step. After $k$ steps, the number of uncovered elements is at most $m \cdot (1 - 1/d_{\max})^k$ where $d_{\max}$ is the max set size. Setting this $\le 1$ gives $k = O(d_{\max} \log m)$. A refined analysis using harmonic numbers $H(d_{\max})$ yields: $$c_{\text{greedy}} = H(d_{\max}) \le \ln d_{\max} + 1 \approx \ln n - \ln \ln n + \Theta(1)$$ matching the lower bound up to lower-order terms. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern)** > > 1. **Forgetting the constants $\alpha, \beta$ explicitly.** Many students write "$\mathrm{OPT}_2 \le \mathrm{OPT}_1$" without stating the multiplicative constant. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this. > 2. **Confusing AP-reduction with L-reduction.** An AP-reduction is *more general*; every L-reduction is an AP-reduction, but not vice versa. Stating the reverse is a **−1 mark** error. > 3. **Skipping the "approximation preservation" check in (C2).** Students often verify (C1) but skip (C2). This costs **3 marks** in 14-mark questions. > 4. **Confusing MAX-INDEPENDENT-SET with MIN-INDEPENDENT-SET.** MAX-IS is NP-hard; MIN-IS is trivially solvable (pick any vertex). Reading the problem variant carefully is essential. > 5. **Omitting the role of the PCP theorem in MAX-3SAT hardness.** The connection is Håstad's *optimal* PCP, not just the basic PCP theorem. Citing the wrong theorem loses **2 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **L-Reduction** $\Pi_1 \le_L^{\alpha, \beta} \Pi_2$: two conditions (C1) optimum growth bounded by $\alpha$, (C2) gap preserved within factor $\beta$. - **AP-Reduction** $\Pi_1 \le_{AP} \Pi_2$: a $c$-approximate solution of target maps to a $h(c)$-approximate solution of source with $h$ non-decreasing, $h(1) = 1$. - **PCP Theorem**: $\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[\log n, 1]$ — cornerstone of modern inapproximability. - **Håstad's 3-Bit PCP**: gives tight $7/8 + \epsilon$ inapproximability of MAX-3SAT. - **MAX-3SAT $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ MAX-IS**: tight L-reduction; yields $n^{1-\epsilon}$ hardness of MAX-IS. - **MIN-DS $\le_L^{(1,1)}$ MIN-SC**: tight L-reduction; yields $(1-\epsilon)\ln n$ hardness of MIN-SC. - **MIN-VC $\le_{S}$ MIN-SC** (strict): identity family construction. - **Composition rule**: $\alpha_{\text{comp}} = \alpha_1 \alpha_2$, $\beta_{\text{comp}} = \beta_1 \beta_2$. - **Greedy for SC**: matches the $\ln n$ hardness up to $O(\ln \ln n)$ — *asymptotically tight*. - **Engineering applications**: VLSI fault coverage, bio-informatics probe design, cloud VM consolidation, network dense-subgraph extraction. - **Common exam trap**: stating hardness *results* without the *reduction* and its constants.

topics.title: PCP Theorem and Its Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST749 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PCP Theorem and Its Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of PCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCP (Probabilistically Checkable Proofs)** is a model of computation in which a probabilistic polynomial-time verifier examines a purported proof by querying only a *small number of randomly chosen bits* of it. The class **PCP[r(n), q(n)]** denotes the set of languages decidable by a verifier that uses at most **r(n) random bits** and queries at most **q(n) bits** of the proof, where **n** is the input length.

A language **L** is in **PCP[r(n), q(n)]** if there exists a polynomial-time probabilistic verifier **V** with oracle access to a proof string **π** such that for every input **x ∈ {0,1}ⁿ**:

- **Completeness:** If **x ∈ L**, then there exists a proof **π** for which **Pr[V^π(x) accepts] = 1**.
- **Soundness:** If **x ∉ L**, then for every proof **π**, **Pr[V^π(x) accepts] ≤ s**, where **s < 1** (typically **s = 1/2**).

### 1.2 The PCP Theorem — Formal Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **PCP Theorem (Arora–Safra, 1992; Arora–Lund–Motwani–Sudan–Szegedy, 1998):**
> $$\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[O(\log n),\ O(1)]$$
> Equivalently, every NP statement has a proof that can be *verified* by examining only a **constant number of bits**, after flipping only **O(log n) random coins**.

The sharper version, due to **Håstad (1999)**, gives:
$$\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[\log n,\ 3] \text{ with soundness } s = 1/2 + \varepsilon$$

This means NP problems can be verified by reading just **3 bits** of the proof, while the verifier uses only **logarithmic randomness**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spot-Check Examiner"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a strict examiner who must certify a student's 500-page thesis. Reading it fully is impractical. Instead, you flip a fair coin to randomly choose *one single page*, then ask a sharp yes/no question about it. The student submits a structured "proof" (the thesis) carefully arranged so that any genuine mathematical claim can be validated by a *constant* number of spot-checks. If the claim is **false**, the student has no strategy to fool the examiner with probability better than **1/2** — regardless of how cleverly they rearrange the document. The PCP theorem guarantees that *every* NP statement admits such a "self-certifying" format.

### 1.4 Why PCP Matters for Approximation

The PCP theorem is the **cornerstone of hardness of approximation**. It tells us that the apparent difficulty of NP-complete problems extends even to their *approximate solutions*. If we could approximate an NP-hard optimization problem too well, we could solve NP-hard decision problems in polynomial time — contradicting **P ≠ NP**.

### 1.5 Key Constants and Parameters (Bold Emphasis)

- **r(n) = O(log n)** — logarithmic randomness.
- **q(n) = O(1)** — constant number of queries.
- **Completeness c = 1** — perfect acceptance for valid proofs.
- **Soundness s = 1/2** (or any constant **< 1**) — bounded acceptance for false statements.
- **Gap parameter:** the difference **c − s** creates the approximation barrier.
- **OPT** denotes the optimum value of an optimization instance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a PCP System

A PCP system has four interacting components:

1. **Input x ∈ {0,1}ⁿ** — the NP statement to be verified.
2. **Proof string π** — a polynomial-length encoding produced by a (possibly untrusted) prover.
3. **Random string ρ ∈ {0,1}^{r(n)}** — the verifier's private coin flips.
4. **Query pattern q(ρ)** — the positions in **π** selected based on **ρ**; the verifier makes its decision by reading only these bits.

**Decision rule:** The verifier outputs **accept** if the queried bits satisfy a local predicate **Φ(ρ, π_{q(ρ)})**; otherwise **reject**.

### 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why logarithmic randomness?** With **r(n) = c·log n** bits, the verifier can only be in one of **n^c** random states. The proof is therefore structured so that *each random state* tests a *different local property* of the encoded witness.
- **How constant queries work:** The proof **π** is a *redundantly encoded* version of the NP witness. Using error-correcting codes (e.g., the Long Code or Walsh–Hadamard code), the prover commits to the witness in a way that any local inconsistency is amplified.
- **Why a gap appears:** Because the verifier uses randomness, even a *malicious prover* faces a fundamental trade-off — to pass *all* random tests, the proof must be essentially correct; to fail *any* test, the verifier catches it with probability at least **c − s**.

### 2.3 The Gap-Generating Reduction

The PCP theorem does *not* directly say "MAX-3SAT is hard to approximate." Instead, it generates a **gap problem**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gap Problem Definition:** Given an instance of an optimization problem and thresholds **α > β**, the **gap version Gap(α, β)** requires distinguishing whether **OPT ≥ α** or **OPT ≤ β**. The PCP theorem constructs, for any **ε > 0**, a polynomial-time reduction from any NP language to **Gap_{MAX-3SAT}(1, 7/8 + ε)**.

This is the key bridge from *decision* NP-completeness to *approximation* hardness.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **r(n)** | Number of random bits | **O(log n)** |
| **q(n)** | Number of proof queries | **O(1)** (3 in Håstad) |
| **c** | Completeness | **1** |
| **s** | Soundness | **1/2** |
| **n** | Input length | — |
| **π** | Proof oracle string | poly(n) length |
| **V^π** | Verifier with proof access | poly-time |
| **OPT** | Optimal objective value | problem-dependent |
| **Gap(c, s)** | Gap problem thresholds | **c > s** |
| **α, β** | Approximation ratio bounds | e.g., **7/8** for 3SAT |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of PCP Theorem |
|---|---|
| **Cryptography** | Foundation of *zero-knowledge proofs* (e.g., zk-SNARKs, zk-STARKs) used in blockchain and secure computation. |
| **Cloud Computing** | Enables *succinct verifiable computation* — clients verify remote server computations by reading only a few proof bits. |
| **Coding Theory** | Bridges to *locally testable codes* (LTCs) and *locally decodable codes* (LDCs). |
| **Hardness of Approximation** | Provides *unconditional* inapproximability results assuming **P ≠ NP**. |
| **Mechanism Design** | Informs computational complexity of combinatorial auctions. |

### 2.6 Inapproximability Highlights (Consequences)

| Problem | Best Approximation | Hardness Threshold (from PCP) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAX-3SAT** | **7/8** (random) | **7/8 + ε** | Håstad |
| **MAX-CLIQUE** | **O(n / (log n)^2)** | **n^{1 − ε}** | Zuckerman / Håstad |
| **SET-COVER** | **(1 + o(1))·ln n** | **(1 − ε)·ln n** | Raz–Safra, Feige |
| **MIN-VERTEX-COVER** | **2 − Θ(1/√log n)** | **7/6 + ε** (classical PCP gap) | Dinur–Regev |
| **TSP (general)** | **O(log n)** | **any constant** | Papadimitriou–Vempala |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Definition of Gap Problem

**Definition (Gap-MAX-3SAT):** Given a 3-CNF formula **φ** with **m** clauses, define
$$f(\varphi) = \frac{\text{number of clauses satisfied by the best assignment}}{m}$$
The **Gap_{MAX-3SAT}(1, 7/8 + ε)** problem is: given **φ**, decide whether
$$f(\varphi) = 1 \quad \text{(all clauses satisfiable)}$$
$$f(\varphi) \le 7/8 + \varepsilon \quad \text{(no assignment satisfies more than a } 7/8 + \varepsilon \text{ fraction)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: How PCP Implies Gap-Hardness of MAX-3SAT

We now derive the gap-producing reduction in full detail.

**Step 1 — Start from an NP-complete language.** Let **L ∈ NP** and let **x ∈ {0,1}ⁿ** be an arbitrary instance. By the **Cook–Levin theorem**, there is a polynomial-time reduction to a 3-SAT instance **φ_x** such that:

$$
x \in L \iff \varphi_x \text{ is satisfiable}
$$

This yields a *clean* decision: **f(φ_x) = 1** iff **x ∈ L**.

**Step 2 — Apply the PCP encoding to the proof string.** For the same language **L**, the PCP theorem guarantees a PCP verifier **V** with parameters
$$r(n) = c_1 \log n, \quad q(n) = 3, \quad s = 1/2$$
such that:
$$
x \in L \iff \exists \pi: \Pr_\rho \bigl[ V^{\pi}(x, \rho) \text{ accepts} \bigr] = 1
$$
$$
x \notin L \iff \forall \pi: \Pr_\rho \bigl[ V^{\pi}(x, \rho) \text{ accepts} \bigr] \le 1/2
$$

**Step 3 — Translate random-string acceptance into a 3-CNF formula.** The verifier's decision on input **x**, randomness **ρ**, and queried proof bits **π_{i_1}, π_{i_2}, π_{i_3}** is encoded as a Boolean predicate
$$
\Phi(x, \rho, \pi_{i_1}, \pi_{i_2}, \pi_{i_3}) \in \{0, 1\}
$$
We can convert **Φ** into a 3-CNF formula **ψ_{x,ρ}** over the variables **{π_{i_1}, π_{i_2}, π_{i_3}}** (with possible auxiliary variables) such that the *number of satisfying assignments* of **ψ_{x,ρ}** equals **1** if the verifier accepts on **(x, ρ)** and **0** otherwise. The size of **ψ_{x,ρ}** is **O(1)** — a fixed constant per random string.

**Step 4 — Aggregate over all random strings.** Define the full PCP-encoded 3-CNF formula:
$$
\Phi_x \;\equiv\; \bigwedge_{\rho \in \{0,1\}^{r(n)}} \psi_{x, \rho}
$$
The total number of clauses is
$$
M \;=\; 2^{r(n)} \cdot O(1) \;=\; \mathrm{poly}(n)
$$
because **r(n) = O(log n)** implies **2^{r(n)} = poly(n)**.

**Step 5 — Compute the satisfaction fraction.**
- **Case x ∈ L:** There exists a proof **π*** that satisfies *every* random check. Therefore **π*** satisfies every clause of **Φ_x**, giving
$$
f(\Phi_x) = 1
$$
- **Case x ∉ L:** For *any* candidate proof **π**, the verifier rejects on at least **1/2** of the random strings. Hence
$$
f(\Phi_x) \;\le\; 1 - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\text{const}}{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(crude bound)}
$$

To sharpen this to the celebrated **7/8 bound**, we use a refined encoding (Håstad's trick) where each local predicate is encoded as a *3-CNF* with a base acceptance of **7/8** for random bits — yielding:

$$
x \notin L \;\Longrightarrow\; f(\Phi_x) \;\le\; \frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the gap reduction.** Combining both cases:
$$
x \in L \;\Longrightarrow\; f(\Phi_x) = 1
$$
$$
x \notin L \;\Longrightarrow\; f(\Phi_x) \le \frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon
$$
Hence deciding **Gap_{MAX-3SAT}(1, 7/8 + ε)** is **NP-hard**. □

### 3.3 Derivation: From Gap-Hardness to Inapproximability

**Theorem:** Assuming **P ≠ NP**, there is no polynomial-time algorithm that approximates **MAX-3SAT** within a factor greater than **7/8 + ε** for any **ε > 0**.

**Proof by contradiction:**

- **Step 1:** Suppose **A** is a polynomial-time **(7/8 + ε + δ)-approximation** for **MAX-3SAT**, with **δ > 0**.
- **Step 2:** On input **Φ_x**, the algorithm **A** returns a value **A(Φ_x)** satisfying
$$
\max\!\Bigl(1,\ \frac{7}{8} + \varepsilon + \delta\Bigr) \cdot \mathrm{OPT} \;\ge\; A(\Phi_x) \;\ge\; \mathrm{OPT}
$$

- **Step 3:** Distinguish two regimes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f(\Phi_x) = 1 \text{ (i.e., } x \in L\text{):} \quad & A(\Phi_x) \ge 1 \\
\text{If } f(\Phi_x) \le 7/8 + \varepsilon \text{ (i.e., } x \notin L\text{):} \quad & A(\Phi_x) \le (7/8 + \varepsilon + \delta) \cdot (7/8 + \varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Step 4:** Choose **δ** small enough so that the two ranges **{A ≥ 1}** and **{A ≤ (7/8 + ε + δ)(7/8 + ε) < 1}** are *disjoint*. Then we can decide whether **x ∈ L** in polynomial time, contradicting **P ≠ NP**.

Therefore no such approximation algorithm exists. □

### 3.4 Compact Analytical Matrix of Implications

| Source NP Problem | Gap Problem Constructed | Resulting Hardness Ratio | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Any **L ∈ NP** | Gap-MAX-3SAT(1, 7/8 + ε) | **7/8 + ε** | Håstad (1999) |
| Any **L ∈ NP** | Gap-INDEPENDENT-SET(n, n^{1−ε}) | **n^{1−ε}** | Zuckerman (2006) |
| Any **L ∈ NP** | Gap-SET-COVER((1−ε)ln n, ln n) | **(1−ε)ln n** | Raz–Safra (1998) |
| Any **L ∈ NP** | Gap-VERTEX-COVER(2, 7/6 − ε) | **7/6** | Khot–Reis (2005) |
| Any **L ∈ NP** | Gap-MAX-CUT(1, 1 − ε) | **1** (worst-case) | PCP via 2-prover games |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
